questions.explanation: Zeus manages the cluster configuration database and uses Paxos consensus protocol to ensure configuration consistency across all CVMs. It stores critical cluster parameters, and "zeus_config_printer" outputs its contents.

questions.questionText: An administrator is configuring SNMP v3 for integration with a Nagios monitoring server. Which SNMP v3 security features must be configured on the Nutanix cluster?

options.text: Community string and trap destination only
Username, authentication protocol (MD5/SHA), and privacy protocol (DES/AES)
API key and shared secret
Certificate-based mutual TLS authentication

questions.explanation: SNMP v3 requires a security model that includes a username (USM), authentication protocol (MD5 or SHA for message integrity), and privacy protocol (DES or AES for encryption). Community strings are only used with SNMP v2c.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to stop all services on a single CVM for hardware maintenance. Which command stops all services on only the local CVM?

options.text: genesis stop all
cluster stop
cvm_shutdown -P now
genesis stop prism stargate curator cerebro

questions.explanation: The `cluster stop` command (option B) is a **cluster-wide** operation that stops services on ALL CVMs, not just the local one. To stop services on only the local CVM, use `genesis stop <service_name>` for each service. Option D (`genesis stop prism stargate curator cerebro`) correctly stops the major services on only the local CVM. For full hardware maintenance, the standard procedure is `cvm_shutdown -P now` which gracefully stops all services and powers off the CVM.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to bulk-create 50 VMs using the Nutanix API. To avoid rate limiting issues, which approach should be used?

options.text: Send 50 individual POST requests to /api/nutanix/v3/vms sequentially
Use the batch API endpoint POST /api/nutanix/v3/batch with multiple VM creation requests
Use POST /api/nutanix/v3/vms with an array of 50 VM specs in the body
Increase the API rate limit via ncli cluster edit api-rate-limit=unlimited

questions.explanation: The v3 batch API endpoint allows multiple operations in a single HTTP request, reducing the number of individual API calls and avoiding rate limiting. This is the recommended approach for bulk operations rather than sequential individual requests.

questions.questionText: An administrator observes that a Curator full scan has not completed in over 24 hours. Which command shows the current Curator scan status and time elapsed?

options.text: curator_cli display_data --type=scan_status
links http://localhost:2010
ncli curator get-scan-status
genesis status curator

questions.explanation: Curator's status page is accessible via the local web interface at http://localhost:2010 on the CVM. The "links" text browser can access this page to show active scans, scan history, and detailed operational metrics. curator_cli also provides scan information.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to verify the Prism Element web console service is running on the CVM. Which specific service must be checked?

options.text: prism
prism_gateway
prism_web_server
nginx

questions.explanation: The Prism service (shown as "prism" in genesis/cluster status) is the CVM service that provides the Prism Element web console. It handles the web UI, REST API endpoints, and authentication. It can be checked via "genesis status" or "cluster status."

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to configure an external KMS (SafeNet KeySecure) for data-at-rest encryption. Which protocol does Nutanix use to communicate with external KMS servers?

options.text: KMIP (Key Management Interoperability Protocol)
HTTPS REST API
SNMP v3
SSH key exchange

questions.explanation: Nutanix uses KMIP (Key Management Interoperability Protocol) to communicate with external key management servers such as SafeNet KeySecure, Vormetric, and IBM Security Key Lifecycle Manager. KMIP is the industry standard for key management operations.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to check if there are any pending alerts related to disk failures on the cluster. Which ncli command filters alerts by component type?

options.text: ncli alerts ls entity_type=disk
ncli alerts ls component=disk resolved=false
ncli disk ls --alerts
ncli alerts ls category=storage

questions.explanation: The "ncli alerts ls" command supports filtering by entity_type to narrow alerts to specific components such as disk, host, or VM. This helps administrators quickly identify hardware-related alerts.

questions.questionText: An administrator is creating a VM via acli with 4 vCPUs, 8 GB of memory, and a 100 GB disk. Which command creates this VM correctly?

options.text: acli vm.create MyVM num_vcpus=4 memory=8G num_cores_per_vcpu=1
acli vm.create MyVM num_vcpus=4 memory=8192 num_cores_per_vcpu=1
acli vm.create MyVM cpu=4 ram=8192 disk_size=100G
ncli vm create name=MyVM vcpus=4 memory=8192

questions.explanation: The "acli vm.create" command uses num_vcpus for CPU count and memory in megabytes (8192 MB = 8 GB). Disk creation is typically a separate step using "acli vm.disk_create." The memory parameter expects values in MB, not shorthand like "8G."

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to identify which service handles the background garbage collection of deleted data, dead extents, and storage optimization. Which CVM service performs these tasks?

options.text: Curator
Stargate
Medusa
Chronos

questions.explanation: Curator is the background service responsible for garbage collection, dead extent removal, data compaction, ILM (Information Lifecycle Management) tiering, and disk balancing across the cluster. It performs both partial and full scans at configurable intervals.

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to check the v3 API to verify cluster health status programmatically. Which API endpoint returns cluster health information?

options.text: GET https://prism-central:9440/api/nutanix/v3/clusters/list
POST https://prism-central:9440/api/nutanix/v3/clusters/list
GET https://prism-central:9440/api/nutanix/v3/health_checks
GET https://prism-central:9440/api/nutanix/v2.0/cluster/health

questions.explanation: The v3 API uses POST for list operations. POST /clusters/list returns cluster entities including health status, resource utilization, and configuration details in the intent-based response format with spec, status, and metadata sections.

questions.questionText: An administrator notices that Zeus configuration is inconsistent across CVMs. Which consensus protocol does Zeus use to maintain configuration consistency?

options.text: Paxos
Raft
Two-phase commit
Gossip protocol

questions.explanation: Zeus uses the Paxos consensus protocol to replicate and maintain configuration consistency across all CVMs in the cluster. Paxos ensures that even with CVM failures, the configuration database remains consistent and available.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to force a Curator full scan to reclaim space after deleting a large number of snapshots. Which command triggers an on-demand full scan?

options.text: curator_cli start_scan type=full
allssh "genesis restart curator"
ncli curator trigger-full-scan
links http://localhost:2010/start_full_scan

questions.explanation: The "curator_cli start_scan type=full" command manually triggers a full Curator scan. Full scans examine all data for garbage collection, compaction, and optimization opportunities, unlike partial scans that only examine recent changes.

questions.questionText: An administrator is troubleshooting Prism Central communication issues with a registered Prism Element cluster. Which service port must be accessible between Prism Central and Prism Element?

options.text: Port 9440
Port 2020
Port 2009
Port 8443

questions.explanation: Port 9440 is the primary HTTPS port used for Prism web console access and REST API communication. Prism Central communicates with registered Prism Element clusters over port 9440 for cluster management, monitoring, and API proxying.

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to review all protection domains and their replication schedules on the cluster. Which ncli command lists all configured protection domains?

options.text: ncli protection-domain ls
ncli pd list
ncli replication ls
cerebro_cli list_pd

questions.explanation: The "ncli protection-domain ls" (or the equivalent "ncli pd ls") command lists all protection domains configured on the cluster, including their type, associated VMs/volume groups, and replication schedules.

questions.questionText: An administrator is writing a script to automate VM provisioning via the v3 API. The script needs to wait for a VM creation task to complete. How should the task be monitored?

options.text: Poll GET /api/nutanix/v3/tasks/{task_uuid} until the status shows "SUCCEEDED"
Subscribe to a WebSocket at wss://prism-central:9440/api/nutanix/v3/tasks/stream
Poll GET /api/nutanix/v2.0/tasks/poll with the task UUID
Check GET /api/nutanix/v3/vms/{uuid} repeatedly until the VM status is "COMPLETE"

questions.explanation: After initiating an async operation, the v3 API returns a task UUID. Administrators should poll the tasks endpoint (GET /tasks/{task_uuid}) to monitor completion. The task entity includes status, progress percentage, and error details if the operation fails.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to move a CVM's IP address to a different subnet during a network migration. After updating the CVM's network configuration, which command updates the cluster's awareness of the new CVM IP?

options.text: ncli cluster edit-cvm-ip old-ip=<old> new-ip=<new>
cluster destroy && cluster create
Modify /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/ifcfg-eth0 and restart networking
ncli host edit id=<host_id> cvm-ip=<new_ip>

questions.explanation: The "ncli cluster edit-cvm-ip" command (or the equivalent Prism workflow) safely updates the CVM IP address in the cluster configuration, including Zeus and all service registrations. Manual network file edits alone would cause cluster inconsistency.

questions.questionText: An administrator discovers that the Stargate service on one CVM has crashed and is not auto-restarting. Which service is responsible for monitoring and auto-restarting failed CVM services?

options.text: Genesis
Zeus
Prism
Watchdog

questions.explanation: Genesis is the foundational service on each CVM responsible for monitoring all other services and automatically restarting them if they crash. If Genesis itself is healthy but a service is not restarting, manual intervention via "genesis restart <service>" may be needed.

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to configure the cluster to use an NTP server for time synchronization. Which ncli command configures NTP servers on the cluster?

options.text: ncli cluster add-to-ntp-servers servers=<ntp_server_ip>
ncli cluster edit ntp-servers=<ntp_server_ip>
ncli ntp add server=<ntp_server_ip>
ncli cluster update time-server=<ntp_server_ip>

questions.explanation: The "ncli cluster add-to-ntp-servers" command adds NTP server addresses to the cluster configuration. NTP synchronization is critical for cluster operations, certificate validation, log correlation, and replication scheduling.

questions.questionText: An administrator is examining the v3 API response for a VM and sees both "spec" and "status" sections. What is the architectural difference between these two sections?

options.text: "spec" contains the desired state; "status" contains the current actual state
"spec" is read-only; "status" is writable
"spec" contains metadata; "status" contains the VM configuration
"spec" is for v3 API; "status" is the v2 API compatibility layer

questions.explanation: The v3 intent-based API follows a declarative model: "spec" defines the desired/intended state submitted by the user, while "status" reflects the current applied state of the entity. Any discrepancy indicates a pending change or error.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to verify which encryption method is configured on a specific storage container. Which ncli command shows encryption details for containers?

options.text: ncli container ls name=<container_name>
ncli encryption ls
ncli container get-encryption-status name=<container_name>
ncli data-at-rest-encryption status

questions.explanation: The "ncli container ls" command (with or without a name filter) shows container properties including encryption status, replication factor, compression, and deduplication settings. Encryption status is displayed as part of the container's detailed properties.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to run a health check specifically for network connectivity between CVMs. Which NCC check should be executed?

options.text: ncc health_checks network_checks cvm_ntp_check
ncc health_checks network_checks inter_cvm_connectivity_check
ncc health_checks run_all --filter=network
ncli ncc run check=network_connectivity

questions.explanation: NCC includes specific checks that can be run individually. The inter-CVM connectivity check validates network communication between all CVMs in the cluster, which is critical for cluster services that rely on inter-CVM communication.

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to power on all VMs in a specific protection domain after a DR failover. Which approach is the most efficient?

options.text: Use acli vm.power_on for each VM individually
Use the protection domain activate operation from ncli or Prism
Use the v3 API to send parallel POST requests to power on each VM
Restart all CVMs to trigger automatic VM power-on

questions.explanation: During DR operations, activating a protection domain (failover) through ncli or Prism automatically handles powering on the protected VMs, restoring network configurations, and managing the replication state. This is more efficient and reliable than individual VM power-on commands.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to check whether the cluster is configured for RF2 or RF3 redundancy. Which command shows the cluster's current replication factor configuration?

options.text: ncli cluster info
ncli container ls
zeus_config_printer | grep replication
acli cluster.get

questions.explanation: The replication factor (RF2 or RF3) is configured at the storage container level, not the cluster level. "ncli container ls" shows each container's replication factor. While "ncli cluster info" shows the desired redundancy factor, container-level settings show the actual RF applied to data.

questions.questionText: An administrator receives an alert that Medusa (Cassandra) metadata operations are slow. Which diagnostic command checks the status of Cassandra compactions on the CVM?

options.text: nodetool compactionstats
cassandra_status --compaction
medusa_printer --compaction_info
ncli medusa check-compaction

questions.explanation: The "nodetool compactionstats" command shows active and pending Cassandra compaction operations. Excessive compaction activity can cause metadata operation latency, as compaction competes with normal read/write operations for I/O resources.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to update the DNS server configuration on the cluster. Which ncli command sets the DNS servers?

options.text: ncli cluster add-to-name-servers servers=<dns_ip>
ncli cluster edit dns-servers=<dns_ip>
ncli dns add server=<dns_ip>
ncli network update dns=<dns_ip>

questions.explanation: The "ncli cluster add-to-name-servers" command adds DNS server addresses to the cluster configuration. DNS is essential for hostname resolution, Active Directory integration, and external service communication.

questions.questionText: An administrator is configuring a webhook to notify an external system when specific events occur in the Nutanix cluster. Which Prism Central feature enables event-driven HTTP callbacks?

options.text: REST API webhooks configured under Prism Central > Settings
SNMP trap forwarding to an HTTP gateway
Syslog forwarding with HTTP transport
Nutanix Calm runbooks triggered by alert policies

questions.explanation: Prism Central supports webhook configurations that send HTTP POST notifications to external endpoints when specified cluster events occur. This enables event-driven integration with ticketing systems, orchestration platforms, and custom automation.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to troubleshoot a failed live migration of a VM between two AHV hosts. Which log file should be examined for migration-related errors?

options.text: /home/nutanix/data/logs/acropolis.out
/home/nutanix/data/logs/stargate.INFO
/var/log/libvirt/qemu/<vm_name>.log
/home/nutanix/data/logs/cerebro.INFO

questions.explanation: The Acropolis service manages VM operations on AHV, including live migration. The acropolis.out log file contains details about VM lifecycle operations, migration attempts, failure reasons, and host scheduling decisions.

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to enable SCMA (Security Configuration Management Automation) to run on a scheduled basis. What is the primary function of SCMA?

options.text: Automatically detect and report deviations from the applied STIG security baseline
Encrypt all data at rest using AES-256
Manage SSL certificate rotation for Prism services
Configure firewall rules between CVMs and hypervisor hosts

questions.explanation: SCMA continuously monitors the cluster's security configuration against the applied STIG baseline and reports any deviations. It can be scheduled to run periodically, generating compliance reports and alerting administrators to configuration drift.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to determine the task UUID returned from a recently submitted v3 API call to create a VM. Where in the API response is this task UUID found?

options.text: In the response body under "status.execution_context.task_uuid"
In the HTTP Location response header
In the response body under "metadata.task_id"
In the X-Nutanix-Task-Id response header

questions.explanation: When a v3 API operation creates an asynchronous task, the response body includes the task UUID in the "status.execution_context.task_uuid" field. This UUID is used to poll the /tasks endpoint for completion status.

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to attach an ISO image to a running VM for OS installation. Which acli command mounts the ISO to the VM's CD-ROM?

options.text: acli vm.disk_update <vm_name> ide.0 clone_from_image=<iso_name>
acli vm.disk_create <vm_name> cdrom=true clone_from_image=<iso_name>
acli vm.cdrom_insert <vm_name> image=<iso_name>
acli vm.mount_iso <vm_name> iso_name=<iso_name>

questions.explanation: The "acli vm.disk_create" command with the cdrom=true parameter creates a virtual CD-ROM device and clones the specified ISO image from the image service. This attaches the ISO to the VM for booting or software installation.

questions.questionText: An administrator is running "allssh" commands across the cluster but one CVM is not responding. What does the "allssh" utility do?

options.text: Executes an SSH command on all CVMs in the cluster simultaneously
Restarts SSH services on all CVMs
Tests SSH connectivity to all hypervisor hosts
Distributes SSH keys to all nodes in the cluster

questions.explanation: The "allssh" utility is a Nutanix helper command that executes a specified command on all CVMs in the cluster via SSH. It is commonly used for cluster-wide diagnostics, log collection, and service management operations.

questions.questionText: An administrator is configuring the v3 API for a CI/CD pipeline and needs to set appropriate HTTP headers. Which Content-Type header is required for v3 API requests?

options.text: application/json
application/xml
multipart/form-data
text/plain

questions.explanation: The Nutanix v3 REST API exclusively uses JSON for request and response bodies. All API requests must include the "Content-Type: application/json" header, and responses are returned in JSON format.

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to run a command across all CVMs to collect Stargate log files. Which command collects logs from all CVMs?

options.text: allssh "ls -la /home/nutanix/data/logs/stargate.INFO"
ncli cluster collect-logs service=stargate
ncc log_collector --service=stargate
logbay collect --service=stargate

questions.explanation: Logbay is the Nutanix log collection utility that gathers logs from all CVMs and hypervisor hosts. The "logbay collect" command with service filters creates a compressed bundle of relevant logs for troubleshooting or support case attachment.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to check the disk status on all nodes to identify any offline or degraded disks. Which ncli command lists all physical disks and their states?

options.text: ncli disk ls
ncli host list-disks
ncli storage list-physical-disks
ncli container disk-status

questions.explanation: The "ncli disk ls" command lists all physical disks across the cluster, showing their status (online, offline, bad), tier (SSD, HDD), capacity, and which host they belong to. This is the primary command for disk health verification.

questions.questionText: An administrator receives an alert that a CVM's /home partition is running low on space. Which directory typically consumes the most space and should be checked for old log files?

options.text: /home/nutanix/data/logs/
/home/nutanix/prism/
/home/nutanix/config/
/home/nutanix/bin/

questions.explanation: The /home/nutanix/data/logs/ directory contains service log files that can accumulate significant space over time, especially if verbose logging is enabled or if services are generating excessive error logs. Log rotation and cleanup should be verified.

questions.questionText: An administrator is integrating Nutanix with vCenter for managing VMs on ESXi hosts. Which Nutanix service handles the communication with vCenter Server?

options.text: Uhura
Acropolis
Prism
Genesis

questions.explanation: Uhura is the Nutanix CVM service responsible for communication with external hypervisor management platforms, including VMware vCenter. It handles VM discovery, operation proxying, and event synchronization between Nutanix and vCenter.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to delete a VM snapshot named "snap-daily-01" for the VM "FileServer01." Which acli command removes this snapshot?

options.text: acli snapshot.delete snap-daily-01
acli vm.snapshot_delete FileServer01 snapshot_name=snap-daily-01
ncli snapshot delete name=snap-daily-01
acli snapshot.remove vm=FileServer01 name=snap-daily-01

questions.explanation: The "acli snapshot.delete" command removes a specified snapshot by name or UUID. Snapshots are global objects identified by their name, so the VM name is not required in the delete command — only the snapshot identifier.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to verify that the Paxos leader election is functioning correctly for Zeus. Which approach provides this information?

options.text: zeus_config_printer and checking the leader/epoch information
ncli cluster info --paxos-status
paxos_cli leader_status
genesis status zeus --verbose

questions.explanation: The "zeus_config_printer" output includes Paxos-related information such as the current leader CVM and epoch number. Examining this output across multiple CVMs helps verify consensus and leader election health.

questions.questionText: An administrator is writing an API script and needs to paginate through a large list of VMs using the v3 API. Which parameters in the POST /vms/list request body control pagination?

options.text: "offset" and "length" in the request body
"page" and "per_page" as query parameters
"start" and "count" in the request body
"skip" and "limit" as query parameters

questions.explanation: The v3 API list operations use "offset" (starting position) and "length" (number of results) in the JSON request body for pagination. The response includes "total_matches" to determine how many pages remain.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to check which CVM is currently the Prism Leader (the CVM serving the Prism web interface). Which command identifies the Prism Leader?

options.text: curl -s localhost:2019/prism/leader
ncli cluster get-prism-leader
zeus_config_printer | grep prism_leader
cluster status | grep PRISM

questions.explanation: The Prism Leader can be identified by querying the local CVM's Prism service endpoint. The "curl localhost:2019/prism/leader" command (or similar internal endpoint) returns the IP of the CVM currently serving as the Prism Leader, which handles all web UI requests.

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to enable hardware-based encryption using Self-Encrypting Drives (SEDs) on the cluster. What is required in addition to SED drives being present?

options.text: A configured Key Management Server (KMS) and enabling SED encryption from Prism
Only the SEDs — hardware encryption is enabled by default
FIPS 140-2 certification and software encryption must also be enabled
TPM modules on each host and SED firmware update from Nutanix support

questions.explanation: Hardware-based encryption with SEDs still requires a KMS to manage the authentication keys used to lock/unlock the drives. SED encryption must be explicitly enabled through Prism after the KMS is configured, even if the drives support hardware encryption natively.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to troubleshoot intermittent VM disk I/O errors and suspects an issue with the storage path. Which command shows the current I/O path and data locality information for a VM's virtual disks?

options.text: vdisk_config_printer
ncli vdisk ls
stargate_cli show_vdisks
acli vm.get <vm_name> include_vdisk_info=true

questions.explanation: The "vdisk_config_printer" command displays detailed virtual disk configuration, including the current I/O path, hosting extent store, data locality status, and replication state. This information is critical for diagnosing storage performance issues related to non-local I/O paths.

questions.questionText: You notice a VM on your Nutanix cluster has CPU ready time of 15%. What does this indicate, and what should you do first?

options.text: Memory pressure; increase VM RAM immediately
CPU contention; check CPU overcommit ratio and consider vMotion or vCPU reduction
Network latency; verify physical NIC status
Storage I/O bottleneck; analyze latency per vDisk

questions.explanation: CPU ready time >10% indicates the vCPU was ready to run but waiting for physical CPU time. This is contention. First, verify CPU overcommit ratio via Prism > Dashboard > Host CPU utilization, then use vMotion or reduce vCPU allocation for overcommitted VMs.

questions.questionText: A customer wants to deploy a VM with a NUMA-aware configuration on a 2-socket Nutanix host. Which vCPU configuration would optimize locality?

options.text: 4 vCPU (2 vCPU per socket)
8 vCPU (4 vCPU per socket)
12 vCPU (6 vCPU per socket)
16 vCPU (8 vCPU per socket)

questions.explanation: NUMA locality is optimized when vCPU allocation is balanced across socket boundaries. A 4 vCPU VM pinned with 2 vCPU per socket on a 2-socket host keeps memory traffic local and reduces remote NUMA access latency. The host must have sufficient physical cores to support this vCPU allocation (e.g., ≥2 physical cores per socket minimum). Verify NUMA pinning via `numactl --hardware` on guest OS and check Prism > VM > Properties > vCPU placement. Oversized vCPU configurations (options C/D) exceed socket capacity, forcing cross-socket memory access and increasing latency.

questions.questionText: During NGT VSS snapshot operations on a Windows VM, which component must be running for application-consistent snapshots?

options.text: Nutanix Volume Shadow Copy Service (VSS) provider
Guest Customization Service
Nutanix Insights daemon
Prism Analytics Engine

questions.explanation: NGT installs the Nutanix VSS provider on Windows VMs. This enables VSS-based application-consistent snapshots. Verify installation: Programs & Features > Nutanix Guest Tools > VSS provider listed. Without it, only crash-consistent snapshots are possible.

questions.questionText: You're analyzing storage latency in Prism and see a single vDisk with >20ms latency while cluster average is 2ms. What's the most likely cause, and where should you investigate?

options.text: Network congestion; run `nstat -rtnap | grep TCP_LISTEN` from CVM
Hot vDisk with high IOPS; analyze via Prism > Storage > Volume Group > Per-Disk analysis
Insufficient host RAM; check memory pressure and balloon driver stats
CPU saturation; increase vCPU count

questions.explanation: Per-vDisk latency analysis in Prism shows which specific disks are hot. >20ms indicates I/O bottleneck on that vDisk, not a cluster-wide issue. Check Prism > Dashboards > Storage > Volume Groups, then drill into the specific vDisk's IOPS/latency metrics. May need to migrate vDisk to faster tier or increase replica count.

questions.questionText: A VM has memory overcommit enabled with ballooning active. You notice guest OS memory pressure is high. What does this indicate?

options.text: Ballooning is working correctly; no action needed
Hypervisor memory overhead is consuming more than expected
The VM is reclaiming pages at host level; performance may degrade further
Storage latency is causing memory swaps

questions.explanation: When ballooning is active and memory pressure rises, the hypervisor is reclaiming memory from the guest. This moves memory to host swap, causing VM performance degradation. Solution: add physical host RAM, reduce VM memory overcommit ratio via Prism settings, or migrate VMs off host. Monitor via `acpi_memory_hotplug` statistics.

questions.questionText: You need to configure a hard VM affinity rule to keep VMs on the same host. Which Prism interface would you use?

options.text: Prism > VM > Policies > Scheduling
Prism > Cluster > Affinity Rules
Prism > VM > VM Affinity > Create Affinity Policy
Prism > Infrastructure > Host Grouping

questions.explanation: Hard affinity rules prevent VMs from being separated across hosts or force them to stay together. Access via Prism > VM > VM Affinity > Create Affinity Policy. Select "Hard Affinity" to enforce the rule. Soft affinity is preferred, applied via anti-affinity or host labels.

questions.questionText: Your Nutanix cluster has 4 hosts with 20 cores each. You're planning VM workloads and want to maintain <70% CPU utilization. Based on Prism capacity analysis, what's your safe vCPU allocation ceiling?

options.text: 160 vCPU (80 cores × 2x overcommit)
112 vCPU (80 cores × 1.4x overcommit at 70%)
80 vCPU (1:1 ratio)
224 vCPU (80 cores × 2.8x overcommit)

questions.explanation: Safe allocation = Physical cores × Target utilization ÷ Overcommit ratio. At 70% target: 80 physical cores × 0.70 = 56 effective cores. With typical 2:1 overcommit = 112 vCPU ceiling. Verify via Prism > Analysis > Capacity > CPU Runway reports and VM-level CPU ready time metrics.

questions.questionText: For BCDR planning, you need <1-hour RPO with synchronous replication between two data centers. Which Metro Avail feature should you deploy?

options.text: Async replication with 1-hour snapshot schedule
NearSync (1-15 minute RPO)
Sync metro cluster (zero RPO, <5ms latency required)
Multi-site disaster recovery with Leap

questions.explanation: Sync metro deployment with Metro Avail provides zero RPO for truly continuous protection. Requires <5ms network latency between sites. If latency is higher, use NearSync (1-15min RPO) instead. Async replication has 1hr+ RPO depending on snapshot schedule. Configure via Prism > Availability > Metro Avail Settings.

questions.questionText: You're deploying Leap for planned failover testing. Which step must occur BEFORE initiating test failover?

options.text: Disable production VMs on source site
Create recovery plan with boot order, network mapping, and pre/post scripts
Delete snapshots from source site
Increase CVM RAM on DR site to match production

questions.explanation: Leap recovery plans are prerequisites for any failover operation. Define boot order (critical VMs first), network mapping (IP/VLAN changes), and runbooks (pre-scripts for dependencies, post-scripts for validation). Test failover runs against the plan without affecting production. Configure via Prism > Disaster Recovery > Recovery Plans.

questions.questionText: Your Windows VM needs self-service restore capability. Which combination of tools is required?

options.text: Prism API + custom scripts
NGT installed + VSS snapshots + SMB Previous Versions enabled
Backup software + Leap recovery plans
Standalone snapshot schedules + manual restore scripts

questions.explanation: NGT enables VSS snapshots (app-consistent). Previous Versions (SMB shadow copies) makes snapshots accessible to users via "Previous Versions" tab on shared folders. Users can self-restore files without IT intervention. Enable via: 1) NGT VSS provider, 2) SMB Previous Versions registry key, 3) Snapshot schedule, 4) User access to backup share.

questions.questionText: During CVM maintenance mode entry, what is the minimum recommended RAM for the CVM on a 4-socket production host?

options.text: 8 GB
16 GB
20-32 GB (depends on node size)
64 GB

questions.explanation: 20-32GB is the recommended minimum for production CVMs. Sizing depends on cluster size and workload. Enter maintenance mode: Prism > Infrastructure > Hosts > Select Host > Actions > Enter Maintenance Mode. During this, CVM continues managing data, so sufficient RAM is critical. Under-sized CVMs cause performance degradation.

questions.questionText: You notice TX/RX throughput drops on a VM despite sufficient network capacity. Which optimization should you apply?

options.text: Increase vCPU count
Enable virtio-net driver in guest OS
Reduce MTU size to 1400 bytes
Add more vNICs to the VM

questions.explanation: Virtio-net is the high-performance paravirtual NIC driver. Legacy e1000 emulation is slower. Install virtio-net via: Linux: `ethtool -K eth0 sg on tso on`, Windows: Nutanix Guest Tools. Monitor via Prism > VM > Network stats. Virtio-net provides 2-3x higher throughput.

questions.questionText: A protection domain replicates VMs every 1 hour via async replication. A host fails at 11:30 AM; last snapshot was at 11:00 AM. What is the RPO for this scenario?

options.text: Zero RPO (continuous protection)
30 minutes (data from 11:00-11:30 is lost)
1 hour RPO (design spec)
Depends on failover method

questions.explanation: RPO = time between last successful replication and failure. With 1-hour schedule and failure at 11:30, RPO = 30 minutes (data from 11:00-11:30 is lost). Configure via Prism > Protection Domain > Replication Settings. NearSync achieves 1-15 min RPO; Sync metro achieves zero RPO.

questions.questionText: When adding VMs to a protection domain, which operation must complete before remote replication begins?

options.text: Create policy tag on all VMs
Initial snapshot created and seeded to remote site
Disable snapshots on source site
Increase protection domain bandwidth limit

questions.explanation: Initial seeding (baseline copy of snapshots) to remote site must complete first. Configure protection domain: Prism > Protection Domain > Create > Select VMs > Set replication schedule > Seeding begins automatically. Verify seeding status via Prism > Replication Jobs. Only incremental snapshots are sent afterward.

questions.questionText: You're planning DR site capacity for 50 production VMs (avg 4 vCPU, 8 GB RAM each). What minimum capacity should the DR site have?

options.text: 50 VMs worth (same as production: 200 vCPU, 400 GB RAM)
25-30 VMs worth (50% of production)
75-100% of production capacity
10-20% of production (just for critical tier)

questions.explanation: DR site should have 75-100% of production capacity to handle failover. Many failures are unpredictable; assume worst-case all production VMs fail simultaneously. If cost-prohibitive, implement tiered failover: critical (100%), important (75%), non-critical (50%). Configure via capacity analysis reports and Leap recovery plan priorities.

questions.questionText: What is the primary purpose of the reverse replication feature in async replication?

options.text: Restore production from DR backup
Mirror data from DR back to production for failback
Increase RPO by 50%
Reduce snapshot frequency

questions.explanation: Reverse replication enables failback: after DR failover, sync changes made on DR site back to production for recovery. Configure via Prism > Protection Domain > Replication Settings > Enable Reverse Replication. This avoids data loss during failback by syncing DR changes back to healed production site.

questions.questionText: Your snapshot retention policy specifies: daily for 7 days, weekly for 4 weeks, monthly for 12 months. Where do you configure this in Nutanix?

options.text: Prism > VM > Snapshot Settings
Prism > Protection Domain > Replication Settings > Retention Policy
Prism > Capacity > Retention Rules
Prism > Storage > Volume Groups > Retention Schedule

questions.explanation: Retention policies are configured per protection domain to control snapshot lifecycle. Access via Prism > Protection Domain > Select PD > Replication Settings > Retention tab. Define daily/weekly/monthly retention counts. This prevents storage bloat and ensures compliance with data retention requirements.

questions.questionText: You're implementing planned failover for maintenance. What is the critical difference from unplanned failover?

options.text: Planned failover requires more bandwidth
Planned failover is bi-directional; unplanned is one-way
Planned failover allows graceful VM shutdown and minimal data loss; unplanned triggers immediate failover
Planned failover only works with sync replication

questions.explanation: Planned failover: shut down source VMs gracefully, ensure all data is replicated, then activate at DR. Unplanned: source site is unavailable; DR site activates immediately with potential data loss (up to RPO). Both use Leap recovery plans; planned has additional pre-shutdown validation steps. Configure via Prism > Disaster Recovery > Execute Failover > Planned vs Unplanned.

questions.questionText: During a test failover using Leap, what is protected from being affected?

options.text: Production snapshots on source site
Live production VMs on source site
Replication schedule
All of the above

questions.explanation: Test failover is non-destructive. Production VMs remain untouched, snapshots on source stay intact, replication continues. DR site spins up isolated copies to test recovery plan. After validation, discard test VMs. This enables safe DR drills without impacting production. Run via Prism > Disaster Recovery > Test Failover.

questions.questionText: You need to analyze per-core CPU utilization across all nodes. Which Prism interface provides this visibility?

options.text: Prism > Dashboard > CPU Summary
Prism > Analysis > CPU > Per-Core Breakdown
Prism > Infrastructure > Hosts > Select Host > CPU Performance Graph
SSH to CVM and run `mpstat -P ALL`

questions.explanation: Per-host CPU analysis: Prism > Infrastructure > Hosts > Select Host > Performance tab > CPU graph (shows per-core and aggregate). Per-socket/per-core breakdown helps identify NUMA imbalance, hot cores, or cache contention. `mpstat` on CVM is also valid for detailed kernel-level analysis.

questions.questionText: A host consistently shows high CPU overhead in Prism compared to other hosts. Which component is likely consuming the overhead?

options.text: Guest OS processes
CVM services (metadata, replication, snapshot)
Network drivers
Physical hardware management controller

questions.explanation: CVM CPU overhead varies by role: storage/replication intensive CVMs consume more CPU. Compare via Prism > Infrastructure > Hosts > CPU metrics. If one CVM is hot, check replication jobs, snapshot operations, or storage I/O on `ps aux` on that CVM. May need to balance workload or increase CVM vCPU.

questions.questionText: Your VM requires SCSI disks instead of virtio. What is the primary tradeoff of this choice?

options.text: Better performance due to native support
More realistic simulation of physical hardware; slight performance penalty vs. virtio
Required for Windows Server Failover Clustering
No tradeoff; use SCSI for all VMs

questions.explanation: SCSI emulation is more realistic but has ~5-10% performance overhead vs. virtio-blk (paravirtual). Use SCSI for compatibility with legacy applications, WSFC, or 3rd-party tools that require true SCSI support. Use virtio for performance-critical VMs. Configure via Prism > VM > Edit > Disk Controller Type.

questions.questionText: You're sizing VMs for a database with 50,000 IOPS requirement. Prism analysis shows average vDisk utilization is 35%. What should you do?

options.text: Use SSDs only; vDisk utilization is already high
Analyze hot vDisks to identify bottlenecks; 35% avg may hide peaks
Reduce vCPU to lower I/O contention
Increase vDisk count to distribute load

questions.explanation: Average utilization hides hot spots. Analyze via Prism > Storage > Volume Group > Select VG > Per-Disk breakdown. Some vDisks may be at 80%+ while others idle. Distribute load: split database files across vDisks, or move hot vDisk to faster tier. Peak analysis is critical for right-sizing.

questions.questionText: Your Leap recovery plan has a boot order: Web Tier → App Tier → Database. What does this prevent?

options.text: Network latency between tiers
App Tier connecting to non-existent Database
Memory contention during simultaneous boot
Excessive network traffic

questions.explanation: Boot order ensures dependencies are satisfied. App Tier boots only after Database is ready, preventing connection failures. Configure via Leap > Recovery Plan > Define Groups > Boot Sequence Priority. Validates pre-scripts: DB readiness check runs before app startup.

questions.questionText: You enable SMB Previous Versions on a file server VM. Where are point-in-time copies accessible to users?

options.text: Prism > Snapshots > Share with Users menu
Right-click file > Properties > Previous Versions tab
Command line: `vssadmin list shadows`
Backup software restore interface

questions.explanation: After enabling SMB Previous Versions (Windows feature) and NGT VSS snapshots, users see "Previous Versions" tab in file properties. No IT intervention needed for self-service restore. Requires: registry key `HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanServer\Parameters\EnableFileCache=1`, plus snapshot schedule (e.g., hourly).

questions.questionText: A VM fails with high page fault rate. Which memory diagnostic should you run first?

options.text: Increase vCPU to reduce memory pressure
Check balloon driver stats in Prism > VM > Memory metrics
Add 50% more RAM to VM
Enable memory compression

questions.explanation: Page faults indicate memory pressure. Check Prism > VM > Select VM > Monitor > Memory tab for: active/granted/swapped memory. If swapped > 10%, ballooning is active and VM is using disk swap. Solution: reduce overcommit, add host RAM, or migrate VM. Adding vCPU doesn't help memory pressure.

questions.questionText: You need to implement a multi-site DR topology: Site A → Site B (primary) and Site A → Site C (secondary). How many protection domains are required?

options.text: 1 (A → B/C both in same PD)
2 (separate PDs for B and C)
3 (one for each pair)
Depends on RPO requirement

questions.explanation: One protection domain can replicate to one remote site only. For A → B and A → C, create 2 PDs: PD1 (A → B), PD2 (A → C). Configure via Prism > Protection Domain > Create > Set remote site. Allows different retention/RPO policies per remote site.

questions.questionText: Your protection domain replication is failing. You check Prism > Replication Jobs and see "Replication Capacity Reached." What is the immediate solution?

options.text: Increase snapshot frequency
Increase replication bandwidth limit via Prism > Network > QoS
Delete old snapshots to free capacity
Disable and recreate protection domain

questions.explanation: Replication bandwidth limit is a QoS setting. Increase via Prism > Network > Quality of Service > Edit Replication Policies > Bandwidth Limit. Default is conservative to protect production traffic. Increase gradually and monitor impact on cluster performance.

questions.questionText: Leap runbook contains a pre-failover script that validates database consistency. When does this script execute?

options.text: Only on recovery plan creation
Before failover is initiated; if script fails, failover is blocked
After failover completes; before services start
Only during test failover

questions.explanation: Pre-scripts run before failover; failures block the operation. Post-scripts run after VMs boot on DR. Validation scripts should check: network connectivity, shared storage accessibility, critical service status. Configure via Leap > Recovery Plan > Pre/Post Scripts section.

questions.questionText: You observe dropped packets on a VM vNIC in Prism metrics. What is the most likely cause on Nutanix?

options.text: Physical NIC is full; bandwidth throttling is dropping frames
Virtio-net ring buffer overflow; upgrade to virtio-net v1.1
CVM network I/O processing bottleneck on over-committed host
Guest OS network driver issue; update via NGT

questions.explanation: Dropped packets typically indicate: CVM network processing is saturated from many VM traffic flows, or vhost-net kernel thread is CPU-bound. Check via `ethtool -S vmX` (drop counters) and CVM `top` for vhost processes. Solution: vMotion one or more VMs off host, or optimize CVM network performance via NIC tuning (RX/TX ring size).

questions.questionText: A VM is configured with hot vCPU add support. During operation, you add 4 vCPUs via Prism > VM > Edit. What happens inside the guest?

options.text: New vCPUs are immediately available to kernel; no reboot needed
ACPI hotplug event is triggered; guest OS must enable CPUs manually
VM must be powered off; vCPU change takes effect on reboot
Hypervisor waits for guest acknowledgment before assigning vCPUs

questions.explanation: Hot vCPU addition triggers ACPI hotplug events. Guest OS must register new vCPUs. Linux: `echo 1 > /sys/devices/system/cpu/cpuX/online`. Windows: Device Manager detects "Unknown Device" (new vCPU), drivers are auto-loaded. To verify: `lscpu` (Linux) or Device Manager (Windows). Some workloads require restart for kernel thread affinity.

questions.questionText: Your cluster has a skewed VM distribution: Node A (50 VMs), Node B (10 VMs), Node C (8 VMs). How does this impact cluster performance?

options.text: No impact; Nutanix load-balances automatically at hypervisor level
Node A is hot; its CVM is handling excess I/O; vMotion other VMs off
Node B/C are underutilized but stable; rebalancing causes no benefit
Network performance degrades due to traffic asymmetry

questions.explanation: Skewed distribution causes one CVM to handle disproportionate metadata/replication load. Check via Prism > Infrastructure > Hosts > Compare CPU/Memory across CVMs. Rebalance: vMotion VMs to under-utilized nodes. Aim for even VM count distribution to balance CVM load. Use `nstat` on CVMs to verify traffic patterns.

questions.questionText: You're configuring a snapshot schedule for a protection domain: every 2 hours with 4-day retention. How many snapshots can exist simultaneously?

options.text: 12 (24 hours ÷ 2 hours per snapshot)
24 (4 days × 6 snapshots per day)
48 (4 days × 24 hours ÷ 2 hours)
Depends on available storage capacity

questions.explanation: 4-day retention with 2-hour interval = (4 × 24 hours) ÷ 2 hours = 48 snapshots max. Each snapshot consumes space; verify via Prism > Storage > Usage to ensure adequate capacity. Older snapshots are deleted per retention policy.

questions.questionText: A VM experiences CPU ready time of 8%. Is this acceptable?

options.text: Yes; <10% is the recommended threshold
No; any CPU ready time indicates contention
Depends on workload; 8% is borderline
Yes; only >20% is problematic

questions.explanation: <10% CPU ready time is acceptable. >10% indicates contention. 8% is healthy. Monitor via Prism > VM > Monitor > CPU tab or vSphere Performance Charts (if using ESXi). For non-latency-sensitive workloads, up to 15% may be tolerable. Critical apps should maintain <5%.

questions.questionText: You need to verify CVM maintenance mode completed successfully. Which command on the CVM confirms its status?

options.text: `cvm_status.sh`
`manage_cvm.sh --status`
SSH to cluster and run `cluster status` at Prism UI
Check Prism > Infrastructure > Hosts > Host status field

questions.explanation: Prism > Infrastructure > Hosts shows host status: Normal, Maintenance Mode (if in progress), or alerts. During maintenance mode, the host is removed from cluster operations. The CVM still runs cluster services. Verify completion: Host status returns to Normal, and cluster state is stable. Also check `cluster status` via SSH: `nutanix@cvm$ cluster status` (shows all nodes).

questions.questionText: A user reports VM snapshots are not appearing in Previous Versions. You've already confirmed NGT is installed. What is the next diagnostic step?

options.text: Check snapshot schedule exists and last run was recent
Restart CVM to refresh VSS provider
Delete and recreate VM snapshots
Verify SMB Previous Versions registry setting is enabled

questions.explanation: Snapshots must exist before Previous Versions can show them. Check Prism > VM > Snapshots tab for recent snapshots. If none exist, create manual snapshot. Then verify: 1) SMB Previous Versions enabled (Windows feature), 2) Snapshot schedule configured, 3) NGT VSS service running. If schedule exists but no snapshots, check Prism > Protection Domain > Replication Jobs for errors.

questions.questionText: Your cluster is running with 5 nodes, each with 20TB raw capacity. You want 2x replication. What is usable capacity?
questions.explanation: With 2x replication: usable capacity = (Total raw capacity) ÷ (Replication factor). 100TB raw ÷ 2 = 50TB usable. This assumes full redundancy. With 3x replication: 100TB ÷ 3 = 33TB. Calculate via Prism > Capacity > Effective Usable Capacity or Prism > Storage > Capacity Summary.

questions.questionText: You want to prevent a critical database VM from migrating off its current host. Which VM affinity configuration should you use?

options.text: Soft affinity with preferred host label
Hard affinity to current host with override disabled
Host policy restriction via cluster settings
vNUMA pinning to physical NUMA nodes

questions.explanation: Hard affinity with override disabled prevents any vMotion of that VM. Configure via Prism > VM > VM Affinity > Hard Affinity > Select Target Host > Disable Override. Note: This can impact load balancing and HA recovery if host fails. Consider soft affinity instead: create label "critical_db" and assign preferred hosts, allowing flexibility during maintenance.

questions.questionText: A Leap recovery plan for a 3-tier app shows: Tier 1 boot time 2 minutes, Tier 2 boot time 5 minutes, Tier 3 boot time 3 minutes. What is the minimum failover RTO if booted sequentially?

options.text: 5 minutes (longest tier)
10 minutes (Tier 1 + Tier 2)
10 minutes (2 + 5 + 3, sequential)
3 minutes (parallel boot of all tiers)

questions.explanation: Sequential boot with dependencies: Tier 1 boots (2 min), then Tier 2 (5 min), then Tier 3 (3 min) = 10 minutes total RTO. If tiers are independent and boot in parallel, RTO = 5 minutes (longest tier). Configure boot groups in Leap > Recovery Plan > Tier Groups. Parallel boot reduces RTO significantly.

questions.questionText: You're analyzing a VM's CPU performance and notice CPU%SYS is very high (50%) while CPU%USER is low (10%). What does this indicate?

options.text: Application is compute-bound
Kernel is spending excessive time in system calls (likely I/O or lock contention)
vCPU count is too low
CVM is consuming host resources

questions.explanation: High %SYS indicates heavy system call load: likely storage I/O, network I/O, or inter-process locks. Analyze via: Linux `vmstat 1` (sys column), Windows Performance Monitor > Processor > % Privileged Time. Check `iostat` for disk I/O and `tcpdump` for network. Solution: reduce I/O operations, optimize lock usage, or increase vCPU to reduce context switches.

questions.questionText: A protection domain snapshot schedule is set to every 6 hours with 2-day retention. An RPO audit requires <3-hour max data loss. What is the problem?

options.text: Snapshot frequency is too low; change to every 2 hours
Retention is insufficient; extend to 5 days
Replication is asynchronous; must use synchronous Metro Avail
No problem; 2-day retention exceeds audit requirement

questions.explanation: RPO = time between failures and last good backup. 6-hour schedule = worst-case 6-hour data loss. Audit requires <3 hours, so change schedule to every 2 hours (or adjust as needed). Retention doesn't affect RPO; it affects recovery window. Configure via Prism > Protection Domain > Replication Settings > Snapshot Schedule.

questions.questionText: You're troubleshooting a VM's high memory usage. Prism shows: Active=6GB, Granted=8GB, Swapped=2GB. What does this reveal?

options.text: VM has true memory leak; restart required
Hypervisor is ballooning; memory pressure is real; VM needs more RAM or host capacity
VM is caching aggressively; normal behavior
Network memory transfer is active; wait for completion

questions.explanation: Breakdown: Active (6GB) = actually used by guest; Granted (8GB) = allocated; Swapped (2GB) = ballooned out to host swap. Total needed: 8GB, but only 6GB active. This indicates memory pressure: either over-committed VM or under-provisioned host. Solution: migrate VM to less-loaded host or reduce overcommit ratio.

questions.questionText: Leap executes a recovery plan on DR site, and a VM fails to boot. The recovery plan includes a post-boot validation script. What happens?

options.text: Recovery plan marks VM as "Failed"; stops remaining boot sequence
VM continues running; post-script reports error but doesn't block
Depends on script error handling: if script exit code ≠ 0, failover can be rolled back
Post-script only runs if boot succeeds; VM failure prevents script execution

questions.explanation: Post-boot scripts only execute if VM boots successfully. Pre-boot scripts run before boot and can block failover. If VM fails to boot, post-script doesn't run. Configure via Leap > Recovery Plan > Edit VM > Post-Boot Scripts. Ensure boot order and pre-scripts satisfy VM dependencies so boot doesn't fail.

questions.questionText: You implement a multi-site DR: 3 protection domains (PD1, PD2, PD3) all replicating from Site A to Site B. How do you prevent Site B from becoming oversubscribed?

options.text: Configure PD replication schedules to stagger: PD1 every 2h, PD2 every 2h+30m, PD3 every 2h+60m
Set QoS bandwidth limits per PD to balance replication traffic
Capacity plan: ensure Site B has ≥ capacity of Site A for all PDs combined
All of the above

questions.explanation: All three strategies apply: 1) Stagger schedules to avoid simultaneous replication spikes, 2) QoS limits prevent network congestion, 3) Capacity planning ensures Site B can accommodate all VMs if failover occurs. Configure via: Prism > Network > QoS > Set Per-PD Bandwidth Limits, and Prism > Protection Domain > Schedule > Stagger times.

questions.questionText: A cluster node fails and a VM on that host is protected by a protection domain. Will Leap DR automatically failover the VM?

options.text: Yes; Leap triggers automatic failover if node fails
No; Leap requires manual failover initiation
Depends on protection domain "Auto Failover" setting
Only if VM is configured with HA affinity rules

questions.explanation: Leap is a manual failover tool; it doesn't auto-trigger on node failure. Nutanix native HA will restart VM on another node if possible. Leap is for planned/unplanned DR failover to remote site. If VM is in a protection domain and you decide to failover to remote site due to node failure, you manually execute Leap failover. Configure via Prism > Disaster Recovery > Execute Failover.

questions.questionText: You're configuring a vNIC for optimal performance on a Linux VM. Which settings should you enable?

options.text: Virtio-net driver, GSO (Generic Segmentation Offload), checksum offload
E1000 emulation, TSO disabled, checksum in-kernel
SRIOV passthrough, disable CVM monitoring
Jumbo frames 9000 MTU, disable virtio

questions.explanation: Virtio-net with GSO/TSO/checksum offload provides optimal performance: reduces CPU overhead by offloading segmentation/checksums. Verify on Linux: `ethtool -k eth0` (check gso, tso on) and `ethtool -c eth0` (rx/tx descriptors). Jumbo frames help but aren't critical if default MTU 1500 works. SRIOV bypasses CVM, reducing visibility and live migration capability.

questions.questionText: A VM's vDisk performance shows 10ms latency during peak hours, but 2ms during off-peak. Which analysis should you perform?

options.text: Ignore; normal variation is expected
Check vDisk IOPS and queue length to identify peak load characteristics
Increase vCPU to handle peaks
Migrate vDisk to SSD tier

questions.explanation: Latency increases with queue depth (load). Analyze via Prism > Storage > Volume Group > Per-Disk breakdown > Peak IOPS. If peak IOPS are acceptable but latency is high, it's queue depth/congestion. Solutions: distribute load across vDisks, optimize query/workload to reduce IOPS, or migrate to faster tier. SSD is option only if all tiers are saturated.

questions.questionText: Your snapshot schedule creates snapshots but replication isn't happening. Where should you check first?

options.text: Prism > Protection Domain > Remote site configuration
Prism > Replication Jobs > Check for errors
CVM → run `afs -afs showrepl`
SSH to remote site and verify cluster is reachable

questions.explanation: Prism > Replication Jobs shows real-time replication status with specific error indicators. Common errors include: "Authentication Failed — invalid credentials on remote cluster", "Network Unreachable — cannot reach remote Prism IP on port 9440", "Insufficient Capacity on Remote — not enough free storage", "Remote Cluster Offline — no response from remote site CVMs", or "PD Not Configured on Remote — protection domain does not exist at destination". If replication jobs are missing entirely, the protection domain may not have a remote site configured (check A). Check Replication Job details for exact error message and remediation steps. `afs showrepl` on CVM is valid for deep debugging but Prism Replication Jobs is the authoritative first diagnostic point.

questions.questionText: You want to monitor CVM network I/O to identify if CVM network processing is the bottleneck. Which CVM command shows per-interface statistics?

options.text: `ethtool -S eth0` (detailed stats)
`iftop` (live bandwidth per flow)
`nstat -rtnap | grep TCP` (TCP connection stats)
All can be useful; use `ethtool` first

questions.explanation: Different tools serve different purposes: `ethtool -S` shows detailed NIC stats (RX/TX drops, errors), `iftop` shows real-time bandwidth per connection, `nstat` shows protocol stats. For CVM bottleneck analysis, start with `top` (CVM CPU), then `ethtool -S` (check for drops), then `iftop` (identify heavy flows). Combine for full picture.

questions.questionText: A VM requires NGT installation but the host kernel version is 4.x. Will NGT install successfully?

options.text: Yes; NGT supports all Linux kernels
No; NGT requires kernel 5.x or later
Yes; but with reduced functionality (no VSS snapshots on 4.x kernels)
Depends on specific kernel patch level

questions.explanation: NGT supports kernel 4.x and later. VSS snapshots are Windows-specific (use VSS provider). Linux VMs use crash-consistent snapshots or app-consistent snapshots via custom pre/post scripts. Installation: Linux → download NGT ISO, mount, run installer. Verify: `rpm -q nutanix-guest-tools` or `dpkg -l | grep nutanix`.

questions.questionText: You're analyzing a VM's NUMA performance. The VM has 8 vCPU (4 cores per socket on 2-socket host), 16GB RAM. Memory access times are slow. What should you check?

options.text: Run `numactl --hardware` on guest to verify NUMA configuration
Check guest OS process affinity is aligned with NUMA nodes
Verify vCPU placement in Prism > VM > Properties > NUMA Pinning
All of the above

questions.explanation: NUMA optimization requires: 1) numactl to understand host/guest topology, 2) process affinity via taskset/numactl to bind processes to specific nodes, 3) Prism to verify vCPU placement. Slow memory access indicates remote access (memory on opposite socket). Solution: pin vCPU to socket, allocate memory on same socket, or use interleave if app requires cross-socket access.

questions.questionText: A protection domain is configured to replicate to a remote site but "Seeding" status has been "In Progress" for 24 hours. What is the likely issue?

options.text: Network bandwidth between sites is insufficient
Remote site cluster has insufficient disk space
Seeding is blocked by cluster lock acquisition
A or B

questions.explanation: Slow seeding typically indicates: insufficient bandwidth (increase QoS limit for initial seeding), insufficient remote capacity (check via Prism on remote site), or network connectivity issues. Check Prism > Replication Jobs > Seeding Job > Monitor > bandwidth utilization. If bandwidth shows zero, network path is broken. Verify: `ping remote_cluster_vip`, `nstat` on CVM.

questions.questionText: You're planning snapshot retention for compliance: data must be recoverable for 90 days. What retention policy should you set?

options.text: 90 days of daily snapshots (90 snapshots)
Daily for 30 days, weekly for 12 weeks, monthly for 6 months
Minimum daily snapshots for 90 days; configure via protection domain retention policy
Keep all snapshots indefinitely; delete manually

questions.explanation: Retention policy defines how long snapshots are kept. Set via Prism > Protection Domain > Replication Settings > Retention Policy. For 90-day compliance, minimum is daily snapshots for 90 days = 90 snapshots. Option B (30 daily + 12 weekly + 6 monthly) covers 90+ days with fewer total snapshots (better storage efficiency). Configure whichever meets compliance requirements and storage budget.

questions.questionText: A VM with high I/O workload is on a host with high CVM CPU. Should you immediately vMotion the VM?

options.text: Yes; the CVM is overloaded and can't handle the I/O
No; move the VM only if its own CPU/memory is high
First investigate: CVM CPU may be handling replication for other VMs, not necessarily this one
Move only if accompanied by CVM RAM increase

questions.explanation: CVM CPU usage includes: metadata, replication, snapshots, and deduplication for all VMs on host. High CVM CPU doesn't necessarily mean the high-I/O VM is the cause. Investigate: check CVM network/disk I/O per VM, identify actual source. Then vMotion only the hot VM if it's the cause. Blind vMotion risks moving the wrong VM.

questions.questionText: Your Prism shows a VM with "CPU.EffectiveReadyTime" metric of 50ms. Does this indicate contention?

options.text: Yes; any >0 ready time is contention
No; 50ms is negligible for non-real-time workloads
Depends on measurement interval; 50ms effective over 1 second = 5% ready time (acceptable)
50ms always indicates critical contention

questions.explanation: CPU ready time is a percentage, not an absolute value. 50ms ready time within a 1-second interval = 50/1000 = 5% ready time (acceptable). The metric interpretation depends on sampling interval. Check Prism metric definitions: typically reports as percentage. If metric shows raw milliseconds, divide by sampling interval to get percentage.

questions.questionText: You want to create a recovery plan that boots VMs in parallel (not sequential). How do you configure this in Leap?

options.text: Add all VMs to the same boot group
Set boot group priority to 0 for all VMs
Create separate boot groups with same numeric sequence number
Cannot be configured; Leap always boots sequentially

questions.explanation: Leap recovery plans use boot groups. All VMs in the same group boot in parallel; groups boot sequentially. To parallelize all VMs, add them all to Group 1. To sequence dependencies, create Group 1 (database), Group 2 (app), Group 3 (web). Configure via Leap > Recovery Plan > VM Groups > Assign VMs > Set Groups.

questions.questionText: A Windows VM has NGT installed and VSS snapshots enabled. However, SMB Previous Versions doesn't show up in file properties. What should you verify?

options.text: SMB Previous Versions feature is enabled in Windows Features
Registry setting `EnableSnapshots` is set to 1
Network share has "Restore Previous Versions" permission
All of the above

questions.explanation: All three are required: 1) Windows Feature (Server Roles > File Services > File Server > VSS Agent), 2) Registry `HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanServer\Parameters` (EnableFileCache=1, EnableSnapshots=1), 3) Share permissions allow restoration. Verify each via: Windows Features UI, Registry Editor, and Share Permissions dialog.

questions.questionText: A cluster has two hosts, each with 4 NIC uplinks to the same L2 network. One uplink on Host A fails. What happens to VM network traffic from Host A VMs?

options.text: VMs lose connectivity; manual intervention needed
Traffic fails over to remaining 3 uplinks on Host A automatically
Prism alerts but doesn't auto-recover
Depends on NIC teaming/bonding configuration

questions.explanation: Failover depends on NIC bonding mode: Active-Backup = traffic immediately shifts to standby NIC (transparent), Balance-ALB = traffic rebalances across active NICs, LACP = depends on switch config. Nutanix doesn't mandate a specific bonding mode; cluster inherits host OS config. Verify via: Linux `cat /proc/net/bonding/bond0` or Windows `Get-NetLbfoTeam`. Prism alerts on NIC failure regardless.

questions.questionText: You're right-sizing a VM after analyzing 30 days of performance data. Current: 8 vCPU, avg CPU utilization 25%, peak 45%. Recommendation?

options.text: Keep 8 vCPU; add RAM instead
Reduce to 4 vCPU; 25% utilization on 8 cores = 2 core equivalent
Increase to 16 vCPU; include 50% headroom above peak
Reduce to 6 vCPU; maintain some headroom for bursts

questions.explanation: Right-sizing formula: (Peak utilization ÷ 100) × Current vCPU + headroom. Peak 45% on 8 vCPU = 3.6 cores needed. Add 30-50% headroom for growth/bursts: 3.6 + (1-2 cores) = 5-6 cores target. Reducing to 4 vCPU is risky (no headroom). Verify change by setting new config, monitoring for 7 days, ensuring CPU ready time <10%.

questions.questionText: A VM's storage latency is consistently >20ms. You've ruled out IOPS saturation. What should you investigate next?

options.text: Network latency between cluster and remote storage
CVM disk I/O processing time via `iostat` on CVM
Hypervisor cache effectiveness: check hit rates in Prism
Physical hardware: SSD wear, SATA speed limitations

questions.explanation: If IOPS are acceptable but latency is high, investigate CVM I/O path: CVM disk processing, extent group calculations, replication overhead. Run on CVM: `iostat -xmt 1 10` to see avg I/O time per disk. If CVM I/O time is high, issue is in CVM processing. If CVM latency is low but guest sees high latency, bottleneck is network/replication. Check Prism > Storage > Latency Breakdown to identify source.

questions.questionText: Which two steps are required to enable self-service file restore on a Windows file server VM? (Select 2)

options.text: Install Nutanix Guest Tools with VSS provider enabled
Enable SMB Previous Versions in Windows Features
Configure Leap recovery plan for the file server
Set snapshot schedule in protection domain
Create snapshots and ensure snapshot schedule is active

questions.explanation: Self-service restore requires: 1) NGT VSS provider (captures application-consistent snapshots), 2) SMB Previous Versions enabled (makes snapshots accessible in file properties). C is for DR failover (not needed for self-service). D and E are supportive but optional if manual snapshots are created. VSS + Previous Versions = complete self-service solution.

questions.questionText: Which two configurations ensure optimal NUMA performance for a memory-intensive VM on a 2-socket host? (Select 2)

options.text: vCPU count equal to one physical socket core count
Pin vCPU to specific socket via NUMA affinity
Allocate all VM RAM to single NUMA node
Set memory overcommit to 2x to leverage both sockets
Disable vNUMA to prevent remote memory access

questions.explanation: NUMA optimization: 1) Match vCPU count to socket topology (e.g., 4 vCPU per socket on 2-socket host), 2) Pin vCPU to socket to enforce locality. C is incorrect (memory interleave is sometimes better than single node). D increases remote access (bad). E disables NUMA benefits. vNUMA pinning is correct; disable NUMA entirely is wrong.

questions.questionText: Which two of these are valid RPO targets for different replication scenarios? (Select 2)

options.text: Async replication with 6-hour schedule: 6-hour RPO
NearSync replication: 1-15 minute RPO
Sync Metro Avail: 500ms RPO
Snapshot-based recovery: zero RPO
Manual daily backups: 24-hour RPO

questions.explanation: Valid RPO options: A) Async = snapshot interval = RPO (6hr schedule = 6hr worst-case loss), B) NearSync = 1-15min RPO (designed for medium-distance replication). C is wrong (Metro = zero RPO, not 500ms). D is wrong (snapshots have RPO equal to snapshot interval). E is correct RPO but question asks "which two valid scenarios" and A+B are most common/correct.

questions.questionText: Which two settings prevent a database VM from migrating during a critical operation? (Select 2)

options.text: Hard VM affinity to current host with override disabled
CVM anti-affinity rules preventing migrations
Host maintenance mode to block incoming vMotion
Set storage affinity to prevent volume migration
Soft affinity with high priority weight

questions.explanation: Hard affinity (A) explicitly prevents vMotion. Maintenance mode (C) blocks VM placement but allows existing VMs to stay. B is invalid (CVMs don't use anti-affinity for user VMs). D doesn't prevent VM migration (storage affinity doesn't exist in standard Nutanix). E (soft affinity) doesn't prevent migration, only discourages it. A+C are enforcement mechanisms.

questions.questionText: Which two protection domain settings directly impact RPO and recovery capability? (Select 2)

options.text: Snapshot schedule frequency and retention period
Replication bandwidth QoS limit
Remote site IP/address and replication protocol
VM backup software integration
Protection domain description name

questions.explanation: A) Snapshot frequency = RPO (every 1 hour = 1hr RPO), retention = recovery window (7-day retention = can recover snapshots up to 7 days old). C) Remote site config = which site receives replicas (affects RTO/RPO if remote site is down). B impacts speed but not RPO/RTO. D is backup, not protection domain. E is metadata only.

questions.questionText: During planned failover using Leap, which two steps ensure minimal data loss? (Select 2)

options.text: Gracefully shut down all source site VMs before failover
Verify all pending snapshots are replicated to remote site
Delete old snapshots before initiating failover
Trigger an immediate snapshot of all VMs before failover
Disable replication jobs after failover completes

questions.explanation: Planned failover best practices: A) Graceful shutdown prevents dirty data loss, B) Confirm replication is current (no pending snapshots). C is risky (loses recovery points). D is good but not required if schedule just ran. E disables failback capability. A+B provide safety: shutdown + confirmation = zero data loss.

questions.questionText: Which two metrics indicate that memory ballooning is actively reclaiming VM memory? (Select 2)

options.text: Granted memory equals active memory
Swapped memory >0 GB (VM is using host swap)
Balloon driver stats show pages reclaimed/released
VM page fault rate is zero
CVM memory utilization is high

questions.explanation: B) Swapped >0 = hypervisor is actively ballooning (moving memory to swap). C) Balloon driver statistics directly show reclaim activity. A is wrong (granted > active indicates headroom, not ballooning). D is not related (page faults are guest-level). E (CVM memory) doesn't indicate guest ballooning. B+C are direct indicators.

questions.questionText: Which two configurations ensure optimal per-vDisk performance for high-IOPS workloads? (Select 2)

options.text: Increase vCPU count of VM to handle more I/O operations
Distribute database files across multiple vDisks to reduce per-disk queue depth
Increase snapshot frequency to clear I/O cache
Place vDisk on SSDs or faster storage tier
Enable iSCSI instead of virtio-blk disk protocol

questions.explanation: B) Multiple vDisks reduce queue depth per disk, improving latency under load. D) Faster storage (SSD) reduces I/O service time. A is wrong (vCPU doesn't affect disk I/O directly). C increases overhead (bad). E) iSCSI requires storage target; virtio-blk is better for local Nutanix. B+D are direct optimizations for per-vDisk performance.

questions.questionText: Which two components must be configured to enable reverse replication for failback after async DR? (Select 2)

options.text: Enable "Reverse Replication" in protection domain settings
Create separate protection domain from remote site back to production
Verify remote site cluster has sufficient replication capacity
Set failback snapshot schedule to mirror production schedule
Configure pre-failover scripts on remote site

questions.explanation: A) Reverse replication toggle enables bidirectional replication (required for failback). C) Remote site capacity ensures failback is feasible. B is wrong (not a separate PD; same PD reverses direction). D (snapshot schedule) is inherited, not separate. E (pre-scripts) is optional. A+C are core requirements.

questions.questionText: Which two network configurations optimize VM-to-CVM communication for storage I/O? (Select 2)

options.text: Enable virtio-net driver on VM vNICs
Configure separate physical NICs for storage traffic (if available)
Set vNIC MTU to 1500 bytes (standard)
Enable TCP offload (TSO/GSO) for checksum and segmentation
Disable CVM network monitoring to reduce overhead

questions.explanation: A) Virtio-net provides paravirtual performance (lower overhead than e1000 emulation). D) TCP offload (TSO/GSO) reduces CPU overhead by offloading segmentation. B is not always available/necessary (storage uses same NICs in most Nutanix configs). C (1500 MTU) is standard, not an optimization. E disables visibility (bad). A+D are actual performance optimizations.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to create a new virtual machine named "prod-web01" with 4 vCPUs and 8 GB of RAM using the AHV command line. Which command is correct?

options.text: `ncli vm create name=prod-web01 num_vcpus=4 memory=8G`
`acli vm.create prod-web01 num_vcpus=4 memory=8G`
`acli vm.create prod-web01 cpu=4 ram=8192`
`nuclei vm.create prod-web01 --vcpus 4 --memory 8G`

questions.explanation: The `acli vm.create` command uses positional VM name followed by `num_vcpus=` and `memory=` parameters. `ncli` does not have a `vm create` subcommand — VM lifecycle is managed through `acli` on AHV clusters.

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to create a VLAN-backed network named "Production-VLAN100" on VLAN 100 with an IP pool for DHCP. Which command correctly creates the network?

options.text: `ncli net create name=Production-VLAN100 vlan-id=100 ip-config=10.1.1.0/24`
`acli net.create Production-VLAN100 vlan=100 ip_config=10.1.1.0/24,10.1.1.100,10.1.1.200,10.1.1.1`
`acli network.create Production-VLAN100 --vlan 100 --subnet 10.1.1.0/24`
`acli net.create Production-VLAN100 vlan=100 network=10.1.1.0/24 gateway=10.1.1.1`

questions.explanation: The `acli net.create` syntax uses `vlan=` for the VLAN ID and `ip_config=` with a comma-separated list of network CIDR, pool start, pool end, and default gateway. Option D uses incorrect parameter names (`network=` and `gateway=` are not valid `acli` parameters).

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to create an asynchronous protection domain named "PD-Finance" for replication. Which command is correct?

options.text: `ncli pd create name=PD-Finance type=async`
`ncli protection-domain create name=PD-Finance mode=async`
`acli pd.create PD-Finance replication_type=async`
`ncli pd create name=PD-Finance replication-mode=asynchronous`

questions.explanation: The `ncli pd create` command uses `name=` and `type=async` to create an asynchronous protection domain. The full subcommand is `pd`, not `protection-domain`, and the type parameter accepts `async` or `sync`.

questions.questionText: A new storage container named "Gold-Tier" needs to be created on the existing storage pool "SP-01" with compression enabled. Which command is correct?

options.text: `ncli container create name=Gold-Tier sp-name=SP-01 compression-enabled=true`
`ncli ctr create name=Gold-Tier storage-pool=SP-01 enable-compression=yes`
`acli container.create Gold-Tier pool=SP-01 compression=on`
`ncli container create name=Gold-Tier sp-name=SP-01 compression=inline`

questions.explanation: The `ncli container create` command uses `sp-name=` to specify the storage pool and `compression-enabled=true` to enable inline compression. Option D incorrectly uses `compression=inline` which is not a valid parameter format.

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to verify the overall cluster health, including service status and cluster configuration. Which pair of commands provides the most comprehensive view?

options.text: `ncli cluster info` and `ncli cluster status`
`ncli cluster get-params` and `ncli cluster list`
`acli cluster.info` and `acli cluster.health`
`cluster status` and `cluster info`

questions.explanation: `ncli cluster info` displays cluster configuration details (name, ID, IPs, version), while `ncli cluster status` shows real-time service health across all CVMs. These are the two primary commands for cluster verification. `acli` does not have cluster-level commands.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to add an existing disk image to a VM named "test-vm01" as a SCSI disk using `acli`. Which command correctly attaches the disk?

options.text: `acli vm.disk_create test-vm01 clone_from_image=CentOS-8 bus=scsi`
`acli vm.add_disk test-vm01 image=CentOS-8 type=scsi`
`acli vm.disk_create test-vm01 image_name=CentOS-8 bus=scsi`
`acli vm.disk_attach test-vm01 clone_from_image=CentOS-8 bus=scsi`

questions.explanation: The correct `acli` syntax uses `vm.disk_create` with `clone_from_image=` to clone from an existing image and `bus=scsi` to specify the bus type. There is no `vm.add_disk` or `vm.disk_attach` subcommand in `acli`.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to list all VMs on the cluster and filter for those in a powered-on state. Which command is correct?

options.text: `acli vm.list power_state=on`
`ncli vm list power-state=on`
