Prism Central > Storage Policies > Create Policy > Condition: "Category Compliance contains PCI" > Action: "Enable encryption for these VMs" > Exclude others from encryption > Save and apply
Container Settings > Encryption Policy > Enable category-based encryption > Category Scope: Compliance:PCI > Encryption algorithm: AES > Apply

questions.explanation: Use Storage Policy Based Encryption (SPBE) in Prism Central. Create a new policy named "PCI-Encrypt" with a category filter matching "Compliance = PCI". Enable AES-256-GCM encryption and scope the policy to only VMs matching this category. VMs without the PCI category tag remain unencrypted. Apply the policy to activate encryption selectively.

questions.questionText: Your security policy requires AIDE monitoring on both CVMs and hypervisors to detect unauthorized file modifications. What command enables AIDE on both, and what is the update schedule?

options.text: ncli cluster edit-cvm-security-params enable-aide=yes AND ncli cluster edit-hypervisor-security-params enable-aide=yes - AIDE runs a full baseline check weekly and monitors for changes
ncli security enable-aide --scope=all-cvms,all-hypervisors - AIDE scans run every 12 hours
Prism Element > Security > AIDE Configuration > Enable on CVMs > Enable on Hypervisors > Schedule: Daily > Apply
ncli cluster aide-enable --cvm --hypervisor --schedule=weekly

questions.explanation: Use two commands: `ncli cluster edit-cvm-security-params enable-aide=yes` to enable on CVMs and `ncli cluster edit-hypervisor-security-params enable-aide=yes` to enable on hypervisors. AIDE automatically runs a weekly baseline establishment and then monitors for unauthorized file system modifications across both layers. Verify status with `ncli cluster get-cvm-security-config`.

questions.questionText: You want to implement network segmentation for VDI desktops using Flow. The policy should apply security rules to VMs in the "VDI:Production" category, which is dynamically populated from an AD group. What are the policy evaluation order and Flow configuration steps?

options.text: Flow > Policy > VDI Policy > Security Category: "VDI:Production" (mapped to AD group) > Rules in order: Quarantine > Isolation > Application > VDI > Apply block by default > Save

Flow > Create Policy > Name: "VDI-Security" > Scope: LDAP attribute mapping AD group to "VDI:Production" category > Flow evaluation: System > Quarantine > Isolation > Application > Custom rules > Deny all else > Activate

Flow > Policies > New VDI Policy > Scope by category "VDI:Production" (LDAP backend configured) > Rules evaluation order: Quarantine (highest priority) → Isolation → Application → VDI (lowest priority) > Configure rules per tier > Apply
Administration > Flow > VDI Category Policy > Map AD group to category > Configure Isolation rules > Set Application whitelist > VDI policy rules > Enforcement order > Save

questions.explanation: In Flow, create a new VDI policy scoped to the "VDI:Production" category (configured with LDAP backend to sync from AD group membership, requires LDAP port 389). The evaluation order is fixed: Quarantine rules (highest priority) → Isolation rules → Application rules → VDI rules (lowest priority). Configure rules at each tier and apply; default behavior is deny-all for traffic not matching a rule.

questions.questionText: Your security team requires network segmentation to isolate CVM management traffic from VM production traffic. How should you validate your VLAN configuration against design specifications?

options.text: Navigate to Prism Element > Network > Virtual Networks > Verify CVM management VLAN (dedicated, restricted to CVM subnet) is separate from VM production VLANs > Check AHV Host VLAN isolation > Review against NVD-2031 networking guide
Use AHV host CLI to verify: `ovs-vsctl show` displays bridge configuration; confirm CVM management bridge separate from br-int (VM bridge); validate VLAN tags don't overlap
In Prism, check Cluster > Network Configuration > CVM VLAN: one dedicated IP range, separate from VM network ranges; verify firewall rules restrict CVM-to-VM direct traffic
All of the above validate proper network segmentation per NVD-2031

questions.explanation: All steps are necessary to validate proper segmentation. In Prism, verify CVM management VLAN uses a dedicated, restricted subnet separate from VM production networks. On AHV hosts, use `ovs-vsctl show` to confirm bridge separation (CVMs on dedicated bridge, VMs on br-int). Review firewall policies to ensure CVM management traffic cannot route directly to VM production VLANs. Cross-reference against NVD-2031 security architecture guide.

questions.questionText: Your cluster needs to host temporary build artifacts and staging data that doesn't require redundancy. When is an RF=1 (Replication Factor 1) container appropriate, and how do you create it?

options.text: RF=1 is appropriate for temp data, build caches, non-critical staging workloads. Trade-off: no data protection if node fails, but significantly better performance. Create: Prism Element > Storage > Containers > Create > Name, pool selection > Replication Factor: 1 > Advanced: no snapshots > Create
RF=1 should never be used in production. Only acceptable for development/test VMs. Use ncli ctr create --container-name=scratch --pool=pool1 --replication-factor=1
RF=1 containers lack HA. Use only for read-only workloads or data that can be regenerated. Prism: Storage > Containers > Advanced Settings > RF = 1 > Accept warning > Save
Cannot create RF=1 on clusters with HA enabled. Requires standalone single-node mode.

questions.explanation: RF=1 containers are appropriate for temporary scratch data, build caches, non-critical staging workloads, and test environments. The tradeoff is NO data redundancy if a node fails (data loss is possible), but you gain significant performance improvement due to no replication overhead. Create via Prism Element > Storage > Containers > Create, select pool, and set Replication Factor to 1.

questions.questionText: You're deploying an Oracle RAC cluster requiring shared storage with load balancing across multiple CVMs for iSCSI connectivity. How do you configure a Volume Group to enable load balancing for RAC iSCSI initiators?

options.text: Prism Element > Storage > Volume Groups > Create > Add vDisks > Enable "Load Balancing" toggle under iSCSI settings > Path selection policy: Round-Robin > Active-Active mode > Save

Create Volume Group with multiple vDisks > iSCSI Configuration > Enable vDisk load balancing > Set path selection to "Active-Active" across multiple Stargate instances > Configure multipathing on hosts
Volume Groups > New VG > vDisk layout: distribute across multiple CVMs > iSCSI settings > Load Balancing: "Enabled" > Automatic failover: yes > MPIO on Oracle hosts required
Storage > Volume Groups > iSCSI Load Balancing: set "Round-Robin" load distribution across available CVMs > Create multiple iSCSI target portals per VG > Configure MPIO on Oracle initiators

questions.explanation: Create a Volume Group with multiple vDisks distributed across different CVMs/pools. In iSCSI configuration, enable "vDisk load balancing" to distribute I/O across multiple Stargate instances. Set path selection to "Active-Active" mode allowing simultaneous use of all paths. Configure MPIO (Multipath I/O) on Oracle RAC hosts to leverage the multiple iSCSI paths for load distribution and resilience.

questions.questionText: Your security team requires validation that cluster VLAN configuration aligns with Nutanix's NVD-2031 network design specification. What deviations should you identify and correct?

options.text: Compare current VLAN setup to NVD-2031 spec: management VLAN should be isolated from VM network VLAN, storage traffic may share VM VLAN or be separate, iLO/BMC on separate out-of-band VLAN, AHV migration/heartbeat on dedicated VLAN. Document any deviations for remediation.
NVD-2031 specifies mandatory VLAN separation: CVM management, VM production, iSCSI storage, replication, iLO. Any shared VLAN is a design violation requiring refactoring.
Check Prism > Network > VLAN assignments match NVD-2031 minimum requirements: separate CVM management, dedicated VM VLAN, storage on same as VMs acceptable per standard
NVD-2031 recommendations are optional guidance. Standard best practice is: 1 VLAN for all cluster traffic, separate management subnet for security

questions.explanation: Reference NVD-2031 network design guide. Compare your configuration against these baseline requirements: CVM management traffic on isolated VLAN separate from VM network VLANs; storage traffic on dedicated VLAN or shared with VMs (both acceptable); iLO/IPMI on separate out-of-band VLAN; AHV host heartbeat on dedicated VLAN. Document any deviations (shared management/VM VLAN, storage on management VLAN, etc.) for remediation.

questions.questionText: Your cluster design must provide N+1 redundancy per NVD specifications. How do you validate HA is properly configured in Prism, and what settings must you verify?

options.text: Prism Element > Cluster > Availability > Verify "Redundancy Factor: N+1" enabled > HA reservation shows nodes reserved for failover > Admission control rejects VMs that exceed available capacity > Validate RF=2 minimum for critical containers

Check Prism > Administration > High Availability > Status: "HA Healthy" > Verify VM protection enabled by default > Reservation: Sufficient nodes reserved > Create test VM > Live migrate to confirm HA restart capability

Cluster settings > HA Configuration > Admission Control: "Enabled" > Reservation: minimum N+1 capacity reserved > All nodes healty and connected > vMotion/Live migration working > Verify RF=2 on user containers
Settings > Cluster Redundancy > Confirm Redundancy Factor > HA Orchestration running > Isolation Response configured > Verify all VMs protected by HA

questions.explanation: Validate HA configuration in Prism Element > Cluster Settings > High Availability. Confirm: (1) Admission Control is enabled to prevent over-provisioning, (2) at least N nodes worth of capacity is reserved for failover (where N = cluster node count), (3) all nodes are healthy and connected, (4) vMotion/Live migration is functional, (5) critical user-created containers use RF=2 or higher for data protection. These elements together ensure N+1 redundancy.

questions.questionText: A protection policy configured with automatic target cluster assignment stops assigning new VMs. Previously created VMs replicate normally, but new VMs are not added to the policy. What are the likely root causes and diagnostic steps?

options.text: Navigate to Prism Central > Data Protection > Policies > Check "Automatic target assignment" toggle is still enabled > Verify categories on new VMs match policy filter > Check remote cluster connectivity in "Clusters" page > Verify replication licenses on source and target

Diagnostic: 1) Check policy category scope - new VMs may not have required category tags. 2) Verify target cluster capacity - assignment halts if target full. 3) Check Remote Site status is "Active" in Prism Central > Administration > Cluster Connections

Likely cause: Category mismatch (new VMs not tagged with protection policy category) or target cluster connection lost. Verify: 1) New VM has category matching policy filter, 2) Target cluster reachable: `ncli remote-site get-info`, 3) Replication service healthy on both sites
All of the above are valid diagnostic approaches

questions.explanation: All are essential diagnostics. Check that automatic assignment is enabled in the protection policy. Verify new VMs have the categories matching the policy filter (category mismatch is the most common cause). Verify target cluster connectivity: navigate to Prism Central > Administration > Cluster Connections and confirm status is "Active". Check target cluster has available replication capacity. Use `ncli remote-site get-info` or Prism to verify connectivity.

questions.questionText: You're creating a recovery plan for a database VM with static IP 10.1.1.50 on production VLAN 100. The DR site has VLAN 200 (10.2.1.0/24). You want the VM to get static IP 10.2.1.50 during failover. How do you configure network mapping?

options.text: Recovery Plan > Network Mapping > Source VLAN: 100 (10.1.1.0/24) > Target VLAN: 200 (10.2.1.0/24) > IP Mapping Mode: "Static" > Add mapping: 10.1.1.50 → 10.2.1.50 > Save. Requires NGT installed on VM.
Prism Central > Recovery Plans > Network Configuration > Map Production VLAN 100 to DR VLAN 200 > Enable IP translation > Add IP mapping entry > Requires GuestTools for NGT
Recovery Plan > Network > Add Network Mapping > Source: 10.1.1.0/24 on VLAN 100 > Target: 10.2.1.0/24 on VLAN 200 > Static IP mappings: source→target > Enable network virtualization > Apply
Advanced network mapping requires Calm automation. Use Recovery Plan only for VLAN mapping; IP assignment handled by DHCP on target site.

questions.explanation: In the Recovery Plan, configure Network Mapping: map source VLAN 100 (10.1.1.0/24) to target VLAN 200 (10.2.1.0/24). Set IP Mapping Mode to "Static" and add specific IP mapping entry: source 10.1.1.50 → target 10.2.1.50. This requires Nutanix Guest Tools (NGT, also called GuestTools) installed on the VM; NGT applies the IP address at boot time during failover. Test the mapping in a failover drill before production failover.

questions.questionText: Your protection policy currently uses asynchronous replication (RPO ~1 hour). Your compliance requirements changed to require zero RPO. How do you convert to synchronous replication, and what prerequisites must be met?

options.text: Edit Protection Policy > Replication Mode: "Synchronous" > Prerequisites: <5ms latency between sites, Metro Availability license required, witness VM must be operational. Failover behavior changes to immediate consistency.
Protection > Policies > Select policy > Change replication: "Sync" > Requirements: both sites must be <10ms apart, metro license, stable network, no bandwidth constraints. Test with one VM first.

Prism Central > Data Protection > Policy > Edit > Replication Type: "Synchronous RPO=0" > Prerequisite checks: confirm <5ms latency, Metro license available, witness configured, both sites optimal state > Apply
Synchronous requires dedicated cluster: can't mix sync and async on same policy. Create new sync-only policy with Metro Availability license, meet latency/witness requirements.

questions.explanation: Edit the Protection Policy and set Replication Mode to "Synchronous" for zero RPO. Prerequisites: network latency <5ms between source and target (critical), Metro Availability license, witness VM operational and reachable. Synchronous mode ensures every write is replicated before acknowledgment, guaranteeing zero data loss at the cost of higher I/O latency. Test on non-critical VMs before production deployment.

questions.questionText: During a failover test, VMs boot successfully on the target cluster but receive incorrect IP addresses from DHCP instead of the mapped static IPs configured in the recovery plan. The network mapping shows correct source→target VLAN and IP mappings. What is the root cause?

options.text: NGT (Nutanix Guest Tools) not running on VMs prevents IP mapping application during boot. Verify: `netsh interface ipv4 show config` (Windows) or `ip addr` (Linux) shows DHCP instead of static. Solution: ensure NGT is installed and running on all protected VMs.
IP mapping not applied because recovery plan network mapping isn't enabled. Enable "Apply Network Configuration" toggle in Recovery Plan before test.
DHCP server on target VLAN 200 is assigning incorrect addresses. Add exclusion range or reconfigure DHCP to not serve the static IP range.
Recovery plan metadata not updated. Resave the network mapping configuration to force sync with latest settings.

questions.explanation: The most common cause is NGT (Nutanix Guest Tools) not installed or running on the protected VMs. NGT is required to apply the static IP address mappings at boot time during failover. Verify NGT is installed and running on protected VMs: on Windows check Services for "Nutanix Guest Tools"; on Linux check `service nutanix-guest-tools status`. Install NGT on all protected VMs before failover. Without NGT, the VM boots with DHCP-assigned addresses ignoring recovery plan mapping.

questions.questionText: Your Metro Availability setup stops replicating after a brief network outage. Prism Central shows "Metro Disabled" status. The witness VM is unreachable from the source cluster. How do you recover Metro Availability?

options.text: Restore witness VM network connectivity first. Verify witness VM is operational and reachable: `ping witness-ip` from source cluster. Once connected, Metro auto-recovers within 2 minutes. Verify replication resumes: Prism > Data Protection > Metro status shows "Active"
In Prism Central, navigate to Data Protection > Metro Availability > Disabled > Click "Enable Metro" button > System re-establishes witness connection > Confirm status changes to "Active"
Manual recovery: 1) Restore witness VM connectivity, 2) Prism Central > Data Protection > Edit Metro setup > Verify witness IP is correct > Apply > Metro re-syncs
Metro cannot recover automatically after network event. Must create new Metro Availability setup with new witness VM.

questions.explanation: The witness VM is critical for Metro Availability quorum. First, restore its network connectivity: verify it's running and reachable via `ping witness-ip` from the source cluster. Once the witness is reachable, Metro Availability automatically recovers and resumes replication within ~2 minutes. Verify recovery: check Prism Central > Data Protection > Metro status shows "Active" and replication metrics show resuming activity.

questions.questionText: During failover, you receive error "VM network [name] not found on target cluster." The recovery plan network mapping is configured. What is the cause and how do you resolve it?

options.text: The virtual network referenced in the recovery plan doesn't exist on the target cluster. Create matching virtual network on target cluster: Prism Element > Network > Virtual Networks > Create > Name/VLAN matching source network > Create. Then update recovery plan network mapping > Apply and retry failover.

Network mapping in recovery plan doesn't match actual target cluster network. Edit recovery plan > Network Mapping > Verify target network name/VLAN is correct for target cluster > Correct any mismatches > Save > Retry failover.

Virtual network name exists but with different VLAN tag. Edit recovery plan mapping: ensure mapped target network uses same VLAN or confirm target has network on correct VLAN. Failover requires exact VLAN match.
All of the above - verify network exists on target, recovery plan mapping is correct, VLAN tags match

questions.explanation: The error indicates the virtual network referenced in the recovery plan doesn't exist on the target cluster. Verify: (1) the target cluster has a virtual network matching the recovery plan's target network name, (2) the network uses the correct VLAN (e.g., VLAN 200 for the mapped target network), (3) recovery plan network mapping points to the correct network on the target. Create the network if missing, correct the mapping if needed, then retry failover.

questions.questionText: Your recovery plan includes a pre-recovery script (database pre-flight checks) that times out during failover test. Error: "Script exceeded execution timeout of 120 seconds." The script actually takes 180 seconds to complete. How do you resolve this?

options.text: Recovery Plan > Execution Settings > Pre-Recovery Script > Timeout: increase from default 120s to 180s (or higher) > Apply > Retry test failover
Optimize the pre-recovery script to complete in <120 seconds. Refactor database checks for parallel execution or simplify validation logic.
Split pre-recovery script into multiple smaller scripts, each <120s, executed sequentially in the recovery plan runbook
A or B - either increase timeout or optimize the script

questions.explanation: You have two options: (1) increase the timeout threshold - edit the Recovery Plan, locate Execution Settings or Pre-Recovery Script configuration, increase timeout from default 120 seconds to 180+ seconds, and re-test, OR (2) optimize the pre-recovery script to complete within 120 seconds by parallelizing checks or removing unnecessary steps. For compliance-critical scripts, increasing timeout is often the safer approach to ensure completeness.

questions.questionText: Your security audit requires verification that AIDE (Advanced Intrusion Detection Engine) is properly configured on all CVMs with daily baselines, and SCMA (Secure Cluster Monitoring Agent) is scheduled correctly. What commands provide this verification?

options.text: `ncli cluster get-cvm-security-config` shows "Enable Aide: true" and "Aide Schedule: DAILY". `ncli cluster get-scma-info` shows schedule and status.
SSH to CVM and check: `/var/log/aide-baseline.log` shows daily scheduled execution, `systemctl status aide-monitor` running
Prism Element > Security > AIDE Configuration shows "Enabled: yes" and "Schedule: Daily". Verify SCMA: Administration > Monitoring > SCMA Status shows "Active" with daily collection
A and B together provide full verification - ncli commands show policy, log files and systemctl confirm actual execution

questions.explanation: Use `ncli cluster get-cvm-security-config` to verify AIDE is enabled and scheduled. The output displays "Enable Aide: true" and "Aide Schedule: WEEKLY" confirming the weekly baseline and monitoring configuration. Use `ncli cluster get-scma-info` to verify SCMA configuration and collection schedule. These commands provide the authoritative configuration state. Verify the schedule matches security policy requirements and AIDE status is "true". This satisfies security audit requirements for AIDE configuration verification.

questions.questionText: You need to collect diagnostic logs for a Stargate service crash that occurred in the last 2 hours. You want to gather logs from all CVMs covering this timeframe from Prism Element UI (not CLI). How do you do this?

options.text: Prism Element > Gear icon > Log Collector > Set Start time: 2 hours ago > Select Components: Stargate > Download logs > Logs include all CVMs with Stargate activity in the window
Administration > Logs > Filter: Service=Stargate, Time range=Last 2 hours > Export > Logs saved to local directory
Cluster > Diagnostics > System Logs > Time Range: Last 2 hours > Services: Stargate > Collect > Wait for bundle to complete > Download
Prism Element > System > Logs > Advanced Filter > Stargate errors, last 2 hours > Generate Report > Download

questions.explanation: Navigate to Prism Element, click the Gear icon (Administration) > Log Collector. Set the start time to 2 hours ago (or select a time range). Select "Stargate" from the Components list. Click Collect/Download to gather logs from all CVMs covering the Stargate service during the specified timeframe. The log bundle will include Stargate daemon logs and related system messages helpful for root-cause analysis.

questions.questionText: You've been asked to troubleshoot why a protection policy (Prism Central managed) isn't replicating a VM to the target site. The policy is category-based and should apply to all VMs tagged "Compliance:Backup=yes". The VM exists but isn't replicating. What are the diagnostic steps?

options.text: Verify VM has correct category assigned: Prism Central > VMs > Select VM > Categories tab > Confirm category "Compliance:Backup" with value "yes" is present. Check policy scope: Data Protection > Policies > Verify policy filter matches. Check remote site: Clusters > Remote sites shows target site "Active". If any missing, correct and replication starts within minutes.

Confirm protection policy (not protection domain) is in use. Protection domains are PE-managed legacy feature; protection policies are PC-managed (current). Verify: Prism Central > Data Protection > Policies (not Domains). Check VM category match, remote site connectivity, replication license availability.

Run diagnostic: `ncli datastore get-protection-policies` on PE shows policy configured. Verify VM is assigned: `ncli vm get vm-name` shows protection policy in metadata. Check connectivity: `ncli remote-site get-info` shows target site reachable.
All of the above - comprehensive approach checks category assignment, protection policy scope, remote site connectivity, and licensing

questions.explanation: Comprehensive troubleshooting requires checking multiple factors: (1) Verify the VM has the correct category tag matching the protection policy filter (Prism Central > VMs > Categories), (2) Confirm you're using protection policies (PC-managed, current) not protection domains (PE-managed, legacy), (3) Check remote site connectivity (Prism Central > Administration > Clusters > Remote Sites shows "Active"), (4) Verify replication license availability. Category mismatch is the most common cause; incorrect scope or missing remote site connectivity are also typical issues.

questions.questionText: A data scientist needs to deploy a 70B parameter LLM on an NAI cluster. The cluster has nodes with NVIDIA T4 GPUs (16GB VRAM each). What is the primary concern?

options.text: T4 GPUs have insufficient VRAM for a 70B model even with INT4 quantization; A100 or H100 GPUs are needed
The T4 GPU does not support TensorRT-LLM inference engine
NAI does not support models larger than 13B parameters
The NKP cluster requires a minimum of 8 T4 GPUs for any model deployment

questions.explanation: A 70B parameter model requires approximately 35GB+ of VRAM even with INT4 quantization. The T4 has only 16GB VRAM, which is insufficient. A100 or H100 GPUs with 80GB VRAM are required for models of this size.

questions.questionText: An infrastructure engineer is planning an NAI deployment and wants to confirm that the Kubernetes layer is healthy before installing the NAI Operator. Which component must be operational as a prerequisite?

options.text: Nutanix Kubernetes Platform (NKP) cluster with GPU-enabled worker nodes
Nutanix Objects bucket configured with S3-compatible model storage
NVIDIA DCGM Exporter running on all cluster nodes
vLLM inference pods pre-deployed in the default namespace

questions.explanation: NAI runs on NKP clusters. The NKP cluster must be healthy with GPU-enabled worker nodes detected before the NAI Operator can be installed and begin managing AI workloads.

questions.questionText: During an NAI pre-deployment check, the GPU detection step fails on a node that has a physical NVIDIA A100 installed. What should the administrator verify first?

options.text: That GPU passthrough is enabled for the VM in AHV and the correct PCI device is assigned
That the Nutanix CSI driver version matches the A100 firmware revision
That Prism Central has the GPU Analytics license applied
That the NKP control plane is running on a separate GPU-enabled node

questions.explanation: GPUs must be passed through to VMs via AHV GPU passthrough. If the PCI device is not properly assigned to the worker node VM, Kubernetes will not detect the GPU resource.

questions.questionText: A network engineer is configuring the environment for NAI. Which network requirement is essential for successful NAI deployment?

options.text: DNS resolution must be functional for NAI service endpoints and external model registries
All GPU nodes must reside on a VXLAN overlay network with jumbo frames enabled
The NAI Operator requires a dedicated VLAN with BGP peering to the inference pods
Multicast must be enabled on the GPU node subnet for inter-pod GPU communication

questions.explanation: DNS resolution is required so that NAI endpoints can be reached by clients and so the cluster can resolve external registries like Hugging Face Hub for model downloads.

questions.questionText: An administrator deploys NAI but notices that inference endpoint URLs return TLS certificate errors in client applications. What is the most likely cause in a test environment?

options.text: Self-signed TLS certificates were used for NAI endpoints and the client does not trust the CA
The NAI Operator automatically disables TLS when GPU passthrough is active
vLLM inference pods only support mutual TLS with client certificates
The Nutanix CSI driver is intercepting HTTPS traffic on the storage network

questions.explanation: NAI supports self-signed certificates for testing, but clients must trust the certificate authority or explicitly skip verification. In production, valid TLS certificates should be configured.

questions.questionText: Which statement about the NAI Operator is correct?

options.text: It is a Kubernetes operator that manages the full lifecycle of NAI components including inference servers and model repositories
It is a Prism Central plugin that replaces NKP for AI-specific workloads
It runs exclusively on the AHV hypervisor outside of any Kubernetes cluster
It is an NVIDIA-provided binary that must be installed on bare-metal GPU hosts

questions.explanation: The NAI Operator is a Kubernetes operator deployed within the NKP cluster. It automates the deployment, scaling, and lifecycle management of inference servers, model repositories, and API gateways.

questions.questionText: An engineer needs to provide persistent storage for downloaded model files in the NAI environment. Which Nutanix component fulfills this requirement?

options.text: Nutanix CSI driver providing persistent volume claims backed by Nutanix storage
Nutanix Era database provisioning for model metadata
Prism Central image service hosting model binaries as disk images
AHV volume groups directly mounted to GPU passthrough devices

questions.explanation: The Nutanix CSI (Container Storage Interface) driver integrates with Kubernetes to provide persistent volumes for NAI workloads, including model file storage via persistent volume claims.

questions.questionText: During NAI deployment, the storage class availability check fails. The NKP cluster is running and GPU nodes are detected. What should the administrator do?

options.text: Verify that the Nutanix CSI driver is installed and a default StorageClass is configured in the NKP cluster
Restart the NVIDIA GPU Operator to re-initialize the storage subsystem
Increase the Prism Central storage container replication factor to 3
Convert the Nutanix cluster from hybrid to all-flash before retrying

questions.explanation: NAI requires a Kubernetes StorageClass backed by the Nutanix CSI driver to provision persistent volumes for model storage. If the CSI driver is missing or no StorageClass exists, the pre-check will fail.

questions.questionText: A customer asks whether they can install NAI without Prism Central. Which response is accurate?

options.text: Prism Central is required as it provides centralized management, RBAC, and the NAI deployment interface
Prism Central is optional; NAI can be fully managed through kubectl and Helm charts alone
Prism Central is only needed if the cluster uses Nutanix Objects for model storage
Prism Central is replaced by the NVIDIA AI Enterprise Manager in NAI deployments

questions.explanation: Prism Central is a required component for NAI. It provides the centralized management plane, role-based access control, project management, and serves as the primary interface for deploying and managing NAI.

questions.questionText: An engineer is sizing GPU resources for a deployment of a 13B parameter model in FP16 precision. Which GPU option provides sufficient VRAM?

options.text: A single NVIDIA A100 with 80GB VRAM
A single NVIDIA T4 with 16GB VRAM
Two NVIDIA T4 GPUs with 16GB VRAM each in a non-MIG configuration
A single NVIDIA A100 in MIG mode with a 10GB partition

questions.explanation: A 13B parameter model in FP16 requires approximately 26GB of VRAM. A single A100 with 80GB provides ample room. A T4 (16GB) is insufficient, and MIG partitions at 10GB would also be too small.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is NOT a supported GPU for NAI deployments?

options.text: NVIDIA RTX 4090
NVIDIA A100
NVIDIA H100
NVIDIA L40S

questions.explanation: NAI supports enterprise-grade datacenter GPUs including the A100, H100, L40S, and T4. Consumer GPUs like the RTX 4090 are not on the supported hardware compatibility list for NAI.

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to deploy NAI using the command line instead of the Prism Central UI. Which approach is valid?

options.text: Deploy the NAI Operator using kubectl apply with the appropriate manifests on the NKP cluster
Run the NAI installer binary directly on the AHV hypervisor host via SSH
Use the Nutanix CLI (ncli) to create an NAI container on Prism Element
Execute a PowerShell script on the Prism Central VM to bootstrap NAI services

questions.explanation: NAI can be deployed via Prism Central UI or by using kubectl to apply Kubernetes manifests directly to the NKP cluster, giving administrators CLI-based deployment flexibility.

questions.questionText: After deploying NAI, an engineer runs a test inference request but gets a connection timeout. The NAI pods show Running status. What should be checked next?

options.text: Kubernetes ingress configuration and whether the API Gateway service has an external IP or NodePort assigned
Whether the NVIDIA GPU driver has been compiled with inference timeout support
The Prism Central network visualization tab for dropped GPU packets
Whether the model repository pod has enough CPU to serve HTTP health checks

questions.explanation: Even if pods are running, the inference endpoint must be exposed externally via Kubernetes ingress or a LoadBalancer/NodePort service. A missing or misconfigured ingress will cause client connection timeouts.

questions.questionText: What role does the API Gateway play in the NAI architecture?

options.text: It provides an OpenAI-compatible REST API endpoint that routes inference requests to the appropriate inference server
It acts as a reverse proxy between Prism Central and the AHV hypervisor for GPU telemetry
It translates Kubernetes API calls into Nutanix Acropolis API calls for storage provisioning
It manages TLS certificate renewal for all NKP cluster nodes

questions.explanation: The NAI API Gateway exposes OpenAI-compatible endpoints (such as /v1/chat/completions) and routes incoming inference requests to the backend inference server pods running vLLM or TensorRT-LLM.

questions.questionText: An NKP cluster has three worker nodes, each with one NVIDIA H100 GPU. An administrator deploys a model that requires two GPUs. What happens?

options.text: The model pod is scheduled across two worker nodes using tensor parallelism if the inference engine and cluster networking support it
NAI automatically enables MIG mode on one H100 to simulate two GPUs on a single node
The deployment fails because NAI requires all GPUs to be on the same physical node
Prism Central migrates one H100 GPU to another node using live GPU migration

questions.explanation: When a model requires multiple GPUs and they span nodes, tensor parallelism over the network can be used if supported. However, single-node multi-GPU is preferred for performance. The key is that NAI and NKP scheduling work together to allocate the needed GPU resources.

questions.questionText: During NAI installation, which Kubernetes resource does the NAI Operator create to store downloaded model files?

options.text: PersistentVolumeClaim backed by the Nutanix CSI StorageClass
ConfigMap with base64-encoded model weights
Kubernetes Secret containing the serialized model binary
EmptyDir volume on the inference pod's local disk

questions.explanation: The NAI Operator creates PersistentVolumeClaims to request storage from the Nutanix CSI driver, ensuring model files persist independently of pod lifecycle and can be reused across pod restarts.

questions.questionText: An engineer notices NTP synchronization is not configured on the GPU worker nodes. Why is this a concern for NAI?

options.text: Time skew between nodes can cause TLS certificate validation failures and Kubernetes scheduling issues
GPU clock speeds are regulated by NTP and will throttle without synchronization
The vLLM inference engine uses NTP timestamps to order token generation
Nutanix CSI driver snapshots rely on NTP for point-in-time consistency

questions.explanation: NTP synchronization is required to prevent certificate validation errors (certificates have validity windows) and to ensure consistent Kubernetes cluster operations including logging, scheduling, and health checks.

questions.questionText: A customer wants to use Nutanix Files for storing AI model files that will be imported into NAI. How would these models be accessed?

options.text: Models stored on Nutanix Files can be uploaded to the NAI model repository for use by inference endpoints
NAI inference pods directly mount Nutanix Files shares via NFS at runtime as the primary model source
Nutanix Files automatically converts model files to SafeTensors format before NAI ingestion
The NAI Operator replaces Nutanix Files with a built-in S3-compatible model store

questions.explanation: Nutanix Files or Objects can serve as external storage for model files. Administrators upload models from these locations into the NAI model repository, which then serves them to inference endpoints.

questions.questionText: Which Kubernetes component is responsible for exposing NAI inference endpoints to external clients?

options.text: Ingress controller with configured rules for the NAI API Gateway service
KubeProxy with GPU-aware load balancing extensions
CoreDNS with inference-specific service discovery plugins
The Nutanix CSI driver's built-in HTTP proxy mode

questions.explanation: Kubernetes Ingress resources, managed by an ingress controller, define external routing rules that expose the NAI API Gateway service to clients outside the cluster.

questions.questionText: An administrator is performing post-deployment verification of NAI. Which action confirms the inference pipeline is fully operational?

options.text: Sending a test prompt to the /v1/chat/completions endpoint and receiving a generated response
Checking that the NAI Operator pod has zero restart counts in kubectl
Verifying that Prism Central shows the NKP cluster in a healthy state
Confirming that the Nutanix CSI driver PersistentVolumes are in Bound status

questions.explanation: The definitive test of a working NAI deployment is an end-to-end inference request. Sending a prompt to the OpenAI-compatible endpoint and receiving a valid generated response confirms all components are functioning.

questions.questionText: What is the relationship between NAI and GPT-in-a-Box?

options.text: NAI (Nutanix Enterprise AI) is the current product name; GPT-in-a-Box was the former name for the same solution
GPT-in-a-Box is a separate Nutanix product focused exclusively on OpenAI GPT models
NAI is the cloud-hosted version while GPT-in-a-Box is the on-premises version
GPT-in-a-Box is an NVIDIA product that NAI replaced with a Nutanix-native inference engine

questions.explanation: Nutanix Enterprise AI (NAI), formerly known as GPT-in-a-Box, is Nutanix's solution for deploying and managing large language models on Nutanix infrastructure with GPU-enabled nodes.

questions.questionText: An engineer plans to deploy NAI on an existing NKP cluster that currently runs stateless web applications. What must be added to the cluster?

options.text: GPU-enabled worker nodes with NVIDIA GPUs passed through from AHV and the NVIDIA GPU device plugin
A dedicated NKP control plane running on GPU nodes separate from the existing control plane
Nutanix Calm blueprints for AI workload orchestration replacing NKP scheduling
A Prism Element storage-only node to host the vLLM binary artifacts

questions.explanation: An existing NKP cluster can be extended for NAI by adding GPU-enabled worker nodes. The GPU device plugin in Kubernetes allows the scheduler to allocate GPU resources to NAI pods.

questions.questionText: During NAI deployment, an administrator receives an error that no GPU resources are available in the Kubernetes cluster despite GPUs being physically installed. What is the most likely missing component?

options.text: The NVIDIA GPU device plugin or GPU Operator is not deployed on the NKP cluster
The Nutanix CSI driver does not have GPU resource quotas configured
Prism Central's GPU licensing has expired
The AHV hypervisor requires a reboot to activate GPU passthrough after initial configuration

questions.explanation: Kubernetes requires the NVIDIA GPU device plugin (often deployed via the GPU Operator) to discover and advertise GPU resources. Without it, the cluster scheduler cannot see or allocate GPUs.

questions.questionText: Which statement best describes how GPU passthrough works for NAI on AHV?

options.text: AHV assigns the physical GPU directly to a VM, giving the guest OS exclusive access to the GPU hardware
AHV virtualizes the GPU using SR-IOV, sharing it equally across all VMs on the host
AHV uses NVIDIA GRID vGPU to provide virtual GPU profiles to Kubernetes pods
AHV emulates GPU hardware in software so that any VM can use GPU acceleration

questions.explanation: GPU passthrough on AHV assigns the physical GPU device directly to a specific VM via PCI passthrough, giving that VM (and its Kubernetes workloads) exclusive, bare-metal access to the GPU.

questions.questionText: An enterprise customer requires high availability for their NAI inference service. Which Kubernetes-native approach supports this?

options.text: Deploying multiple inference pod replicas behind a Kubernetes Service with load balancing
Running the NAI Operator in active-passive mode across two separate NKP clusters
Configuring Prism Central to live-migrate GPU VMs between hosts during failures
Using Nutanix Metro Availability to replicate GPU memory state across sites

questions.explanation: Kubernetes Services distribute traffic across multiple inference pod replicas. If one pod fails, the Service routes requests to healthy replicas, providing high availability for the inference endpoint.

questions.questionText: An architect is designing a network topology for an NAI deployment. Which network consideration is specific to GPU-enabled nodes?

options.text: GPU nodes should be on a dedicated network segment to ensure sufficient bandwidth for model loading and inference traffic
GPU nodes require InfiniBand networking; standard Ethernet is not supported for inference
Each GPU must have its own dedicated NIC for direct GPU-to-GPU RDMA communication
GPU nodes must use IPv6 exclusively as NAI does not support IPv4 for inference traffic

questions.explanation: A dedicated network for GPU nodes helps ensure that model data transfer and inference traffic are not contended by other cluster traffic, improving performance and reliability.

questions.questionText: What happens if the NAI Operator pod crashes or is evicted in a running NAI environment?

options.text: Kubernetes restarts the NAI Operator pod automatically; existing inference endpoints continue serving requests during the restart
All inference pods are immediately terminated and client requests fail until the Operator is manually redeployed
Prism Central takes over NAI management until the Operator pod recovers
The NVIDIA GPU Operator assumes NAI Operator responsibilities as a failover mechanism

questions.explanation: The NAI Operator is managed by a Kubernetes Deployment, so it is automatically restarted. Existing inference pods are independent and continue serving traffic since they are already running.

questions.questionText: An administrator is reviewing the minimum requirements for an NAI deployment. Which combination is necessary?

options.text: Prism Central, NKP cluster, GPU-enabled nodes, Nutanix CSI driver, and sufficient storage capacity
Prism Element only, Nutanix Calm, GPU-enabled nodes, and NVIDIA AI Enterprise license
Prism Central, AOS 5.x, Kubernetes vanilla cluster, and CPU-only nodes with AVX-512
Prism Element, Nutanix Karbon, NVIDIA GRID license, and vSphere hypervisor

questions.explanation: NAI requires Prism Central for management, an NKP cluster for Kubernetes orchestration, GPU-enabled nodes for inference, and the Nutanix CSI driver for persistent storage.

questions.questionText: A junior administrator accidentally deletes the PersistentVolumeClaim used by the NAI model repository. What is the impact?

options.text: Downloaded model files are lost; models must be re-downloaded or re-uploaded to a newly created PVC
The NAI Operator automatically recreates the PVC with all data intact from Nutanix snapshots
Inference endpoints continue working indefinitely as models are cached in GPU VRAM
Prism Central restores the PVC from its built-in model version control system

questions.explanation: PVCs store model files persistently. If the PVC is deleted and no backup or reclaim policy preserves the data, the model files are lost and must be re-downloaded or re-uploaded.

questions.questionText: An engineer wants to verify that GPU resources are correctly detected by the NKP cluster before deploying NAI. Which kubectl command provides this information?

options.text: kubectl describe nodes and checking for nvidia.com/gpu in the Allocatable resources section
kubectl get configmaps -n gpu-system to view GPU allocation maps
kubectl logs kube-scheduler to find GPU registration events
kubectl get pv to check for GPU-backed PersistentVolumes

questions.explanation: Running `kubectl describe nodes` shows the allocatable resources for each node, including `nvidia.com/gpu` entries that confirm GPUs are detected and available for scheduling.

questions.questionText: Which inference server architecture component in NAI handles the tokenization and batching of incoming requests?

options.text: The inference engine (vLLM or TensorRT-LLM) which manages request batching, tokenization, and GPU execution
The Kubernetes Horizontal Pod Autoscaler which batches requests before forwarding to pods
The Nutanix CSI driver which caches tokenized inputs on persistent storage
The NAI Operator which pre-processes all API requests before routing them

questions.explanation: The inference engine (vLLM or TensorRT-LLM) is responsible for receiving prompts, tokenizing them, batching multiple requests for efficient GPU utilization, and executing the model inference.

questions.questionText: During NAI deployment via Prism Central, the wizard indicates that the NKP cluster is unreachable. All NKP pods are running normally. What should the administrator check?

options.text: Network connectivity between Prism Central and the NKP cluster API server, including firewall rules and DNS resolution
Whether the NVIDIA GPU Operator is conflicting with Prism Central's cluster agent
That the NAI model repository has been pre-populated before the wizard can proceed
Whether the Nutanix CSI driver has consumed all available IP addresses in the subnet

questions.explanation: Prism Central must be able to reach the NKP API server over the network. Firewall rules, DNS resolution, and routing between Prism Central and the NKP cluster are common points of failure.

questions.questionText: Which of the following describes the correct order of NAI deployment steps?

options.text: Provision NKP cluster → Configure GPU passthrough → Install NVIDIA GPU Operator → Deploy NAI Operator → Create inference endpoints
Deploy NAI Operator → Provision NKP cluster → Install NVIDIA GPU Operator → Configure GPU passthrough → Create inference endpoints
Create inference endpoints → Deploy NAI Operator → Provision NKP cluster → Configure GPU passthrough → Install NVIDIA GPU Operator
Install NVIDIA GPU Operator → Configure GPU passthrough → Create inference endpoints → Deploy NAI Operator → Provision NKP cluster

questions.explanation: The deployment follows a logical dependency chain: the NKP cluster must exist first, then GPUs are configured and detected, then the NAI Operator is deployed, and finally inference endpoints are created.

questions.questionText: An administrator observes that an NAI inference pod is stuck in Pending state with the event "Insufficient nvidia.com/gpu." What does this indicate?

options.text: The cluster does not have enough available GPU resources to satisfy the pod's GPU request
The NVIDIA GPU driver inside the pod has failed to initialize
The model format is incompatible with the allocated GPU type
The NAI Operator has reached its maximum endpoint license limit

questions.explanation: A pod stuck in Pending with "Insufficient nvidia.com/gpu" means the Kubernetes scheduler cannot find a node with enough free GPU resources to fulfill the pod's resource request.

questions.questionText: What is the primary purpose of the model repository component in the NAI architecture?

options.text: It stores and manages downloaded or uploaded model files so they can be served by inference endpoints
It provides a Git-based version control system for tracking model code changes
It runs continuous training jobs to improve model accuracy over time
It acts as a Kubernetes admission controller to validate model deployment manifests

questions.explanation: The model repository is a storage component that holds model files (weights, configuration, tokenizer) making them available for inference endpoints to load and serve.

questions.questionText: A systems engineer is deploying an NAI cluster in an air-gapped environment with no internet access. How can models be made available?

options.text: Manually upload model files from local storage to the NAI model repository after transferring them via secure media
NAI requires internet access to Hugging Face Hub at all times and cannot operate air-gapped
Configure Prism Central to act as a proxy for Hugging Face Hub model downloads
Use NVIDIA NGC's offline satellite server which is bundled with the NAI Operator

questions.explanation: In air-gapped environments, models can be downloaded externally, transferred to the secure environment, and manually uploaded to the NAI model repository for deployment.

questions.questionText: Which NVIDIA GPU feature allows a single A100 or H100 GPU to be partitioned into multiple smaller GPU instances for NAI workloads?

options.text: Multi-Instance GPU (MIG) which creates isolated GPU partitions with dedicated memory and compute
NVIDIA vGPU (GRID) which provides time-sliced virtual GPU access to multiple VMs
NVLink which combines multiple GPUs into a single logical GPU
NVIDIA CUDA MPS (Multi-Process Service) which shares a single GPU context

questions.explanation: Multi-Instance GPU (MIG), available on A100 and H100 GPUs, allows hardware-level partitioning into isolated GPU instances, each with its own memory and compute resources, enabling fractional GPU allocation.

questions.questionText: An NAI deployment uses self-signed certificates for testing. The administrator now needs to move to production. What must be updated?

options.text: Replace self-signed certificates with certificates issued by a trusted Certificate Authority for all NAI endpoints
Disable TLS entirely since production NAI traffic should use IPsec instead
Move the NAI Operator to a different namespace that has built-in TLS termination
Enable NVIDIA Confidential Computing which replaces TLS with GPU-encrypted channels

questions.explanation: Production NAI deployments should use TLS certificates from a trusted CA to ensure client trust and secure communication without requiring clients to bypass certificate verification.

questions.questionText: An engineer is choosing between deploying NAI on a single-node NKP cluster versus a multi-node cluster. What is a key limitation of the single-node approach?

options.text: No high availability for inference workloads; if the single node fails, all inference endpoints become unavailable
Single-node NKP clusters cannot use GPU passthrough on AHV
The NAI Operator cannot run on the same node as inference pods
Single-node deployments are limited to the vLLM engine and cannot use TensorRT-LLM

questions.explanation: A single-node NKP cluster has no redundancy. If that node fails, all Kubernetes workloads including NAI inference endpoints go down, making it unsuitable for production without external HA measures.

questions.questionText: Which Nutanix storage option can be used with the CSI driver to provide persistent volumes for NAI model storage?

options.text: Nutanix Volumes (block storage) provisioned through the Nutanix CSI driver as ReadWriteOnce PersistentVolumes
Nutanix Mine backup appliance acting as the primary model store
Prism Central image library serving model files as ISO images to pods
AHV local disk passthrough volumes that bypass the Nutanix storage layer entirely

questions.explanation: The Nutanix CSI driver supports provisioning PersistentVolumes backed by Nutanix storage (Volumes/Files), which NAI uses for model repository storage and other persistent data needs.

questions.questionText: An NAI administrator needs to grant a data scientist the ability to create inference endpoints and run inference but not manage other users. Which role should be assigned?

options.text: User role, which permits endpoint deployment, inference execution, and management of own resources
Admin role with a custom Prism Central policy restricting user management APIs
Kubernetes cluster-admin ClusterRole bound to the scientist's service account
NVIDIA NGC Viewer role federated through Prism Central SSO

questions.explanation: The User role in NAI provides the ability to deploy endpoints, run inference, and manage one's own resources without granting administrative capabilities like user provisioning.

questions.questionText: A security team requires that all programmatic access to NAI inference endpoints use unique, traceable credentials. Which NAI feature addresses this?

options.text: Per-user API keys that identify individual users and can be rotated according to policy
Shared Kubernetes ServiceAccount tokens distributed to all inference clients
NVIDIA NGC API keys imported into Prism Central for unified access
OAuth2 client credentials provisioned by the Nutanix CSI driver

questions.explanation: NAI supports per-user API keys for programmatic access. Each key is tied to a specific user, providing traceability and the ability to rotate or revoke individual keys.

questions.questionText: An organization uses Hugging Face Hub as its primary model source. An NAI administrator wants to import a Llama 2 model. What is the correct workflow?

options.text: In NAI, select import from Hugging Face Hub, specify the model repository ID, and NAI downloads and registers the model
Clone the Hugging Face Git repository to a Nutanix Files share and NFS-mount it directly into inference pods
Use the NVIDIA NGC CLI to convert the Hugging Face model to TensorRT format before NAI can accept it
Upload the model to Prism Central's image service and attach it as a virtual disk to the inference pod

questions.explanation: NAI integrates with Hugging Face Hub as a model source. Administrators specify the model repository ID, and NAI handles downloading the model files and registering them in the model repository.

questions.questionText: A data engineer wants to reduce the GPU memory footprint of a 7B parameter model currently running in FP16. Which quantization option provides the greatest memory reduction?

options.text: INT4 quantization, which reduces memory usage by approximately 75% compared to FP16 with some quality tradeoff
FP32 up-casting, which compresses the model through higher precision redundancy elimination
INT8 quantization with FP16 activations, which doubles the memory compared to pure FP16
BF16 conversion, which halves the memory footprint compared to FP16

questions.explanation: INT4 quantization reduces each parameter from 16 bits to 4 bits, cutting memory usage by roughly 75%. This is the most aggressive supported quantization, with an expected minor quality tradeoff.

questions.questionText: Which model format is commonly used with vLLM inference engine in NAI for efficient model loading?

options.text: SafeTensors, which provides safe and efficient serialization of model tensors
ONNX Runtime format optimized for CPU-only inference
TensorFlow SavedModel format for TPU acceleration
Apple CoreML packages for GPU inference on ARM-based servers

questions.explanation: SafeTensors is a widely used format for storing model weights safely and efficiently. vLLM supports loading models in SafeTensors format for inference on NVIDIA GPUs.

questions.questionText: An administrator is configuring an NAI endpoint with auto-scaling. Which metric does NAI use to determine when to scale out inference replicas?

options.text: Request queue depth, scaling out when pending requests exceed the configured threshold
GPU temperature readings from NVIDIA DCGM, scaling out when thermal throttling is detected
Nutanix cluster CPU utilization reported by Prism Element
Number of unique API keys that have sent requests in the last minute

questions.explanation: NAI auto-scaling is based on request queue depth. When the number of queued inference requests exceeds the threshold, additional replicas are spun up to handle the load.

questions.questionText: A developer integrates their application with an NAI inference endpoint. Which API path should they use for chat-based interactions?

options.text: /v1/chat/completions, the OpenAI-compatible endpoint for conversational inference
/api/v2/inference/predict, the Nutanix-proprietary inference endpoint
/v1/models/chat, the model-specific chat handler
/nvidia/trt/inference, the TensorRT-LLM native protocol endpoint

questions.explanation: NAI exposes OpenAI-compatible API endpoints. For chat-based interactions, clients use /v1/chat/completions, making it easy to integrate with existing OpenAI client libraries.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of LoRA adapters in the context of NAI model management?

options.text: LoRA adapters enable model customization by training small additional parameter matrices without retraining the full base model
LoRA adapters convert models from SafeTensors format to GGUF format for quantized inference
LoRA adapters provide network-level load balancing between multiple inference endpoints
LoRA adapters are Kubernetes resource adapters that translate GPU resource requests for the scheduler

questions.explanation: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) adapters allow fine-tuning of models by training small, additional weight matrices. This enables model customization at a fraction of the computational cost of full retraining.

questions.questionText: An NAI administrator creates a new endpoint and selects "2" for the minimum replicas and "5" for maximum replicas. What does this configuration mean?

options.text: The endpoint always has at least 2 inference pods running and can scale up to 5 pods based on demand
The endpoint uses 2 GPUs minimum and 5 GPUs maximum per inference pod
The model is sharded across 2 to 5 nodes using tensor parallelism
The endpoint retains 2 model versions and can cache up to 5 versions simultaneously

questions.explanation: Auto-scaling configuration sets the floor and ceiling for inference pod replicas. With min=2 and max=5, at least 2 pods always run for availability, scaling up to 5 under load.

questions.questionText: Which inference engine should an administrator choose for maximum performance on NVIDIA H100 GPUs with a supported model?

options.text: TensorRT-LLM, which is optimized by NVIDIA for maximum performance on NVIDIA GPUs through model compilation
vLLM, which provides exclusive support for H100 FP8 tensor cores
ONNX Runtime with DirectML backend for H100 GPU acceleration
PyTorch eager mode execution which bypasses the need for an inference engine

questions.explanation: TensorRT-LLM is NVIDIA's optimized inference engine that compiles models for maximum throughput and lowest latency on NVIDIA GPUs, making it the highest-performance option for H100s.

questions.questionText: A developer notices that the /v1/models endpoint on their NAI deployment returns an empty list. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: No inference endpoints have been created yet, or existing endpoints have no models loaded and ready
The vLLM engine does not support the /v1/models endpoint; only /v1/completions is available
The developer's API key has insufficient permissions to list GPU resources
The NAI Operator has not synchronized with the NVIDIA NGC model catalog

questions.explanation: The /v1/models endpoint lists models that are actively deployed and serving. If no endpoints have been created or models are not in a ready state, the list will be empty.

questions.questionText: An organization needs to control which users can deploy specific models on NAI. Which mechanism provides this governance?

options.text: Prism Central RBAC using categories and projects to restrict user access to specific NAI resources
Kubernetes NetworkPolicies that block unauthorized pods from accessing model files
NVIDIA GPU Operator admission webhooks that validate user identity before GPU allocation
Model-level encryption keys where only authorized users hold the decryption key

questions.explanation: Prism Central's RBAC system uses categories and projects to define fine-grained access policies, controlling which users and groups can access and manage specific NAI resources.

questions.questionText: What is the GGUF model format and when is it relevant for NAI?

options.text: GGUF is a quantized model format optimized for efficient inference that supports various quantization levels
GGUF is a Nutanix-proprietary format that encrypts model weights for secure storage on Nutanix Objects
GGUF is the GPU Graphical Unified Format used exclusively by TensorRT-LLM for model compilation
GGUF is a container image format that packages models as OCI-compatible container layers

questions.explanation: GGUF (GPT-Generated Unified Format) is a model file format that supports various quantization levels and is designed for efficient model loading and inference.

questions.questionText: An NAI administrator needs to rotate API keys for all users as part of a security compliance requirement. What is the recommended approach?

options.text: Generate new API keys for each user through the NAI management interface and revoke the old keys according to the rotation policy
Restart the NAI Operator which automatically regenerates all API keys with new secrets
Delete and recreate all user accounts, which issues fresh API keys
Update the Kubernetes Secret containing the shared API key that all users reference

questions.explanation: API key rotation involves generating new per-user keys and revoking old ones. NAI supports this through its management interface, allowing controlled rotation without disrupting user accounts.

questions.questionText: An engineer deploys a model endpoint and wants to limit the maximum number of requests per minute from a single client. Which NAI feature addresses this?

options.text: Rate limiting configuration on the endpoint, which caps the number of requests allowed per time period
Kubernetes ResourceQuota on the namespace, which limits pod CPU cycles available for request processing
Nutanix Flow microsegmentation rules that drop packets exceeding a bandwidth threshold
NVIDIA MPS (Multi-Process Service) throttling, which limits GPU context switches per client

questions.explanation: NAI supports configurable rate limiting per endpoint. This allows administrators to set request-per-minute caps to prevent any single client from overwhelming the inference service.

questions.questionText: Which quantization level offers the best balance between model quality and memory savings for most production deployments?

options.text: INT8 quantization, which reduces memory by approximately 50% with minimal quality degradation for most models
INT2 quantization, which provides 87.5% memory savings with negligible quality impact
FP64 precision, which improves quality enough to compensate for increased memory use
INT4 with FP32 activations, which is the default and only supported quantization in NAI

questions.explanation: INT8 quantization is generally considered the best balance for production, cutting memory usage roughly in half while preserving most of the model's output quality across diverse tasks.

questions.questionText: An administrator notices that model inference responses are being cut off mid-sentence. Which endpoint configuration parameter should be adjusted?

options.text: Maximum token limit (max_tokens) for the inference endpoint, which controls the maximum length of generated responses
GPU memory overcommit ratio, which when too low truncates output tensors
Kubernetes pod liveness probe timeout, which kills pods that take too long to respond
Nutanix CSI volume throughput limit, which throttles model weight reads during generation

questions.explanation: The max_tokens parameter controls how many tokens the model generates per response. If set too low, responses will be truncated. Increasing this value allows longer completions.

questions.questionText: A customer needs to run inference on a model using the NVIDIA NGC catalog as the source. How does NAI support this?

options.text: NAI can import models directly from the NVIDIA NGC catalog by specifying the NGC model path during model import
NGC models must first be converted to ONNX format using NVIDIA's nemo2onnx tool before NAI import
NAI only supports Hugging Face Hub; NGC models must be re-uploaded to Hugging Face first
The NVIDIA NGC catalog is automatically mirrored to the NAI model repository on a daily schedule

questions.explanation: NAI supports NVIDIA NGC as a model source alongside Hugging Face Hub. Administrators can specify the NGC model identifier during import, and NAI handles the download.

questions.questionText: An administrator is configuring fractional GPU access so that two small models can share a single A100 GPU. Which technology enables this?

options.text: Multi-Instance GPU (MIG) on the A100, which partitions the GPU into isolated instances with dedicated memory
Kubernetes CPU-based request limits applied to GPU resources via the NAI scheduler plugin
NVIDIA Unified Memory which automatically pages GPU memory to system RAM when overcommitted
vLLM's built-in model multiplexing which loads both models into a single inference process

questions.explanation: MIG on A100 (and H100) GPUs creates hardware-isolated partitions, each with its own GPU memory and compute resources, allowing multiple smaller models to run on a single physical GPU.

questions.questionText: Which vLLM feature makes it particularly efficient for serving LLMs with variable-length sequences?

options.text: PagedAttention, which manages attention key-value cache in non-contiguous memory pages to reduce waste
FlashAttention-3, which compiles attention kernels at deployment time for each model architecture
NVIDIA NCCL collective operations for distributing attention computation across multiple GPUs
Speculative decoding with a draft model that pre-allocates fixed memory blocks for all possible sequences

questions.explanation: PagedAttention in vLLM manages the KV cache using a paging mechanism similar to OS virtual memory, reducing memory fragmentation and waste when serving variable-length sequences.

questions.questionText: A data science team wants to test a new fine-tuned model before exposing it to production users. What is the recommended approach in NAI?

options.text: Create a separate endpoint for the new model, test with internal users, then promote to production once validated
Overwrite the production model in-place since NAI automatically rolls back if errors exceed 5%
Use Nutanix Calm to orchestrate a blue-green deployment of the entire NKP cluster
Modify the production endpoint's TensorRT-LLM compilation flags to enable A/B testing mode

questions.explanation: NAI allows creating multiple endpoints. The recommended practice is to deploy the new model on a separate test endpoint, validate its performance, and then update or replace the production endpoint.

questions.questionText: Which Admin role capability is NOT available to users with the standard User role in NAI?

options.text: User provisioning and role management for other NAI users
Creating new inference endpoints with assigned GPU resources
Running inference requests against deployed model endpoints
Managing their own API keys and viewing their resource usage

questions.explanation: The Admin role includes user provisioning and role management capabilities. The User role can create endpoints, run inference, and manage own resources but cannot manage other users.

questions.questionText: An engineer needs to configure a timeout for long-running inference requests that process large prompts. Where is this setting configured?

options.text: On the NAI endpoint configuration, where request timeout values can be set per endpoint
In the NVIDIA GPU driver settings by adjusting the GPU compute timeout registry value
On the Kubernetes cluster by modifying the kube-apiserver request timeout flag
In Prism Central's network configuration under TCP session timeout policies

questions.explanation: NAI endpoints support configurable timeout values. This allows administrators to set appropriate timeouts for different use cases, such as longer timeouts for endpoints processing large prompts.

questions.questionText: A company has uploaded several models to NAI but wants to remove unused ones to free storage. How should the administrator proceed?

options.text: Delete the unused models from the NAI model repository, which releases the associated PersistentVolume storage
Reduce the Nutanix CSI StorageClass reclaim policy timer to auto-delete old models
Scale the model repository pod to zero replicas to trigger garbage collection
Use Prism Central Data Lens to identify and archive unused model files to cold storage

questions.explanation: Unused models can be deleted from the NAI model repository through the management interface, freeing the persistent storage that was allocated for those model files.

questions.questionText: What is the difference between FP16 and BF16 precision for model inference in NAI?

options.text: Both are 16-bit formats, but BF16 has a larger exponent range matching FP32 while FP16 has higher mantissa precision; BF16 is often preferred for training stability
FP16 is for NVIDIA GPUs only while BF16 is exclusively for AMD GPUs
BF16 uses 16 bits for the mantissa and 16 bits for the exponent, totaling 32 bits
FP16 is the inference format while BF16 is only used during model fine-tuning in NAI

questions.explanation: BF16 (BFloat16) has the same exponent range as FP32 (8 bits) but fewer mantissa bits (7 vs 23), while FP16 has a smaller exponent range (5 bits) but more mantissa bits (10). BF16's wider range reduces overflow issues.

questions.questionText: An NAI endpoint is configured with auto-scaling, but during a traffic spike the endpoint takes too long to scale up. What configuration should be reviewed?

options.text: The request queue depth threshold and scale-up cooldown period, which may be too conservative
The Nutanix cluster CPU overcommit ratio in Prism Element
The NVIDIA GPU Operator node labeling frequency
The Kubernetes CoreDNS cache TTL for inference service endpoints

questions.explanation: If scaling is too slow, the queue depth threshold may be set too high (requiring too many queued requests before scaling) or the cooldown period may delay successive scale-up actions.

questions.questionText: Which API endpoint should a developer use for text completion (non-chat) workloads on an NAI deployment?

options.text: /v1/completions, the OpenAI-compatible endpoint for single-turn text completion
/v1/embeddings, which generates completions as vector embeddings
/v1/chat/completions with an empty system message array
/v1/fine-tunes, which generates completions while simultaneously fine-tuning the model

questions.explanation: For non-chat text completion tasks, NAI exposes the /v1/completions endpoint, following the OpenAI API specification for standard text generation without chat message formatting.

questions.questionText: An administrator configures an NAI endpoint with GPU allocation set to "full passthrough" rather than MIG. What is the implication?

options.text: Each inference pod receives an entire physical GPU with all memory and compute resources exclusively allocated
The GPU is shared across all pods on the node using time-slicing with no memory isolation
The inference pod receives a virtual GPU profile with a guaranteed minimum of 50% GPU resources
The GPU is placed in performance mode where all error-correcting memory (ECC) is disabled

questions.explanation: Full GPU passthrough means the inference pod gets exclusive access to the entire physical GPU, including all VRAM and compute units, with no sharing.

questions.questionText: A data scientist uploads a custom model to NAI but receives an error about unsupported model format. Which formats should they verify the model is saved in?

options.text: SafeTensors, GPTQ, or AWQ — the supported model formats for vLLM-based NAI inference
TensorFlow Lite (.tflite) or Apple CoreML (.mlmodel) format
Pickled PyTorch state dict (.pkl) with no additional metadata files
Caffe model format (.caffemodel) with Prototxt architecture definition

questions.explanation: NAI's vLLM engine supports SafeTensors (FP16/BF16), GPTQ, and AWQ quantized formats. GGUF is designed for llama.cpp runtimes and is not natively supported by vLLM. Models in other formats need conversion before deployment.

questions.questionText: An operations team wants to use existing OpenAI client libraries to interact with NAI without code changes. Is this possible?

options.text: Yes, NAI provides OpenAI-compatible API endpoints, so clients only need to change the base URL and API key
No, NAI uses a proprietary protocol that requires the Nutanix Python SDK
Yes, but only if the client library version matches the exact NAI API version
No, NAI only supports gRPC-based inference and does not expose REST endpoints

questions.explanation: NAI's OpenAI-compatible API means existing client libraries (like the openai Python package) work by simply pointing the base URL to the NAI endpoint and providing an NAI API key.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to deploy a model that requires TensorRT-LLM but the model has not been compiled for the target GPU architecture. What must be done?

options.text: The model must be compiled using TensorRT-LLM's build process for the specific GPU architecture before deployment
NAI automatically compiles all models to TensorRT format when an endpoint is created
Switch to a CPU-only inference mode which bypasses the need for GPU-specific compilation
Install the NVIDIA CUDA Compatibility Layer which allows any compiled model to run on any GPU

questions.explanation: TensorRT-LLM requires model compilation for the specific target GPU architecture. This compilation step optimizes the model for that GPU's capabilities and must be completed before deployment.

questions.questionText: A multi-tenant organization wants to ensure that one team's NAI workloads cannot access another team's models. Which Nutanix feature supports this isolation?

options.text: Prism Central projects with separate namespaces and RBAC policies isolating resources between teams
AHV network microsegmentation applied at the GPU passthrough device level
Nutanix CSI driver encryption with per-tenant encryption keys applied to PersistentVolumes
NVIDIA GPU Operator namespace isolation which creates separate GPU driver instances per team

questions.explanation: Prism Central projects provide multi-tenancy by isolating Kubernetes namespaces and applying RBAC policies. Each team's NAI resources exist in separate projects with controlled access.

questions.questionText: When choosing between vLLM and TensorRT-LLM for an NAI endpoint, which factor favors vLLM?

options.text: Need for broad model compatibility and faster deployment without a model compilation step
Requirement for maximum throughput on NVIDIA H100 GPUs with compiled model optimization
Deployment of models exclusively from the NVIDIA NGC catalog
Running inference on AMD Instinct MI300 GPUs alongside NVIDIA GPUs

questions.explanation: vLLM supports a wider range of models out-of-the-box and does not require a compilation step, making it easier and faster to deploy new models. TensorRT-LLM offers better performance but requires compilation.

questions.questionText: An administrator configures an endpoint with INT4 quantization for a model that was originally trained in FP16. What tradeoff should they communicate to the data science team?

options.text: Significantly reduced GPU memory usage and faster inference, but potential degradation in output quality for nuanced tasks
Improved output quality due to reduced numerical noise, but doubled memory consumption
No change in memory or quality; INT4 only affects disk storage format, not GPU memory
The model will only support English language prompts since quantization removes multilingual embeddings

questions.explanation: INT4 quantization reduces memory usage by ~75% and can speed up inference, but the aggressive reduction in precision may cause noticeable quality degradation, especially for tasks requiring nuanced reasoning.

questions.questionText: A customer asks how many concurrent inference endpoints NAI can support. What primarily determines this limit?

options.text: Available GPU resources in the NKP cluster — each endpoint requires dedicated GPU allocation
The Prism Central license tier, which caps the number of endpoints at 5, 10, or unlimited
The NAI Operator version, with each major release supporting a fixed maximum endpoint count
The number of Kubernetes namespaces, since NAI allows exactly one endpoint per namespace

questions.explanation: The number of concurrent endpoints is limited by available GPU resources. Each endpoint requires GPU allocation, so the total GPU capacity determines how many endpoints can be active simultaneously.

questions.questionText: An engineer wants to fine-tune a base Llama model deployed on NAI for domain-specific customer support tasks. Which approach minimizes compute requirements?

options.text: Apply LoRA fine-tuning with a domain-specific dataset, which trains small adapter layers without modifying the full model weights
Retrain the full Llama model from scratch on the customer support dataset using all available GPUs
Use INT2 quantization on the base model, which simulates fine-tuning through extreme weight compression
Deploy a prompt engineering service in front of NAI that reformats all queries for the base model

questions.explanation: LoRA fine-tuning trains small adapter matrices (often <1% of total parameters) that augment the base model. This requires a fraction of the compute needed for full model retraining.

questions.questionText: An NAI endpoint is returning 503 Service Unavailable errors under heavy load despite auto-scaling being enabled. What should the administrator check?

options.text: Whether the maximum replica count has been reached and no additional GPU resources are available for scaling
Whether the Nutanix CSI driver has reached its maximum PersistentVolume count
