questions.explanation: PagedAttention is vLLM's key innovation that manages the key-value (KV) cache using a paging mechanism similar to OS virtual memory. It eliminates memory fragmentation and waste in the KV cache, allowing more concurrent requests and higher throughput.

questions.questionText: Which statement about TensorRT-LLM is CORRECT?

options.text: TensorRT-LLM is a Python-only framework that interprets models at runtime
TensorRT-LLM compiles models into optimized CUDA kernels specific to the target GPU, yielding lower latency
TensorRT-LLM supports AMD GPUs through ROCm integration
TensorRT-LLM does not support quantization of any kind

questions.explanation: TensorRT-LLM compiles model graphs into highly optimized CUDA kernels tailored to the specific GPU architecture (e.g., A100, H100). This compilation step produces lower inference latency compared to interpreted frameworks, but requires upfront build time.

questions.questionText: A developer reports that their vLLM endpoint handles 50 concurrent users well but TensorRT-LLM serves the same model with 20% lower per-request latency. What explains this?

options.text: TensorRT-LLM uses more VRAM, which always means faster inference
TensorRT-LLM's compiled engine executes optimized CUDA kernels with less overhead, reducing per-token latency
vLLM cannot handle concurrent users
The developer miscounted the users

questions.explanation: TensorRT-LLM's ahead-of-time compilation produces fused, architecture-specific CUDA kernels that reduce per-operation overhead. This typically yields lower per-request latency. vLLM's advantage is in throughput via PagedAttention and dynamic batching, not raw per-request latency.

questions.questionText: An organization wants to serve the same model on both NVIDIA and non-NVIDIA (AMD) hardware. Which engine is the better choice?

options.text: TensorRT-LLM — it supports all GPU vendors
vLLM — it has broader hardware support including AMD GPUs via ROCm
Both engines are vendor-agnostic
Neither engine supports AMD GPUs

questions.explanation: vLLM has experimental/growing support for AMD GPUs via ROCm, making it more suitable for multi-vendor environments. TensorRT-LLM is tightly coupled to NVIDIA's CUDA and TensorRT stack and does not support non-NVIDIA hardware.

questions.questionText: When should an administrator choose TensorRT-LLM over vLLM for an NAI deployment?

options.text: When they need the fastest possible setup with no model preparation
When they prioritize minimum per-request latency on NVIDIA GPUs and can invest time in model compilation
When they are running on ARM-based servers
When the model is only available in GGUF format

questions.explanation: TensorRT-LLM is the better choice when the deployment target is exclusively NVIDIA GPUs, the model is stable (not frequently changing), and the team can invest in the compilation step. The resulting engine provides superior per-request latency and GPU utilization.

questions.questionText: A vLLM deployment is experiencing out-of-memory errors during peak traffic despite the model fitting in VRAM at startup. What is the MOST likely cause?

options.text: The model weights grew larger during inference
The KV cache expanded as concurrent requests increased, consuming the remaining VRAM
vLLM does not support concurrent requests
The GPU firmware is outdated

questions.explanation: vLLM dynamically allocates KV cache memory for each active request. During high concurrency, the KV cache for all active sequences can consume significant VRAM beyond the model weights. Tuning `--gpu-memory-utilization` and `--max-num-seqs` helps manage this.

questions.questionText: Which feature allows vLLM to efficiently batch requests that arrive at different times?

options.text: Static batching — all requests must arrive within the same time window
Continuous batching — new requests can join an in-progress batch dynamically
Manual batching — the developer must group requests in application code
TensorRT compilation — which pre-determines batch sizes

questions.explanation: vLLM implements continuous (or iteration-level) batching, where new requests can be added to the batch at each decoding iteration. This eliminates the head-of-line blocking problem in static batching, significantly improving throughput and GPU utilization.

questions.questionText: An engineer compiled a model with TensorRT-LLM on an A100 GPU. They then move the compiled engine to an H100 node. What happens?

options.text: The engine runs identically on both GPUs since they are both NVIDIA
The engine may fail or run suboptimally because TensorRT-LLM compiles for a specific GPU architecture
The engine automatically recompiles on the H100
TensorRT-LLM engines are fully architecture-independent

questions.explanation: TensorRT-LLM engines are compiled for specific GPU compute capabilities (e.g., sm_80 for A100, sm_90 for H100). An engine compiled for A100 may not run or may not leverage H100-specific features. The model should be recompiled for the target architecture.

questions.questionText: A benchmark shows vLLM achieving higher throughput (tokens/second across all users) while TensorRT-LLM achieves lower latency (time-to-first-token). Which deployment scenario favors vLLM?

options.text: A real-time voice assistant requiring sub-100ms responses
A high-traffic API serving thousands of concurrent document summarization requests
A single-user interactive coding assistant
A latency-sensitive trading system

questions.explanation: vLLM excels in high-throughput scenarios with many concurrent requests due to PagedAttention and continuous batching. TensorRT-LLM's lower per-request latency is preferred for interactive, latency-sensitive applications with fewer concurrent users.

questions.questionText: What is the MINIMUM Kubernetes platform requirement for deploying Nutanix AI (NAI)?

options.text: Any CNCF-conformant Kubernetes cluster version 1.24+
Nutanix Kubernetes Platform (NKP) with GPU-capable worker nodes
OpenShift 4.12+ with Nutanix CSI driver
Amazon EKS with Nutanix storage classes

questions.explanation: NAI is designed to run on NKP (Nutanix Kubernetes Platform). It requires NKP with properly configured GPU worker nodes, the NVIDIA GPU Operator, and Nutanix storage integration for model persistence.

questions.questionText: An NAI endpoint pod is stuck in "Pending" state. The `kubectl describe pod` output shows: "0/5 nodes are available: 5 Insufficient nvidia.com/gpu." What is the cause?

options.text: The NVIDIA driver is not installed on the cluster
All GPU resources on the cluster are already allocated to other pods, or the requested GPU count exceeds available GPUs
The model file is corrupted
The Kubernetes API server is down

questions.explanation: The error "Insufficient nvidia.com/gpu" means the scheduler cannot find a node with enough unallocated GPUs to satisfy the pod's resource request. Either all GPUs are in use, or the pod requests more GPUs than any single node has available.

questions.questionText: Which Kubernetes component is responsible for making NVIDIA GPUs visible as schedulable resources in the cluster?

options.text: Nutanix CSI Driver
NVIDIA GPU Operator (including the device plugin)
Kubernetes Metrics Server
CoreDNS

questions.explanation: The NVIDIA GPU Operator deploys the NVIDIA device plugin, driver containers, and related components. The device plugin registers GPUs as extended resources (`nvidia.com/gpu`) with the kubelet, making them visible to the Kubernetes scheduler.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to configure persistent storage for downloaded model files in an NAI deployment. Which storage component is REQUIRED?

options.text: Nutanix Volumes with iSCSI only
A Kubernetes StorageClass backed by the Nutanix CSI Driver with ReadWriteMany (RWX) support for shared model access
Local SSD storage on each GPU node
An external NFS server not managed by Nutanix

questions.explanation: Model files often need to be accessible by multiple pods (for multi-replica endpoints). A StorageClass using the Nutanix CSI Driver with ReadWriteMany (RWX) access mode — typically backed by Nutanix Files — enables shared, persistent model storage across pods.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of an Ingress controller in the context of NAI?

options.text: It manages GPU driver installations on worker nodes
It routes external HTTP/HTTPS traffic to NAI inference endpoint services within the cluster
It schedules pods onto GPU nodes
It monitors GPU temperature and throttling

questions.explanation: The Ingress controller (e.g., NGINX Ingress, Traefik, or Istio) provides external access to NAI inference endpoints by routing incoming HTTP/HTTPS requests to the appropriate Kubernetes services. It handles TLS termination, path-based routing, and load balancing.

questions.questionText: A GPU node in the NKP cluster becomes unavailable. What happens to NAI inference endpoints that had replicas running on that node?

options.text: All inference endpoints across the entire cluster immediately stop
Kubernetes reschedules the affected pods to other GPU-capable nodes if resources are available; other replicas continue serving
The NAI control plane automatically provisions a new physical node
The model must be re-downloaded from Hugging Face before rescheduling

questions.explanation: Kubernetes detects the node failure and marks the pods as terminated. If the deployment has multiple replicas, the remaining replicas on healthy nodes continue serving. The scheduler attempts to reschedule the failed pods to other GPU-capable nodes with available resources.

questions.questionText: An admin configures a GPU node selector as `nvidia.com/gpu.product: "NVIDIA-A100-SXM4-80GB"` on an NAI endpoint. The pod stays in Pending state even though A100 nodes exist. What is the MOST likely issue?

options.text: The label value on the actual nodes does not exactly match the selector string (e.g., different formatting or casing)
A100 GPUs do not support node selectors
The NAI endpoint ignores Kubernetes scheduling constraints
Node selectors only work with CPU resources

questions.explanation: Node selector labels must match exactly, including casing, hyphens, and spacing. The GPU product label is auto-generated by the NVIDIA device plugin and may vary (e.g., "NVIDIA-A100-SXM4-80GB" vs "A100-SXM-80GB"). Always verify actual node labels with `kubectl get nodes --show-labels`.

questions.questionText: Which Kubernetes resource type does NAI typically use to manage inference endpoint pods?

options.text: DaemonSet — to run one inference pod per node
Deployment — to manage replicated inference pods with rolling updates
StatefulSet — because each pod needs a unique network identity
CronJob — to run inference on a schedule

questions.explanation: NAI uses Kubernetes Deployments to manage inference endpoint pods. Deployments provide replica management, rolling updates, and rollback capabilities. Inference pods are stateless (model weights are loaded from shared storage), so StatefulSets are unnecessary.

questions.questionText: An NKP cluster has the NVIDIA GPU Operator installed, but `kubectl describe node` shows no `nvidia.com/gpu` resource. What should the admin check FIRST?

options.text: Whether the Nutanix CSI driver is installed
Whether the NVIDIA device plugin pods are running and the GPU drivers loaded successfully on the nodes
Whether the model has been downloaded
Whether the Ingress controller has TLS configured

questions.explanation: If `nvidia.com/gpu` does not appear in node resources, the NVIDIA device plugin is either not running or has failed. The admin should check GPU Operator pod status (`kubectl get pods -n gpu-operator`), driver container logs, and verify that the physical GPUs are detected at the OS level.

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to limit NAI inference pods to only use GPU nodes and prevent them from being scheduled on CPU-only nodes. What is the BEST approach?

options.text: Delete all CPU-only nodes from the cluster
Apply a taint (e.g., `nvidia.com/gpu=present:NoSchedule`) to GPU nodes and add a matching toleration to NAI pods, combined with node affinity rules
Set CPU resource requests to zero on inference pods
Disable the Kubernetes scheduler entirely

questions.explanation: Using a combination of taints/tolerations and node affinity ensures that GPU workloads land on GPU nodes and non-GPU workloads are repelled from GPU nodes. This is the standard Kubernetes pattern for dedicated GPU node pools.

questions.questionText: A developer wants to use the OpenAI Python SDK to call an NAI endpoint. What changes are needed?

options.text: Complete SDK replacement — NAI uses a proprietary API incompatible with OpenAI
Only change `base_url` to the NAI endpoint URL and `api_key` to the NAI API key; the rest of the code remains the same
Install a separate Nutanix Python SDK
Convert all requests to GraphQL format

questions.explanation: NAI endpoints are OpenAI API-compatible. Developers simply set `base_url` to the NAI endpoint URL and `api_key` to their NAI key. All standard OpenAI SDK methods (`chat.completions.create`, etc.) work without modification.

questions.questionText: A developer enables `stream=True` in their API request. What format does the NAI endpoint use for streaming responses?

options.text: WebSocket frames with JSON payloads
Server-Sent Events (SSE) with `data:` prefixed JSON chunks, ending with `data: [DONE]`
HTTP chunked transfer encoding with raw text
gRPC streaming with protobuf messages

questions.explanation: NAI (like OpenAI) uses the Server-Sent Events (SSE) protocol for streaming. Each chunk is a `data:` prefixed JSON object containing a delta token. The stream terminates with `data: [DONE]`. This is standard for OpenAI-compatible APIs.

questions.questionText: An API response shows: `"usage": {"prompt_tokens": 150, "completion_tokens": 85, "total_tokens": 235}`. A developer is optimizing costs. Which action MOST directly reduces `prompt_tokens`?

options.text: Lowering the `temperature` parameter
Reducing the length of the system prompt and user message
Setting `max_tokens` to a lower value
Switching from streaming to non-streaming mode

questions.explanation: `prompt_tokens` counts the tokenized input (system prompt + user message + conversation history). The only way to reduce it is to shorten the input content. `max_tokens` only limits output length, `temperature` affects randomness, and streaming mode doesn't affect token counts.

questions.questionText: A production application sends requests to an NAI endpoint and occasionally receives HTTP 429 responses. What does this indicate and how should the developer handle it?

options.text: HTTP 429 means the model is corrupted; redeploy the endpoint
HTTP 429 indicates rate limiting; the developer should implement exponential backoff and retry logic
HTTP 429 means the API key is invalid; generate a new key
HTTP 429 indicates a GPU hardware failure

questions.explanation: HTTP 429 "Too Many Requests" means the client has exceeded the rate limit. The correct handling is to implement exponential backoff with jitter and retry. Application code should also consider queuing requests and respecting `Retry-After` headers if provided.

questions.questionText: A developer calls the `/v1/chat/completions` endpoint but receives `"error": {"type": "invalid_request_error", "message": "model not found"}`. What is the MOST likely cause?

options.text: The GPU has run out of memory
The model name in the request does not match the model name configured on the NAI endpoint
The API key has expired
The Kubernetes cluster is at full capacity

questions.explanation: The `model` field in the API request must exactly match the model identifier configured when the NAI endpoint was created. A mismatch (e.g., "llama-2-7b" vs "meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-chat-hf") returns this error. Check the endpoint configuration for the exact model name.

questions.questionText: When implementing a chat application using an NAI endpoint, why is it important to manage the conversation history (messages array) carefully?

options.text: NAI endpoints automatically store all conversation history server-side
The entire messages array is sent with each request, consuming prompt tokens and potentially exceeding the model's context window
Conversation history is only relevant for fine-tuning, not inference
NAI endpoints reject requests with more than 3 messages

questions.explanation: LLM inference is stateless — the full messages array is sent with every request. As conversation grows, prompt tokens increase linearly, eventually hitting the model's context window limit. Developers must implement conversation truncation or summarization strategies.

questions.questionText: A developer wants to make concurrent API requests to an NAI endpoint for batch processing. What is the recommended approach?

options.text: Send all requests sequentially to avoid overloading the endpoint
Use async HTTP clients to send concurrent requests, respecting the endpoint's concurrency limits and implementing rate limit handling
Create a separate NAI endpoint for each concurrent request
Batch all texts into a single request body with line separators

questions.explanation: Concurrent requests are expected and efficient — NAI endpoints support continuous batching internally. Use async HTTP clients (aiohttp, httpx) with a connection pool, respect rate limits (handle 429s), and monitor endpoint metrics to find the optimal concurrency level.

questions.questionText: What is the key difference between `max_tokens` and the model's context window size?

options.text: They are the same parameter with different names
Context window is the total limit for input + output tokens; `max_tokens` limits only the output (completion) tokens within that window
`max_tokens` applies only to streaming mode; context window applies to non-streaming
Context window is a hardware limit; `max_tokens` is a software limit

questions.explanation: The context window (e.g., 4096, 8192, 128K tokens) is the model's maximum combined input and output length. `max_tokens` controls only the maximum output length. If prompt_tokens + max_tokens exceeds the context window, the request will fail or be truncated.

questions.questionText: An application uses the NAI endpoint with `temperature=0`. What behavior should the developer expect?

options.text: The model will refuse to generate any output
The output will be nearly deterministic — the model selects the highest-probability token at each step
The output will be maximally creative and random
Temperature only affects streaming speed, not output quality

questions.explanation: Temperature=0 makes the model greedy, always selecting the most probable next token. This produces nearly deterministic output (ideal for factual Q&A, code generation). Higher temperatures increase randomness and creativity by flattening the probability distribution.

questions.questionText: A developer notices that their streaming response occasionally includes empty `content` fields in the delta. Is this expected?

options.text: No — empty deltas indicate a server error and the request should be retried
Yes — the first chunk often has an empty content delta (containing only the role), and this is normal SSE behavior
Empty deltas mean the model has been rate-limited mid-response
This only happens with INT4 quantized models

questions.explanation: In OpenAI-compatible SSE streaming, the first chunk typically contains `"delta": {"role": "assistant"}` with no `content` field. Subsequent chunks contain `"delta": {"content": "token"}`. This is standard protocol behavior, not an error.

questions.questionText: An organization requires that each development team has a unique API key for their NAI endpoint. What is the PRIMARY benefit of per-team API keys?

options.text: Per-team keys automatically improve inference speed
They enable granular usage tracking, independent rate limiting, and the ability to revoke access for a single team without affecting others
They allow each team to use a different model version simultaneously
Per-team keys eliminate the need for TLS encryption

questions.explanation: Per-team API keys provide accountability (track which team makes which requests), independent rate limiting per consumer, and granular revocation. If a key is compromised, only that team's access is revoked without disrupting other teams.

questions.questionText: An NAI inference endpoint is accessible over HTTPS. What happens if a client sends a request over plain HTTP?

options.text: The request succeeds with a warning header
Depending on Ingress configuration, the request should be rejected or redirected to HTTPS to prevent credentials from being transmitted in plaintext
HTTP requests are automatically faster than HTTPS
The model output is encrypted regardless of protocol

questions.explanation: Best practice is to configure the Ingress controller to either redirect HTTP to HTTPS (301/308) or reject HTTP entirely. API keys transmitted over plain HTTP are exposed in plaintext, creating a serious security vulnerability.

questions.questionText: How does Prism Central provide RBAC for NAI resources?

options.text: Prism Central has no integration with NAI
Through projects and categories that assign role-based permissions, controlling who can create, manage, and access NAI endpoints
By directly modifying Kubernetes RBAC roles in the NKP cluster
Through SSH key management on GPU nodes

questions.explanation: Prism Central uses projects and categories to implement RBAC for NAI. Administrators assign users/groups to projects with specific roles (e.g., admin, operator, viewer), controlling access to NAI endpoints, models, and configurations within the project scope.

questions.questionText: An API key for an NAI endpoint is accidentally committed to a public GitHub repository. What is the CORRECT immediate response?

options.text: Delete the GitHub commit history and assume the key is safe
Immediately rotate (revoke and regenerate) the API key, audit logs for unauthorized usage, and implement secrets scanning in CI/CD
Change the model served by the endpoint to invalidate the key
Restart the inference pods to invalidate active sessions

questions.explanation: A leaked API key must be immediately revoked and regenerated. Audit logs should be reviewed for any unauthorized requests made with the compromised key. Preventive measures like GitHub secret scanning, pre-commit hooks, and secrets management tools should be implemented.

questions.questionText: An organization needs to demonstrate compliance with data access regulations. Which NAI capability supports this?

options.text: Model quantization — smaller models are inherently more compliant
Audit logging — tracking API calls, user access, and endpoint operations for compliance reporting
Using INT4 instead of FP16 precision
Running inference on CPU instead of GPU

questions.explanation: Audit logging records who accessed which endpoints, when, and what operations were performed. This creates an audit trail required by regulations like GDPR, HIPAA, and SOC2 for demonstrating data access governance and accountability.

questions.questionText: What is the recommended approach for storing NAI API keys in a production application?

options.text: Hardcode them in application source code for convenience
Store them in environment variables or a secrets management system (e.g., Kubernetes Secrets, HashiCorp Vault) and never in source code
Store them in a shared text file accessible to all developers
Embed them in Docker image layers during the build process

questions.explanation: API keys should be managed through Kubernetes Secrets, environment variables injected at runtime, or dedicated secrets management systems. They must never appear in source code, configuration files checked into version control, or Docker image layers.

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to restrict NAI endpoint access to only internal corporate network traffic. Which is the MOST effective approach?

options.text: Use a weak API key that's easy to remember
Configure the Ingress controller or network policies to restrict access to specific IP ranges (CIDR blocks) and deploy the endpoint on an internal-only load balancer
Disable HTTPS so external traffic cannot connect securely
Set the model's max_tokens to 1 for external users

questions.explanation: Network-level controls are the most effective restriction. Use Kubernetes NetworkPolicies and Ingress annotations to whitelist internal IP ranges, deploy on internal-only (ClusterIP or internal LoadBalancer) services, and optionally add a VPN requirement.

questions.questionText: How should API key rotation be handled for an NAI endpoint with zero downtime?

options.text: Revoke the old key, then generate a new key, then update applications (brief outage expected)
Generate a new API key, update all client applications to use the new key, verify connectivity, then revoke the old key
Key rotation always requires endpoint redeployment
Keys do not expire and never need rotation

questions.explanation: Zero-downtime rotation follows a parallel key strategy: issue the new key first, update all clients, verify they work with the new key, then revoke the old key. This ensures no window where valid credentials are unavailable.

questions.questionText: A security auditor asks how NAI ensures that inference data is not accessible between different tenants on the same cluster. What is the PRIMARY isolation mechanism?

options.text: Each tenant gets a separate physical GPU
Kubernetes namespaces with network policies and RBAC ensure logical isolation of endpoints, API keys, and network traffic between tenants
Data is encrypted with a tenant-specific GPU hardware key
NAI does not support multi-tenancy

questions.explanation: Multi-tenant isolation in NAI leverages Kubernetes namespaces for resource separation, network policies to prevent cross-namespace traffic, RBAC for access control, and separate API keys per tenant. This provides logical isolation without requiring dedicated hardware.

questions.questionText: An organization using NAI must comply with requirements that all inference requests and responses be logged for 90 days. Where should this logging be implemented?

options.text: Inside the model weights themselves
At the API gateway / Ingress level and within the application layer, forwarding logs to a centralized logging system (ELK, Splunk, etc.)
On the GPU driver level
This is not possible with NAI

questions.explanation: Compliance logging should be implemented at the API gateway/Ingress level (capturing request metadata) and in the application layer (capturing request/response content if required). Logs should be forwarded to a centralized, tamper-evident logging system with appropriate retention policies.

questions.questionText: An NAI inference endpoint returns CUDA Out of Memory (OOM) errors after running successfully for several hours. What is the MOST likely cause?

options.text: The GPU hardware has physically degraded
Memory leaks in the KV cache, or increasing concurrent requests are exhausting GPU memory not reserved for the KV cache
The model file has grown larger on disk
The Kubernetes scheduler has moved the pod to a smaller GPU

questions.explanation: Gradual OOM is typically caused by KV cache growth as concurrent requests increase, or memory leaks in the inference engine. Solutions include setting `--gpu-memory-utilization` limits, capping `--max-num-seqs`, and monitoring GPU memory usage over time.

questions.questionText: An administrator notices that GPU utilization for an inference endpoint is consistently below 20%. What should they investigate FIRST?

options.text: Replace the GPU with a newer model
Check if request volume is too low to saturate the GPU, and consider increasing batch sizes or reducing the number of replicas
Increase the VRAM allocation beyond the physical GPU limit
Switch from GPU to CPU inference

questions.explanation: Low GPU utilization usually indicates insufficient request volume to keep the GPU busy. Solutions include consolidating replicas (fewer replicas, higher traffic per replica), enabling continuous batching, increasing `--max-num-seqs`, or serving multiple models on the same GPU.

questions.questionText: A pod running an NAI inference endpoint enters CrashLoopBackOff. The logs show "CUDA error: no CUDA-capable device is detected." What is the cause?

options.text: The model is too large for the GPU
The pod is scheduled on a node without a GPU, or the NVIDIA device plugin is not properly exposing GPUs to the container runtime
The API key is misconfigured
The container image does not include Python

questions.explanation: This CUDA error means the container cannot see any GPU. Either the pod was scheduled on a CPU-only node (missing GPU resource request), the NVIDIA device plugin is not running, or the container runtime (nvidia-container-runtime) is not configured properly.

questions.questionText: A user reports that NAI inference responses are extremely slow (>30 seconds per request) even though the endpoint is healthy. What should the administrator check?

options.text: The user's internet browser version
GPU utilization (thermal throttling?), request queue depth, batch size settings, and whether the model is swapping between GPU and CPU memory
The color scheme of the Prism Central dashboard
The DNS TTL settings for the cluster

questions.explanation: Slow inference can result from: GPU thermal throttling, high request queue depth (too many concurrent requests), suboptimal batch sizes, the model being partially loaded on CPU (exceeding VRAM), or network latency. Check `nvidia-smi` for GPU stats and endpoint metrics.

questions.questionText: An NAI endpoint URL returns "502 Bad Gateway" when accessed from a browser. What should the admin troubleshoot?

options.text: The model's training data is invalid
The Ingress controller is receiving the request but the backend service/pod is unavailable — check pod readiness, service endpoints, and Ingress configuration
The browser does not support AI inference
The GPU temperature is too high

questions.explanation: A 502 from the Ingress means the reverse proxy cannot reach the backend. Check: (1) pod status (`kubectl get pods`), (2) service endpoints (`kubectl get endpoints`), (3) pod readiness probes, (4) Ingress backend configuration, and (5) pod logs for startup failures.

questions.questionText: An inference endpoint pod is running but readiness probes are failing. The pod shows as "Running" but "0/1 Ready." What is the impact?

options.text: The pod continues to receive and process all traffic normally
Kubernetes removes the pod from the Service endpoints, so no traffic is routed to it until it becomes ready
The pod is immediately deleted and restarted
The readiness probe has no effect on traffic routing

questions.explanation: Failed readiness probes cause Kubernetes to remove the pod from the Service's endpoint list, stopping traffic from being routed to it. This is actually a safety feature — the model may still be loading into VRAM. Once the probe passes, traffic resumes.

questions.questionText: After scaling an NAI endpoint from 1 to 3 replicas, only 1 replica starts successfully while the other 2 remain in Pending state. What is the MOST likely issue?

options.text: NAI only supports single-replica deployments
Insufficient GPU resources — the cluster does not have enough available GPUs to schedule all 3 replicas
The model license only permits one concurrent instance
The Kubernetes scheduler has a bug

questions.explanation: Each replica requires its own GPU allocation. If the cluster has only enough free GPUs for one replica, the remaining pods stay Pending with "Insufficient nvidia.com/gpu." The admin must add GPU nodes or free up existing GPUs.

questions.questionText: A developer reports that their NAI endpoint returns different results for the same prompt each time. Is this a bug?

options.text: Yes — LLMs should always produce identical output
No — LLMs are stochastic by default due to sampling; setting `temperature=0` and `seed` parameter produces near-deterministic output
This only happens with quantized models and indicates corruption
The model needs to be re-downloaded

questions.explanation: LLMs use probabilistic sampling by default, introducing randomness. For deterministic output, set `temperature=0` (greedy decoding). Some engines also support a `seed` parameter. Minor variation across runs can also occur due to floating-point non-determinism in GPU operations.

questions.questionText: An administrator upgrades the NVIDIA GPU Operator on the NKP cluster and NAI endpoints stop working. Pods show "Error: container runtime error." What happened?

options.text: The model files became incompatible with the new operator
The GPU Operator upgrade may have changed the nvidia-container-runtime version or configuration, causing compatibility issues with existing pod configurations
GPU hardware has failed
The API keys expired during the upgrade

questions.explanation: GPU Operator upgrades can modify the nvidia-container-runtime, driver versions, or device plugin configuration. If the new versions are incompatible with existing pod specs or node configurations, containers cannot access GPUs. Rolling back or updating pod configurations may be needed.

questions.questionText: An NAI endpoint intermittently returns "connection reset" errors under heavy load. What should the administrator investigate?

options.text: The model's vocabulary file is corrupted
Resource exhaustion: check pod memory limits, Ingress timeout settings, kernel connection limits, and whether the inference engine is crashing under load
The TLS certificate is self-signed
DNS entries for the endpoint have incorrect TTL

questions.explanation: Intermittent connection resets under load indicate resource exhaustion or timeouts. Check: (1) pod OOM kills (`kubectl describe pod`), (2) Ingress proxy timeouts, (3) kernel socket/connection limits, (4) inference engine logs for crash/restart, and (5) load balancer connection limits.

questions.questionText: When should an organization choose NAI over a direct manual Kubernetes deployment for LLM serving?

options.text: Only when running models smaller than 7B parameters
When they want simplified lifecycle management, Prism Central integration, built-in model management, and don't want to manually configure vLLM/TRT-LLM, GPU operators, and storage
Only when using non-NVIDIA GPUs
NAI is always inferior to manual deployment

questions.explanation: NAI provides an opinionated, integrated platform that simplifies endpoint creation, model management, GPU allocation, and monitoring through Prism Central. Manual Kubernetes deployment offers more flexibility but requires deep expertise in Kubernetes, GPU operators, inference engines, and storage configuration.

questions.questionText: A company plans to serve 5 different models simultaneously on a single NKP cluster. What is the KEY architectural consideration?

options.text: Only one model can be served per cluster
Total GPU capacity must accommodate all models' VRAM requirements plus overhead, and proper resource quotas should prevent any single model from consuming all cluster GPUs
Each model requires its own separate NKP cluster
Multiple models require a paid NAI Enterprise license per model

questions.explanation: Multi-model serving on a shared cluster requires careful GPU capacity planning. Sum all models' VRAM needs (including KV cache overhead), add headroom for scaling, and implement Kubernetes resource quotas to ensure fair GPU allocation across endpoints.

questions.questionText: An architect is planning for high availability of an NAI inference endpoint. What is the MINIMUM deployment configuration?

options.text: A single replica with daily snapshots
At least 2 replicas spread across different nodes, with health checks and a load-balanced service
A single replica with a restart policy
Three NKP clusters in different regions

questions.explanation: HA for inference requires at least 2 replicas on separate nodes so that a single node failure doesn't cause an outage. The Kubernetes Service load-balances across healthy replicas, and readiness probes ensure traffic only goes to ready instances.

questions.questionText: How should an architect calculate the number of GPUs needed for a target concurrent user count?

options.text: One GPU per concurrent user
Benchmark the model's throughput (tokens/second) per GPU, then divide total required throughput (concurrent users × average tokens per request) by per-GPU throughput
Concurrent users have no impact on GPU requirements
Use one GPU per 1000 concurrent users regardless of model size

questions.explanation: Capacity planning requires benchmarking: measure tokens/second per GPU for the specific model and configuration, estimate per-user token requirements (prompt + completion), multiply by target concurrent users, then divide by per-GPU throughput to determine GPU count.

questions.questionText: An organization wants to run inference on a 70B model with the lowest possible latency. They have budget for either 4× A100 80GB or 2× H100 80GB. Which should they choose for latency?

options.text: 4× A100 — more GPUs always means lower latency
2× H100 — the H100's Transformer Engine, higher memory bandwidth, and NVLink 4.0 typically deliver lower per-request latency with fewer cards
They are identical in latency performance
Neither can run a 70B model

questions.explanation: H100s offer significantly higher memory bandwidth (3.35 TB/s vs 2.0 TB/s for A100), the Transformer Engine with FP8 support, and NVLink 4.0. For latency-sensitive inference, 2 faster GPUs with less inter-GPU communication overhead typically outperform 4 slower ones.

questions.questionText: A team deploys a model with 3 replicas across 3 GPU nodes. During a rolling update to a new model version, what ensures zero-downtime?

options.text: All 3 replicas are stopped and replaced simultaneously
Kubernetes rolling update strategy creates new pods before terminating old ones, ensuring at least some replicas are always available during the transition
Rolling updates are not supported for GPU workloads
The Ingress controller caches responses during the update

questions.explanation: The Kubernetes Deployment rolling update strategy (`maxUnavailable` and `maxSurge` settings) ensures that new pods with the updated model version are created and pass readiness checks before old pods are terminated. This maintains continuous availability.

questions.questionText: What is the PRIMARY challenge of serving a model that requires more VRAM than a single GPU provides?

options.text: The model cannot be served under any circumstances
Tensor parallelism must be used to shard the model across multiple GPUs, requiring high-bandwidth interconnects (NVLink) and increasing deployment complexity
The model must be retrained to use less VRAM
VRAM can be supplemented with system RAM with no performance impact

questions.explanation: When a model exceeds single-GPU VRAM, tensor parallelism splits the model across GPUs. This requires NVLink for acceptable inter-GPU communication bandwidth. PCIe-based parallelism introduces significant latency. This adds complexity in configuration, scheduling, and resource management.

questions.questionText: An architect needs to plan for GPU hours in a shared NAI environment. What is a "GPU hour"?

options.text: The number of hours a GPU operates at maximum temperature
One GPU running for one hour — used as a capacity unit for planning and chargeback (e.g., 4 GPUs for 6 hours = 24 GPU hours)
The time required to download a model to GPU memory
The latency of a single GPU inference request in hours

questions.explanation: A GPU hour is a standard capacity measurement: one GPU allocated for one hour. It's used for capacity planning, budgeting, and chargeback in multi-tenant environments. For example, a 70B model using 4 GPUs running 24/7 consumes 4 × 24 = 96 GPU hours per day.

questions.questionText: An organization is evaluating whether to add a second NKP cluster for NAI or scale their existing cluster. What factors favor adding a second cluster?

options.text: A second cluster is never beneficial
Fault isolation (blast radius), geographic distribution for lower latency, regulatory requirements for data residency, and separate lifecycle management for different environments (dev vs prod)
A second cluster doubles inference speed for all endpoints
Kubernetes cannot scale beyond 5 nodes

questions.explanation: A second cluster provides fault isolation (cluster-level failure doesn't affect the other), geographic proximity for global users, data residency compliance, and environment separation. Single-cluster scaling is simpler and more cost-effective when these requirements don't apply.

questions.questionText: An architect is designing an NAI deployment for a customer who will start with one 7B model and gradually expand to ten models including a 70B model. What is the BEST approach?

options.text: Deploy the maximum GPU capacity needed on day one for all 10 models
Start with GPU capacity for the initial model, design the architecture for horizontal scaling, and plan incremental GPU node additions as models are added
Deploy all 10 models immediately on a single T4 GPU
Use CPU-only nodes and add GPUs later

questions.explanation: A phased approach aligns costs with actual usage. Start with sufficient GPUs for the initial workload, but design the NKP cluster architecture (node pools, storage, networking) to accommodate horizontal scaling. Add GPU nodes incrementally as new models are deployed, ensuring the cluster can grow to support the 70B model's multi-GPU requirements.

questions.questionText: A customer wants to fine-tune a 70B parameter model using 4-bit quantization on Nutanix AI. They need to understand the VRAM requirement per GPU. Assuming the model needs 70B parameters × 0.5B per parameter (INT4), plus 40GB for optimizer state and batch processing, what is the minimum GPU required?

options.text: NVIDIA T4 (16GB) - insufficient
NVIDIA L40S (48GB) - insufficient
NVIDIA A100 (80GB) - sufficient
NVIDIA H100 (80GB) - sufficient, but requires MIG partitioning

questions.explanation: INT4 quantization of 70B model = 35GB base + 40GB overhead (optimizer state, batch processing, activations) = 75GB total. The A100 with 80GB provides the minimum single-GPU VRAM needed with adequate headroom, while T4 and L40S lack sufficient capacity. H100 can also work but does not require MIG partitioning for this use case (MIG would be necessary only for splitting across multiple smaller partitions).

questions.questionText: Which of the following best describes the primary role of the NAI Operator in a Nutanix AI deployment on NKP (Nutanix Kubernetes Platform)?

options.text: It handles all GPU scheduling and CUDA version management directly on nodes
It manages the lifecycle of NAI components including model serving, API endpoints, and controller reconciliation
It provides persistent storage exclusively through NFS integration
It replaces Helm and manages all Kubernetes manifests manually

questions.explanation: The NAI Operator is a Kubernetes operator that watches for custom resources and reconciles the desired state, managing model serving deployments, API endpoints, and other NAI lifecycle aspects. GPU scheduling is Kubernetes' responsibility via node selectors.

questions.questionText: A customer deploys a Llama 2 13B model in FP16 format on NAI. What is the approximate VRAM requirement for the model weights alone?

options.text: 13GB
26GB
52GB
104GB

questions.explanation: FP16 (half-precision) = 2 bytes per parameter. 13B parameters × 2B = 26GB for model weights only, before accounting for KV cache or optimizer state.

questions.questionText: When configuring a model serving deployment on NAI, which inference engine would you select if you need the broadest LLM support with Python-based optimization and moderate performance requirements?

options.text: TensorRT-LLM compiled with ONNX runtime
vLLM with PagedAttention optimization
NVIDIA Triton without PagedAttention
JAX-based inference server

questions.explanation: vLLM is Python-based, supports a wide range of models, and uses PagedAttention for efficient KV cache management. TensorRT-LLM is more specialized and requires compilation; it's better for maximum performance but narrower model support.

questions.questionText: A NAI deployment requires GPU passthrough for a specialized ML workload. You notice the GPU is not detected inside the pod despite being assigned to the node. Which is the MOST likely cause?

options.text: The node lacks nvidia.com/gpu node selectors
NVIDIA container toolkit is not installed or NVIDIA drivers are outdated on the node
The pod is using a non-NVIDIA container image
The NAI Operator hasn't been updated to latest version

questions.explanation: GPU passthrough requires NVIDIA drivers and the container runtime to support GPU access. Without proper drivers or container toolkit, the GPU won't appear inside containers despite passthrough being configured.

questions.questionText: Which model format is preferred for quantized inference on NAI when using vLLM, offering broad framework support and excellent compression?

options.text: ONNX only
SafeTensors, GPTQ, and AWQ (quantized formats optimized for vLLM)
Raw PyTorch pickles
TensorFlow SavedModel

questions.explanation: vLLM natively supports SafeTensors, GPTQ, and AWQ formats, which provide excellent compression and framework compatibility. While GGUF is a popular quantized format, it's primarily designed for llama.cpp rather than vLLM. vLLM's optimization focuses on the aforementioned formats for maximum performance and compatibility.

questions.questionText: An organization implements NAI with OpenAI-compatible API endpoints. What is the correct endpoint path for sending a completion request that returns token usage information?

options.text: /v1/chat/completions
/v1/completions
/v1/models
/v1/tokens

questions.explanation: The /v1/chat/completions endpoint is the standard OpenAI-compatible endpoint for chat-based completions and returns detailed token usage metrics. The /v1/completions endpoint is for older completion models.

questions.questionText: A model deployment on NAI experiences OOM (Out of Memory) errors during inference. The model is Mistral 7B in FP16 (14GB), but 4 concurrent requests cause failure on a V100-class 32GB GPU. What is the most likely issue?

options.text: FP16 precision is too low for this model
KV cache accumulation with batch size 4 exceeds available VRAM
The model requires INT4 quantization regardless
The GPU is faulty

questions.explanation: With batch size 4, KV cache (key-value tensors from attention layers) accumulates significantly, easily consuming 18-20GB additional VRAM on top of the 14GB model, exceeding 32GB total. Reducing batch size or enabling PagedAttention would help.

questions.questionText: NAI deployments require persistent storage for model artifacts. Which Nutanix component provides Kubernetes-compatible persistent volumes?

options.text: Nutanix Prism Central storage policy management
Nutanix CSI (Container Storage Interface) driver
Nutanix NTNX volumes mounted directly
Kubernetes hostPath only

questions.explanation: The Nutanix CSI driver integrates Nutanix storage with Kubernetes, providing PersistentVolumes for model artifacts, datasets, and logs. hostPath is not recommended for production due to node affinity limitations.

questions.questionText: When using the Nutanix CSI driver with NAI model deployments, which statement accurately describes the workflow?

options.text: All models must be pre-downloaded to node local storage before pod creation
PersistentVolumes are dynamically provisioned on Nutanix storage, mounted at pod startup
The CSI driver only supports read-only volumes
Models must be stored in HDFS

questions.explanation: The CSI driver dynamically provisions Nutanix storage volumes, which are mounted into pods at startup. This enables flexible model management without node-local storage constraints.

questions.questionText: A customer has trained a 30B parameter model with LoRA fine-tuning for domain adaptation. After quantizing to INT8 (1B per parameter), what is the approximate base model size plus LoRA adapter overhead?

options.text: 15GB base + 5GB adapter = 20GB
30GB base + 5GB adapter = 35GB
30GB base + 1GB adapter = 31GB
60GB base + 10GB adapter = 70GB

questions.explanation: Base model: 30B × 1B (INT8) = 30GB. LoRA adapters are typically 1-2% of base model size, so ~0.3-0.6GB. Total ~31GB. LoRA doesn't require full optimizer state at inference time.

questions.questionText: You're deploying vLLM on NAI with a 13B model and notice inference throughput is suboptimal despite sufficient VRAM. Which vLLM feature directly addresses KV cache memory pressure and batch size limitations?

options.text: Mixed precision training
PagedAttention (virtual paging for KV cache)
Tensor parallelism only
Beam search optimization

questions.explanation: PagedAttention pages KV cache blocks like OS virtual memory, allowing larger batch sizes and higher throughput by managing VRAM more efficiently than traditional dense KV storage.

questions.questionText: A NAI model serving deployment must support OpenAI SDK as a drop-in replacement. What is the minimum configuration change to the client code?

options.text: Rewrite all API calls to use gRPC
Change base_url to the NAI OpenAI-compatible endpoint; Bearer token auth
Switch to a different API library entirely
No changes needed; it works automatically

questions.explanation: OpenAI SDK supports custom base_url and custom API keys (Bearer token). Setting `client = OpenAI(api_key="token", base_url="https://nai-endpoint")` enables drop-in compatibility with NAI.

questions.questionText: Your organization requires per-user API key management and audit logging for NAI API access. Which security mechanism aligns with this requirement?

options.text: Single shared API key for all users
Per-user API keys managed in Prism Central RBAC with API audit logging
No authentication required for internal use
Use Kubernetes secrets without RBAC

questions.explanation: Nutanix Prism Central provides RBAC for per-user API key generation and rotation, with audit logging tracking all API requests by user. This satisfies compliance requirements for multi-user environments.

questions.questionText: A model serving pod fails with CrashLoopBackOff status. The most direct troubleshooting step is:

options.text: Restart the entire NAI Operator
Check pod logs and describe pod events for resource constraints or configuration errors
Assume the model is incompatible and select a different model
Increase node CPU to arbitrary high value

questions.explanation: CrashLoopBackOff indicates the container is crashing repeatedly. `kubectl logs` and `kubectl describe pod` reveal the actual error (missing volume mount, insufficient memory requests, syntax errors, etc.).

questions.questionText: When configuring TensorRT-LLM for a 70B model on NAI, what additional step is required compared to vLLM?

options.text: Dynamic batch size tuning at runtime
Compilation of the model for target GPU architecture
No difference; both use identical configuration
Manual CUDA kernel writing

questions.explanation: TensorRT-LLM requires an explicit compilation step that generates optimized CUDA kernels for the target GPU (A100, H100, etc.). This provides maximum performance but adds deployment complexity versus vLLM's dynamic approach.

questions.questionText: A NAI cluster with Kubernetes auto-scaling is configured with replica counts: min=2, max=10. During peak load, the API response time increases from 50ms to 500ms despite 6 active replicas. What is the FIRST troubleshooting step?

options.text: Increase max replicas to 20
Check individual pod performance metrics; the issue may be per-pod resource limits, not scaling
Reduce min replicas to 1
Disable auto-scaling

questions.explanation: Slow responses despite sufficient replicas indicate individual pods are resource-constrained (CPU/memory limits, slow disk I/O for model loading). Scaling won't help if each pod is bottlenecked. Inspect resource requests/limits and pod metrics.

questions.questionText: Which Kubernetes primitive is used in NAI deployments to schedule GPU-requiring pods on appropriate nodes?

options.text: PodPreset
NodeAffinity with nvidia.com/gpu labels
Namespace policies
Service mesh routing

questions.explanation: Kubernetes nodes with NVIDIA GPUs are labeled with `nvidia.com/gpu=true`. Pod node selectors or affinity rules match this label to ensure GPU pods run on GPU-capable nodes.

questions.questionText: A NAI deployment requires TLS encryption for API endpoints. Where is the certificate typically managed?

options.text: Hardcoded in Python application code
Kubernetes Secret mounted into the NAI pod, referenced by Ingress or NAI configuration
NVIDIA driver manages TLS
No TLS needed for internal Kubernetes communication

questions.explanation: TLS certificates are stored in Kubernetes Secrets and referenced by Ingress objects or NAI service configuration, enabling secure external API access without hardcoding secrets.

questions.questionText: You're building a RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) pipeline with NAI. Which components are essential?

options.text: LLM inference endpoint + vector database for embeddings + retrieval mechanism
LLM alone; retrieval is automatic
Vector database only
External GPUs not needed

questions.explanation: RAG requires: (1) embedding model to vectorize documents, (2) vector store for similarity search, (3) LLM inference for generating responses. All three are essential for effective retrieval and generation.

questions.questionText: A customer deploys a model and configures an Ingress for the NAI API endpoint. After successful deployment, external clients cannot reach the endpoint despite pod logs showing normal operation. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The pod is crashing silently
Ingress routing rules don't match the service port or hostname
Models don't support Ingress
The NAI cluster is misconfigured at the operator level

questions.explanation: Ingress routes external traffic to Kubernetes services based on hostname and path rules. Mismatched port numbers, hostname rules, or missing service selector bindings prevent traffic from reaching the pod.

questions.questionText: For a 7B parameter model in FP16, which GPU can comfortably support it with room for KV cache and batch processing?

options.text: NVIDIA T4 (16GB)
NVIDIA L40S (48GB)
NVIDIA A100 (80GB)
All of the above

questions.explanation: 7B × 2B (FP16) = 14GB. L40S with 48GB provides ample headroom for 14GB model + 20-30GB KV cache and batch processing. T4's 16GB is too tight; A100/H100 are overkill for this size.

questions.questionText: A NAI model update requires rolling new container images without downtime. What Kubernetes deployment strategy enables this?

options.text: Delete old pod, launch new pod
Readiness probes with rolling updates (RollingUpdateStrategy)
Blue-green deployment requires cluster downtime
Manual pod replacement one at a time

questions.explanation: RollingUpdateStrategy progressively replaces old pods with new ones, using readiness probes to verify health before removing old replicas. This ensures zero-downtime updates.

questions.questionText: MIG (Multi-Instance GPU) partitioning on Nutanix AI is supported on which GPU types?

options.text: T4 and L40S
A100 and H100 only
All NVIDIA GPUs support MIG
MIG is not supported on any NAI GPU

questions.explanation: Only A100 and H100 support MIG mode, dividing a single GPU into multiple isolated instances. Older generations like T4 and newer data center GPUs like L40S don't support MIG.

questions.questionText: When using MIG on an H100 with a 40B parameter model, you partition the GPU into two 40GB instances. What is a key constraint you must consider?

options.text: MIG partitions can dynamically share memory between instances
Each MIG instance is independently managed; a 40GB partition cannot borrow from the other
MIG doesn't work on H100
All H100 memory is shared regardless of MIG partition

questions.explanation: MIG creates independent logical GPUs with isolated memory pools. A 40GB partition cannot access the adjacent partition's memory, so model size must fit within the partition size.

questions.questionText: A customer uploads a custom model to NAI via manual artifact upload. Which formats are immediately supported for inference without conversion?

options.text: SafeTensors, GPTQ, and AWQ formats
Raw PyTorch checkpoints only
TensorFlow SavedModel exclusively
All formats require conversion to ONNX first

questions.explanation: SafeTensors (efficient tensor serialization), GPTQ (quantized), and AWQ (quantized) are directly supported by vLLM on NAI. GGUF is designed for llama.cpp and is not natively supported by vLLM or TensorRT-LLM. Raw PyTorch checkpoints require loading as Python objects.

questions.questionText: Which Nutanix component ensures LoRA fine-tuning adapters are properly versioned and retrieved during model serving on NAI?

options.text: NVIDIA driver versioning
Helm chart with ConfigMap for adapter registry
Kubernetes CSI driver and PersistentVolume management
Prism Central storage snapshots only

questions.explanation: LoRA adapters are small files stored in PersistentVolumes via the Nutanix CSI driver. This provides versioning, retrieval, and management of adapter artifacts.

questions.questionText: A NAI cluster requires Prometheus monitoring for inference metrics. Which metric is most critical for performance troubleshooting?

options.text: Pod restart count only
Inference latency (p50, p95, p99) + token generation throughput + model memory usage
Node CPU count
Kubernetes API server uptime

questions.explanation: For inference workloads, latency percentiles reveal user experience, throughput indicates efficiency, and memory tracks resource pressure. These directly correlate with model performance and scaling needs.

questions.questionText: When rotating API keys in a NAI multi-tenant environment, which best practice ensures uninterrupted access?

options.text: Revoke old key immediately, generate new key
Generate new key first, allow dual operation for a grace period, then revoke old key
Disable RBAC during rotation to avoid lockout
Keys never need rotation

questions.explanation: Grace period rotation (overlap) prevents client lockout. Generate new key → communicate to users → allow both to work temporarily → retire old key after confirmed adoption.

questions.questionText: A model's inference is slow despite sufficient GPU VRAM. Prometheus metrics show low GPU utilization (~20%) and high I/O wait. What is the likely bottleneck?

options.text: Model requires recompilation
Slow model artifact loading from storage or slow PersistentVolume I/O
Batch size is too small
The GPU needs more VRAM

questions.explanation: Low GPU utilization + high I/O wait indicates storage/I/O bottleneck (slow PersistentVolume, slow network storage). Model isn't starved for compute; it's starved for data. Optimize CSI driver, storage backend, or model caching.

questions.questionText: A customer needs to fine-tune a model using HuggingFace transformers on NAI. What is the recommended workflow?

options.text: Run training directly in production pods
Use a training pod with GPU, save model, store in CSI volume, then deploy for inference
Training cannot be done on NAI
Download model, train locally, manually reupload

questions.explanation: NAI training pods run in isolated environments with GPU access, save results to Nutanix CSI volumes, then redeploy the trained model via the serving stack. This separates training from inference workloads.

questions.questionText: An NGC (NVIDIA GPU Cloud) model is imported into NAI via manual pull. Which statement is correct?

options.text: NGC models are proprietary and cannot run on NAI
NGC models (pre-optimized containers, weights) can be pulled, containerized, and deployed on NAI
NAI automatically fetches NGC models without explicit import
NGC models require special licensing for NAI use

questions.explanation: NGC hosts optimized containers and model weights (quantized, compiled TensorRT-LLM, etc.). These can be pulled and deployed on NAI with proper licenses, offering performance advantages.

questions.questionText: A NAI Helm deployment fails with "nvidia.com/gpu resource not available" error. What is the root cause?

options.text: Kubernetes version is too old
NVIDIA GPU device plugin not installed or configured on the cluster
Helm chart is outdated
The cluster has no GPUs

questions.explanation: The NVIDIA GPU device plugin is a Kubernetes DaemonSet that advertises nvidia.com/gpu as a schedulable resource. Without it, GPUs exist but aren't discoverable by the scheduler.

questions.questionText: SSE (Server-Sent Events) streaming is enabled for NAI chat completions. What is the primary benefit for users?

options.text: Faster response times by 50%
Token-by-token streaming for real-time output display (better UX, reduced perceived latency)
Lower API cost
No functional benefit; purely aesthetic

questions.explanation: SSE streaming returns tokens as they're generated, enabling real-time display in client applications. Users see partial responses immediately rather than waiting for complete generation.

questions.questionText: Rate limiting is configured on NAI API endpoints (100 requests/minute per key). A client exceeds limits. What HTTP status code should the API return?

options.text: 200 OK
404 Not Found
429 Too Many Requests
500 Internal Server Error

questions.explanation: HTTP 429 indicates rate limit exceeded. Client should implement exponential backoff and retry after the delay specified in response headers.

questions.questionText: A deployed model requires inference across multiple GPUs (tensor parallelism). Which is true for vLLM?

options.text: vLLM doesn't support tensor parallelism
vLLM supports tensor parallelism via --tensor-parallel-size flag, splitting model across GPUs
Tensor parallelism requires manual CUDA coding
Only TensorRT-LLM supports tensor parallelism

questions.explanation: vLLM's `--tensor-parallel-size` flag enables tensor parallelism, distributing model layers across multiple GPUs on the same node. This is simpler than TensorRT-LLM's approach.

questions.questionText: A customer requests function calling support via NAI OpenAI-compatible endpoints. Which model family natively supports this feature?

options.text: Llama 2 (base model)
GPT-3.5 and higher, or Mistral-Nemo, or open models fine-tuned for tool use
All models support function calling automatically
Function calling is not possible on open-source models

questions.explanation: Function calling requires models trained with tool-use instructions (GPT models inherently, or open models like Mistral-Nemo fine-tuned for it). Base Llama 2 lacks this capability unless fine-tuned.

questions.questionText: An embeddings endpoint is deployed on NAI alongside an LLM. A client calls /v1/embeddings with a 1000-token document. What determines the output dimensions?

options.text: GPU VRAM size
Token count of input
The specific embedding model used (e.g., BERT = 768D, NVIDIA e5-large = 1024D)
OpenAI's default is always 1536D

questions.explanation: Embedding model architecture (transformer layers, hidden size) determines output dimension, independent of input length or hardware. BERT ≈768D, e5-large ≈1024D.

questions.questionText: A NAI namespace isolates a customer's models and API keys from others. Which Kubernetes RBAC element enforces this isolation?

options.text: Ingress policies alone
NetworkPolicy and RBAC Role/RoleBinding scoped to the namespace
Pod names are unique globally
No isolation is possible on shared clusters

questions.explanation: Kubernetes RBAC (Role, RoleBinding) and NetworkPolicy enforce namespace isolation, preventing cross-namespace API calls and resource access. ServiceAccounts are namespace-scoped.

questions.questionText: A model deployment has replica count = 3. One pod is evicted due to node failure. How does auto-scaling respond?

options.text: Auto-scaling is disabled when a node fails
The Deployment controller immediately spins up a replacement pod on a healthy node
Manual intervention is required
Replicas remain at 2 permanently

questions.explanation: Kubernetes Deployment controller maintains desired replica count automatically. If a pod is evicted or node fails, a new pod launches on another available node.

questions.questionText: Certificate rotation for NAI TLS endpoints must occur every 90 days. Which approach integrates with Kubernetes Secret management?

options.text: Manual certificate update to each pod
cert-manager operator watches Kubernetes Secrets and auto-rotates before expiry
Certificates never need rotation
NVIDIA driver manages certificates

questions.explanation: cert-manager is a Kubernetes operator that automates certificate lifecycle management (issuance, renewal, rotation) before expiry, storing certificates in Secrets.

questions.questionText: A model serving pod requests 8GB GPU memory but the node only has 6GB available. What happens?

options.text: Kubernetes allows the pod to launch and GPU oversubscription occurs
The pod remains Pending (unschedulable) until a node with 8GB is available
The pod launches with reduced performance
GPU memory is automatically borrowed from CPU RAM

questions.explanation: Kubernetes checks resource requests before scheduling. If no node matches the request, the pod stays Pending in queue until a suitable node is available.

questions.questionText: A customer uses Nutanix Files NFS mount for model artifacts. Performance degrades when 5 concurrent pods load a 50GB model. What is the primary bottleneck?

options.text: NFS is not suitable for model serving
Network bandwidth saturation: 5 × 50GB concurrent reads overwhelm NFS network throughput
NVIDIA drivers don't support NFS
Pod CPU is insufficient

questions.explanation: NFS uses network bandwidth for all I/O. Concurrent large model loads can saturate network, causing slowness. Solution: pre-cache models locally, use Nutanix iSCSI block storage, or implement read-only PersistentVolume replicas.

questions.questionText: When deploying a model via Helm on NAI, where are model URIs and hyperparameters specified?

options.text: Hardcoded in Python source
Helm values.yaml or ConfigMap referenced by pod
NVIDIA environment variables only
Kubernetes secrets exclusively

questions.explanation: Helm charts use values.yaml to externalize configuration (model URI, inference engine, batch size, etc.). ConfigMaps provide runtime configuration, decoupling model artifacts from application code.

questions.questionText: A 13B model fine-tuned with LoRA is deployed. The base model and LoRA adapter are stored separately. At inference, what is loaded?

options.text: Base model only; LoRA is ignored
LoRA only; base model is not needed
Base model weights merged with LoRA adapter at pod startup
Both are concatenated without merging

questions.explanation: During inference initialization, LoRA adapters are merged (injected into attention/MLP modules) with base model weights, creating an effective combined model. This merging happens once at startup.

questions.questionText: A customer requires audit logging of all API requests (model, user, timestamp, tokens) for compliance. Which component logs this?

options.text: NVIDIA driver audit module
NAI API server with structured logging to Kubernetes cluster log aggregator (Loki, ELK, etc.)
Prism Central only
Audit logs are not supported

questions.explanation: NAI API endpoints log requests structured as JSON (model, user, timestamp, tokens, latency). Kubernetes log aggregators (Loki, ELK, Splunk) centralize these for audit trails and compliance reporting.

questions.questionText: A model checkpoint stored as SafeTensors is 60GB. After quantization to INT8, the new size is approximately:

options.text: 15GB
30GB
40GB
60GB

questions.explanation: Quantization reduces bit depth through the formula: params × bytes_per_param. FP32 → INT8 reduces from 4 bytes/param to 1 byte/param, a 4x reduction. For a 60B parameter model: 60GB (FP32) ÷ 4 = 15GB (INT8). Note: Actual file size may vary slightly depending on the model's original precision and metadata included in SafeTensors, but the 4x reduction factor for FP32→INT8 conversion is consistent.

questions.questionText: vLLM's PagedAttention optimization works by:

options.text: Pre-allocating all KV cache at pod startup
Paging KV cache blocks to disk during inference
Dynamically allocating/deallocating KV cache pages in GPU VRAM as requests arrive and complete
Disabling attention computation

questions.explanation: PagedAttention manages KV cache in small pages, allocating only what's needed per request and deallocating when requests complete. This memory-efficient approach enables higher batch concurrency and reduces peak GPU memory usage. Unlike static pre-allocation (option A), PagedAttention avoids wasting memory on unused allocations, while remaining strictly on GPU (option B describes disk paging, which would be much slower).

questions.questionText: A Kubernetes pod fails with "ImagePullBackOff" status when deploying a custom NAI model container. What is the root cause?

options.text: The container image doesn't exist at the specified registry path or pull credentials are missing
GPU is not available
Kubernetes version is incompatible
The model file is too large

questions.explanation: ImagePullBackOff indicates the pod failed to pull the container image—either the image name/tag is wrong, the registry is unreachable, or pull credentials (imagePullSecret) are missing.

questions.questionText: A NAI cluster is upgraded from vLLM 0.2 to 0.4. The deployment manifest references a specific vLLM version. How should you update it?

options.text: No action needed; Kubernetes automatically uses latest version
Update image tag in Deployment spec to 0.4, trigger rolling update
Manually pull and rebuild containers
Version upgrades are not supported

questions.explanation: Update the container image tag in Deployment spec (e.g., `vllm:0.4-openai`), then Kubernetes applies a rolling update. Readiness probes ensure new version is healthy before removing old pods.

questions.questionText: A model's inference latency is 2 seconds per token on a single H100. A customer wants sub-second per-token latency. Which optimization is NOT directly applicable?

options.text: Increase batch size to 32
Enable PagedAttention to reduce memory pressure
Use speculative decoding (draft model for faster prediction)
Switch to smaller model (7B instead of 70B)

questions.explanation: Increasing batch size improves *throughput* (tokens/second across all requests) but increases *latency* per request (more queuing). For single-request latency, batch size doesn't help. Speculative decoding, smaller models, or faster inference engines reduce latency.

questions.questionText: A customer's API key is exposed in a GitHub commit. What is the immediate remediation on NAI?

options.text: No action needed; GitHub will hide it
Immediately revoke the compromised key in Prism Central, generate new key, update clients
Change only the RBAC role, keep the same key
Keys cannot be revoked

questions.explanation: Compromised keys must be revoked immediately in Prism Central (removes API access), then a new key is generated and distributed to clients. This prevents unauthorized API usage.

questions.questionText: A NAI pod has resource requests (GPU: 1, Memory: 16Gi) but limits (GPU: 1, Memory: 32Gi). The pod crashes after 10 minutes with OOM. What is the issue?

options.text: Requests are too high
Limits are too low; the running model consumes >32GB memory (model + batch + KV cache)
GPU and memory limits must be equal
Kubernetes misconfigured

questions.explanation: Memory limit (32Gi) is the hard cap; exceeding it terminates the pod (OOM Kill). If model + KV cache + batch > 32GB, it will crash. Increase memory limit or reduce model size/batch size.

questions.questionText: TensorRT-LLM requires explicit compilation. What is the primary purpose of this build step?

options.text: To convert model weights to ONNX format
To generate optimized CUDA kernels for target GPU and model architecture
To add NVIDIA driver support
To apply quantization

questions.explanation: TensorRT-LLM compilation generates hand-optimized CUDA kernels specific to the target GPU (A100, H100, etc.) and model structure (layer counts, dimensions), delivering maximum performance but reducing portability.

questions.questionText: A NAI deployment uses ServiceMonitor (Prometheus operator) to scrape metrics from model serving pods. What metrics are typically exposed?

options.text: Pod creation timestamp only
Inference latency, token throughput, model load time, GPU memory usage, request queue length
Kubernetes API server metrics only
Metrics are not supported in NAI

questions.explanation: Model serving pods expose application-level metrics (latency percentiles, throughput) alongside resource metrics (GPU memory, CPU). ServiceMonitor defines scrape configuration for Prometheus collection.

questions.questionText: A model supporting function calling is deployed. The client sends a request with tools parameter. What must the model's response include?

options.text: Plain text response only
Structured response with tool_calls array (function name, arguments) and text; client executes tools, sends results back
Immediate tool execution by the model
Function calling doesn't involve responses

questions.explanation: Function-enabled models return structured JSON with `tool_calls` (name, arguments) alongside optional text. The client executes the requested tool, then sends results back for model's next response iteration.

questions.questionText: A NAI cluster scales to 10 nodes, each with 4 × H100 GPUs. How many A100 GPU equivalents is this in terms of FP32 compute?

options.text: 40 GPUs (not directly comparable)
H100 ≈ 2× A100 performance, so 40 H100s ≈ 80 A100 equivalents
H100s are slower than A100s
Comparison is meaningless

questions.explanation: H100 delivers roughly 2–3× the inference throughput of A100 depending on workload (A100 SXM FP32 ≈ 19.5 TFLOPS, H100 SXM FP32 ≈ 67 TFLOPS; for FP16 with Tensor Cores the gap widens further). So 40 H100s ≈ 80–120 A100 equivalents in compute. This helps capacity planning when mixing GPU types.

questions.questionText: An inference request specifies max_tokens=2000 for a 7B model. The API returns 2048 tokens. Why?

options.text: max_tokens is not enforced
max_tokens is a soft limit; the model stops at completion token or max_tokens, but may include special tokens beyond max_tokens
The model is misconfigured
48 tokens were hallucinated

questions.explanation: max_tokens typically limits generated content tokens, but special tokens (EOS, BOS) or token-counting differences can result in slightly more output tokens. True limit is approximately honored.

questions.questionText: A customer requires multi-language support (English, Mandarin, Spanish) for model inference. Which model characteristic is most relevant?

options.text: GPU memory only
Model tokenizer and training data language coverage (e.g., multilingual models like Llama 2-7B which support 6+ languages)
NVIDIA driver version
Kubernetes namespace count

questions.explanation: Model's tokenizer and training data determine language support. Multilingual models (Llama 2, Mistral) have diverse language coverage; English-only models fail on other languages. Check model card for language support matrix.

questions.questionText:
The NAI team monitors a 99th percentile latency (p99) SLO of 1 second. A spike occurs where p99 = 3 seconds. Prometheus shows:
- GPU utilization: 95%
- Memory: 80%
- CPU: 30%
- Network: 10%

What is the likely cause?

options.text: Network congestion
CPU bottleneck
GPU compute or memory saturation (queue buildup, long kernel execution)
Model is faulty

questions.explanation: High GPU utilization and memory with low CPU/network indicate GPU is the bottleneck. Requests queue waiting for GPU, causing latency spike. Solution: reduce batch size, add GPUs, or enable model parallelism.

questions.questionText: An organization is evaluating whether to deploy Nutanix AI (NAI) on NKP or on a vanilla upstream Kubernetes cluster. Which capability is available **exclusively** when NAI is installed via the NKP App Catalog?

options.text: Serving models through an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint
One-click NAI installation, lifecycle management, and integrated upgrades through the NKP platform UI
Using vLLM as the inference engine for LLM workloads
Mounting persistent volumes via Nutanix CSI for model storage

questions.explanation: The NKP App Catalog provides a GUI-driven, one-click install and upgrade experience that manages the NAI Operator lifecycle directly from the NKP management plane. Core inference features like the OpenAI-compatible API, vLLM engine, and Nutanix CSI storage are available regardless of whether NAI is deployed on NKP or vanilla Kubernetes.

questions.questionText: An administrator is deploying NAI on an upstream Kubernetes cluster that is **not** running NKP. Which installation method must they use?

options.text: Deploy through the NKP App Catalog by adding the upstream cluster as a managed cluster
Use `kubectl apply` with Helm charts and manually deploy the NAI Operator CRDs and manifests
Install NAI directly from Prism Central's LCM (Life Cycle Manager) module
Run the `nai-cli bootstrap` command, which auto-detects any CNCF-conformant cluster

questions.explanation: Without NKP, the App Catalog is unavailable. The administrator must use kubectl and Helm to manually install the NAI Operator, apply CRDs, configure prerequisites (GPU Operator, CSI driver), and manage upgrades. Prism Central LCM manages AOS/AHV lifecycle, not Kubernetes workloads, and there is no standalone `nai-cli bootstrap` tool.

questions.questionText: A solutions architect is comparing NKP-based NAI deployment to a non-NKP kubectl-based deployment. Which statement accurately describes a difference between the two approaches?

options.text: Non-NKP deployments cannot use NVIDIA GPU Operator; GPU scheduling must be configured manually through device plugins
NKP deployments automatically handle NAI Operator version compatibility with the GPU Operator, whereas non-NKP deployments require the administrator to manually verify version compatibility
Only NKP deployments support multi-node GPU clusters; non-NKP is limited to single-node inference
Non-NKP deployments require a separate commercial license for vLLM since it is bundled only with NKP

questions.explanation: NKP's App Catalog validates and enforces version compatibility between the NAI Operator, GPU Operator, and the NKP platform during install and upgrade. On non-NKP clusters, the administrator must manually consult the compatibility matrix and ensure all component versions align. Both deployment types support NVIDIA GPU Operator, multi-node inference, and vLLM without separate licensing.

questions.questionText: An NAI platform administrator wants to identify which API keys are generating the most inference traffic across all deployed endpoints. Where in the NAI web UI should they look?
