questions.questionText: An organization has production workloads on NC2 on Azure and requires a dedicated connection from their primary data center. They also need the connection to support higher bandwidth than a VPN. Which connectivity option should they choose?

options.text: Azure ExpressRoute with a private peering circuit
Azure VPN Gateway with a BGP-enabled S2S tunnel
Azure Virtual WAN with a Basic hub
Azure Front Door with Private Link origins

questions.explanation: Azure ExpressRoute with private peering provides a dedicated, high-bandwidth connection that doesn't traverse the public internet. It supports bandwidth up to 100 Gbps and is recommended for production workloads requiring consistent performance.

questions.questionText: After deploying NC2 on Azure, an administrator notices that VMs on Nutanix overlay networks cannot reach resources in a peered Azure VNet. Flow Gateway VMs are deployed, but no dynamic routes appear in Azure. What is the most likely issue?

options.text: BGP peering between the Flow Gateway VMs and Azure Route Server has not been established
The Azure VNet peering has not enabled "Allow Gateway Transit"
The Flow Gateway VMs do not have public IP addresses assigned
Azure Firewall is blocking BGP packets on TCP port 179

questions.explanation: For Azure to learn routes to Nutanix overlay networks, the Flow Gateway VMs must establish BGP sessions with Azure Route Server. Without this peering, Azure has no knowledge of the Nutanix overlay subnets.

questions.questionText: An administrator is troubleshooting an NC2 on AWS deployment where Prism Central can reach the cluster but VM live migration between hosts is failing. Which security group configuration should be investigated?

options.text: Intra-cluster communication rules allowing all traffic between NC2 node security group members
The inbound rule for TCP port 9440 from Prism Central
The outbound rule for TCP port 443 to AWS API endpoints
The inbound rule for TCP port 3260 from the CVM network

questions.explanation: Live migration requires extensive intra-cluster communication between AHV hosts. If the security group does not allow all traffic (or the specific ports for migration) between cluster nodes, VM migrations will fail.

questions.questionText: An NC2 on AWS administrator needs to decide between a VPN and Direct Connect for hybrid connectivity. Their workload requires less than 5 ms latency and 10 Gbps throughput. Which option meets these requirements?

options.text: AWS Direct Connect with a dedicated 10 Gbps connection
AWS Site-to-Site VPN with two tunnels for redundancy
AWS PrivateLink configured for the NC2 VPC
AWS CloudFront with origin set to the NC2 VPC

questions.explanation: AWS Direct Connect provides dedicated physical connectivity with consistent low latency and supports bandwidth up to 100 Gbps. A VPN traverses the public internet and cannot guarantee the required latency or throughput.

questions.questionText: An NC2 on Azure administrator creates a Nutanix Flow application security policy and assigns categories to workload VMs. The policy allows web tier VMs to communicate only with app tier VMs on TCP 8080. Where should the administrator verify the policy is active?

options.text: Prism Central > Policies > Security Policies, checking the applied policy and rule hit counts
Azure Portal > Network Security Groups, looking for auto-generated rules
NC2 Console > Networking > Firewall Rules section
Azure Monitor > Network Watcher > Flow Logs

questions.explanation: Flow security policies are managed and monitored in Prism Central. The Security Policies view shows policy status, applied rules, and hit counts. Azure NSGs and Azure Monitor do not display Nutanix Flow policy information.

questions.questionText: A hybrid environment has NC2 on AWS and on-premises Nutanix clusters. The administrator wants a consistent microsegmentation strategy across both. Which statement is correct?

options.text: Flow categories and security policies in Prism Central apply uniformly to both on-premises and NC2 clusters
Flow policies must be created separately for on-premises and NC2 clusters in their respective Prism Elements
NC2 clusters require AWS-native security groups instead of Flow for microsegmentation
Flow is only available on NC2 on Azure, not NC2 on AWS

questions.explanation: Prism Central provides a single pane of glass for managing Flow microsegmentation. Categories and security policies defined in Prism Central are applied consistently across all registered clusters, whether on-premises or NC2.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to expand an NC2 on AWS cluster from 3 nodes to 5 nodes to handle increased workload demand. Where is this scaling operation performed?

options.text: NC2 Console (my.nutanix.com)
Prism Central > Cluster Settings > Add Node
AWS EC2 Console by launching new bare-metal instances manually
Prism Element > Hardware > Add Host

questions.explanation: Node scaling for NC2 clusters is performed through the NC2 Console (my.nutanix.com). The NC2 Console handles cloud-specific provisioning of bare-metal instances and their integration into the existing Nutanix cluster.

questions.questionText: A finance team asks an administrator to minimize cloud costs for a development NC2 on AWS cluster that is only used during business hours. Which NC2 feature allows this?

options.text: Node hibernation to suspend NC2 nodes and stop compute billing
Shutting down all VMs but keeping the cluster running
Reducing the cluster to 1 node during off-hours
Converting to reserved instances during non-business hours

questions.explanation: Node hibernation suspends NC2 nodes, placing the cluster into maintenance mode and stopping compute charges for the hibernated instances. This is ideal for dev/test environments with predictable usage patterns.

questions.questionText: During node hibernation of an NC2 cluster, what state does the cluster enter?

options.text: Maintenance mode — no VMs run but metadata is preserved
Degraded mode — VMs continue with reduced redundancy
Standby mode — only CVMs remain active
Archive mode — all data is moved to S3/Azure Blob

questions.explanation: When nodes are hibernated, the NC2 cluster enters maintenance mode. All VMs are shut down, but cluster metadata and storage data are preserved. The cluster can be resumed when needed.

questions.questionText: An administrator must update AOS on an NC2 on AWS cluster. Which tool is used to perform this update?

options.text: Life Cycle Manager (LCM) in Prism Central or Prism Element, same as on-premises
NC2 Console's built-in AOS updater
AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager
SSH into each CVM and run the cluster upgrade CLI

questions.explanation: AOS and AHV updates on NC2 clusters are performed through LCM (Life Cycle Manager), the same process used for on-premises clusters. LCM performs rolling upgrades one node at a time.

questions.questionText: Which updates are managed through the NC2 Console rather than LCM?

options.text: Cloud-specific infrastructure updates such as bare-metal host firmware
AOS software version upgrades
AHV hypervisor patches
Prism Central version updates

questions.explanation: The NC2 Console manages cloud-specific updates including bare-metal host firmware and NC2 platform components. AOS, AHV, and Prism Central updates continue to use LCM, just like on-premises.

questions.questionText: An administrator attempts to remove a node from a 3-node NC2 cluster to reduce costs. The operation fails. Why?

options.text: NC2 clusters require a minimum of 3 nodes at all times
Nodes can only be removed when cluster utilization is below 50%
The administrator must first disable erasure coding
Node removal requires a support ticket with Nutanix

questions.explanation: Nutanix clusters, including NC2, require a minimum of 3 nodes for data redundancy and cluster quorum. The cluster will not allow a scale-down operation that would bring the node count below this minimum.

questions.questionText: An NC2 administrator wants to monitor both Nutanix cluster health and the underlying AWS infrastructure. Which combination provides comprehensive monitoring?

options.text: Prism Central dashboards for Nutanix metrics and Amazon CloudWatch for AWS infrastructure metrics
Prism Element exclusively, which integrates CloudWatch data automatically
AWS CloudWatch exclusively, which collects all CVM metrics natively
NC2 Console dashboards that unify both Nutanix and AWS metrics

questions.explanation: Prism Central provides Nutanix-specific metrics (cluster health, VM performance, storage utilization) while CloudWatch monitors AWS infrastructure (instance health, network throughput, bare-metal status). Both are needed for full visibility.

questions.questionText: An NC2 on Azure cluster shows a critical alert in Prism Central about cloud connectivity loss. Where else should the administrator check for more context?

options.text: NC2 Console alerts and Azure Monitor for instance and network health
Prism Element > Alerts only, as NC2-specific alerts are mirrored there
Azure Active Directory logs for authentication failures
Nutanix Insights portal for real-time cloud status

questions.explanation: NC2-specific alerts related to cloud connectivity and instance health appear in the NC2 Console. Azure Monitor provides additional context on Azure-side networking and compute health that complement Prism alerts.

questions.questionText: An administrator is planning storage capacity for an NC2 on AWS cluster. Which statement about NC2 storage is correct?

options.text: NC2 uses only local NVMe drives on bare-metal instances; EBS volumes cannot be used to expand storage
NC2 uses a combination of local NVMe and EBS gp3 volumes for storage tiering
NC2 storage is backed entirely by Amazon S3 with local NVMe as cache
NC2 supports adding EBS io2 volumes to expand storage pool capacity

questions.explanation: NC2 on AWS clusters use only the local NVMe storage on the bare-metal instances. There is no option to attach EBS volumes to expand the storage pool. Capacity is increased by adding more nodes.

questions.questionText: Before performing maintenance on an NC2 cluster, what should the administrator run to identify potential issues?

options.text: NCC (Nutanix Cluster Check) health checks
AWS Trusted Advisor infrastructure scan
Prism Central capacity planning report
LCM inventory collection

questions.explanation: NCC (Nutanix Cluster Check) runs comprehensive health checks to identify cluster issues before maintenance. This is the same best practice as on-premises and should always be executed before planned maintenance activities.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to perform maintenance on a single AHV host in an NC2 cluster. What is the correct procedure?

options.text: Place the host in maintenance mode to migrate VMs off, then perform the maintenance
Shut down the host directly from the AWS/Azure console
Power off all VMs on the host manually, then reboot via Prism
Detach the host from the cluster using the NC2 Console, then service it

questions.explanation: The correct procedure is to enter maintenance mode on the host through Prism, which live-migrates all running VMs to other nodes. Once the host is evacuated, maintenance can be performed safely.

questions.questionText: An NC2 on AWS administrator needs to restart the CVM on a specific node after a troubleshooting session. Which method is consistent with on-premises procedures?

options.text: SSH to the CVM and use genesis restart or the standard CVM restart procedure
Reboot the bare-metal instance from the AWS EC2 console
Use the NC2 Console's CVM restart button
Stop and start the CVM from Prism Element's VM management page

questions.explanation: CVM maintenance on NC2 follows the same procedures as on-premises. Administrators SSH to the CVM and use standard commands like genesis restart for service-level restarts or the documented CVM restart workflow.

questions.questionText: An NC2 on AWS cluster has suffered a catastrophic failure. The administrator needs to rebuild the cluster. Where is the cluster metadata backup stored for recovery?

options.text: Amazon S3, from which the cluster can be rebuilt using the NC2 Console
Local NVMe drives on the surviving bare-metal instances
An EBS snapshot created automatically during the last LCM update
Prism Central's internal database on the management VPC

questions.explanation: NC2 cluster metadata is backed up to Amazon S3 (or Azure Blob for Azure deployments). In a disaster scenario, the cluster can be rebuilt from this metadata backup through the NC2 Console.

questions.questionText: An NC2 on Azure cluster needs to be rebuilt after a disaster. Where is the metadata backup located?

options.text: Azure Blob Storage
Azure Managed Disks snapshots
Azure Table Storage
Prism Central's recovery database

questions.explanation: For NC2 on Azure, cluster metadata backups are stored in Azure Blob Storage. The NC2 Console can orchestrate cluster rebuild using this metadata, similar to how S3 is used for AWS deployments.

questions.questionText: An administrator receives an NC2-specific alert indicating an AWS bare-metal instance has a scheduled retirement event. What should the administrator do?

options.text: Place the host in maintenance mode, migrate VMs, then use the NC2 Console to replace the node
Ignore the alert because AWS automatically migrates the instance
Shut down the cluster and wait for AWS to provision a replacement
Contact AWS support to cancel the retirement event

questions.explanation: When an instance retirement is scheduled, the administrator should proactively place the affected host in maintenance mode to migrate workloads, then use the NC2 Console to remove the retiring node and add a replacement.

questions.questionText: An administrator notices that the NC2 on AWS cluster's storage usage is at 85%. What is the recommended approach to increase capacity?

options.text: Add additional nodes to the cluster through the NC2 Console
Attach EBS gp3 volumes to existing bare-metal instances
Enable cloud tiering to offload cold data to S3
Resize the existing NVMe drives through the AWS console

questions.explanation: Since NC2 uses only local NVMe storage, the only way to increase storage capacity is by adding more nodes through the NC2 Console. EBS expansion, cloud tiering, and NVMe resizing are not supported for NC2 storage pools.

questions.questionText: How does licensing and billing work for NC2 clusters?

options.text: Usage-based billing tracked through the NC2 Console with consumption-based charges
Traditional Nutanix perpetual licenses applied via Prism Element
AWS Marketplace hourly billing based on EC2 instance type
Annual subscription tied to the number of VMs deployed

questions.explanation: NC2 uses a consumption-based billing model tracked through the NC2 Console (my.nutanix.com). Charges are based on actual usage of compute resources, providing flexibility to scale costs with demand.

questions.questionText: An administrator is performing an AOS upgrade via LCM on an NC2 cluster. During the rolling upgrade, one node fails to come back online. What is the immediate impact?

options.text: The cluster continues operating in a degraded state with reduced redundancy; VMs on remaining nodes are unaffected
The entire cluster shuts down to prevent data corruption
All VMs are automatically migrated to a standby NC2 cluster
LCM automatically reverts the upgrade on all nodes

questions.explanation: Nutanix clusters are designed for fault tolerance. If one node fails during an upgrade, the cluster operates in a degraded state. VMs on other nodes continue running, but redundancy is reduced until the node is recovered.

questions.questionText: An NC2 on AWS cluster shows increased CVM latency in Prism Central. The administrator suspects a noisy-neighbor issue. Which monitoring tool provides host-level bare-metal performance data?

options.text: Amazon CloudWatch metrics for the bare-metal instance
Prism Central's Analysis dashboard for CVM metrics
AWS X-Ray distributed tracing
NC2 Console performance tab

questions.explanation: CloudWatch provides host-level metrics for bare-metal instances including CPU, network, and disk I/O. Since NC2 runs on dedicated bare-metal instances, noisy-neighbor issues are unlikely, but CloudWatch helps identify actual host performance problems.

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to schedule node hibernation for an NC2 dev/test cluster. The cluster runs 3 nodes. What happens when hibernation is initiated?

options.text: All VMs are shut down, nodes are suspended, and the cluster enters maintenance mode
VMs are live-migrated to a single node, and two nodes are suspended
Only compute is suspended; storage remains accessible via the CVM
The cluster creates VM snapshots in S3 before shutting down

questions.explanation: Node hibernation shuts down all running VMs and suspends the bare-metal instances. The cluster enters maintenance mode where no workloads run, but metadata is preserved for quick resumption.

questions.questionText: After resuming an NC2 cluster from hibernation, which component should the administrator verify first?

options.text: NCC health checks to confirm all services are running and the cluster is healthy
AWS billing dashboard to confirm charges have resumed
Prism Central registration to re-register the cluster
LCM to run a firmware update before starting VMs

questions.explanation: After resuming from hibernation, running NCC health checks verifies that all Nutanix services (Stargate, Curator, etc.) are running correctly and the cluster is in a healthy state before starting workloads.

questions.questionText: An administrator manages both on-premises Nutanix clusters and NC2 on AWS clusters from Prism Central. Monitoring dashboards show metrics from all clusters. Which statement is accurate?

options.text: Prism Central dashboards display unified metrics for both on-premises and NC2 clusters
NC2 cluster metrics only appear in the NC2 Console, not in Prism Central
Prism Central requires a separate license for NC2 cluster monitoring
NC2 cluster metrics are limited to storage metrics only in Prism Central

questions.explanation: Prism Central provides unified monitoring for all registered clusters. NC2 clusters appear alongside on-premises clusters with the same dashboards, alerts, and analysis capabilities.

questions.questionText: An NC2 cluster has five nodes and the administrator wants to remove two nodes to save costs. How many nodes can be removed?

options.text: Two nodes can be removed, leaving the minimum of three nodes
Only one node can be removed at a time, requiring 24 hours between removals
No nodes can be removed from a five-node cluster due to RF2 requirements
All nodes except the CVM leader can be removed

questions.explanation: Since the minimum node count is 3, the administrator can remove up to 2 nodes from a 5-node cluster. The NC2 Console orchestrates the removal, ensuring data is rebalanced before each node is decommissioned.

questions.questionText: An NC2 on AWS cluster alert shows "Cloud Connectivity Degraded." The administrator checks and finds the cluster can reach the internet but certain AWS API calls are failing. What should be investigated?

options.text: IAM role permissions attached to the NC2 bare-metal instances for required AWS API access
The VPC internet gateway configuration
DNS resolution for public AWS endpoints
The NC2 license expiration date

questions.explanation: NC2 requires specific IAM permissions for AWS API calls (EC2, S3, etc.). If IAM role permissions are modified or insufficient, cloud connectivity alerts may trigger even when general network connectivity is working.

questions.questionText: An administrator is planning a maintenance window for an NC2 on AWS cluster. Which pre-maintenance step is NOT required?

options.text: Draining the AWS ALB target group associated with the cluster
Running NCC health checks to identify existing issues
Verifying cluster has sufficient capacity to handle node maintenance
Reviewing active Prism alerts for unresolved warnings

questions.explanation: AWS ALB target groups are not a standard component of NC2 cluster architecture. NCC health checks, capacity verification, and alert review are all standard pre-maintenance best practices.

questions.questionText: An administrator uses LCM to update the AHV hypervisor on an NC2 cluster. The update process places one node in maintenance mode at a time. What determines whether the upgrade can proceed to the next node?

options.text: LCM verifies the updated node has successfully rebooted and all services are healthy before proceeding
A 30-minute mandatory wait time between each node update
The administrator must manually approve each subsequent node
AWS health checks must report the instance as "running" in the EC2 console

questions.explanation: LCM performs rolling upgrades by verifying that each updated node has successfully rebooted, rejoined the cluster, and all Nutanix services are healthy before proceeding to the next node.

questions.questionText: An NC2 on AWS cluster experiences a CVM crash on one node. What is the expected cluster behavior?

options.text: The cluster redirects storage I/O to remaining CVMs; VMs on the affected host continue with remote I/O
All VMs on the affected host are immediately shut down
The entire cluster enters read-only mode until the CVM recovers
Prism Central automatically provisions a replacement CVM from an AMI

questions.explanation: Nutanix's distributed storage architecture redirects I/O to healthy CVMs when a CVM fails. VMs on the affected host continue running using remote CVM I/O paths until the local CVM recovers.

questions.questionText: An administrator manages multiple Nutanix environments including on-premises clusters and NC2 on AWS. Which tool provides a single management interface for all clusters?

options.text: Prism Central
NC2 Console
Prism Element
AWS Systems Manager

questions.explanation: Prism Central serves as the centralized management plane for all Nutanix clusters. Both on-premises and NC2 clusters register with Prism Central for unified management, monitoring, and policy enforcement.

questions.questionText: An administrator creates categories in Prism Central for policy enforcement. Which statement about categories in a hybrid NC2 environment is correct?

options.text: Categories defined in Prism Central apply uniformly to VMs on both on-premises and NC2 clusters
Separate categories must be created for on-premises and NC2 clusters
Categories on NC2 clusters are automatically synced to AWS resource tags
Categories only work with Flow policies, not with other Prism Central features

questions.explanation: Prism Central categories are a unified construct that spans all registered clusters. The same categories and their associated policies (Flow, DR, etc.) apply consistently to VMs regardless of whether they run on-premises or on NC2.

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to automate VM deployments across both on-premises and NC2 clusters. Which Nutanix service provides blueprint-based hybrid cloud automation?

options.text: NCM Self-Service (formerly Calm)
NC2 Console deployment wizard
Prism Element VM provisioning
AWS CloudFormation with Nutanix plugin

questions.explanation: NCM Self-Service (Calm) provides blueprint-based automation for deploying applications across multiple Nutanix clusters. Blueprints can target both on-premises and NC2 clusters, enabling consistent hybrid cloud deployments.

questions.questionText: An organization wants to run containerized microservices on their NC2 on AWS cluster. Which Nutanix service deploys Kubernetes clusters on NC2?

options.text: Nutanix Kubernetes Engine (NKE) or Nutanix Kubernetes Platform (NKP)
Prism Central Container Service
AWS EKS integrated with NC2
Nutanix Docker Engine (NDE)

questions.explanation: NKE (Nutanix Kubernetes Engine) or NKP (Nutanix Kubernetes Platform) can deploy and manage Kubernetes clusters on NC2, providing cloud-native workload support with the same Nutanix infrastructure management.

questions.questionText: An administrator configures disaster recovery between an on-premises Nutanix cluster and an NC2 on AWS cluster. Which Nutanix feature provides orchestrated failover with recovery plans?

options.text: Leap with protection domains and recovery plans
Nutanix Move with scheduled replication
Prism Central Async DR with manual failover
AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery integrated with NC2

questions.explanation: Nutanix Leap provides DR orchestration with protection domains for replication and recovery plans for automated failover. Leap works across on-premises and NC2 clusters, enabling hybrid DR strategies.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to integrate Prism Central with the company's Active Directory for user authentication on an NC2 environment. Which statement is correct?

options.text: AD/LDAP integration for Prism Central works the same way on NC2 as on-premises
NC2 requires Azure AD integration instead of on-premises AD
AD integration is only available through the NC2 Console for NC2 clusters
NC2 clusters require a separate AD domain from on-premises

questions.explanation: Active Directory and LDAP integration for Prism Central and Prism Element works identically on NC2 as it does on-premises. The same directory services configuration applies regardless of where the cluster runs.

questions.questionText: A company wants SSO access to Prism Central for managing both on-premises and NC2 clusters. Which authentication protocol should be configured?

options.text: SAML for Prism Central SSO
OAuth 2.0 via the NC2 Console
Kerberos delegation through Prism Element
OpenID Connect via AWS Cognito

questions.explanation: Prism Central supports SAML-based SSO, allowing integration with enterprise identity providers. This provides a unified authentication experience for administrators managing hybrid environments.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to migrate VMs from an on-premises Nutanix cluster to an NC2 on AWS cluster. Which Nutanix tool is specifically designed for this task?

options.text: Nutanix Move
Nutanix Leap
Prism Central VM clone
NC2 Console import wizard

questions.explanation: Nutanix Move is the purpose-built migration tool for moving VMs between environments. It supports migrations between on-premises Nutanix clusters and NC2 clusters with minimal downtime.

questions.questionText: An architect is planning a migration from NC2 on AWS to NC2 on Azure. Which approach enables cross-cloud migration?

options.text: Use Nutanix Move to migrate VMs from the NC2 AWS cluster to the NC2 Azure cluster
Export VMs as AMIs and convert them to Azure VHDs manually
Use AWS-to-Azure native migration services
Cross-cloud migration is not supported; VMs must be rebuilt on the target

questions.explanation: Nutanix Move supports cross-cloud VM migration between NC2 clusters. Since both clusters run AHV with the same Nutanix stack, Move can migrate VMs between NC2 on AWS and NC2 on Azure.

questions.questionText: An administrator uploads a VM disk image to the Prism Central image service. The image needs to be available for deployment on both on-premises and NC2 clusters. How is this achieved?

options.text: Prism Central image service distributes images across registered clusters, including NC2
Images must be uploaded separately to each cluster's Prism Element
Images are stored in S3 and referenced by both environments
NC2 clusters can only use cloud marketplace images

questions.explanation: The Prism Central image service manages VM templates and disk images across all registered clusters. Images uploaded to Prism Central can be placed on or replicated to any cluster, including NC2.

questions.questionText: An organization deploys a Calm blueprint that provisions a multi-tier application. The web tier runs on NC2 on AWS, while the database tier runs on-premises. Which Calm feature makes this possible?

options.text: Multi-cloud blueprints that target different providers/clusters for different application tiers
Calm requires all tiers to be deployed on the same cluster
AWS CloudFormation nested stacks called from within Calm
Separate blueprints must be created for each tier and manually coordinated

questions.explanation: Calm (NCM Self-Service) supports multi-cloud blueprints where different application components can target different clusters or cloud providers. This enables true hybrid deployment scenarios.

questions.questionText: An administrator sets up Leap DR between an on-premises cluster and NC2 on AWS. The recovery plan specifies an RPO of 1 hour. Which replication mechanism does Leap use?

options.text: Asynchronous replication with scheduled snapshots based on the RPO schedule
Synchronous replication requiring less than 5 ms latency
Continuous data protection (CDP) with journal-based recovery
AWS S3 cross-region replication for the protection domain

questions.explanation: Leap uses asynchronous replication with scheduled snapshots for DR between on-premises and NC2 clusters. The snapshot frequency is configured based on the RPO requirement — a 1-hour RPO means snapshots replicate every hour.

questions.questionText: After a successful Leap failover from on-premises to NC2 on AWS, VMs are running on the NC2 cluster. The on-premises site has been recovered. What is the next step to restore the original configuration?

options.text: Execute a planned failback using Leap's recovery plan to migrate VMs back to on-premises
Use Nutanix Move to migrate VMs from NC2 back to on-premises
Manually recreate VMs on-premises and restore data from backup
Delete the NC2 cluster and re-register the on-premises cluster in Prism Central

questions.explanation: Leap recovery plans support planned failback operations. The administrator initiates a failback through Prism Central, which reverses replication and migrates VMs back to the on-premises cluster.

questions.questionText: An NC2 administrator wants to use Prism Central to apply different security policies to VMs running on NC2 versus on-premises. How should categories be structured?

options.text: Create environment-specific category values (e.g., Environment:NC2-AWS, Environment:OnPrem) and use them in policy rules
Create separate Prism Central instances for each environment
Use AWS tags for NC2 VMs and Prism categories for on-premises VMs
Flow policies cannot differentiate between on-premises and NC2 VMs

questions.explanation: Category values can distinguish environments. By assigning different category values to VMs based on their location, administrators can create targeted security policies while maintaining centralized management in Prism Central.

questions.questionText: A company runs NKE (Nutanix Kubernetes Engine) on their NC2 on AWS cluster. How are Kubernetes worker node VMs stored?

options.text: On the Nutanix distributed storage fabric using local NVMe on the NC2 bare-metal instances
On Amazon EBS volumes attached to the worker node VMs
On Amazon EFS shared file storage
On a separate AWS EKS-managed storage layer

questions.explanation: NKE worker node VMs and their persistent volumes are stored on the Nutanix distributed storage fabric (DSF), which uses the local NVMe drives on NC2 bare-metal instances, just like any other Nutanix VM.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to provide self-service VM provisioning to developers using NC2 on AWS infrastructure. Which Nutanix service provides role-based self-service with quotas?

options.text: NCM Self-Service (Calm) with projects, roles, and quotas configured in Prism Central
AWS Service Catalog integrated with NC2
Prism Element's custom dashboard with restricted access
NC2 Console user management with granular permissions

questions.explanation: NCM Self-Service (Calm) provides project-based self-service where administrators define roles, quotas, and approved blueprints. Developers can deploy VMs and applications within their project boundaries on NC2 clusters.

questions.questionText: An administrator has configured Prism Central to manage both on-premises and NC2 clusters. During a Prism Central upgrade, what happens to NC2 cluster operations?

options.text: NC2 clusters continue operating normally; Prism Element provides local management during the PC upgrade
NC2 clusters enter maintenance mode during the Prism Central upgrade
All Flow policies on NC2 clusters are suspended until Prism Central is back online
NC2 clusters must be hibernated before upgrading Prism Central

questions.explanation: Prism Central provides centralized management, but clusters operate independently via Prism Element. During a Prism Central upgrade, all clusters (including NC2) continue running; only centralized features like Calm and global Flow management are temporarily unavailable.

questions.questionText: An administrator is deploying a Calm blueprint to an NC2 cluster and needs the blueprint to execute a post-deployment script that registers the VM with an external monitoring tool. Which Calm feature enables this?

options.text: Task library with shell/PowerShell scripts in the blueprint's post-deployment actions
AWS Lambda functions triggered by NC2 Console webhooks
Prism Central's custom action framework with REST API calls
AHV guest tools executing a registration agent automatically

questions.explanation: Calm blueprints support scripted tasks (shell, PowerShell, EScript) that run during various lifecycle stages. Post-deployment scripts can register VMs with monitoring tools, configure applications, or perform other automation tasks.

questions.questionText: An organization uses Nutanix Move to migrate a Windows Server VM from on-premises to NC2 on AWS. The migration completes but the VM cannot reach the network. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The target VM network and IP configuration on NC2 need to be correctly mapped during the Move migration plan
Windows Server VMs cannot run on NC2 on AWS
The VM's MAC address was not preserved during migration
AWS security groups block all traffic to migrated VMs by default

questions.explanation: During Nutanix Move migration, the target network must be correctly mapped in the migration plan. If the VM is placed on the wrong network or the IP configuration doesn't match the NC2 environment, network connectivity will fail.

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to use the same VM template for deployments across on-premises and NC2 clusters. Where should the template be managed?

options.text: Prism Central image service, which replicates images to all registered clusters
Each cluster's local image repository in Prism Element
An S3 bucket that both environments reference
The NC2 Console's image management section

questions.explanation: The Prism Central image service provides centralized image management with replication capabilities. Templates managed in Prism Central can be distributed to both on-premises and NC2 clusters for consistent deployments.

questions.questionText: An administrator sets up a Leap protection domain for VMs running on NC2 on AWS with recovery to an on-premises cluster. The protection domain uses categories to select VMs. What happens when a new VM matching the category is created on NC2?

options.text: The VM is automatically included in the protection domain based on its category assignment
The administrator must manually add the VM to the protection domain
The VM is protected only after the next manual sync
New VMs require a separate protection policy until the next scheduled replication

questions.explanation: Category-based protection domains in Leap automatically include VMs that match the specified categories. When a new VM is created and assigned a matching category, it is automatically protected by the existing protection domain.

questions.questionText: An administrator deploys NKP (Nutanix Kubernetes Platform) on an NC2 on Azure cluster. The Kubernetes applications require persistent storage. Which Nutanix storage feature provides this?

options.text: Nutanix CSI (Container Storage Interface) driver connected to the Nutanix storage fabric
Azure Managed Disks mounted directly to Kubernetes pods
AHV Volume Groups exposed as raw block devices to containers
NFS exports from the CVM shared with the Kubernetes namespace

questions.explanation: The Nutanix CSI driver provides persistent storage for Kubernetes workloads by connecting to the Nutanix distributed storage fabric. This allows dynamic provisioning of persistent volumes on NC2 clusters.

questions.questionText: A company operates in a regulated industry and must ensure that their NC2 environment meets compliance requirements. Which integration allows centralized audit logging across on-premises and NC2?

options.text: Prism Central audit logs combined with syslog forwarding to a SIEM solution
AWS CloudTrail forwarding all NC2 operations to the compliance team
NC2 Console's built-in compliance dashboard
Nutanix Flow generating compliance reports automatically

questions.explanation: Prism Central provides audit logging for all management operations. By configuring syslog forwarding to a SIEM (e.g., Splunk, QRadar), organizations can centralize audit data from both on-premises and NC2 clusters for compliance.

questions.questionText: An administrator uses Calm to deploy a blueprint that spans on-premises and NC2 on AWS. The blueprint references a credential stored in Calm. How are credentials managed in this hybrid scenario?

options.text: Credentials are stored centrally in Calm and are available for use across all target environments
Separate credentials must be created for each target cluster
Credentials are stored in AWS Secrets Manager and referenced by Calm
Calm does not support credential management for NC2 targets

questions.explanation: Calm stores credentials centrally within its credential store. These credentials can be used across different target environments in multi-cloud blueprints, simplifying secrets management for hybrid deployments.

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to extend their on-premises Nutanix data protection strategy to include NC2 on AWS workloads. Which approach provides the most comprehensive protection?

options.text: Configure Leap protection domains in Prism Central covering both on-premises and NC2 VMs with cross-site recovery plans
Use AWS Backup to protect NC2 VMs independently of Nutanix DR
Create separate Nutanix protection domains in each Prism Element for local protection only
Rely on the NC2 metadata backup in S3 as the primary DR strategy

questions.explanation: Leap in Prism Central provides unified data protection across all registered clusters. Protection domains can span on-premises and NC2, with recovery plans enabling failover in either direction for comprehensive hybrid DR.

questions.questionText: An organization successfully migrates workloads to NC2 on AWS using Nutanix Move. Post-migration, they want to implement consistent security and governance across their hybrid environment. Which combination of Nutanix features achieves this?

options.text: Prism Central categories for unified tagging, Flow for microsegmentation, and NCM Self-Service for governance with projects and quotas
AWS IAM policies for NC2 and Prism Element RBAC for on-premises
Flow for on-premises and AWS Security Groups for NC2
NC2 Console governance policies combined with Prism Element access control

questions.explanation: Prism Central provides the unified platform for hybrid governance: categories ensure consistent tagging, Flow enforces microsegmentation across all clusters, and NCM Self-Service (Calm) provides project-based governance with role-based access and resource quotas.

questions.questionText: When deploying NC2 on AWS, you need to ensure compute instances are placed in an VPC with a minimum subnet size. What is the minimum subnet size (CIDR notation) required for an NC2 deployment?
questions.explanation: NC2 on AWS requires a VPC /25 subnet minimum to accommodate the cluster nodes, Flow Gateway instances, and management VMs. This provides 32 usable IP addresses, which is the minimum for a 3-node cluster with additional infrastructure.

questions.questionText: You are designing an NC2 on AWS cluster with i3.metal instances. Where does Nutanix expect to store cluster metadata backups in AWS?

options.text: EBS volumes attached to each node
AWS S3 only
EFS shared storage
DynamoDB

questions.explanation: NC2 on AWS uses local NVMe for all cluster data, while S3 is used exclusively for metadata backup only. This prevents any dependency on EBS performance and keeps data local for optimal performance.

questions.questionText: An NC2 cluster on AWS requires Direct Connect connectivity. What is the primary purpose of using AWS Direct Connect instead of public internet connectivity?

options.text: To reduce AWS data transfer costs
To provide a dedicated, low-latency network connection between on-premises and AWS
To enable automatic failover between regions
To bypass IAM permission requirements

questions.explanation: AWS Direct Connect provides a dedicated, private network connection from on-premises to AWS with consistent network performance and lower latency compared to internet-based connections, which is critical for hybrid cloud deployments and Leap disaster recovery.

questions.questionText: When configuring security groups for NC2 on AWS, which three ports must be explicitly allowed for proper cluster functionality?
questions.explanation: NC2 on AWS security groups must allow: 9440 (Prism Central/Element UI), 2049 (NFS), and 3260 (iSCSI). These ports enable management, storage connectivity, and inter-node communication.

questions.questionText: You are deploying NC2 on Azure using BareMetal Infrastructure. What is the purpose of Flow Gateway VMs in the Azure deployment?

options.text: To provide backup and disaster recovery capabilities
To bridge AHV overlay network connectivity to the Azure VNet underlay
To reduce storage costs by tiering data to Azure blob storage
To provide encryption key management

questions.explanation: Flow Gateway VMs on Azure BareMetal bridge the AHV overlay network to the Azure VNet underlay, enabling connectivity between cluster VMs and the Azure virtual network. They work with Azure Route Server and BGP for dynamic routing.

questions.questionText: An NC2 on Azure deployment uses a delegated subnet. What is the minimum requirement for the delegated subnet configuration?

options.text: /24 subnet
/26 subnet
It must be the same size as the cluster VLAN
There is no minimum; any size works

questions.explanation: The delegated subnet in Azure for NC2 BareMetal deployments requires a /24 minimum to accommodate the BareMetal instances and associated management infrastructure.

questions.questionText: When using Azure ExpressRoute with NC2 BareMetal, what protocol is used for dynamic routing between the BareMetal infrastructure and Azure?

options.text: OSPF with direct peering
BGP with Azure Route Server
RIP version 2
IS-IS

questions.explanation: NC2 on Azure uses BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) with Azure Route Server to enable dynamic routing. This allows the BareMetal cluster to advertise routes and receive updates from the Azure vNet.

questions.questionText: You are configuring Flow Gateway VMs for an NC2 Azure deployment. What is the maximum number of Flow Gateway VMs you can configure for ECMP (Equal Cost Multi-Path) routing?
questions.explanation: ECMP on NC2 Azure supports a maximum of 4 Flow Gateway VMs for load-balanced routing across multiple gateways, improving redundancy and network throughput.

questions.questionText: After initial NC2 cluster deployment, you need to access the management console. Which Nutanix portal should you use to manage your NC2 cluster?

options.text: Nutanix.cloud web portal
my.nutanix.com console
Prism Element
AWS CloudFormation dashboard

questions.explanation: my.nutanix.com is the unified portal for managing NC2 clusters across AWS and Azure. This single-pane-of-glass console provides access to Prism Central, licensing, and cluster management.

questions.questionText: What is the minimum number of nodes required for an NC2 cluster deployment?

options.text: 1 node
2 nodes
3 nodes
5 nodes

questions.explanation: NC2 requires a minimum of 3 nodes for production deployment. This ensures quorum for the metadata service and provides redundancy for the Nutanix cluster.

questions.questionText: You are deploying an NC2 cluster. The deployment wizard asks about the operating system and hypervisor stack. What is included in the full stack deployed with NC2?

options.text: AOS only
AHV only
AOS + AHV
AOS + KVM

questions.explanation: NC2 deployments include the full AOS (Acropolis Operating System) and AHV (Acropolis Hypervisor) stack pre-installed on cloud instances. Foundation is not needed because the cloud instances come with the full stack pre-loaded.

questions.questionText: During NC2 deployment, the wizard asks whether you need Foundation. What is the correct approach?

options.text: Foundation must be used for cloud deployments
Foundation is optional but recommended
Foundation is NOT needed; cloud instances come pre-configured
Foundation must be run in a container on the first node

questions.explanation: Foundation is not used for NC2 deployments because the cloud instances come pre-configured with AOS and AHV. The deployment process is simplified and Foundation is unnecessary.

questions.questionText: Your organization has deployed multiple NC2 clusters across AWS and Azure. What licensing model is supported for NC2?

options.text: Traditional perpetual licensing only
Subscription-based licensing only
Subscription-based licensing with consumption tracking
Both A and C

questions.explanation: NC2 uses subscription-based licensing with consumption tracking. You are charged based on the number of nodes deployed and subscription term, enabling flexibility for cloud deployments.

questions.questionText: You need to reduce costs for a development and testing NC2 environment during non-business hours. What Nutanix feature can help minimize costs?

options.text: Reserved Instances only
Node hibernation for dev/test
Automatic cluster shutdown
Converting to standard licensing

questions.explanation: Node hibernation is primarily an Azure feature that allows you to put NC2 nodes into a sleep state during off-peak hours, reducing compute costs while maintaining the ability to resume quickly. On AWS, cluster-level hibernation with metadata backup to S3 achieves similar cost savings. This is ideal for dev/test environments.

questions.questionText: When configuring security for an NC2 cluster on AWS, you need to assign IAM roles to EC2 instances. What is the minimum IAM permission requirement?

options.text: Full EC2 admin access
Permissions for EC2, S3 (metadata backup), and security group management
Read-only IAM permissions
No IAM roles are needed

questions.explanation: NC2 on AWS requires IAM roles that grant permissions for EC2 instance management, S3 access for metadata backups, and security group modifications. Full admin access is not required—only the specific permissions needed for NC2 operations.

questions.questionText: You are planning an NC2 deployment on AWS and need to determine the AZ (Availability Zone) requirement. What is the critical requirement regarding Availability Zones?

options.text: All NC2 nodes must be in the same AZ
NC2 nodes can be distributed across multiple AZs
AZ placement doesn't matter for NC2
Each node must be in a different AZ

questions.explanation: NC2 on AWS requires all cluster nodes to be in the same Availability Zone. This ensures low-latency communication between nodes and is a strict architectural requirement.

questions.questionText: Your organization is considering NC2 on AWS with i3.metal instances. What storage is used for cluster data?

options.text: EBS volumes only
Local NVMe storage only
Mix of local NVMe and EBS
AWS Glacier for long-term storage

questions.explanation: NC2 on AWS uses local NVMe storage (which comes with i3.metal instances) exclusively for cluster data. EBS is not used for cluster data—only S3 is used for metadata backups.

questions.questionText: You need to set up VPC peering between your on-premises network and an NC2 cluster on AWS. What networking component facilitates this connectivity?

options.text: Direct Connect only
Transit Gateway with Direct Connect
VPN gateway only
NAT Gateway

questions.explanation: AWS Transit Gateway combined with Direct Connect enables secure, scalable connectivity between on-premises networks and NC2 clusters. Transit Gateway acts as a central hub for network routing.

questions.questionText: An NC2 cluster requires split-horizon DNS configuration. What is the purpose of split-horizon DNS?

options.text: To reduce DNS query latency
To provide different DNS responses based on where the query originates (internal vs. external)
To enable DNS failover between regions
To encrypt DNS traffic

questions.explanation: Split-horizon DNS returns different IP addresses based on the requestor's location—internal IP addresses for on-premises clients and public IPs for cloud clients. This ensures optimal routing and connectivity.

questions.questionText: You are designing a VPN failover solution for NC2 on AWS. Which networking configuration allows automatic failover for VPN connections?

options.text: Single VPN connection to NC2
Dual VPN connections with failover policy
VPN with Load Balancer only
BGP routing with redundant connections

questions.explanation: BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) with redundant VPN connections enables automatic failover. BGP dynamically updates routing tables when a connection fails, ensuring uninterrupted connectivity.

questions.questionText: You are using Nutanix Leap to replicate a VM from on-premises Nutanix to NC2 on AWS. What is the primary advantage of using Leap for this migration?

options.text: Leap requires zero data transfer
Leap enables continuous replication and planned/unplanned failover capabilities
Leap only works for VM cloning
Leap requires manual failover only

questions.explanation: Leap provides continuous replication between on-premises and NC2, supporting both planned failover (scheduled migrations) and unplanned failover (disaster recovery). It enables bidirectional replication for flexibility.

questions.questionText: Your organization uses Nutanix Move to migrate VMs to NC2. During migration, what validation step is critical before completing the cutover?

options.text: Only verify VM power state
Perform test migration in an isolated network
Skip validation to reduce migration time
Only check network connectivity

questions.explanation: Before cutover, Nutanix Move allows test migration to an isolated test network where you can validate VM functionality without affecting production. This ensures successful migration before final cutover.

questions.questionText: After a failover from on-premises to NC2, you want to revert to the on-premises environment. What is this process called?

options.text: Forward failover
Planned failover
Reverse replication
Migration rollback

questions.explanation: Reverse replication enables bidirectional data sync, allowing you to fail back to the on-premises environment if needed. This is critical for disaster recovery and provides a fallback mechanism.

questions.questionText: You are setting up Prism Central to manage both on-premises Nutanix clusters and NC2 clusters. What is the primary benefit of this unified management approach?

options.text: Reduced licensing costs
Single dashboard for monitoring, alerts, and policy enforcement across environments
Automatic replication between on-premises and NC2
Elimination of security group requirements

questions.explanation: Prism Central provides a unified dashboard for managing multiple Nutanix environments including on-premises and NC2 clusters. This enables consistent policy enforcement, monitoring, and operational oversight.

questions.questionText: You need to categorize VMs across both on-premises and NC2 environments for resource allocation and billing. How does Nutanix support this?

options.text: Categories only work on-premises
Separate category systems for each environment
Categories work consistently across on-premises and NC2 environments in Prism Central
Categories must be manually synced

questions.explanation: Categories in Prism Central work across both on-premises and NC2 environments, enabling consistent resource tagging, policy enforcement, and billing allocation across the hybrid cloud infrastructure.

questions.questionText: You want to deploy Nutanix Calm blueprints on your NC2 cluster. What application framework is supported?

options.text: Calm only works on-premises
Calm blueprints work on NC2 for multi-cloud application deployment
Only IaaS blueprints are supported on NC2
Calm requires separate licensing on NC2

questions.explanation: Nutanix Calm blueprints are fully supported on NC2, enabling infrastructure-as-code deployment of multi-tier applications across on-premises and cloud environments with the same blueprint.

questions.questionText: You want to run Kubernetes workloads on your NC2 cluster. Which Nutanix Kubernetes platform can be deployed on NC2?

options.text: NKE only (Nutanix Kubernetes Engine)
NKP only (Nutanix Kubernetes Platform)
Both NKE and NKP
Kubernetes is not supported on NC2

questions.explanation: Both Nutanix Kubernetes Engine (NKE) and Nutanix Kubernetes Platform (NKP) are supported on NC2, enabling container workload deployment alongside traditional VMs in the same environment.

questions.questionText: You are troubleshooting an NC2 deployment failure on AWS. The error message indicates "Insufficient EC2 capacity." What is the most likely cause?

options.text: Your security group is misconfigured
AWS quota for i3.metal instances has been reached
The VPC subnet is too small
IAM role permissions are missing

questions.explanation: "Insufficient EC2 capacity" typically means AWS deployment quotas have been reached for the instance type. You need to request a quota increase from AWS Support or choose a different region/AZ.

questions.questionText: During NC2 deployment on Azure, you receive an error about the delegated subnet. What validation should you perform first?

options.text: Verify the subnet is /24 or larger and properly delegated to BareMetal
Check only the region
Verify the storage account exists
Check the Virtual Network name only

questions.explanation: When delegated subnet errors occur, verify that the subnet is at least /24, properly delegated to the BareMetal service in Azure, and not already hosting other resources. Delegation is critical for BareMetal infrastructure.

questions.questionText: An NC2 node fails in production. What is the correct recovery procedure?

options.text: Repair the node using Nutanix tools
Destroy the failed node and add a new node to the cluster
Wait for automatic recovery
Reboot the node

questions.explanation: Failed NC2 nodes must be destroyed and replaced with new instances. Repair operations are not supported for cloud-based nodes because you cannot repair cloud infrastructure—you must re-provision it.

questions.questionText: Your NC2 cluster is running out of storage capacity. What is the appropriate scaling approach?

options.text: Replace existing nodes with larger instances
Attach additional EBS volumes
Add new nodes to the cluster
Use S3 for additional capacity

questions.explanation: NC2 storage is scaled by adding new nodes to the cluster. You cannot expand per-node storage because i3.metal instances have fixed NVMe capacity. Horizontal scaling (adding nodes) is the only option.

questions.questionText: You are validating IAM permissions for NC2 on AWS and encounter "UnauthorizedOperation" errors. Which AWS service most likely needs additional permissions?

options.text: S3 only
EC2 and S3
CloudWatch only
RDS

questions.explanation: NC2 on AWS requires IAM permissions for EC2 (instance management, security group modifications) and S3 (metadata backup storage). Missing permissions to either service will cause deployment or operational failures.

questions.questionText: You need to backup metadata for your NC2 cluster on AWS. Where should this backup be stored?

options.text: EBS volumes
AWS S3
Local NVMe
AWS Glacier

questions.explanation: AWS S3 is the designated location for NC2 metadata backups on AWS. While cluster data uses local NVMe, metadata backups must be stored in S3 for disaster recovery and external redundancy.

questions.questionText: You are planning a Nutanix Move migration of 100 VMs from on-premises to NC2 on Azure. What is the recommended approach for large migrations?

options.text: Migrate all VMs simultaneously
Migrate in batches with validation between batches
Use Leap for all migrations
Migrate only small VMs first

questions.explanation: Large migrations should be done in batches with validation between each batch. This approach reduces risk, allows for performance monitoring, and enables issue identification before migrating all workloads.

questions.questionText: An NC2 cluster on AWS needs to communicate securely with an on-premises data center. Which Direct Connect configuration is recommended?

options.text: Public Virtual Interface (PublicVIF) only
Private Virtual Interface (PrivateVIF) with encryption
Both public and private VIFs
No Direct Connect is needed

questions.explanation: Private Virtual Interface (PrivateVIF) is used for on-premises to AWS NC2 communication. Adding site-to-site encryption provides additional security for hybrid connectivity.

questions.questionText: You notice high latency in your NC2 cluster on AWS. All nodes are properly configured and in the same AZ. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: Nodes are in different AZs
Security groups are blocking traffic on required ports
NVMe performance degradation
VPC subnet is too small

questions.explanation: High latency with proper node placement typically indicates network issues, most commonly missing security group rules for inter-node communication (ports 9440, 2049, 3260).

questions.questionText: Your organization wants to run test workloads on NC2 during business hours only, then suspend them at night. What cost optimization feature should you use?

options.text: Reserved Instances
Node hibernation
On-Demand instances only
Spot instances

questions.explanation: Node hibernation allows you to suspend NC2 nodes during off-peak hours, reducing cloud costs while maintaining the ability to resume quickly. This is ideal for dev/test environments with predictable usage patterns.

questions.questionText: You are comparing costs between Reserved Instances and On-Demand instances for a 3-year NC2 deployment. Which offers the best cost savings?

options.text: On-Demand only
Reserved Instances with 3-year commitment
Mix of Reserved and On-Demand
Spot instances

questions.explanation: Reserved Instances with a 3-year upfront commitment provide significant cost savings (typically 40-50%) compared to On-Demand pricing for long-term, predictable workloads.

questions.questionText: Flow microsegmentation has been enabled on your NC2 cluster. What does this enable?

options.text: Automatic VM replication
Policy-based VM-to-VM network segmentation and access control
Storage tiering to cloud providers
Automatic backup to cloud

questions.explanation: Flow provides microsegmentation on NC2, enabling policy-based network access control between VMs. This provides zero-trust security by limiting traffic to only explicitly allowed connections.

questions.questionText: You are designing NC2 on AWS with a 3-node cluster. Each node requires a unique IP address. What VPC subnet size calculation is correct?

options.text: /30 (2 usable IPs)
/27 (30 usable IPs)
/25 (123 usable IPs on AWS)
/24 (254 usable IPs)

questions.explanation: A /25 subnet provides 128 total addresses. AWS reserves 5 addresses (network, broadcast, AWS router, AWS DNS, AWS future use), leaving 123 usable IP addresses. This is sufficient for a 3-node cluster (3 IPs), Flow Gateway instances, NAT Gateways, and management infrastructure with room for growth and operational requirements.

questions.questionText: You are configuring Azure Network Security Groups (NSGs) for an NC2 BareMetal cluster. What ports must be allowed for proper cluster functionality?

options.text: Only 443 for HTTPS
9440 (management), 2049 (NFS), 3260 (iSCSI)
All ports from 1024-65535
22 (SSH) only

questions.explanation: NSGs for NC2 on Azure must allow the same critical ports as AWS security groups: 9440 (Prism UI), 2049 (NFS for storage), and 3260 (iSCSI for block storage).

questions.questionText: Your NC2 deployment on AWS spans multiple VPCs for security isolation. How should you connect these VPCs to the NC2 cluster?

options.text: VPC peering only
Transit Gateway with proper routing and security policies
NAT Gateway only
Direct Connect only

questions.explanation: AWS Transit Gateway is the recommended approach for multi-VPC connectivity to NC2, providing centralized routing, security policy enforcement, and scalability without direct VPC-to-VPC peering complexity.

questions.questionText: You need to enable Prism Central to manage an NC2 cluster across the internet with security. What is the recommended architecture?

options.text: Direct internet connection to Prism Central
Prism Central with VPN tunnel or private connectivity via Direct Connect/ExpressRoute
Prism Central public IP only
No encryption needed for management traffic

questions.explanation: Prism Central should be connected to NC2 via secure, private connectivity (VPN tunnel or Direct Connect/ExpressRoute) to protect management traffic. Internet-only connections are less secure and higher latency.

questions.questionText: An NC2 cluster on AWS experiences a node failure. The cluster is 3 nodes total. What is the status after one node failure?

options.text: Cluster continues operating at reduced capacity
Cluster immediately shuts down
Automatic failover to Azure
Cluster is unavailable until node is repaired

questions.explanation: A 3-node cluster can lose one node and continue operating (the remaining 2 nodes maintain quorum for metadata services). However, you should destroy and replace the failed node promptly to restore fault tolerance.

questions.questionText: You want to implement automatic VM failover from on-premises to NC2 during a disaster. What Nutanix feature provides this capability?

options.text: Prism Central only
Leap with continuous replication and automated failover policies
Snapshots only
Manual Calm blueprints

questions.explanation: Leap enables continuous replication and supports automated failover policies based on RPO/RTO requirements, enabling automatic failover from on-premises to NC2 during unplanned failures.

questions.questionText: You have deployed NC2 on both AWS and Azure using the same Prism Central instance. How are policies maintained across both environments?

options.text: Policies must be manually synced
Only AWS policies are supported
Policies are automatically consistent across all registered environments
Each environment requires separate policy definitions

questions.explanation: Prism Central maintains consistent policies across all registered environments including both AWS and Azure NC2 clusters, ensuring unified security and resource management policies.

questions.questionText: Your NC2 cluster requires encrypted communication with on-premises systems. What is the encryption approach for Direct Connect connections?

options.text: No encryption available
AWS-provided encryption with VPN overlay
TLS encryption at application layer only
IPSec VPN tunnel over Direct Connect

questions.explanation: IPSec VPN tunnels can be configured over Direct Connect connections for encrypted communication. This provides encryption at the network layer while leveraging Direct Connect's dedicated connection.

questions.questionText: You are troubleshooting an NC2 deployment on Azure that fails during initial provisioning. The error states "Insufficient BareMetal capacity." What should you attempt first?

options.text: Increase the subnet size
Retry in the same region after 15 minutes
Change Azure region or wait for capacity
Modify the Prism Central settings

questions.explanation: "Insufficient BareMetal capacity" means Azure currently has no available BareMetal instances in that region. Retry later, change to a different region, or contact Azure Support to understand availability.

questions.questionText: An NC2 cluster is configured with Foundation image backup. How should metadata backups be validated on AWS?

options.text: No validation is needed
Verify S3 bucket access permissions and backup file integrity
Only check backup file names
Validate backups automatically occur

questions.explanation: Metadata backup validation requires verifying S3 bucket access permissions (IAM roles grant access) and backup file integrity to ensure disaster recovery capability. Regular validation tests should be performed.

questions.questionText: You are planning NC2 on AWS and need to calculate the subnet IP allocation. For a 3-node cluster with 4 Flow Gateway instances and additional management IPs, what is the minimum subnet?

options.text: /25 (123 usable IPs on AWS)

questions.explanation: A /25 subnet provides 128 total addresses. After AWS reserves 5 addresses, 123 usable IPs remain, which is the Nutanix-documented minimum for NC2 on AWS. This accommodates cluster nodes (3), Flow Gateways (4+), management infrastructure, and DHCP/routing overhead.

questions.questionText: You want to replicate VMs continuously between on-premises and NC2 using Leap. What metric defines the maximum acceptable data loss in case of disaster?

options.text: RTO (Recovery Time Objective)
RPO (Recovery Point Objective)
MTR (Mean Time to Recover)
MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)

questions.explanation: RPO (Recovery Point Objective) defines the maximum acceptable data loss—the point in time to which data can be recovered. Leap replication frequency determines RPO.

questions.questionText: You are designing a hybrid cloud architecture with on-premises Nutanix and NC2 on AWS. What connectivity approach provides the lowest latency?

options.text: Public internet only
AWS Direct Connect
AWS VPN only
Combination of VPN and S3

questions.explanation: AWS Direct Connect provides dedicated, private network connectivity with consistent, low-latency performance compared to public internet connections. This is essential for replication and hybrid workloads.

questions.questionText: A user reports that their VM on NC2 cannot reach the on-premises database. You verify the VM is powered on and has network connectivity. What should you check next?

options.text: VM RAM allocation
DNS resolution and security group/NSG rules for database ports
Storage capacity
Hypervisor version

questions.explanation: Network connectivity issues should be diagnosed by checking: DNS resolution (can the VM resolve the database hostname?), and security group/NSG rules (are database ports allowed in both directions?).

questions.questionText: Your NC2 cluster metadata backup fails silently (backup script runs but produces no output). Where should you check for error logs?

options.text: AWS CloudWatch only
S3 bucket for backup files
NC2 cluster logs (via Prism) and S3 bucket verification
Local node logs only

questions.explanation: Diagnose backup failures by checking: NC2 cluster logs accessible through Prism Central, and verifying S3 bucket contains recent backup files. Both sources provide diagnostic information.

questions.questionText: You are implementing business continuity for an NC2 cluster on Azure. What is the minimum number of Flow Gateway VMs needed for redundancy?

options.text: 1 Flow Gateway VM
2 Flow Gateway VMs
4 Flow Gateway VMs
The minimum depends on ECMP configuration

questions.explanation: Minimum 2 Flow Gateway VMs provide redundancy on Azure NC2. While ECMP supports up to 4 for load balancing, 2 is the minimum for high availability.

questions.questionText: During NC2 migration, you want to avoid impacting production workloads on-premises. What Nutanix feature allows you to test failover without affecting production?

options.text: Live migration only
Test failover to an isolated network
Snapshot replication
Continuous backup only

questions.explanation: Nutanix Leap and Move support test failover to an isolated test network (separate VLAN/subnet), allowing validation without production impact. This enables safe failover testing before actual cutover.

questions.questionText: Your NC2 deployment requires redundant management paths. You have deployed Prism Central both on-premises and replicated to NC2. How are these instances synchronized?

options.text: Manual sync required
Continuous replication via Leap
Database replication via Nutanix support
Not synchronized—use separate instances

questions.explanation: Nutanix Leap can replicate the entire Prism Central VM from on-premises to NC2, ensuring synchronized management infrastructure and enabling failover if needed.

questions.questionText: You receive an alert that the NC2 cluster on AWS is experiencing high CPU utilization. All nodes show similar utilization. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: Insufficient memory
Network bandwidth saturation
High workload demand requiring cluster scaling
Storage controller failure

questions.explanation: Consistently high CPU utilization across all nodes typically indicates high demand from running workloads. This is resolved by horizontal scaling (adding nodes) or optimizing workload distribution.

questions.questionText: You are planning a DR test failover from on-premises to NC2 on Azure. The test failover is scheduled for 2 AM on Sunday. What networking requirement allows isolated test traffic?

options.text: Test network must use different NSGs than production
Test network must be on a separate subnet with no production traffic
Test network requires separate NC2 cluster
Direct internet access for test traffic

questions.explanation: Test failover networks must be isolated on separate subnets with dedicated NSGs (Azure) or security groups (AWS). This prevents test traffic from reaching production infrastructure or databases.

questions.questionText: A critical application on NC2 requires <15 minute recovery time (RTO). You need to establish replication from on-premises. What configuration ensures this RTO?

options.text: Daily snapshots only
Hourly snapshots
Continuous replication via Leap with frequent RPO intervals
Weekly backups

questions.explanation: Achieving <15 min RTO requires continuous replication (Leap) so recovery points are recent and failover can execute rapidly. RPO (Recovery Point Objective—maximum acceptable data loss) and RTO (Recovery Time Objective—maximum acceptable recovery time) are independent metrics. Both require frequent replication to maintain low values; a low RPO does not automatically guarantee low RTO if failover execution is slow. Continuous Leap replication enables both metrics to be achieved.

questions.questionText: Your organization is migrating a Tier-1 application to NC2 on AWS. Which two components must be configured correctly for the migration to succeed? (Choose two.)

options.text: Application code modifications
AWS IAM roles with EC2 and S3 permissions
VPC /25 subnet with same AZ placement
AWS Reserved Instances purchase
Prism Central network connectivity

questions.explanation: Success requires: B) IAM roles with appropriate permissions (EC2, S3, security groups), and C) VPC /25 subnet minimum in the same AZ for all cluster nodes. Application code typically requires no changes; Reserved Instances are optional for cost; Prism Central is beneficial but not mandatory for basic deployment.

questions.questionText: When establishing hybrid cloud connectivity between on-premises and NC2 on AWS, which two connectivity options are recommended for production deployments? (Choose two.)

options.text: Public internet VPN only
AWS Direct Connect
AWS Transit Gateway for multi-VPC routing
NAT Gateway for all traffic
S3 endpoint as primary connectivity

questions.explanation: Production deployments should use: B) AWS Direct Connect for dedicated, low-latency connectivity, and C) AWS Transit Gateway for centralized routing and security. Public internet VPN lacks performance guarantees; NAT Gateway is for specific use cases; S3 is for data, not primary connectivity.

questions.questionText: You are troubleshooting deployment failures on NC2 across AWS and Azure. Which two types of errors are most critical to resolve during initial deployment? (Choose two.)

options.text: Non-critical VM naming issues
Insufficient cloud infrastructure quotas (EC2 capacity/BareMetal availability)
Missing IAM permissions or Azure RBAC assignments
Optional Prism Central connectivity delays
Cosmetic UI display issues

questions.explanation: Critical deployment failures result from: B) Insufficient infrastructure quotas (quota increases needed), and C) Missing IAM/RBAC permissions (prevents instance creation and operations). Other items are non-blocking or cosmetic.

questions.questionText: Your NC2 environment on Azure uses Flow for microsegmentation. Which two components are required for Flow to function on Azure BareMetal? (Choose two.)

options.text: Azure Load Balancer
Flow Gateway VMs with BGP and Azure Route Server
Azure Kubernetes Service
Delegated subnet for BareMetal infrastructure
Public IP addresses for all VMs

questions.explanation: Flow on Azure requires: B) Flow Gateway VMs configured with BGP to communicate with Azure Route Server for dynamic routing, and D) Delegated subnet properly configured for BareMetal. Load Balancer is optional; AKS is unrelated; public IPs are not required.

questions.questionText: You are planning disaster recovery for NC2 with Leap replication. Which two configuration items are essential for both planned and unplanned failover scenarios? (Choose two.)

options.text: Daily snapshot schedule only
Continuous replication with configured RPO/RTO
Separate isolated test network for failover testing
AWS Reserved Instances on DR site
Reverse replication capability for failback

questions.explanation: Essential for failover: B) Continuous replication with appropriate RPO/RTO settings to minimize data loss and recovery time, and E) Reverse replication to enable failback to on-premises if needed. Test networks are useful but not essential; Reserved Instances are optional; snapshots alone are insufficient.

questions.questionText: You are configuring security for an NC2 cluster on AWS with multi-environment access. Which two security controls must be implemented? (Choose two.)

options.text: Security groups allowing ports 9440, 2049, 3260
IAM roles limiting permissions to required services only
Public internet access for all management traffic
Encryption for on-premises to NC2 connectivity via VPN/Direct Connect
Elimination of all password-based authentication

questions.explanation: Required security controls: A) Security groups with explicit port rules (9440, 2049, 3260) for cluster communication, and B) IAM roles following least-privilege principle (EC2, S3, security groups only). Public internet access weakens security; encryption is important but depends on connectivity type; password elimination is a best practice but not mandatory.

questions.questionText: You are migrating a database VM from on-premises to NC2 on Azure using Nutanix Move. Which two validation steps must be completed before production cutover? (Choose two.)

options.text: Verify VM CPU utilization is below 50%
Perform test failover in isolated network and validate database functionality
Confirm application connectivity and database replication status
Verify AWS region selection
Confirm all VMs are created on day 1

questions.explanation: Pre-cutover validation: B) Test failover in isolated network to validate functionality without production impact, and C) Confirm application connectivity (can apps reach the migrated DB?) and replication is healthy. CPU utilization is not a pre-cutover requirement; AWS region is irrelevant for Azure migration; VM creation timing is not a validation criterion.

questions.questionText: Your NC2 deployment requires cost optimization while maintaining performance. Which two approaches align with NC2 cost management best practices? (Choose two.)

options.text: Reserved Instances for predictable, long-term workloads
Node hibernation for dev/test environments during off-peak hours
Conversion to smaller instance types
Elimination of all security groups to reduce overhead
Maximum node count regardless of demand

questions.explanation: Cost optimization: A) Reserved Instances for production workloads with predictable utilization (40-50% savings), and B) Node hibernation for dev/test to reduce cloud spending during off-hours. Smaller instances reduce performance; security group elimination weakens security; over-provisioning increases costs.

questions.questionText: You are setting up Prism Central to manage NC2 clusters across AWS and Azure regions. Which two capabilities does Prism Central provide in this hybrid environment? (Choose two.)

options.text: Unified monitoring, alerting, and policy enforcement across environments
Automatic datastore capacity management
Consistent categorization for resource organization and billing
Automatic VM replication between AWS and Azure
Elimination of need for cloud-specific networking

questions.explanation: Prism Central provides: A) Unified monitoring, alerting, and policy enforcement across registered environments (both AWS and Azure), and C) Consistent categories that work across all environments for resource tracking and billing. Datastore management is limited to storage; automatic replication requires Leap (separate tool); cloud networking still required.

questions.questionText: During an NC2 cluster node failure, which two actions are necessary for proper recovery? (Choose two.)

options.text: Attempt to repair the node using Nutanix repair tools
Destroy the failed cloud instance
Provision a new instance to replace it
Wait 24 hours for automatic recovery
Migrate workloads to different cloud region

questions.explanation: Proper recovery requires: B) Destroy the failed cloud instance (repair not possible for cloud infrastructure), and C) Provision a new instance to re-add to the cluster. Repair is not an option; automatic recovery doesn't occur; immediate action is required; migration is unnecessary if the rest of the cluster is healthy.

questions.questionText:
What is the correct order of steps to deploy an NC2 cluster on AWS from scratch?
1. Create VPC with /25 subnet in desired AZ
2. Configure IAM roles with EC2 and S3 permissions
3. Request EC2 quota for i3.metal instances
4. Access my.nutanix.com and initiate cluster deployment
5. Configure security groups for ports 9440, 2049, 3260
6. Wait for instances to launch and cluster initialization to complete
questions.explanation: Correct sequence: 1) Request EC2 quota first to ensure availability, 2) Create VPC/subnet infrastructure, 3) Configure IAM permissions (required before deployment), 4) Configure security groups, 5) Initiate deployment via my.nutanix.com, 6) Monitor and wait for completion. This sequence ensures prerequisites are met before attempting deployment.

questions.questionText:
