What is the proper sequence for migrating a production VM from on-premises to NC2 on AWS using Nutanix Move?
1. Establish continuous replication from source to NC2
2. Configure test isolated network for failover testing
3. Perform test failover and validate functionality
4. Verify source and destination networks are connected
5. Execute planned failover during maintenance window
6. Validate application functionality and connectivity on NC2
7. Optionally configure reverse replication for failback
questions.explanation: Correct sequence: 1) Verify network connectivity first, 2) Establish replication, 3) Set up test network, 4) Test failover (validate without production impact), 5) Execute planned failover in maintenance window, 6) Validate production functionality, 7) Configure reverse replication. Pre-cutover validation is critical before production failover.

questions.questionText:
What is the correct order of configuration steps for NC2 on Azure BareMetal?
1. Create Azure delegated subnet (/24 minimum)
2. Deploy BareMetal instances via Nutanix provisioning
3. Configure Flow Gateway VMs with BGP
4. Connect Azure Route Server for dynamic routing
5. Configure NSGs for cluster communication ports
6. Access my.nutanix.com to complete cluster setup
questions.explanation: Correct sequence: 1) Create delegated subnet infrastructure, 2) Deploy BareMetal instances, 3) Configure NSGs, 4) Deploy and configure Flow Gateways with BGP, 5) Connect Route Server (this enables routing updates), 6) Complete cluster setup via my.nutanix.com. Infrastructure prerequisites must be in place before instance deployment.

questions.questionText:
What is the proper order of establishing hybrid cloud disaster recovery between on-premises Nutanix and NC2?
1. Deploy Prism Central on-premises (if not already present)
2. Establish network connectivity (Direct Connect/VPN/ExpressRoute)
3. Register NC2 cluster with Prism Central
4. Configure Leap replication for critical VMs
5. Set RPO/RTO policies and replication frequency
6. Perform test failover to NC2 in isolated network
7. Document failover procedures and runbooks
questions.explanation: Correct sequence: 1) Ensure Prism Central exists, 2) Establish network connectivity (prerequisite for management), 3) Register clusters in Prism Central, 4) Enable Leap replication, 5) Configure RPO/RTO and replication schedule, 6) Test failover to validate, 7) Document procedures. Each step depends on previous steps being complete.

questions.questionText:
What is the correct order of steps when a node fails in a production NC2 cluster?
1. Verify remaining cluster is stable and healthy
2. Destroy the failed cloud instance
3. Receive alert about node failure
4. Provision new cloud instance to NC2 specifications
5. Re-add new node to cluster via Prism
6. Monitor cluster resynchronization and data rebalancing
7. Verify all cluster services are healthy and operational
questions.explanation: Correct sequence: 1) Receive alert, 2) Verify remaining cluster is stable, 3) Destroy failed instance (no repair possible), 4) Provision replacement, 5) Re-add to cluster, 6) Monitor recovery and rebalancing, 7) Verify health. Destroying failed node before verifying cluster stability could cause quorum loss.

questions.questionText:
What is the proper sequence for scaling NC2 storage capacity?
1. Assess current storage utilization and growth projections
2. Add new nodes to the cluster via my.nutanix.com
3. Monitor rebalancing and storage distribution
4. Request cloud infrastructure quota if needed
5. Verify application performance remains acceptable
6. Document capacity planning and scaling events
questions.explanation: Correct sequence: 1) Assess requirements, 2) Request quota if needed (ensure cloud capacity), 3) Add nodes via my.nutanix.com, 4) Monitor rebalancing, 5) Verify performance, 6) Document event. Quota request must occur before adding nodes to ensure instance provisioning succeeds.

questions.questionText:
What is the correct order for enabling Flow microsegmentation on a production NC2 cluster?
1. Enable Flow microsegmentation in Prism Central
2. Plan VM categorization and traffic policies
3. Create categories and traffic flow rules
4. Define ingress/egress policies for critical applications
5. Test policies in non-critical VMs first
6. Apply policies to production VMs gradually
7. Monitor and alert on policy violations
questions.explanation: Correct sequence: 1) Plan categorization and policies first, 2) Enable Flow in Prism Central, 3) Create categories and rules, 4) Define application policies, 5) Test on non-critical VMs (validate before production), 6) Roll out to production gradually, 7) Monitor for issues. Planning before implementation prevents production disruptions.

questions.questionText:
What is the proper sequence for troubleshooting NC2 deployment failures?
1. Collect error messages and logs from deployment console
2. Verify prerequisites (VPC/subnet, IAM roles, quotas, security groups)
3. Check Nutanix deployment documentation for error codes
4. Contact AWS/Azure support if infrastructure-level issues exist
5. Attempt deployment retry after fixing identified issues
6. Escalate to Nutanix support if persistent failures occur
questions.explanation: Correct sequence: 1) Verify all prerequisites first (quick checks often reveal issues), 2) Collect error messages and logs, 3) Reference error codes in documentation, 4) Contact cloud provider for infrastructure issues, 5) Retry deployment, 6) Escalate to Nutanix if still failing. This sequence eliminates common causes efficiently before escalation.

questions.questionText:
What is the correct order for establishing Prism Central connectivity with NC2 on AWS using secure networking?
1. Establish AWS Direct Connect or VPN tunnel to AWS account
2. Configure security groups on NC2 cluster for Prism Central access (port 9440)
3. Configure Prism Central network policies to allow NC2 cluster access
4. Register NC2 cluster in Prism Central using cluster IP address
5. Validate connectivity by checking cluster health in Prism Central
6. Configure SSL certificates if using custom domains
questions.explanation: Correct sequence: 1) Establish network connectivity first (prerequisite), 2) Configure cluster security group (enable Prism port 9440), 3) Configure Prism Central policies, 4) Register cluster, 5) Validate connectivity, 6) Configure SSL/certs. Network connectivity must exist before any management registration.

questions.questionText:
What is the proper order for implementing metadata backup automation for NC2 on AWS?
1. Verify S3 bucket exists and IAM role has access permissions
2. Configure backup schedule in cluster settings (daily/weekly frequency)
3. Create S3 bucket with appropriate retention policies
4. Verify initial backup completes successfully
5. Monitor backup logs and set alerts for backup failures
6. Test metadata restore process from backup
questions.explanation: Correct sequence: 1) Create S3 bucket with retention policies first, 2) Verify bucket access via IAM, 3) Configure backup schedule, 4) Verify initial backup succeeds, 5) Set monitoring and alerts, 6) Test restore capability. Infrastructure (bucket) must exist before configuring backups.

options.text: Deploy SD-WAN virtual appliances directly on the NC2 cluster as AHV VMs and peer with the AWS VPC
Terminate the SD-WAN overlay on an SD-WAN virtual appliance in the AWS VPC and route traffic to the NC2 host subnet via VPC routing
Use AWS Site-to-Site VPN with SD-WAN-aware routing policies injected via BGP into the VPC route table
Connect the SD-WAN controller directly to Prism Central to orchestrate tunnel endpoints on CVM interfaces

questions.explanation: SD-WAN appliances for NC2 on AWS are deployed as EC2 instances (or virtual appliances) within the same VPC, terminating the SD-WAN overlay. Traffic is then routed to the NC2 host subnet using standard VPC route table entries. NC2 nodes themselves do not host SD-WAN appliances directly on the bare-metal infrastructure.

options.text: An AWS Transit Gateway with a Megaport attachment
An AWS Direct Connect hosted connection or hosted VIF at a Megaport-enabled facility
An AWS PrivateLink endpoint service pointing to the NC2 management subnet
An AWS VPN CloudHub endpoint configured for Megaport BGP peering

questions.explanation: Megaport provides Layer 2 connectivity to AWS via hosted connections or hosted Virtual Interfaces (VIFs) at Direct Connect locations. The customer provisions a Megaport Virtual Cross Connect (VXC) that terminates as an AWS Direct Connect hosted connection, which can then be associated with a Direct Connect Gateway or Virtual Private Gateway attached to the NC2 VPC.

options.text: An S3 Interface Endpoint (AWS PrivateLink) in the NC2 subnet with a security group allowing port 443
A VPC Gateway Endpoint for S3 associated with the route table of the NC2 subnet
An S3 Transfer Acceleration endpoint with VPC-restricted bucket policies
An AWS NAT Gateway with an S3-specific elastic IP allowlist

questions.explanation: A VPC Gateway Endpoint for S3 provides private, free-of-charge connectivity from the VPC to S3 without requiring traffic to leave the AWS network. Gateway Endpoints are added as route table entries (prefix list targets) and do not require security group configuration. Interface Endpoints (PrivateLink) also work for S3 but incur additional hourly and data processing charges and are not the standard recommendation.

options.text: In Prism Central under Settings > API Keys
In the NC2 Console (my.nutanix.com) under the user's Profile > API Keys section
In the AWS IAM Console as a Nutanix service-linked access key
In Prism Element under the cluster's REST API configuration page

questions.explanation: NC2 API keys are created and managed through the NC2 Console (my.nutanix.com) under the user profile's API Keys section. These keys authenticate programmatic access to the NC2 management plane for operations like cluster provisioning, scaling, and lifecycle management. They are separate from AWS IAM credentials and Prism Central credentials.

options.text: `az role definition create --role-definition nc2-custom-role.json`
`az ad sp create-for-rbac --name NC2ServicePrincipal --role Contributor`
`az policy assignment create --policy nc2-permissions --scope /subscriptions/{sub-id}`
`az resource lock create --lock-type CanNotDelete --resource-group nc2-rg`

questions.explanation: Custom Azure roles for NC2 are created using `az role definition create` with a JSON definition file that specifies the exact permissions (actions/notActions) required by NC2 — such as Microsoft.Compute, Microsoft.Network, Microsoft.BareMetal, and Microsoft.Storage operations. Option B creates a service principal with the broad built-in Contributor role rather than a scoped custom role.

options.text: Hourly gateway charge only — all data transfer is included
Hourly gateway charge plus egress data transfer charges per GB leaving Azure
Per-tunnel charge only — no hourly gateway fee applies
A flat monthly fee with unlimited bandwidth and tunnels

questions.explanation: Azure VPN Gateway pricing has two components: an hourly charge based on the gateway SKU (Basic, VpnGw1–VpnGw5, etc.) and standard Azure egress data transfer charges for traffic leaving the Azure region. Ingress data is free. Higher SKUs provide more bandwidth and tunnel capacity but at increased hourly cost. This makes VPN Gateway more expensive for high-throughput NC2 workloads compared to ExpressRoute at scale.

options.text: NC2 uses default (shared) tenancy because i3.metal instances are already physically isolated bare-metal hosts
NC2 requires dedicated tenancy to ensure hypervisor-level isolation from other AWS customers
NC2 must use a Dedicated Host to enable per-socket licensing for the Nutanix software
NC2 requires a placement group with partition strategy, and tenancy type is irrelevant

questions.explanation: NC2 on AWS uses i3.metal (or i3en.metal) bare-metal instances, which provide an entire physical server to the customer regardless of the VPC tenancy setting. Since bare-metal instances have no AWS hypervisor, setting dedicated tenancy provides no additional isolation benefit. Default tenancy is used, avoiding the premium pricing associated with dedicated tenancy.

options.text: AmazonEC2FullAccess
AmazonEC2ReadOnlyAccess
A Nutanix-provided custom IAM policy document — no AWS managed policy covers NC2 requirements
PowerUserAccess

questions.explanation: Nutanix provides a specific IAM policy document (available in the NC2 deployment guide) that defines the minimum required permissions for EC2, S3, VPC, and EBS operations. No single AWS managed policy precisely matches NC2's requirements. Using AmazonEC2FullAccess or PowerUserAccess would grant excessive permissions, violating the principle of least privilege.

options.text: The RSYSLOG module under Prism Central Settings > Syslog Configuration
The SMTP module under Prism Central Settings > Alert Notifications
The Nutanix Collector agent deployed as a sidecar VM on each host
The PC Syslog module under Prism Element Settings > Remote Syslog Server with IP, port, transport protocol, and severity level

questions.explanation: Remote syslog forwarding is configured per cluster in Prism Element under Settings > Remote Syslog Server (or via nCLI). The administrator specifies the syslog server IP, port, transport protocol (UDP/TCP/TLS), log severity level, and optional module selection. This applies identically to NC2 clusters as to on-premises clusters since AOS provides the same syslog capabilities regardless of deployment location.

options.text: LOG_DEBUG (level 7)
LOG_INFO (level 6)
LOG_WARNING (level 4)
LOG_CRIT (level 2)

questions.explanation: Setting the severity to LOG_WARNING (level 4) captures warning, error, critical, alert, and emergency messages (levels 4–0) while filtering out informational and debug messages. This provides operationally relevant data for monitoring NC2 clusters without overwhelming the syslog server with verbose debug or informational logs.

options.text: Install the Datadog Agent directly on each CVM and AHV host in the NC2 cluster
Deploy a Datadog Agent on a VM within the NC2 cluster that collects metrics from the Prism APIs and forwards them to Datadog
Configure Datadog's native Nutanix integration which uses SNMP polling against the cluster VIP
Export metrics via NC2 Console's built-in Datadog connector in the Integrations tab

questions.explanation: The recommended approach is to deploy a Datadog Agent on a guest VM running on the NC2 cluster (or in the same VPC) that queries the Prism Element or Prism Central REST APIs (v2/v3) to collect cluster, host, VM, and storage metrics. Direct CVM/AHV agent installation is not supported on NC2. The agent translates Prism metrics into Datadog's format for dashboarding and alerting.

options.text: In the NC2 Console under Cluster Settings > Email Configuration
In Prism Element under Settings > SMTP Server, specifying the relay host, port, sender address, and optional authentication
Via the AHV host command line using `smtp-config` on each node
In AWS SES or Azure Communication Services, which NC2 natively integrates with for alerts

questions.explanation: SMTP configuration for NC2 clusters is done through Prism Element under Settings > SMTP Server, identical to on-premises clusters. The administrator provides the SMTP relay hostname/IP, port (25/587), security mode (STARTTLS), sender email address, and optional credentials. Alert policies in Prism then reference this SMTP configuration to deliver email notifications for cluster events.

options.text: Deploy the HYCU backup controller as an AHV VM on the NC2 cluster and use S3 as the backup target
Deploy HYCU as a Lambda function triggered by Prism alert webhooks to snapshot VMs to EBS
Install the HYCU agent on each CVM to perform block-level backups to EFS
HYCU is not supported on NC2 — only Nutanix Mine and built-in snapshots are available

questions.explanation: HYCU deploys as a virtual appliance (controller VM) on the NC2 cluster running on AHV. It leverages Nutanix Changed Block Tracking (CBT) via Prism APIs for efficient incremental backups and can target AWS S3 (or S3-compatible storage) as the backup repository. This architecture maintains data locality and uses NC2's standard AHV snapshot capabilities.

options.text: Automatic VM-level anti-affinity rules across hosts to survive single-node failures
Automated backup of cluster configuration and metadata to cloud object storage, enabling cluster rebuild/recovery
Live migration of an entire cluster between AWS regions during a regional outage
Encryption of all data at rest on NVMe drives using AWS KMS-managed keys

questions.explanation: Cluster Protect automatically backs up critical cluster configuration data (including VM definitions, network configurations, storage policies, and cluster settings) to cloud object storage (S3 for AWS, Blob Storage for Azure). In a disaster scenario, this backup enables rapid cluster reconstruction and workload recovery without requiring manual reconfiguration.

options.text: Microsoft.Compute only
Microsoft.BareMetalInfrastructure
Microsoft.HybridCompute
Microsoft.VMwareCloudSimple

questions.explanation: The `Microsoft.BareMetalInfrastructure` resource provider must be registered on the Azure subscription before NC2 deployment. This provider enables Azure to provision and manage the dedicated bare-metal hosts that NC2 requires. Without this registration, the NC2 deployment wizard will fail when attempting to allocate physical infrastructure. Additional providers like Microsoft.Compute and Microsoft.Network are typically registered by default.

options.text: NAT mode — translates VM IPs to the Azure VNet address space
noNAT mode — VM IPs are directly routable via Flow Gateway without address translation
L2 stretch mode — extends the on-premises Layer 2 broadcast domain into Azure
Bridge mode — creates a transparent bridge between the AHV internal switch and the Azure VNet

questions.explanation: In noNAT mode, VM traffic passes through the Flow Gateway without IP address translation, making VM IPs directly routable within the Azure VNet and to connected networks. This requires proper route table entries pointing VM subnets to the Flow Gateway. noNAT is the recommended mode for most production deployments as it simplifies troubleshooting and allows direct IP-based communication with Azure-native services.

options.text: When VMs need to maintain Layer 2 adjacency with on-premises hosts
When there are IP address conflicts between the NC2 VM subnets and existing Azure VNet address spaces
When more than 4 Flow Gateway instances are needed for bandwidth scaling
When using Azure ExpressRoute instead of VPN for hybrid connectivity

questions.explanation: NAT mode is appropriate when there are overlapping IP address ranges between NC2 VM subnets and existing Azure or on-premises networks. NAT translates the NC2 VM addresses to the Azure VNet-routable address space, resolving conflicts. This is common in migration scenarios where workloads retain their original on-premises IP addresses that may overlap with Azure address allocations.

options.text: Azure VNet Gateway with GRE tunnel encapsulation
Flow Gateway on the NC2 cluster, establishing a VXLAN tunnel to the on-premises Nutanix cluster
Azure Load Balancer with MAC-based forwarding rules
Nutanix Move agent running as an Azure VM in the same subnet

questions.explanation: Flow Gateway provides the L2 stretch capability by establishing VXLAN tunnels between the NC2 cluster on Azure and the on-premises Nutanix environment. These tunnels encapsulate Layer 2 Ethernet frames, preserving MAC addresses and enabling subnet extension. This is essential for live migration scenarios where VMs must retain their IP and MAC addresses during mobility.

options.text: The Flow Gateway VXLAN MTU exceeds the Azure VNet maximum, causing packet drops
The Azure VNet route table is missing a route pointing the stretched subnet to the Flow Gateway IP as the next hop
The NC2 cluster does not support simultaneous L2 stretch and VNet routing
AHV's OVS bridge is filtering Azure VNet-originated ARP replies

questions.explanation: In an L2 stretch scenario, Azure VNet resources need a route table entry directing traffic for the stretched subnet to the Flow Gateway's Azure-side IP as the next hop. Without this User Defined Route (UDR), Azure VNet traffic destined for the stretched subnet follows the default Azure routing, which has no path to the L2-extended network segment.

options.text: To provide a single highly available virtual IP that moves between Flow Gateway instances for gateway redundancy
To assign public Azure IPs directly to guest VMs without using Azure Load Balancer
To enable DHCP relay for guest VMs on overlay networks
To configure a loopback address on each CVM for Prism Central registration

questions.explanation: A Flow Gateway Floating IP is a shared virtual IP address that provides high availability across multiple Flow Gateway instances. If the active Flow Gateway fails, the Floating IP automatically migrates to a standby instance, ensuring uninterrupted traffic flow. This is similar to a VIP in traditional load balancer or clustering configurations.

options.text: It advertises NC2 VM subnet routes to Azure Route Server via BGP, enabling dynamic route injection into the Azure VNet
It exports Azure VNet routes to the on-premises network via ExpressRoute
It replicates Prism Central routing policies across multiple NC2 clusters
It synchronizes AWS VPC route tables with Azure VNet UDRs for multi-cloud deployments

questions.explanation: External Route Propagation (ERP) enables the Flow Gateway to advertise NC2 VM overlay network routes to Azure Route Server using BGP. This eliminates the need for manually configuring User Defined Routes (UDRs) in Azure route tables for each VM subnet. As VM networks are created or removed in NC2, the routes are dynamically propagated to the Azure networking fabric.

NC2 on Azure supports a maximum of 4 Flow Gateway instances in an ECMP configuration. These four instances distribute traffic across parallel paths to provide both redundancy and increased aggregate throughput. Azure Route Server is used to establish BGP sessions with each Flow Gateway instance to enable ECMP path distribution.

options.text: Increase the Azure VNet MTU to 9000 to enable jumbo frames through the Flow Gateway
Add additional Flow Gateway instances (up to 4 total) to scale aggregate throughput via ECMP
Replace the Flow Gateway with Azure Load Balancer Standard for higher throughput
Enable SR-IOV on the Flow Gateway network interfaces to bypass the virtual switch

questions.explanation: Scaling Flow Gateway throughput is achieved by adding more Flow Gateway instances, up to the maximum of 4 in ECMP mode. Each additional instance adds its bandwidth capacity to the aggregate, distributing flows across all active gateways. Azure Route Server propagates equal-cost routes for each gateway, and traffic is distributed per-flow across the available paths.

options.text: ExpressRoute Global Reach
ExpressRoute FastPath
ExpressRoute Direct
ExpressRoute Premium Add-on

questions.explanation: ExpressRoute FastPath bypasses the ExpressRoute virtual network gateway in the data path, sending traffic directly from the Microsoft Enterprise Edge (MSEE) routers to the VMs/resources in the VNet. This reduces latency by eliminating the gateway hop. FastPath requires the Ultra Performance or ErGw3AZ gateway SKU and is particularly beneficial for latency-sensitive NC2 workloads like database replication.

options.text: NC2 cluster hibernation — pauses the cluster by stopping bare-metal instances while preserving local NVMe data
NC2 cluster deletion with S3 metadata restore — delete the cluster nightly and recreate from backup each morning
EC2 Stop Instance — standard AWS instance stop/start preserves NVMe state
AWS Auto Scaling with scheduled scaling policies to terminate and relaunch nodes

questions.explanation: NC2 provides a cluster hibernation (pause) feature that gracefully stops all VMs, shuts down CVM services, and releases the bare-metal instances while preserving cluster configuration. When resumed, the cluster is reconstituted from the preserved state. This avoids bare-metal instance charges during idle periods. Note that local NVMe data is preserved through the hibernation process, and S3 metadata backup ensures recoverability.

options.text: All guest VM data is migrated to S3 before instance termination and restored on resume
Guest VM data remains on the local NVMe drives of the stopped bare-metal instances, and instances retain their storage allocation
Guest VM data is lost — VMs must be restored from backup after cluster resume
Guest VM data is replicated to EBS snapshots and the NVMe drives are released

questions.explanation: During NC2 hibernation on AWS, the bare-metal instances are stopped (not terminated), which preserves the local NVMe drive contents. When the cluster is resumed, the same physical hosts with their intact NVMe data are started, and AOS rebuilds the storage fabric. Cluster metadata in S3 provides an additional safety net for configuration recovery.

options.text: Deploy a new bare-metal instance manually via the AWS Console, install AHV, and join it to the cluster via Prism Element
Use the NC2 Console to initiate a node expansion, which provisions the new bare-metal instance and automatically joins it to the cluster
Create a second NC2 cluster with one node and use cluster merge to combine them
Modify the NC2 CloudFormation stack to increase the node count parameter and run a stack update

questions.explanation: Node expansion on NC2 is performed through the NC2 Console (or NC2 API), which orchestrates the end-to-end process: provisioning a new bare-metal instance in the same availability zone, imaging it with the matching AOS/AHV version, configuring networking, and joining it to the existing cluster. Manual instance provisioning is not supported because NC2 must manage the full node lifecycle.

options.text: Both clusters must run identical AOS versions and be registered to the same Prism Central instance (or paired Prism Central instances)
The on-premises cluster must be converted to run AHV on bare-metal AWS instances first
Leap requires a dedicated VPN tunnel using only IKEv1 with pre-shared keys
The NC2 cluster must have Nutanix Files deployed as a replication proxy

questions.explanation: Leap DR requires both the source and target clusters to be registered with Prism Central (either the same instance or paired instances connected via a network route). The clusters should run compatible AOS versions to ensure replication compatibility. Network connectivity (VPN, Direct Connect, or ExpressRoute) must exist between the sites for replication traffic, but Leap itself is configured and managed through Prism Central.

options.text: That the Leap recovery plan includes a network mapping that maps on-premises VLANs to NC2 overlay networks connected via Flow Gateway
That Azure Firewall is disabled during the test failover
That the on-premises source VMs were shut down before initiating the test
That the NC2 cluster has at least 50% free storage capacity

questions.explanation: Leap recovery plans include network mappings that define how source networks map to target networks. If VMs are recovered to NC2 but the network mapping does not correctly associate the on-premises VLAN/subnet with an NC2 overlay network that has Flow Gateway connectivity to the Azure VNet, the VMs will be isolated. Verifying and correcting the network mapping in the recovery plan is the first troubleshooting step.

options.text: Azure Network Security Groups (NSGs) applied to the NC2 delegated subnet
Nutanix Flow Network Security with Categories assigned to VMs in Prism Central
Azure Application Security Groups (ASGs) associated with VM NICs
Prism Element firewall rules configured per VM NIC in the VM settings

questions.explanation: Nutanix Flow Network Security (microsegmentation) uses Categories in Prism Central to classify VMs into logical groups (e.g., AppType:Web, Environment:Production). Security policies are then defined between categories to control east-west traffic. This works identically on NC2 as on-premises, providing consistent security policy management independent of the underlying cloud platform's native network security constructs.

options.text: Upgrade AHV first, then AOS, to ensure the hypervisor supports new AOS features
Upgrade AOS first, then AHV — AOS must be upgraded before the hypervisor to maintain compatibility
Both AOS and AHV must be upgraded simultaneously in a single combined upgrade operation
Upgrade Flow Gateway first, then AOS, then AHV to maintain network connectivity throughout

questions.explanation: The standard NC2 cluster upgrade sequence is AOS first, then AHV. AOS is the foundation that manages the storage fabric and cluster services, and newer AHV versions may depend on updated AOS capabilities. The upgrade is performed rolling (one node at a time), with VMs live-migrating off each node before it is upgraded, ensuring zero downtime for guest workloads.

options.text: Whether the Azure VNet DNS is pointing to a custom DNS server that cannot resolve Azure management endpoints
Whether the delegated subnet has a Network Security Group (NSG) blocking outbound HTTPS (443) traffic to Azure services
Whether the AHV hosts have the correct Azure IMDS (Instance Metadata Service) route configured
Whether the Azure subscription has exceeded its public IP quota

questions.explanation: NSGs attached to the NC2 delegated subnet can inadvertently block outbound connectivity required for NC2 management operations. The NC2 nodes need outbound HTTPS (port 443) access to Azure management endpoints (management.azure.com, login.microsoftonline.com, etc.). A restrictive NSG that blocks these outbound flows will cause management plane connectivity failures. Checking and adjusting NSG rules is the first troubleshooting step.

options.text: The delegated subnet has resources (VMs, NICs, or load balancers) already deployed in it
The delegated subnet is using an IPv6 address space
The delegated subnet does not have a default route to the internet via Azure Firewall
The delegated subnet is in a different Azure region than the resource group

questions.explanation: The delegated subnet for NC2 on Azure must be exclusively dedicated to the NC2 deployment — no pre-existing resources (VMs, NICs, service endpoints, or other deployments) can exist in it. Azure subnet delegation grants NC2's resource provider full control over the subnet. If existing resources are present, the delegation and subsequent NC2 deployment will fail. The subnet must be empty before initiating NC2 deployment.

options.text: Hot tier — configured automatically during NC2 deployment by specifying a storage account in the NC2 Console
Cool tier — manually configured in Prism Element under Data Protection > Cloud Connect
Archive tier — configured via Azure Policy to automatically tier metadata after 30 days
Premium tier — required for low-latency metadata access during cluster recovery

questions.explanation: NC2 on Azure uses Azure Blob Storage (Hot tier) for cluster metadata backup, and this is configured during the initial NC2 cluster deployment through the NC2 Console. The administrator specifies an Azure Storage Account, and NC2 automatically manages the metadata backup lifecycle. Hot tier is recommended because metadata must be immediately accessible during recovery scenarios without rehydration delays.

options.text: Any bare-metal instance types can be mixed freely within a single cluster
Nodes must be paired in groups of the same instance type — mixing is allowed only in matched pairs (e.g., 2× type-A + 2× type-B)
All nodes in a single NC2 cluster must be the same instance type — heterogeneous clusters are not supported
Up to two different instance types can be mixed, but the majority (>50%) must be the larger type

questions.explanation: NC2 requires all nodes within a single cluster to be of the same instance type (homogeneous). This ensures consistent storage and compute capacity across all nodes, which is essential for the Nutanix Distributed Storage Fabric's data distribution and rebalancing algorithms. If different workload profiles require different instance types, separate NC2 clusters must be deployed and managed independently.

options.text: PAYG has hourly billing but requires a 12-month minimum commitment
PAYG is billed hourly with no commitment; term-based subscriptions offer lower per-hour costs in exchange for a fixed commitment period
PAYG subscriptions are only available for development/test clusters; production requires term-based commitments
PAYG and term-based models have identical pricing; the difference is only in payment frequency (monthly vs upfront)

questions.explanation: NC2 PAYG subscriptions bill hourly with no minimum commitment, providing flexibility for variable workloads. Term-based (reserved) subscriptions require a commitment period (typically 1–3 years) but offer reduced per-hour rates compared to PAYG. PAYG is appropriate for development, testing, and unpredictable workloads, while term-based subscriptions suit production deployments with stable capacity requirements.

options.text: AWS NAT Gateway in the NC2 host subnet with security group rules allowing outbound internet traffic
An AWS Network Load Balancer (NLB) attached to the NC2 VPC with target groups pointing to the host subnet
A VPC Endpoint for the internet gateway service configured with a route table entry
Direct Internet Gateway attachment to the NC2 overlay network subnets via AHV route forwarding

questions.explanation: For NC2 on AWS, guest VMs on the AHV overlay ultimately route traffic through the host ENIs in the VPC. An AWS NAT Gateway deployed in a public subnet with a route table entry (0.0.0.0/0 → NAT Gateway) on the NC2 private subnet enables outbound internet access for guest VMs. The NAT Gateway provides source NAT so VMs can reach external SaaS services while remaining in a private subnet. VPC Endpoints are for private AWS service connectivity, not general internet access.

options.text: In the Prism Element UI under Settings > System Info > Compatibility Check
On the Nutanix Support Portal (support.nutanix.com) via the Compatibility Matrix tool or Nutanix documentation
In the NC2 Console under My Clusters > Compatibility Reports
Through the AWS Marketplace NC2 product page with automated compatibility verification

questions.explanation: The Nutanix Compatibility Matrix is accessed via the Nutanix Support Portal (support.nutanix.com) and provides version compatibility information for AOS, AHV, Prism Central, and other Nutanix components. Before deploying or upgrading NC2 clusters, architects must verify their target versions against the published matrix to avoid unsupported configurations. This is a critical step in the deployment planning phase.

options.text: The AWS IAM role attached to the EC2 instances lacks permissions for EC2/VPC API calls; verify the IAM role policy includes necessary service permissions
The NC2 host subnet security group is blocking outbound traffic to AWS management endpoints on port 443; adjust the security group to allow HTTPS egress
The AWS account is using an unsupported region; re-deploy the cluster in a region listed on the NC2 regional availability list
The Prism Central instance is not registered to my.nutanix.com; register PC to restore AWS API connectivity

questions.explanation: CloudAPIEndpointUnreachable errors indicate that the bare-metal EC2 instances cannot reach AWS API endpoints due to IAM permission issues or network blocking. The first troubleshooting step is to verify that the IAM role assigned to the EC2 instances includes the necessary permissions for EC2 DescribeInstances, DescribeSecurityGroups, DescribeSubnets, and other required API calls. The second step (option B) would be to verify security group rules if IAM permissions are correct.

options.text: Register the Microsoft.Compute resource provider and assign the Contributor role to the service principal
Create an App Registration in Azure AD and assign it the appropriate RBAC role over the subscription/resource group
Enable Azure Managed Identity on the Subscription and assign it to the NC2 resource provider
Generate an Azure Storage Account access key and assign it to the service principal's key vault

questions.explanation: Before provisioning NC2 in Azure via automation or custom tools, an App Registration must be created in Azure AD. This registration represents the service principal that will authenticate and authorize NC2 resource management. The service principal is then assigned appropriate RBAC roles (Custom Role with NC2-specific permissions or built-in roles like Contributor) over the target subscription or resource group. This is a prerequisite for any programmatic NC2 deployment.

options.text: The Azure Monitor Integration service which polls Azure Management API for subscription-level events
The Flow Gateway syslog module which translates Azure Resource Graph events into Prism format
NC2's cloud integration layer that continuously monitors Azure resource state and translates provider-native events into the unified event framework
Datadog agent deployed on Flow Gateway VMs which forwards Azure Activity Log entries to Prism Central

questions.explanation: NC2 includes a cloud integration layer that monitors the Azure cloud provider's resource state and translates Azure-native events (subscription quotas, resource provisioning, node power states, etc.) into the unified Nutanix event framework visible in Prism Central. This allows administrators to correlate cluster and cloud provider events from a single console without integrating separate monitoring systems.

options.text: Azure NSG Flow Logs enabled on the delegated subnet's Network Security Group, targeting an Azure Storage Account for retention
Azure Application Insights with Network Dependency Monitoring for the resource group containing the NC2 cluster
Azure Firewall Diagnostic Settings with flow trace enabled for the delegated subnet
Flow Gateway packet capture via `ncli cluster logs collect` piped to Azure Blob Storage

questions.explanation: Azure NSG Flow Logs (also called VPC Flow Logs in other platforms) capture Layer 4 flow information for traffic passing through an NSG. When enabled on the NSG attached to the NC2 delegated subnet, they log all allowed and denied traffic flows to an Azure Storage Account, enabling forensics, compliance auditing, and traffic analysis. This is the standard Azure feature for network flow logging and does not require additional agents.

options.text: Deploy Azure Load Balancer health checks pointing to Flow Gateway IP addresses and enable health check logging to Azure Monitor

Configure health checks in Prism Element under Flow Gateway Settings, monitoring latency and packet loss to Flow Gateway interfaces, and add additional Flow Gateway instances if health metrics exceed thresholds
Increase the Azure VNet Gateway SKU from VpnGw2 to VpnGw4 to improve management plane throughput
Enable Azure ExpressRoute FastPath on the management subnet to bypass the gateway for administrative traffic

questions.explanation: Health checks for Flow Gateway should be configured in the Prism Element settings (or via nCLI) to continuously monitor connectivity and throughput metrics. When health metrics (latency, packet loss, throughput) exceed thresholds during peak periods, this indicates Flow Gateway capacity constraints. The remediation is to scale Flow Gateway by adding additional instances (up to 4 total) in ECMP mode, as discussed in Q23, to distribute the load and eliminate intermittent failures.

questions.questionText: An administrator is planning a production deployment of Nutanix Files. What is the **minimum** number of File Server VMs (FSVMs) required for high availability?
questions.explanation: A minimum of 3 FSVMs is required for production deployments to ensure high availability and data resiliency.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is the **maximum** number of FSVMs that can be deployed in a single Nutanix Files cluster?
questions.explanation: Nutanix Files supports a maximum of 16 FSVMs per file server deployment, allowing scale-out as demand grows.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is the **standard** vCPU and memory sizing for each FSVM in a default Nutanix Files deployment?

options.text: 2 vCPU / 8 GB RAM
4 vCPU / 12 GB RAM
8 vCPU / 16 GB RAM
4 vCPU / 16 GB RAM

questions.explanation: The standard FSVM resource allocation is 4 vCPUs and 12 GB RAM per FSVM by default.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: A company wants to deploy Nutanix Files for a small test environment with no HA requirement. What is the minimum deployment option?

options.text: 2 FSVMs
3 FSVMs
1 FSVM (single-node)
Single-node is not supported

questions.explanation: For non-production or test scenarios, Nutanix Files can be deployed with a single FSVM, though this does not provide high availability.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which two networks are required when deploying Nutanix Files? (Choose the BEST answer.)

options.text: Management network and iSCSI network
Client-side network and storage network
Public network and private network
External network and internal network

questions.explanation: Nutanix Files requires a client-side network (for SMB/NFS client access) and a storage network (for FSVM-to-CVM communication on the backplane).
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: During Nutanix Files deployment, which DNS record type must be created for client access to the file server?

options.text: MX record
PTR record
A record
CNAME record

questions.explanation: DNS A records must be created for the file server name, mapping to the FSVM client-side IP addresses for proper name resolution and load distribution.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which requirement must be met before deploying Nutanix Files with SMB share support?

options.text: An LDAP server must be configured
The file server must be joined to an Active Directory domain
A RADIUS server must be configured
A local user database must be populated

questions.explanation: For SMB share support, the file server must be joined to Active Directory so that Kerberos and NTLM authentication can function for Windows clients.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is a prerequisite for deploying Nutanix Objects?

options.text: Prism Element must be version 6.0 or higher
Prism Central must be deployed and registered
A dedicated AHV cluster with no VMs is required
Nutanix Files must be deployed first

questions.explanation: Nutanix Objects is deployed and managed exclusively through Prism Central, making PC a mandatory prerequisite.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is the minimum number of worker nodes required for a Nutanix Objects deployment?
questions.explanation: Nutanix Objects requires a minimum of 3 worker nodes to ensure availability and distributed processing of S3 requests.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which underlying platform does Nutanix Objects use to run its containerized microservices?

options.text: Docker Swarm
Kubernetes via MSP (Microservices Platform)
VMware Tanzu
OpenShift

questions.explanation: Nutanix Objects runs on the Microservices Platform (MSP), which is a Kubernetes-based infrastructure managed by Prism Central for deploying containerized services.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: When deploying Nutanix Objects, which network type must the internal and external networks be?

options.text: Unmanaged AHV networks
VLAN-backed managed networks
Overlay (VPC) networks
Any network type is supported

questions.explanation: Both the internal and external networks for Objects must be VLAN-backed managed networks configured in Prism Central to ensure proper connectivity.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: In Nutanix Objects architecture, which component distributes incoming S3 API requests across worker nodes?

options.text: Atlas service
Object Controller
Load balancer
Metadata service

questions.explanation: Load balancers front the Objects deployment and distribute incoming S3 API requests across the available worker nodes for scalability.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which Nutanix Objects component is responsible for handling I/O operations to the underlying storage?

options.text: Load balancer
Atlas service
Object Controller
MSP controller

questions.explanation: Object Controllers handle the actual data I/O operations, reading and writing object data to the Nutanix storage layer.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is the role of the Atlas service in Nutanix Objects?

options.text: It distributes S3 requests to worker nodes
It manages lifecycle policies and audit logging
It handles iSCSI target discovery
It performs data deduplication

questions.explanation: The Atlas service manages object lifecycle operations and audit/compliance logging within the Objects deployment.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to provide block-level storage to a bare-metal Linux server outside the Nutanix cluster. Which Nutanix service should be used?

options.text: Nutanix Files
Nutanix Objects
Nutanix Volumes
Nutanix Mine

questions.explanation: Nutanix Volumes provides iSCSI-based block storage that external clients (including bare-metal servers) can consume via standard iSCSI initiators.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: When configuring Nutanix Volumes for external client access, which IP address must be configured on the cluster?

options.text: CVM management IP
iSCSI Data Services IP
FSVM client network IP
Objects endpoint IP

questions.explanation: A cluster-wide iSCSI Data Services IP must be configured so external iSCSI initiators can discover and connect to Volume Groups.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: During a Nutanix Files upgrade, how are FSVMs upgraded?

options.text: All FSVMs are upgraded simultaneously
FSVMs are upgraded in a rolling fashion, one at a time
The file server must be taken offline for the upgrade
Only the primary FSVM is upgraded; secondaries auto-sync

questions.explanation: Files uses a rolling upgrade process where one FSVM is upgraded at a time to maintain service availability throughout the process.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What happens to active client connections on an FSVM during a Nutanix Files rolling upgrade?

options.text: Connections are dropped and clients must reconnect manually
Connections are migrated to another FSVM before the upgrade begins on that node
The upgrade waits indefinitely until all clients disconnect
All I/O is paused cluster-wide during the upgrade

questions.explanation: During a rolling upgrade, active connections are gracefully migrated to other healthy FSVMs before the FSVM being upgraded is taken offline.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which pre-check is performed before a Nutanix Files upgrade begins?

options.text: Verification that all shares are empty
Verification of FSVM health, AD connectivity, and network reachability
Verification that no snapshots exist
Verification that all clients are disconnected

questions.explanation: Pre-upgrade checks verify FSVM health, Active Directory connectivity, DNS resolution, network reachability, and other environmental prerequisites.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Before upgrading Nutanix Objects, which component version compatibility must be verified?

options.text: AOS version on PE
Prism Central version
Hypervisor version
Foundation version

questions.explanation: Objects is managed through Prism Central, so the PC version must be compatible with the target Objects version before the upgrade can proceed.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: How are Nutanix Objects worker nodes upgraded?

options.text: All worker nodes are upgraded simultaneously
Worker nodes are upgraded in a rolling fashion
A new set of worker nodes is deployed and traffic is switched over
The object store must be taken offline

questions.explanation: Objects worker nodes are upgraded in a rolling process to maintain availability of the S3 endpoint during the upgrade.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An architect is planning Nutanix Files for a VDI environment with 500 users. The vendor recommends budgeting 5 IOPS per user. How many total IOPS should be planned for?
questions.explanation: 500 users × 5 IOPS per user = 2,500 IOPS total should be planned for the VDI file storage workload.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: When sizing a Nutanix Files deployment for throughput-intensive workloads, which action increases aggregate throughput capacity?

options.text: Increasing the share quota
Adding more FSVMs (scale-out)
Creating more shares on the same FSVM
Enabling compression on the storage container

questions.explanation: Adding more FSVMs scales out the file server, distributing I/O across more nodes and increasing aggregate throughput capacity.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is a best practice for the storage container when deploying Nutanix Files?

options.text: Use the default storage container shared with VMs
Create a dedicated storage container for Files
Use a container with deduplication disabled
Use a container with RF1 for performance

questions.explanation: Creating a dedicated storage container for Files is a best practice, allowing independent management of storage policies without affecting other workloads.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which Nutanix Files share type spreads data across all FSVMs in the file server?

options.text: Standard share
Distributed share
Home share
Nested share

questions.explanation: Distributed shares spread data across all FSVMs in the deployment, with each Top-Level Directory (TLD) assigned to a specific FSVM for higher aggregate throughput.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which Nutanix Files share type stores all data on a single FSVM?

options.text: Distributed share
Home share
Standard share
Clustered share

questions.explanation: Standard shares keep all their data on a single FSVM, making them simpler to manage but with a lower throughput ceiling compared to distributed shares.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: When should an administrator choose a distributed share over a standard share?

options.text: When simplicity is preferred over performance
When higher aggregate throughput is needed across many users
When only a single user will access the share
When the share will store fewer than 100 files

questions.explanation: Distributed shares are ideal when many users need concurrent access and higher aggregate throughput, as data is spread across all FSVMs.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: A company deploys Nutanix Objects and wants to provide access from two geographically separate sites. Which deployment model should be used?

options.text: Single-site object store
Multi-site object store
Replicated object store
Federated object store

questions.explanation: A multi-site object store deployment allows access to the same logical object store from multiple geographic locations for distributed access.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: When configuring a Nutanix Objects endpoint, what protocol do clients use to interact with stored objects?

options.text: NFS v4.1
S3-compatible REST API
SMB 3.0
iSCSI

questions.explanation: Nutanix Objects exposes an S3-compatible REST API endpoint that clients use for all object storage operations.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: How does Nutanix Volumes distribute I/O across the cluster?

options.text: All I/O goes through a single CVM
I/O is distributed across CVMs in the cluster
I/O is routed through a dedicated storage gateway VM
External clients must manually select a CVM

questions.explanation: Volumes distributes I/O across all CVMs in the cluster, leveraging the Nutanix distributed storage fabric for performance and resilience.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of the iSCSI Data Services IP in Nutanix Volumes?

options.text: It provides a floating IP used only for CVM management
It provides a cluster-wide discovery IP for external iSCSI initiators
It provides the IP address for the Prism web console
It provides the replication target IP for DR

questions.explanation: The iSCSI Data Services IP is a cluster-wide virtual IP that external initiators use for iSCSI target discovery and initial connection.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: During FSVM resource scaling, which parameter can an administrator increase to handle more concurrent connections?

options.text: The number of storage containers
The vCPU and RAM allocated to each FSVM
The number of DNS records
The Prism Central memory

questions.explanation: Administrators can scale up individual FSVMs by increasing their vCPU and RAM allocations to handle more concurrent connections and higher workloads.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An administrator deploys Nutanix Files with 3 FSVMs. Six months later, user demand has doubled. What is the recommended scaling approach?

options.text: Delete and redeploy with more FSVMs
Add additional FSVMs to the existing file server (scale-out)
Increase the share quota
Deploy a second file server and manually split users

questions.explanation: Nutanix Files supports non-disruptive scale-out by adding FSVMs to an existing file server deployment, up to the maximum of 16.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which network is used for communication between FSVMs and CVMs on the Nutanix cluster?

options.text: Client-side network
Management network
Storage network (backplane)
iSCSI network

questions.explanation: The storage network serves as the backplane for FSVM-to-CVM communication, carrying all storage I/O traffic internally.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: How many IP addresses are required on the client-side network for a 3-FSVM deployment?

options.text: 3 (one per FSVM)

questions.explanation: Each FSVM requires one IP address on the client-side network, so a 3-FSVM deployment needs 3 client-side IPs.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which component in the Nutanix Objects architecture runs as containerized microservices on the worker VMs?

options.text: Prism Element services
CVM services
Object store services (load balancers, object controllers, metadata services)
Hypervisor management agents

questions.explanation: The worker VMs run containerized microservices including load balancers, object controllers, metadata services, and the Atlas service.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An organization requires a Nutanix Objects deployment that can serve a single location. Which deployment model is appropriate?

options.text: Multi-site object store
Federated object store
Single-site object store
Replicated object store

questions.explanation: A single-site object store is appropriate when all clients are at one location and there is no need for multi-site access.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: When planning capacity for Nutanix Files in a general-purpose file server use case, which metric is MOST important?

options.text: Number of Prism Central instances
Expected capacity, user count, and IOPS profile
Number of hypervisor hosts
Number of Active Directory domains

questions.explanation: Capacity planning for Files should account for expected total capacity, the number of concurrent users, and the expected IOPS profile of the workload.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What happens if a single FSVM fails in a 3-FSVM production deployment?

options.text: The entire file server goes offline
Clients connected to that FSVM are automatically migrated to surviving FSVMs
Only distributed shares are affected
An administrator must manually failover connections

questions.explanation: With 3 or more FSVMs, the file server remains available and client connections are automatically migrated to the surviving FSVMs.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which statement is true about the storage network in a Nutanix Files deployment?

options.text: It must be routable to external clients
It is used only for FSVM-to-CVM internal communication
It requires DNS A records
It must use DHCP addressing

questions.explanation: The storage network is an internal-only network used for FSVM-to-CVM communication and should not be routable to external clients.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: During a Nutanix Files deployment, what must be configured in DNS to enable clients to resolve the file server name?

options.text: A single A record pointing to the Prism Element VIP
Multiple A records (one per FSVM client IP) for the file server name
A CNAME record pointing to the CVM IP
An SRV record for the file server

questions.explanation: Multiple DNS A records for the file server name (one per FSVM client-side IP) provide round-robin DNS for client load distribution.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to scale an FSVM from 4 vCPU/12 GB RAM to 8 vCPU/24 GB RAM. What is the impact?

options.text: The file server must be redeployed
All shares must be recreated
The FSVM must be briefly powered off for the resource change
There is no impact; changes are made live without disruption

questions.explanation: Scaling FSVM resources requires briefly powering off the FSVM to apply the new vCPU and RAM settings, causing a temporary interruption for that FSVM.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which Nutanix Objects component provides a single entry point (virtual IP or FQDN) for S3 clients?

options.text: Object Controller
Atlas service
Load balancer endpoint
MSP controller

questions.explanation: The load balancer provides the S3 endpoint (virtual IP or FQDN) that clients connect to, distributing requests to the backend worker nodes.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: When deploying Nutanix Volumes, what is the Volume Group construct used for?

options.text: Grouping NFS exports
Grouping one or more virtual disks (vDisks) presented as iSCSI targets
Grouping file server shares
Grouping S3 buckets

questions.explanation: A Volume Group aggregates one or more virtual disks and presents them as iSCSI targets to connected initiators (VMs or external clients).
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which of the following is true about single-node Nutanix Files deployments?

options.text: They provide full high availability
They are suitable for production workloads
They use only 1 FSVM and do not provide HA
They require a minimum of 2 storage containers

questions.explanation: Single-node Files deployments use one FSVM, which means there is no failover target, making them unsuitable for production HA requirements.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: During a Nutanix Objects upgrade, what should an administrator verify first?

options.text: That all buckets are empty
That Prism Central is at a compatible version for the target Objects version
That all access keys have been revoked
That the object store is offline

questions.explanation: PC version compatibility is the first and most critical check before upgrading Objects, as Objects is managed and deployed through PC.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An administrator is deploying Nutanix Files on a cluster with 4 nodes. What is the recommended minimum for a production deployment?

options.text: 1 FSVM with 8 vCPU
2 FSVMs with 4 vCPU each
3 FSVMs with 4 vCPU/12 GB RAM each
4 FSVMs with 2 vCPU each

questions.explanation: The production best practice is a minimum of 3 FSVMs, each with the standard 4 vCPU/12 GB RAM configuration, to ensure HA.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What type of shares should be used when each user needs a personal directory on Nutanix Files?

options.text: Standard SMB shares
NFS exports
Home shares (nested inside a distributed share)
WORM-enabled shares

questions.explanation: Home shares are nested within distributed shares and provide per-user home directories, leveraging the distributed architecture for scalability.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which statement correctly describes the relationship between Nutanix Objects and Prism Central?

options.text: Objects can be deployed from either Prism Element or Prism Central
Objects requires Prism Central for deployment and management
Objects only uses Prism Central for monitoring, not deployment
Objects does not require Prism Central

questions.explanation: Nutanix Objects is exclusively deployed and managed through Prism Central and cannot be managed from Prism Element alone.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: How does an FSVM communicate with the Nutanix storage layer?

options.text: Through the client-side network directly to the storage pool
Through the storage network to the local CVM using iSCSI/NFS
Through a dedicated FC (Fibre Channel) network
Through the management network to the Prism API

questions.explanation: FSVMs communicate with CVMs over the storage network using internal protocols to read and write data to the Nutanix distributed storage fabric.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An enterprise wants to deploy Nutanix Files for a VDI environment supporting 2,000 users. Assuming 3 IOPS per user, what is the minimum IOPS the file server must support?
questions.explanation: 2,000 users × 3 IOPS per user = 6,000 IOPS minimum should be planned for this VDI environment.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is the benefit of using a dedicated storage container for Nutanix Files?

options.text: It provides better deduplication ratios
It isolates Files storage policies (RF, compression, EC) from other workloads
It is required to enable SMB shares
It increases the maximum number of FSVMs

questions.explanation: A dedicated container allows independent configuration of storage policies like replication factor, compression, and erasure coding without affecting other workloads.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: When deploying Nutanix Objects, which two separate networks must be configured?

options.text: Storage network and backplane network
Internal network (inter-service) and external network (client-facing S3 endpoint)
Management network and iSCSI network
Client network and AD network

questions.explanation: Objects requires an internal network for inter-service communication between microservices and an external network for the client-facing S3 endpoint.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: How many storage network IPs are needed for a 3-FSVM Nutanix Files deployment?
questions.explanation: Each FSVM needs one IP on the storage network, so a 3-FSVM deployment requires 3 storage network IPs.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: During a Nutanix Files rolling upgrade, what ensures data availability while an FSVM is being upgraded?

options.text: Data is temporarily copied to Prism Central
The remaining healthy FSVMs serve all client requests
A temporary FSVM is deployed to replace the upgrading one
I/O is queued until the FSVM comes back online

questions.explanation: The rolling upgrade ensures that while one FSVM is being upgraded, the remaining healthy FSVMs continue serving all client requests without interruption.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An architect is designing a Nutanix Files solution needing 20 Gbps aggregate throughput. With each FSVM providing approximately 5 Gbps, how many FSVMs are needed?
questions.explanation: 20 Gbps ÷ 5 Gbps per FSVM = 4 FSVMs needed to meet the throughput requirement.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is the function of DNS round-robin in a Nutanix Files deployment?

options.text: It provides automatic failover of the file server
It distributes client connections across multiple FSVM client IPs
It replicates file data across FSVMs
It manages AD join operations

questions.explanation: DNS round-robin distributes client connections across the FSVM client-side IP addresses, providing basic load balancing for new connections.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: When joining the Nutanix file server to Active Directory, which information is NOT required?

options.text: AD domain name
AD administrator credentials
RADIUS shared secret
Preferred domain controller (optional)

questions.explanation: RADIUS is not used for Nutanix Files AD integration. The AD domain name, credentials, and optionally a preferred DC are needed.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Nutanix Objects worker VMs run on which infrastructure?

options.text: Dedicated physical servers outside the Nutanix cluster
AHV VMs on the same Nutanix cluster managed by MSP/Kubernetes
External Kubernetes clusters
Docker containers on Prism Central

questions.explanation: Objects worker VMs are AHV VMs running on the Nutanix cluster, managed by the MSP (Microservices Platform) Kubernetes infrastructure.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What must be configured on each Nutanix cluster node before Volumes can serve external iSCSI clients?

options.text: An NFS export
An iSCSI Data Services IP
A VPN tunnel
Fibre Channel zoning

questions.explanation: The iSCSI Data Services IP must be configured at the cluster level to enable external iSCSI initiators to discover and connect to Volume Groups.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An administrator attempts to deploy Nutanix Objects but the deployment fails. Which is the MOST likely cause?

options.text: The Nutanix cluster has too many VMs
Prism Central is not deployed or registered
The cluster is running AHV 7.0
The storage container is encrypted

questions.explanation: The most common cause of Objects deployment failure is Prism Central not being deployed or not properly registered with the cluster.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which statement is true about Nutanix Files upgrade pre-checks?

options.text: Pre-checks only verify disk space on FSVMs
Pre-checks verify FSVM health, AD connectivity, DNS, and network reachability
Pre-checks are optional and can be skipped
Pre-checks only run on the first FSVM

questions.explanation: Comprehensive pre-checks verify multiple environmental factors including FSVM health, AD connectivity, DNS resolution, and network reachability before starting the upgrade.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: When scaling out Nutanix Files, what happens to existing distributed shares when a new FSVM is added?

options.text: All data must be manually migrated
New TLDs can be assigned to the new FSVM; existing TLDs can be rebalanced
Existing distributed shares must be deleted and recreated
The new FSVM only serves standard shares

questions.explanation: When a new FSVM is added, new Top-Level Directories in distributed shares can be assigned to it, and existing TLDs can be rebalanced across all FSVMs.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: In a Nutanix Objects multi-site deployment, how do clients access the object store?

options.text: Through a global load balancer only
Through site-specific S3 endpoints
Through Prism Central API only
Through a VPN tunnel between sites

questions.explanation: In multi-site deployments, each site has its own S3 endpoint that clients use for access, with data replicated between sites as configured.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of pre-upgrade checks in a Nutanix Objects upgrade?

options.text: To verify all buckets have lifecycle policies
To ensure PC compatibility, worker node health, and MSP readiness
To confirm all access keys are active
To verify WORM policies are disabled

questions.explanation: Objects pre-upgrade checks verify Prism Central compatibility, worker node health, MSP platform readiness, and other environmental prerequisites.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An administrator has a Nutanix cluster with the iSCSI Data Services IP configured. External Windows servers need block storage. Which Nutanix feature provides this?

options.text: Nutanix Files with SMB shares
Nutanix Objects with S3 buckets
Nutanix Volumes with Volume Groups
Nutanix Calm with blueprints

questions.explanation: Nutanix Volumes with Volume Groups provides iSCSI block storage that external Windows servers can access using their built-in iSCSI initiator.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: During Nutanix Files capacity planning, which factor determines the number of FSVMs required?

options.text: The number of Active Directory domains
The number of Prism Central instances
The expected concurrent users, IOPS, and throughput requirements
The number of hypervisor hosts

questions.explanation: FSVM count is determined by workload demands including concurrent user count, IOPS requirements, and aggregate throughput needs.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which Nutanix Objects component manages the Kubernetes pods running on worker VMs?

options.text: Prism Element
Microservices Platform (MSP)
Foundation
Calm

questions.explanation: The Microservices Platform (MSP) manages the Kubernetes infrastructure that orchestrates the containerized services running on Objects worker VMs.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What happens to Nutanix Volumes I/O if the CVM on the host where the iSCSI Data Services IP resides fails?

options.text: All external iSCSI connections are permanently lost
The iSCSI Data Services IP fails over to another CVM, and I/O resumes
The administrator must manually reconfigure the IP
External clients must reconnect to a different IP

questions.explanation: The iSCSI Data Services IP is a floating IP that automatically fails over to another healthy CVM, ensuring continued external iSCSI access.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which deployment consideration is unique to Nutanix Objects compared to Files and Volumes?

options.text: It requires a hypervisor
It requires Prism Central and the MSP platform
It requires Active Directory
It requires a storage container

questions.explanation: Unlike Files (deployed from PE) and Volumes (configured in PE), Objects uniquely requires both Prism Central and the MSP Kubernetes platform for deployment.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to deploy Nutanix Files with 5 FSVMs. How many total IPs are needed (client-side + storage)?
questions.explanation: 5 FSVMs × 2 IPs each (1 client-side + 1 storage) = 10 total IP addresses required.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which best practice should be followed when selecting a storage container for Nutanix Files?

options.text: Reuse the default container for simplicity
Create a dedicated container with appropriate RF and data efficiency settings
Use a container with RF1 for maximum performance
Share the container with Nutanix Objects worker VMs

questions.explanation: Best practice is to create a dedicated container with appropriate replication factor and data efficiency settings optimized for the file workload.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What type of network must the client-side network be for Nutanix Files?

options.text: Any overlay network
A routable network accessible by SMB/NFS clients
An isolated network with no default gateway
A network on the same VLAN as the CVM management network

questions.explanation: The client-side network must be routable and accessible by the SMB and NFS clients that will connect to the file server.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An administrator adds a fourth FSVM to an existing 3-FSVM deployment. Which share type benefits MOST from the additional FSVM?

options.text: Standard shares
Distributed shares
Both benefit equally
Neither benefits

questions.explanation: Distributed shares benefit most because they spread data across all FSVMs, so an additional FSVM increases aggregate throughput and capacity for distributed shares.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: When Nutanix Objects is deployed, how do S3 clients resolve the object store endpoint?

options.text: By using the Prism Central IP
By using a DNS name or IP configured as the Objects endpoint
By querying the CVM directly
By using the hypervisor management IP

questions.explanation: Clients access the object store through a configured DNS name or IP address that serves as the S3 endpoint, backed by the load balancer.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is the impact of running out of FSVM resources (CPU/memory) in a Nutanix Files deployment?

options.text: The file server automatically deploys additional FSVMs
Client performance degrades, with increased latency and potential timeouts
Data corruption occurs
The storage container becomes read-only

questions.explanation: When FSVM resources are exhausted, clients experience degraded performance with higher latency and potential connection timeouts.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which statement about Nutanix Objects internal network is correct?

options.text: It carries client S3 traffic
It carries inter-service communication between microservice pods
It is optional and only needed for multi-site deployments
It must be on the same VLAN as the external network

questions.explanation: The internal network carries communication between the microservice pods on the worker VMs and must be separate from the external client-facing network.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to verify that a Nutanix Files deployment is ready for upgrade. Which tool should be used?

options.text: Prism Central Health Dashboard
The Files upgrade pre-check utility in Prism Element
nCLI cluster status
The Foundation upgrade wizard

questions.explanation: The Files upgrade pre-check utility available in Prism Element verifies all prerequisites are met before initiating the upgrade process.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: When deploying Nutanix Volumes for an external Oracle RAC cluster, what construct is created to present storage?

options.text: An NFS export
An SMB share
A Volume Group with multiple virtual disks
An S3 bucket

questions.explanation: A Volume Group containing multiple virtual disks is created and presented as iSCSI targets to the Oracle RAC nodes for shared block storage.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is the minimum Nutanix Files deployment for a proof-of-concept that does NOT require high availability?

options.text: 3 FSVMs with 4 vCPU/12 GB RAM each
2 FSVMs with 4 vCPU/12 GB RAM each
1 FSVM with 4 vCPU/12 GB RAM
Nutanix Files cannot be deployed without HA

questions.explanation: For a non-HA proof-of-concept, a single FSVM with the standard 4 vCPU/12 GB RAM is the minimum deployment option.
questions.domain: Domain 1: Deploy and Upgrade Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An administrator creates an SMB share on Nutanix Files. Which authentication protocols can be used by Windows clients? (Choose the BEST answer.)

options.text: LDAP and RADIUS
Kerberos and NTLM
SAML and OAuth
Certificate-based and smart card

questions.explanation: Nutanix Files SMB shares support Kerberos (preferred) and NTLM authentication, both requiring Active Directory integration.
