questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is Access-Based Enumeration (ABE) on a Nutanix Files SMB share?

options.text: A feature that encrypts file names
A feature that only shows files and folders to users who have at least read access
A feature that enumerates all Active Directory users
A feature that sorts files alphabetically

questions.explanation: ABE hides files and folders from users who do not have at least read permission, improving security and reducing clutter in share browsing.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An administrator configures an NFS export on Nutanix Files. Which authentication method provides the HIGHEST security?

options.text: AUTH_SYS
Kerberos (krb5)
Kerberos with integrity (krb5i)
Kerberos with privacy/encryption (krb5p)

questions.explanation: Kerberos with privacy (krb5p) provides authentication, integrity checking, AND encryption of all NFS traffic, offering the highest security level.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is the difference between NFS Kerberos krb5i and krb5p?

options.text: krb5i provides encryption; krb5p provides authentication only
krb5i provides integrity checking; krb5p adds encryption on top of integrity
They are identical in security level
krb5i is for NFSv3; krb5p is for NFSv4

questions.explanation: krb5i adds integrity checking (preventing tampering) to Kerberos authentication, while krb5p adds both integrity checking and full encryption of data in transit.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: When using AUTH_SYS for an NFS export, how are users identified?

options.text: By Kerberos principal
By UID/GID sent by the NFS client
By Active Directory SID
By X.509 certificate

questions.explanation: AUTH_SYS uses the UID and GID from the client system to identify users, trusting the client to provide accurate identity information.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of the "squash" option on an NFS export?

options.text: To compress data on the share
To map root or all users to an anonymous UID/GID for security
To limit the number of files per directory
To enable deduplication on the export

questions.explanation: Squash options map specific users (typically root) to an anonymous UID/GID to prevent privileged access from NFS clients to the export.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An NFS export is configured with "root squash" enabled. What happens when a root user on a client mounts and accesses the export?

options.text: The root user has full root access on the export
The root user is mapped to the anonymous nobody user
The mount is denied entirely
The root user can only read files

questions.explanation: Root squash maps the root user (UID 0) from the client to the anonymous nobody user, preventing privileged root access to the NFS export.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: How does an administrator restrict which clients can mount an NFS export on Nutanix Files?

options.text: By configuring a firewall rule in Prism Central
By configuring a client allowlist (whitelist) on the NFS export
By setting an Active Directory group policy
By configuring VLAN tagging on the switch

questions.explanation: Client allowlists on the NFS export specify which IP addresses or subnets are permitted to mount the export.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An organization needs to allow both Windows (SMB) and Linux (NFS) clients to access the same data on Nutanix Files. Which feature should be configured?

options.text: Standard SMB share with NFS gateway
Multi-protocol share
Dual file server deployment
iSCSI shared volume

questions.explanation: Multi-protocol shares allow both SMB and NFS clients to access the same data, with user mapping ensuring consistent permissions.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: When creating a multi-protocol share, which concept determines the primary permission model?

options.text: The hypervisor type
The native protocol selection (SMB-native or NFS-native)
The storage container settings
The FSVM count

questions.explanation: The native protocol (SMB-native or NFS-native) determines which permission model takes precedence and how permissions are managed on the share.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: In a multi-protocol share with SMB as the native protocol, how are NFS user identities mapped to Windows users?

options.text: By matching hostname
Using RFC 2307 attributes (UID/GID mappings) in Active Directory
By matching IP addresses
Manually through Prism Element

questions.explanation: RFC 2307 attributes stored in Active Directory map NFS UIDs/GIDs to Windows user accounts, enabling consistent identity across protocols.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is a key limitation of multi-protocol shares on Nutanix Files?

options.text: Only read access is supported from the non-native protocol
Simultaneous writes from both SMB and NFS clients to the same file are not supported
Multi-protocol shares cannot exceed 1 TB
Multi-protocol shares do not support snapshots

questions.explanation: Concurrent write access from both SMB and NFS clients to the same file simultaneously is not supported, as it can cause data corruption.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: How does a distributed share assign data to FSVMs?

options.text: All data is randomly distributed
Each Top-Level Directory (TLD) is assigned to a specific FSVM
Data is striped at the block level across all FSVMs
The newest FSVM always receives new data

questions.explanation: In a distributed share, each Top-Level Directory (TLD) is assigned to a specific FSVM, and all data within that TLD resides on that FSVM.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is the primary throughput advantage of distributed shares over standard shares?

options.text: Distributed shares use faster disks
Distributed shares leverage multiple FSVMs for higher aggregate throughput
Distributed shares use RDMA
Distributed shares bypass the CVM

questions.explanation: Distributed shares spread I/O across multiple FSVMs, providing higher aggregate throughput since each FSVM contributes its capacity.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which share type on Nutanix Files has a throughput ceiling limited by a single FSVM?

options.text: Distributed share
Home share
Standard share
Multi-protocol share

questions.explanation: Standard shares store all data on a single FSVM, so their maximum throughput is limited by that one FSVM's resources.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Where are home shares created in the Nutanix Files architecture?

options.text: As standalone shares on individual FSVMs
Nested inside a distributed share
On Prism Central storage
On the Nutanix Objects platform

questions.explanation: Home shares are nested within a distributed share, inheriting its distributed architecture to provide scalable per-user home directories.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is the structure of a home share in Nutanix Files?

options.text: A flat directory with all user files mixed together
A directory per user (e.g., \\fileserver\home\username)
A single folder shared by all users
A directory per department

questions.explanation: Home shares automatically create a per-user directory structure, mapping each user's home directory under the share path (e.g., \\fileserver\home\username).
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An administrator sets a hard quota of 10 GB on a Nutanix Files share. What happens when a user reaches 10 GB?

options.text: The user receives a warning but can continue writing
Write operations are blocked for that user
Older files are automatically deleted
The user is disconnected from the share

questions.explanation: A hard quota enforces a strict limit — once reached, all further write operations are blocked until space is freed.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is the behavior of a soft quota on Nutanix Files?

options.text: It blocks writes when the quota is reached
It sends a notification but does NOT block writes
It automatically expands the share
It compresses files to stay under the limit

questions.explanation: A soft quota triggers notifications (alerts/emails) when the threshold is reached but does not prevent users from writing additional data.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Quotas on Nutanix Files can be applied at which levels?

options.text: Only at the share level
Per-user and per-share levels
Only at the storage container level
Only at the FSVM level

questions.explanation: Quotas can be configured at both the per-user level (limiting individual user consumption) and the per-share level (limiting total share consumption).
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What does delegation allow in Nutanix Files share management?

options.text: It delegates CVM management to non-admin users
It allows non-admin users to manage specific shares (create directories, manage permissions)
It delegates AD administration to the file server
It allows users to create new file servers

questions.explanation: Delegation allows administrators to grant share-level management permissions to non-admin users for specific shares without giving full system access.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Self-Service Restore (SSR) for Nutanix Files SMB shares is accessed by Windows users through which feature?

options.text: Recycle Bin
Previous Versions tab in file properties
Windows Backup utility
Shadow Copy Management snap-in

questions.explanation: Windows users access SSR through the "Previous Versions" tab in file or folder properties, which shows available snapshots for self-service recovery.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: How do NFS clients access Self-Service Restore snapshots on Nutanix Files?

options.text: Through the Prism UI
By browsing the .snapshot hidden directory within the export
By mounting a separate snapshot export
NFS does not support Self-Service Restore

questions.explanation: NFS clients access SSR snapshots by navigating to the hidden `.snapshot` directory within the NFS export, which contains point-in-time copies.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is required for Self-Service Restore to function on Nutanix Files?

options.text: A third-party backup agent
Scheduled snapshots must be configured on the share/export
Nutanix Calm must be deployed
Prism Central File Analytics must be enabled

questions.explanation: SSR relies on scheduled snapshots being configured for the share or export — without snapshots, there are no previous versions to restore from.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: When creating a bucket in Nutanix Objects, what credentials are needed for S3 client access?

options.text: Active Directory username and password
An access key and secret key pair
An SSL certificate
A Prism Central API token

questions.explanation: Nutanix Objects uses S3-compatible access key and secret key pairs for authenticating client requests to buckets.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which API standard does Nutanix Objects follow for client compatibility?

options.text: Azure Blob Storage API
Google Cloud Storage API
Amazon S3-compatible API
OpenStack Swift API

questions.explanation: Nutanix Objects implements the Amazon S3-compatible API, allowing standard S3 tools and SDKs to work with the platform.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: How are bucket policies defined in Nutanix Objects?

options.text: In XML format following Azure conventions
In JSON format using AWS IAM-compatible syntax
Through a graphical wizard only
In YAML format

questions.explanation: Bucket policies in Nutanix Objects are defined in JSON format using AWS IAM-compatible policy syntax, specifying principals, actions, and resources.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What does a bucket policy in Nutanix Objects control?

options.text: The physical location of stored objects
Access permissions for specific principals (users) on the bucket
The replication factor of the bucket
The compression algorithm used

questions.explanation: Bucket policies control access permissions by specifying which principals can perform which actions on the bucket and its objects.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An administrator enables WORM on a Nutanix Objects bucket. What is the grace period before objects become immutable?

options.text: 1 hour
12 hours
24 hours
48 hours

questions.explanation: WORM has a 24-hour grace period after an object is written, during which it can still be modified or deleted before becoming immutable.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Once an object in a WORM-enabled bucket becomes immutable (after the grace period), which operation can still be performed?

options.text: Deleting the object
Modifying the object content
Extending the retention period
Reducing the retention period

questions.explanation: After an object becomes immutable, the retention period can only be extended, never shortened. The object cannot be deleted or modified until retention expires.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which S3 feature does Nutanix Objects WORM compliance align with?

options.text: S3 Transfer Acceleration
S3 Object Lock
S3 Glacier
S3 Cross-Region Replication

questions.explanation: Nutanix Objects WORM compliance aligns with the S3 Object Lock specification for immutable object storage.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An administrator enables versioning on a Nutanix Objects bucket. What happens when an existing object is overwritten?

options.text: The original object is permanently deleted
A new version is created with a unique version ID; the original version is preserved
The original object is moved to a trash folder
The upload is rejected

questions.explanation: With versioning enabled, each version of an object receives a unique version ID, and previous versions are preserved when an object is overwritten.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: How does versioning protect against accidental object deletion in Nutanix Objects?

options.text: It prevents all delete operations
A "delete" operation adds a delete marker instead of permanently removing the object; previous versions remain accessible
It creates a backup in Prism Central
It sends an email to the administrator

questions.explanation: When versioning is enabled, delete operations add a delete marker rather than permanently removing the object, allowing recovery of previous versions.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is a lifecycle policy in Nutanix Objects used for?

options.text: Managing FSVM lifecycle
Automating the expiration and deletion of objects based on age or other criteria
Managing user account lifecycle
Controlling CVM restarts

questions.explanation: Lifecycle policies automate object expiration and deletion based on configurable criteria such as age, reducing manual data management overhead.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: When a lifecycle policy is applied to an existing Nutanix Objects bucket, does it apply to objects already in the bucket?

options.text: No, it only applies to new objects
Yes, it is applied retroactively to existing objects
Only if the objects are less than 24 hours old
Only if versioning is disabled

questions.explanation: Lifecycle policies are applied retroactively to existing objects in the bucket, not just to newly created objects.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Does a lifecycle policy override WORM retention in Nutanix Objects?

options.text: Yes, lifecycle policies always take priority
No, lifecycle policies respect WORM retention periods
It depends on the policy order
Only if the lifecycle policy was created first

questions.explanation: Lifecycle policies respect WORM retention periods — they cannot delete objects that are still within their WORM retention period.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is the maximum size of a single object that can be uploaded to Nutanix Objects using multipart upload?

options.text: 1 TB
5 TB
10 TB
50 TB

questions.explanation: The maximum object size with multipart upload is 5 TB, consistent with the S3 API specification.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of object tagging in Nutanix Objects?

options.text: To replicate objects to another cluster
To attach key-value metadata pairs to objects for categorization and policy application
To encrypt objects
To compress objects

questions.explanation: Object tagging allows attaching key-value metadata pairs to objects, which can be used for categorization, searching, and lifecycle policy targeting.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: When configuring Nutanix Volumes for external client access, which port does iSCSI use?
questions.explanation: iSCSI uses TCP port 3260 for target discovery and data transfer between initiators and Nutanix Volumes.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What authentication mechanism can be configured on Nutanix Volumes to secure iSCSI access?

options.text: Kerberos
CHAP (Challenge-Handshake Authentication Protocol)
NTLM
OAuth 2.0

questions.explanation: CHAP authentication can be configured on Volume Groups to authenticate iSCSI initiators before granting access to the volumes.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: How does an administrator restrict which iSCSI initiators can connect to a Nutanix Volume Group?

options.text: By configuring an IP whitelist on the CVM
By configuring an initiator IQN whitelist on the Volume Group
By configuring a VLAN ACL
By setting a Volume Group password

questions.explanation: An IQN (iSCSI Qualified Name) whitelist on the Volume Group restricts access to only authorized iSCSI initiators.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: A Windows server administrator needs to connect to a Nutanix Volume Group. Which built-in Windows tool is used?

options.text: Disk Management
iSCSI Initiator
Storage Spaces
Hyper-V Manager

questions.explanation: The Windows iSCSI Initiator tool is used to discover and connect to Nutanix Volume Group iSCSI targets.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: How does a Linux server connect to a Nutanix Volume Group?

options.text: By mounting an NFS export
By using the open-iscsi initiator (iscsiadm)
By using the SMB client
By installing the Nutanix Guest Agent

questions.explanation: Linux servers use the open-iscsi initiator tools (iscsiadm) to discover and connect to Nutanix Volume Group iSCSI targets.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is the benefit of configuring multipath I/O (MPIO) for Nutanix Volumes?

options.text: It doubles the storage capacity
It provides path redundancy and load balancing for iSCSI connections
It enables encryption of iSCSI traffic
It increases the maximum volume size

questions.explanation: MPIO provides path redundancy and load balancing across multiple iSCSI paths, improving both reliability and performance.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is Nutanix Files Smart DR?

options.text: A backup solution that copies files to tape
Share-level replication to a remote Nutanix Files instance for disaster recovery
A deduplication technology for Files
An automated share deletion feature

questions.explanation: Smart DR provides share-level replication to a remote Nutanix Files deployment, enabling disaster recovery with failover and failback capabilities.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: After a Smart DR failover activates the shares on the remote site, what additional step is required for client access?

options.text: Manually reconfigure all client drive mappings
Update DNS records to point to the remote file server
Reinstall the iSCSI initiator on all clients
Re-join the remote file server to Active Directory

questions.explanation: DNS records must be updated to point the file server name to the IP addresses of the remote site's FSVMs so clients can reconnect transparently.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which Nutanix Files data protection mechanism creates point-in-time copies of shares?

options.text: Erasure coding
Snapshots
Volume Groups
Lifecycle policies

questions.explanation: Snapshots create point-in-time copies of shares that can be used for data protection, SSR, and recovery purposes.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is Nutanix Files Smart Tiering?

options.text: A feature that migrates hot data to SSD
A feature that moves cold (infrequently accessed) data to S3-compatible object storage
A feature that compresses all files based on access frequency
A feature that tiers data between different file servers

questions.explanation: Smart Tiering automatically moves cold (infrequently accessed) data from Nutanix Files to S3-compatible object storage to optimize on-cluster capacity.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What happens when a user accesses a file that has been tiered to object storage by Smart Tiering?

options.text: The user receives an error message
The file is automatically recalled from object storage transparently
The user must manually retrieve the file from the object store
The user is redirected to the object store endpoint

questions.explanation: Smart Tiering automatically and transparently recalls files from object storage when a user accesses them, with no manual intervention required.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to configure an NFS export that only allows connections from the 10.0.1.0/24 subnet. Which setting should be configured?

options.text: Firewall rule in Prism Central
Client allowlist on the NFS export
Security group on the FSVM
Network policy in MSP

questions.explanation: The client allowlist on the NFS export should be configured to permit only the 10.0.1.0/24 subnet, restricting access to authorized clients.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which Nutanix Files quota type should be used to prevent a single user from consuming more than 5 GB of space?

options.text: Per-share soft quota
Per-share hard quota
Per-user hard quota
Per-user soft quota

questions.explanation: A per-user hard quota of 5 GB will enforce a strict limit, blocking writes for any individual user who reaches the 5 GB threshold.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An administrator configures a per-share soft quota of 500 GB. A user writes 510 GB of data. What is the outcome?

options.text: The write at 500 GB is blocked
The data is written successfully but an alert/notification is generated
Data beyond 500 GB is automatically deleted
The FSVM is paused

questions.explanation: With a soft quota, the write is allowed to proceed but a notification or alert is generated to inform administrators that the quota threshold has been exceeded.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: In a distributed share, what determines which FSVM hosts a particular file?

options.text: The file extension
The Top-Level Directory (TLD) the file resides in, which is assigned to a specific FSVM
A random hash of the file name
The time of creation

questions.explanation: Files in a distributed share are located on the FSVM that owns the Top-Level Directory (TLD) where the file is stored.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: A company needs a Nutanix Files share where a team of 50 users will collaborate on a shared project folder. The data set is 200 GB. Which share type is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Distributed share
Standard share
Home share
WORM share

questions.explanation: A standard share is appropriate for a smaller collaborative dataset with moderate user count, as simplicity is preferred when throughput demands are modest.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which protocol-specific permission model is used when an SMB-native multi-protocol share is accessed by an NFS client?

options.text: POSIX permissions derived from Windows NTFS ACLs via RFC 2307 mapping
NFS-native POSIX permissions independently
No permissions are enforced for NFS clients
LDAP group permissions only

questions.explanation: In an SMB-native multi-protocol share, NFS users are mapped to Windows identities via RFC 2307, and permissions are derived from the NTFS ACL model.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to enable encryption for NFS data in transit on a Nutanix Files export. Which Kerberos security flavor should be selected?

options.text: krb5
krb5i
krb5p
AUTH_SYS

questions.explanation: krb5p (Kerberos with privacy) provides encryption of all NFS data in transit, in addition to authentication and integrity checking.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What must be configured in Active Directory for multi-protocol share user mapping to work correctly?

options.text: Service Principal Names (SPNs)
RFC 2307 attributes (uidNumber, gidNumber) on user accounts
Group Policy Objects (GPOs)
Certificate templates

questions.explanation: RFC 2307 attributes (uidNumber and gidNumber) must be populated on AD user accounts to enable correct UID/GID mapping for NFS access.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An administrator creates a bucket in Nutanix Objects. What is the FIRST step for enabling programmatic S3 access?

options.text: Configure a lifecycle policy
Generate an access key and secret key pair for the user
Enable WORM on the bucket
Create a bucket policy

questions.explanation: The first step is generating access key and secret key credentials for the user, which are required for S3 API authentication.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which JSON element in a Nutanix Objects bucket policy specifies who the policy applies to?

options.text: "Action"
"Resource"
"Principal"
"Effect"

questions.explanation: The "Principal" element in the bucket policy JSON specifies which user(s) or account(s) the policy applies to.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An organization needs to ensure that regulatory documents stored in Nutanix Objects cannot be deleted for 7 years. Which feature should be enabled?

options.text: Versioning
Lifecycle policies
WORM with a 7-year retention period
Bucket replication

questions.explanation: WORM with a 7-year retention period ensures that objects are immutable and cannot be deleted or modified for the entire retention duration.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An object is uploaded to a WORM-enabled bucket at 2:00 PM. At what time does the 24-hour grace period end?

options.text: 2:00 AM the next day
2:00 PM the next day
Midnight the same day
24 hours from the bucket creation time

questions.explanation: The 24-hour grace period is calculated from the object upload time, so it ends at 2:00 PM the next day.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: After WORM retention expires on an object, what can an administrator do?

options.text: Nothing — WORM objects are permanent
Delete or modify the object normally
Only extend the retention further
Only add tags to the object

questions.explanation: Once the WORM retention period expires, the object can be deleted or modified like any normal object.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: How does enabling versioning on a Nutanix Objects bucket affect storage consumption?

options.text: It has no effect on storage
Storage consumption increases because all versions of each object are retained
Storage consumption decreases due to deduplication
Only the latest two versions are stored

questions.explanation: Versioning retains all versions of every object, so storage consumption increases proportionally with the number of object versions.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: A lifecycle policy is configured to expire objects older than 90 days. A WORM-locked object has a 365-day retention. What happens at 90 days?

options.text: The object is deleted by the lifecycle policy
The object is NOT deleted because WORM retention takes precedence
The lifecycle policy is automatically disabled
A conflict error is generated

questions.explanation: WORM retention takes precedence over lifecycle policies, so the object remains protected until the full 365-day retention expires.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which statement about multipart upload in Nutanix Objects is correct?

options.text: Multipart upload is limited to 1 GB per object
Multipart upload allows uploading objects up to 5 TB by splitting them into parts
Multipart upload is only available for WORM buckets
Multipart upload requires versioning to be enabled

questions.explanation: Multipart upload allows large objects (up to 5 TB) to be uploaded in manageable parts, which can be uploaded in parallel for better performance.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which Nutanix Volumes feature allows multiple external hosts to access the same Volume Group simultaneously?

options.text: Shared Volume Group with multiple initiator IQNs whitelisted
SMB multi-channel
NFS multi-export
Clustered file system

questions.explanation: A Volume Group can be shared among multiple hosts by whitelisting multiple initiator IQNs, enabling clustered applications like Oracle RAC.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An administrator configures CHAP authentication on a Nutanix Volume Group. What must also be configured on the external server?

options.text: Kerberos keytab
The same CHAP credentials (username/secret) in the iSCSI initiator
SSL certificate
RADIUS server address

questions.explanation: The CHAP username and secret configured on the Volume Group must match the credentials configured on the external server's iSCSI initiator.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: When configuring Smart DR for a Nutanix Files share, what is the replication granularity?

options.text: Per-file replication
Share-level replication
Entire file server replication only
Per-FSVM replication

questions.explanation: Smart DR operates at the share level, allowing administrators to selectively replicate specific shares to a remote site.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: During a Smart DR failover, which action must the administrator take to restore client connectivity?

options.text: Reconfigure all client IP addresses
Update DNS to point the file server name to the remote site's FSVM IPs
Create new shares on the remote site
Re-join the file server to AD

questions.explanation: Updating DNS records is the critical step during failover to redirect client traffic from the failed primary site to the remote recovery site.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: What is the Smart DR failback process?

options.text: Data is manually copied back to the original site
Reverse replication synchronizes changes back to the original site, then shares are failed back
The original site is rebuilt from scratch
Failback is not supported

questions.explanation: Failback uses reverse replication to synchronize any changes made at the remote site back to the original site before restoring the primary.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Protection Domains for Nutanix Files can include which data?

options.text: Only Volume Groups
File server shares and their associated metadata
Only S3 buckets
Only Prism Central configuration

questions.explanation: Protection Domains for Files encompass the file server shares and their associated metadata for snapshot and replication purposes.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to provide per-user home directories that scale across all FSVMs. Which configuration is BEST?

options.text: Create individual standard shares for each user
Create a distributed share with home share functionality enabled
Create NFS exports for each user
Create Volume Groups for each user

questions.explanation: A distributed share with home share functionality provides per-user directories that scale across all FSVMs in the deployment.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: When ABE is enabled on an SMB share, what do users see when browsing the share?

options.text: All files and folders regardless of permissions
Only files and folders they have at least read access to
Only files they own
An empty folder structure until they search for specific files

questions.explanation: With ABE enabled, users only see files and folders for which they have at least read permission, hiding all other content from their view.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to configure an NFS export with Kerberos authentication. Which NFS version is required?

options.text: NFSv2
NFSv3
NFSv4 or higher
Any NFS version supports Kerberos

questions.explanation: NFSv4 (or higher) is required for Kerberos authentication support on NFS exports.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: A user accidentally overwrites an important file on an SMB share. SSR is enabled with daily snapshots. How can the user recover the file?

options.text: Contact the administrator to restore from a backup
Right-click the file > Properties > Previous Versions > Restore the desired version
Access the .snapshot directory from Windows Explorer
Use the Nutanix Objects S3 API to retrieve the file

questions.explanation: Windows users can self-service restore by right-clicking the file, going to Properties > Previous Versions, and restoring the desired version from available snapshots.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which Nutanix Objects operation can be used to organize objects with metadata without modifying the objects themselves?

options.text: Lifecycle policy
Bucket policy
Object tagging
WORM lock

questions.explanation: Object tagging attaches key-value metadata pairs to objects for categorization and management without modifying the object data itself.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: When an NFS-native multi-protocol share is accessed by SMB clients, how are permissions handled?

options.text: SMB users are mapped to POSIX UIDs/GIDs via RFC 2307 and inherit POSIX permissions
SMB users have full admin access
SMB access is read-only
SMB access is denied entirely

questions.explanation: In an NFS-native multi-protocol share, SMB users are mapped to POSIX identities via RFC 2307, and POSIX permission semantics are applied.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An administrator enables Smart Tiering on a Nutanix Files share. Which type of data is automatically moved to object storage?

options.text: Data larger than 1 GB
Cold (infrequently accessed) data based on configurable policies
All data older than 30 days
Only compressed files

questions.explanation: Smart Tiering moves cold data — files that have not been accessed for a configurable period — to S3-compatible object storage.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An external Linux server is configured with MPIO for Nutanix Volumes. What is the primary benefit during a CVM failure?

options.text: The volume is automatically snapshotted
I/O continues on the surviving path through another CVM without interruption
The volume is migrated to a different cluster
MPIO encrypts the iSCSI traffic

questions.explanation: MPIO provides path redundancy, so when one CVM fails, I/O automatically continues through the surviving path via another CVM.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: Which combination of Nutanix Objects features provides the STRONGEST protection against ransomware for backup data?

options.text: Versioning + lifecycle policies
WORM + versioning
Bucket policies + tagging
Lifecycle policies + multipart upload

questions.explanation: WORM combined with versioning provides the strongest ransomware protection: WORM prevents deletion/modification, and versioning preserves all object versions.
questions.domain: Domain 2: Configure and Utilize Nutanix Unified Storage

questions.questionText: An administrator is reviewing the File Analytics dashboard and notices a spike in write operations from a single user account across multiple shares. What should the administrator investigate first?

options.text: Check the ransomware detection alerts for entropy-based anomalies
Review the Data Lens compliance scan results
Examine the Objects lifecycle policy execution log
Verify the Volume Group iSCSI session count

questions.explanation: File Analytics provides ransomware detection using entropy-based analysis. A spike in write operations from a single user across multiple shares is a classic indicator of potential ransomware activity, and the administrator should check ransomware alerts first.

questions.questionText: A Nutanix administrator is planning to deploy File Analytics for a Nutanix Files cluster. Which statement accurately describes a deployment requirement?

options.text: File Analytics is deployed as a container on the Files cluster VMs
File Analytics is deployed as a separate VM and managed through Prism Element
File Analytics must be deployed on Prism Central and supports multiple Files instances simultaneously
File Analytics is a SaaS service activated through the Nutanix Cloud Portal

questions.explanation: File Analytics is deployed as a dedicated, separate VM that is managed through Prism Element. Each File Analytics VM connects to a single Nutanix Files instance.

questions.questionText: An organization has three separate Nutanix Files instances across different clusters. The storage team wants to deploy File Analytics for all of them. How many File Analytics VMs are required?

options.text: One File Analytics VM deployed on Prism Central to monitor all three
Two File Analytics VMs — one for production and one shared for the remaining two
Three File Analytics VMs — one per Files instance
One File Analytics VM deployed on any cluster with network access to all three

questions.explanation: Each File Analytics VM connects to exactly one Nutanix Files instance. Therefore, three separate Files instances require three individual File Analytics VMs.

questions.questionText: An administrator attempts to deploy File Analytics but receives an error that the deployment cannot proceed. Which prerequisite is the administrator most likely missing?

options.text: Prism Central must be registered and the Files instance must be managed from Prism Central
Prism Element must be accessible and the Files instance must be running on the same cluster
Data Lens must be activated before File Analytics can be deployed on-premises
The Objects store must be configured to provide the analytics metadata repository

questions.explanation: File Analytics deployment is performed through Prism Element, and the Files instance must be running on the same cluster where the File Analytics VM will be deployed. Prism Central registration is not a prerequisite for File Analytics deployment.

questions.questionText: An administrator opens the File Analytics dashboard and wants to quickly identify which users are consuming the most storage. Which dashboard widget provides this information?

options.text: Anomaly Detection Summary
Top Contributors
Audit Trail History
Capacity Trending Forecast

questions.explanation: The Top Contributors widget on the File Analytics dashboard displays the users who are consuming the most storage or performing the most file operations, enabling quick identification of heavy storage consumers.

questions.questionText: A storage administrator needs to determine the breakdown of data stored on a Nutanix Files share by file type. Where in File Analytics can this information be found?

options.text: The audit trail detail view filtered by file extension
The file distribution dashboard showing distribution by type, size, and age
The ransomware detection panel listing file entropy by extension
The Data Lens classification report for the Files share

questions.explanation: The File Analytics dashboard includes a file distribution view that categorizes data by type, size, and age. This provides a visual breakdown of what kinds of files are stored on each share.

questions.questionText: An IT manager asks the storage team to provide a report showing how storage consumption on Nutanix Files shares has changed over the last 90 days, including a forecast. Which File Analytics feature should the team use?

options.text: Audit trail export filtered to the last 90 days
Capacity trending with growth rate analysis and prediction
File distribution by age showing files older than 90 days
Anomaly detection timeline with a 90-day lookback window

questions.explanation: File Analytics provides capacity trending that includes growth rate analysis and predictive forecasting for Files shares. This feature can show historical consumption and project future growth.

questions.questionText: A compliance officer needs to know every action a specific user performed on a particular SMB share over the last 30 days, including file reads and permission changes. Which File Analytics capability provides this level of detail?

options.text: Top Contributors filtered by username and share name
Anomaly detection alert log for the specified user
Audit trail tracking per user per share
Data Lens permission scanning for the target share

questions.explanation: File Analytics audit trails track create, read, write, delete, and permission change operations per user per share, providing the detailed activity history the compliance officer needs.

questions.questionText: An administrator is reviewing audit trails in File Analytics and wants to identify all files that had their access permissions modified last week. Which operation type should the administrator filter on?

options.text: Write operations
Delete operations
Permission change operations
Metadata read operations

questions.explanation: File Analytics audit trails specifically track permission change operations as a distinct operation type alongside create, read, write, and delete. Filtering on permission changes will show exactly the files that had access modifications.

questions.questionText: A security team receives an alert from File Analytics indicating that a user account has renamed hundreds of files with unusual extensions in a short time period. Which File Analytics detection mechanism most likely triggered this alert?

options.text: Capacity trending threshold breach
Ransomware detection using entropy-based analysis
Audit trail anomaly from excessive read operations
File distribution deviation from baseline type ratios

questions.explanation: File Analytics ransomware detection uses entropy-based analysis to identify suspicious file modification patterns. Mass renaming of files with unusual extensions is a strong ransomware indicator that triggers this detection mechanism.

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to proactively block known ransomware file extensions on Nutanix Files. Which File Analytics feature supports this?

options.text: Anomaly detection with automatic quarantine policies
Configurable blocklist of file extensions
Data Lens behavioral analysis with extension filtering
Audit trail alerts configured per file extension

questions.explanation: File Analytics provides a configurable blocklist of file extensions as part of its ransomware protection. Administrators can add known ransomware extensions to this blocklist to proactively detect and alert on these file types.

questions.questionText: A Nutanix administrator configures ransomware detection in File Analytics and wants to receive notifications when suspicious activity is detected. Where are these alerts surfaced?

options.text: Only in the File Analytics dashboard under the Anomaly tab
In Prism alerts and the File Analytics dashboard
Exclusively through email notifications configured in Data Lens
In the Objects store event log linked to the Files metadata

questions.explanation: Ransomware detection alerts from File Analytics are surfaced in Prism alerts as well as within the File Analytics dashboard, ensuring administrators can be notified through the standard Nutanix alerting framework.

questions.questionText: An administrator notices that the File Analytics ransomware detection has generated a false positive for a legitimate batch processing application that creates high-entropy compressed files. What is the best corrective action?

options.text: Disable ransomware detection entirely until the batch job completes
Update the blocklist to exclude the file extensions used by the application
Migrate the batch application data to an Objects bucket to bypass file-level monitoring
Increase the entropy analysis threshold and adjust the anomaly detection configuration

questions.explanation: When legitimate applications generate high-entropy files, the administrator should adjust the entropy analysis threshold and anomaly detection configuration to reduce false positives while maintaining protection, rather than disabling detection entirely.

questions.questionText: A storage administrator reviews File Analytics capacity trending and sees that a particular share is growing at 15% per month. The share currently uses 8 TB of its 20 TB allocation. Approximately when will the share reach capacity based on this trend?

options.text: In about 4 months
In about 6 months
In about 8 months
In about 12 months

questions.explanation: At 15% monthly compound growth starting from 8 TB, the share would approach 20 TB in roughly 6 months (8 → 9.2 → 10.6 → 12.2 → 14.0 → 16.1 → 18.5 TB). Capacity trending in File Analytics provides this type of predictive analysis.

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to compare file age distribution across two different Nutanix Files shares to identify stale data candidates. Which File Analytics dashboard capability supports this?

options.text: Audit trail cross-share comparison report
File distribution analysis by age per share
Data Lens stale data classification scan
Capacity trending differential analysis

questions.explanation: File Analytics provides file distribution analysis that can show data categorized by age on a per-share basis. The administrator can review the age distribution for each share to identify where stale data resides.

questions.questionText: A company recently experienced a ransomware incident and wants to review which files were affected. An administrator opens File Analytics and needs to trace all file modifications made by the compromised account during the attack window. Which feature combination provides the most complete picture?

options.text: Ransomware detection alerts combined with the audit trail for the compromised user
Top Contributors widget combined with capacity trending for the affected shares
File distribution by type combined with anomaly detection summary
Data Lens compliance scan combined with permission scanning results

questions.explanation: The ransomware detection alerts identify the scope of suspicious activity, while the audit trail provides a detailed, per-user, per-share record of every create, read, write, delete, and permission change operation during the attack window.

questions.questionText: An administrator deploys File Analytics and notices that historical data prior to deployment is not visible in the audit trail. Why is this expected behavior?

options.text: File Analytics only collects audit data from the moment of deployment forward
Historical audit data requires a separate license activation in Prism Element
The audit trail backfill process runs for 72 hours before historical data appears
File Analytics requires a Data Lens connection to import historical audit records

questions.explanation: File Analytics begins collecting audit trail data from the point of deployment. It does not retroactively capture historical file operations that occurred before the File Analytics VM was deployed and connected to the Files instance.

questions.questionText: A Nutanix Files administrator notices that File Analytics is showing high latency in its dashboard updates. Which action is most appropriate to investigate?

options.text: Check the File Analytics VM resource allocation for CPU and memory constraints
Restart the Prism Central service to reset the analytics connection
Increase the Objects store replication factor for the analytics metadata
Redeploy the Data Lens connector to improve data pipeline throughput

questions.explanation: Since File Analytics runs as a separate VM, performance issues such as dashboard latency are often related to the VM's resource allocation. Checking CPU and memory usage on the File Analytics VM is the appropriate first step.

questions.questionText: An administrator is configuring File Analytics for a new Nutanix Files deployment. During setup, the wizard asks for credentials. What are these credentials used for?

options.text: Authenticating the File Analytics VM to the Objects store for metadata backup
Connecting the File Analytics VM to the Nutanix Files instance for data collection
Registering the File Analytics VM with Data Lens for cloud-based reporting
Enabling CHAP authentication between File Analytics and the Volume Group

questions.explanation: During File Analytics deployment, the credentials provided are used to authenticate and connect the File Analytics VM to the specific Nutanix Files instance it will monitor for data collection and audit trail recording.

questions.questionText: A company wants to perform file analytics on a Nutanix Files cluster but has a strict policy against deploying additional VMs on the cluster. Which alternative can they consider?

options.text: Enable built-in analytics within the Nutanix Files configuration without a separate VM
Use Data Lens as a SaaS-based alternative that does not require an on-premises VM
Deploy File Analytics on Prism Central to share resources with existing management services
Use the Objects monitoring dashboard as a substitute for File Analytics

questions.explanation: Data Lens is the SaaS (cloud-based) alternative to File Analytics. While File Analytics requires a dedicated on-premises VM, Data Lens provides analytics capabilities from the cloud without requiring additional on-premises VM resources.

questions.questionText: A storage team needs visibility into file operations across Nutanix Files clusters in multiple data centers from a single interface. Which Nutanix solution provides this capability?

options.text: File Analytics with cross-cluster federation enabled
Prism Central with Files Manager multi-site view
Data Lens with multi-cluster visibility
Objects Browser with cross-site analytics integration

questions.explanation: Data Lens is a SaaS-based solution that provides multi-cluster visibility, allowing administrators to monitor and analyze file operations across multiple Nutanix Files clusters and data centers from a single cloud-based interface.

questions.questionText: An administrator is comparing Data Lens and File Analytics to determine which solution to deploy. Which statement accurately describes a key difference?

options.text: Data Lens is deployed as an on-premises VM while File Analytics is cloud-based
File Analytics is a SaaS solution while Data Lens requires an on-premises VM
Data Lens is a SaaS (cloud-based) service while File Analytics is an on-premises VM
Both solutions are deployed identically but Data Lens adds Objects monitoring

questions.explanation: The key architectural difference is that Data Lens is a cloud-based SaaS service managed through the Nutanix Cloud Portal, while File Analytics is deployed as a dedicated on-premises VM managed through Prism Element.

questions.questionText: A compliance team requires automated detection of personally identifiable information (PII) stored on Nutanix Files shares. Which Nutanix solution provides data classification with PII detection?

options.text: File Analytics with audit trail keyword scanning
Data Lens with data classification and PII detection
Prism Central with compliance policy enforcement
Objects lifecycle policies with content-aware tiering

questions.explanation: Data Lens includes data classification capabilities with PII detection, sensitive data tagging, and custom regex patterns. This enables automated identification of personally identifiable information stored on Nutanix Files shares.

questions.questionText: An organization needs to identify shares on Nutanix Files that have overly permissive access settings, potentially exposing sensitive data. Which Data Lens feature addresses this requirement?

options.text: Ransomware behavioral analysis
Capacity trending and growth prediction
Permissions scanning for open shares and excessive permissions
Audit trail export with access control filtering

questions.explanation: Data Lens permissions scanning identifies open shares, excessive permissions, and stale data. This feature helps organizations discover and remediate overly permissive access settings that could expose sensitive data.

questions.questionText: A security auditor asks the Nutanix team to identify all shares that contain files matching a specific custom data pattern, such as internal project codes in a particular format. Which Data Lens capability should be used?

options.text: Anomaly detection with custom pattern matching
Data classification with custom regex patterns
Audit trail search with regular expression filters
File distribution analysis filtered by file naming convention

questions.explanation: Data Lens data classification supports custom regex patterns in addition to built-in PII detection. Administrators can define custom patterns to identify and tag files containing specific data formats like internal project codes.

questions.questionText: A Nutanix administrator wants to use Data Lens to detect potential ransomware attacks across the organization's Files environment. How does Data Lens approach ransomware protection differently than File Analytics?

options.text: Data Lens uses file extension blocklists exclusively while File Analytics uses entropy analysis
Data Lens provides behavioral analysis with anomaly scoring while File Analytics uses entropy-based analysis
Data Lens quarantines files automatically while File Analytics only generates alerts
Data Lens scans files with antivirus signatures while File Analytics monitors access patterns

questions.explanation: Data Lens provides ransomware protection through behavioral analysis and anomaly scoring with automatic alerting, while File Analytics uses entropy-based analysis. Both detect ransomware but employ different analytical approaches.

questions.questionText: An administrator has Data Lens enabled and receives an anomaly score alert for a user account. What does a high anomaly score in Data Lens indicate?

options.text: The user has exceeded their storage quota on multiple shares
The user's file access behavior deviates significantly from their established baseline
The user's CHAP authentication has failed multiple times
The user has accessed Objects buckets without proper IAM credentials

questions.explanation: Data Lens anomaly scoring measures how much a user's current behavior deviates from their established baseline patterns. A high anomaly score indicates significant behavioral deviation, which could indicate compromised credentials or insider threats.

questions.questionText: A multinational company needs to scan Nutanix Files data for compliance with data privacy regulations, including tagging files that contain sensitive personal data. Which Data Lens feature set addresses this?

options.text: Audit trail with compliance export and regulatory tagging
Data classification with sensitive data tagging and compliance scanning
Permissions scanning with regulatory access control templates
Capacity trending with compliance-based retention forecasting

questions.explanation: Data Lens provides data classification with sensitive data tagging and compliance scanning capabilities. These features enable organizations to automatically identify, classify, and tag files containing sensitive data for regulatory compliance.

questions.questionText: An administrator notices that Data Lens identifies several "stale data" items across multiple shares. What does Data Lens classify as stale data?

options.text: Files that have been flagged by ransomware detection as potentially encrypted
Files that have not been accessed or modified for an extended period
Files that exceed the maximum size threshold configured in the share policy
Files that have been moved between shares more than three times

questions.explanation: Data Lens permissions scanning identifies stale data as files that have not been accessed or modified for an extended period. Identifying stale data helps organizations reclaim storage and reduce the attack surface.

questions.questionText: An organization uses both File Analytics and Data Lens. An administrator wants to understand when to use each tool. Which statement best describes the appropriate use case for each?

options.text: File Analytics for multi-cluster views and Data Lens for single-cluster deep dives
File Analytics for detailed on-prem auditing of a single Files instance and Data Lens for multi-cluster SaaS-based visibility and classification
File Analytics for Objects monitoring and Data Lens for Files monitoring
File Analytics for ransomware detection only and Data Lens for all other analytics tasks

questions.explanation: File Analytics is best suited for detailed, on-premises auditing of a single Files instance with deep audit trails, while Data Lens provides SaaS-based multi-cluster visibility, data classification, compliance scanning, and permissions analysis across the organization.

questions.questionText: A Data Lens administrator wants to configure automatic alerts when ransomware-like behavior is detected. Which Data Lens mechanism provides this capability?

options.text: Capacity threshold alerts configured per share
Anomaly scoring with automatic alerting based on behavioral analysis
File extension blocklist violation notifications
Audit trail anomaly triggers based on operation count thresholds

questions.explanation: Data Lens provides ransomware protection through behavioral analysis with anomaly scoring and automatic alerting. When user behavior matches ransomware patterns, Data Lens automatically generates alerts based on the anomaly scoring system.

questions.questionText: An administrator configures Data Lens permissions scanning and discovers that several shares have "Everyone" access with full control. What is the recommended next step based on Data Lens findings?

options.text: Enable ransomware detection to monitor the open shares more aggressively
Remediate excessive permissions by restricting access to authorized groups only
Move the affected shares to an Objects bucket for better access control
Deploy additional File Analytics VMs to increase audit coverage on the open shares

questions.explanation: When Data Lens permissions scanning identifies shares with excessive permissions such as "Everyone" with full control, the recommended action is to remediate by restricting access to only authorized groups, reducing the security risk and attack surface.

questions.questionText: An administrator accesses the Objects dashboard in Prism Central and wants to review the performance of a specific S3-compatible bucket. Which metrics are available on the Objects dashboard?

options.text: File distribution by type, user audit trails, and anomaly detection scores
Bucket statistics, throughput, latency, and IOPS per bucket
Volume Group IOPS, iSCSI session count, and CHAP authentication status
Data classification results, PII detection count, and compliance scan status

questions.explanation: The Objects dashboard in Prism Central provides bucket-level metrics including bucket statistics, throughput, latency, and IOPS per bucket, enabling administrators to monitor the performance of individual S3-compatible buckets.

questions.questionText: A developer reports that S3 PUT operations to a Nutanix Objects bucket are experiencing higher than normal latency. Which Objects performance metric should the administrator check first?

options.text: GET latency per bucket in the Objects dashboard
PUT latency per bucket in the Objects dashboard
iSCSI session latency in the Volume Group monitor
File Analytics write operation latency for the connected share

questions.explanation: The Objects dashboard provides specific GET and PUT latency metrics per bucket. Since the developer is reporting slow PUT operations, the administrator should check the PUT latency metric in the Objects dashboard to confirm and investigate the issue.

questions.questionText: An administrator receives an alert that a Nutanix Objects store is approaching its capacity threshold. Which monitoring feature provided this alert?

options.text: File Analytics capacity trending alert for the Objects metadata share
Objects alerts for capacity thresholds configured in Prism Central
Data Lens storage consumption anomaly detection
Volume Group storage utilization alert from the iSCSI monitor

questions.explanation: Nutanix Objects provides configurable alerts in Prism Central for capacity thresholds, performance degradation, and node health. The capacity threshold alert notifies administrators when the object store approaches its configured storage limit.

questions.questionText: A Nutanix administrator needs to track how a specific Objects lifecycle policy is performing, including whether objects are being transitioned and deleted as expected. Which Objects feature provides this visibility?

options.text: File Analytics audit trail filtered to Objects operations
Objects lifecycle monitoring with policy execution tracking
Data Lens compliance scan for Objects data retention
Prism Central task list filtered by Objects background jobs

questions.explanation: Objects lifecycle monitoring includes policy execution tracking, transition status, and deletion queue monitoring. This allows administrators to verify that lifecycle policies are executing correctly and objects are being transitioned or deleted on schedule.

questions.questionText: An administrator notices that objects configured for deletion by a lifecycle policy are accumulating in the deletion queue rather than being removed. What should the administrator check in the Objects monitoring?

options.text: The Objects bucket versioning setting that may be preventing permanent deletion
The lifecycle policy deletion queue status and any associated error alerts
The File Analytics ransomware blocklist that may be protecting the files
The Data Lens data classification tags that may mark objects as retention-required

questions.explanation: Objects lifecycle monitoring includes deletion queue tracking. When objects accumulate instead of being deleted, the administrator should check the deletion queue status and associated alerts to identify any errors or bottlenecks in policy execution.

questions.questionText: A storage administrator needs to determine how much capacity each individual bucket is consuming in a Nutanix Objects store. Which monitoring approach provides this data?

options.text: File Analytics file distribution report filtered by bucket name
Per-bucket usage metrics in the Objects capacity management dashboard
Data Lens storage classification grouped by object store target
Prism Element storage pool utilization filtered by Objects containers

questions.explanation: Objects capacity management in Prism Central provides per-bucket usage metrics, total store utilization, and growth trending. This allows administrators to see individual bucket consumption within the object store.

questions.questionText: An administrator is planning capacity for a Nutanix Objects store that is experiencing 20% monthly growth. Which Objects monitoring feature helps predict when additional capacity will be needed?

options.text: File Analytics capacity trending applied to the Objects metadata share
Objects capacity management with growth trending
Data Lens growth anomaly detection for Objects stores
Prism Central runway analysis for Volume Groups backing the Objects store

questions.explanation: Objects capacity management includes growth trending capabilities that analyze historical consumption patterns and project future capacity needs, helping administrators plan expansion before the store reaches its limits.

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to set up proactive monitoring for a Nutanix Objects store to be alerted when performance drops below acceptable levels. Which Objects alerting capability should be configured?

options.text: File Analytics anomaly detection with custom performance thresholds
Objects alerts for performance degradation in Prism Central
Data Lens behavioral analysis alerts for Objects access patterns
Volume Group performance alerts applied to Objects backend storage

questions.explanation: Objects alerts in Prism Central can be configured for performance degradation, capacity thresholds, and node health. Configuring performance degradation alerts enables proactive notification when Objects store performance drops below acceptable levels.

questions.questionText: An administrator reviews the Objects dashboard and sees a significant spike in GET operations per second on a specific bucket. What additional metric should be reviewed to assess impact on the bucket's performance?

options.text: The bucket's audit trail in File Analytics
The bucket's GET latency and data transfer rate
The bucket's iSCSI session count and initiator connections
The bucket's file age distribution and stale data percentage

questions.explanation: When GET operations per second spike, the administrator should check the corresponding GET latency and data transfer rate to assess whether the increased operations are causing performance degradation for that bucket.

questions.questionText: A Nutanix Objects store has multiple buckets. An administrator needs to determine which bucket is responsible for the highest throughput on the cluster. Where is this information found?

options.text: Prism Element storage container performance view
Objects dashboard in Prism Central showing throughput per bucket
File Analytics top contributors view grouped by Objects bucket
Data Lens multi-cluster dashboard with Objects throughput overlay

questions.explanation: The Objects dashboard in Prism Central displays throughput metrics on a per-bucket basis, allowing administrators to identify which bucket is generating the most throughput on the Objects store.

questions.questionText: An administrator receives an Objects alert about node health degradation in the Objects store. What does this alert typically indicate?

options.text: A Files VM hosting Objects metadata has run out of memory
One or more nodes in the Objects store cluster are experiencing issues
The iSCSI path to the Objects backend Volume Group has failed
The Data Lens connection to the Objects store has timed out

questions.explanation: Objects alerts for node health indicate that one or more nodes participating in the Objects store infrastructure are experiencing issues such as high resource utilization, connectivity problems, or hardware faults.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to verify that a recently created Objects lifecycle policy that transitions objects to a lower storage tier after 90 days is working correctly. Which metric should be monitored?

options.text: Objects file distribution showing age of objects in each tier
Objects lifecycle transition status in the lifecycle monitoring dashboard
File Analytics capacity trending showing reduced primary tier usage
Data Lens data classification showing tier assignment per object

questions.explanation: Objects lifecycle monitoring provides transition status tracking that shows whether objects are being transitioned between tiers as specified by the lifecycle policy, allowing verification that the 90-day transition rule is executing correctly.

questions.questionText: An administrator is monitoring a Nutanix Volume Group and notices inconsistent performance reported by the application using it. Which Volume Group performance metrics should the administrator review?

options.text: File distribution by type, audit trail, and anomaly detection
IOPS, throughput, and latency per Volume Group
Bucket statistics, GET/PUT operations per second, and data transfer rate
Data classification results, PII count, and compliance scan status

questions.explanation: Volume Group performance metrics include IOPS, throughput, and latency measured per Volume Group. These metrics help administrators identify whether the inconsistent application performance correlates with storage-level performance variations.

questions.questionText: A database administrator reports that a critical database running on a Nutanix Volume Group is experiencing periodic latency spikes. Which monitoring approach should the Nutanix administrator take first?

options.text: Check the Objects bucket latency metrics for the database backup target
Review the Volume Group latency metrics during the reported spike periods
Examine the File Analytics audit trail for concurrent file access on the cluster
Check the Data Lens anomaly score for the database server's user account

questions.explanation: The Nutanix administrator should first review the Volume Group's latency metrics during the reported spike periods to determine whether the latency issue originates at the storage level before investigating other potential causes.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to verify which external hosts are currently connected to a Nutanix Volume Group via iSCSI. Which monitoring feature provides this information?

options.text: File Analytics audit trail showing connected clients
iSCSI session monitoring showing active sessions and initiator IPs
Objects dashboard showing connected S3 client endpoints
Data Lens permissions scanning showing authenticated hosts

questions.explanation: iSCSI session monitoring for Volume Groups shows active sessions, initiator IPs, and connection status, allowing administrators to see exactly which external hosts are currently connected via iSCSI.

questions.questionText: An administrator configures a Volume Group with multiple iSCSI paths for high availability. How can the administrator confirm that all paths are active and available?

options.text: Check the File Analytics dashboard for multi-path file access patterns
Review the Volume Group health metrics including path availability status
Verify the Objects lifecycle policy is not competing for the same storage paths
Examine the Data Lens connection health dashboard for iSCSI path status

questions.explanation: Volume Group health monitoring includes path availability status, allowing administrators to verify that all configured iSCSI paths are active and functioning correctly for high availability configurations.

questions.questionText: An administrator enables CHAP authentication on a Volume Group to enhance security. After deployment, the administrator wants to monitor for unauthorized access attempts. What should be monitored?

options.text: File Analytics ransomware detection alerts for the Volume Group's backing files
CHAP authentication monitoring for failed authentication attempts
Objects access logging for S3 requests targeting the Volume Group endpoint
Data Lens behavioral analysis for iSCSI session anomalies

questions.explanation: CHAP authentication monitoring tracks failed authentication attempts and session security status for Volume Groups. Monitoring failed CHAP authentications helps identify unauthorized access attempts against the iSCSI targets.

questions.questionText: A Nutanix administrator notices that a Volume Group shows degraded disk status in the health monitoring panel. What is the most likely implication?

options.text: The Files share backed by the Volume Group will become read-only
One or more virtual disks in the Volume Group may be experiencing issues affecting availability
The Objects store connected to the Volume Group will pause lifecycle operations
The Data Lens agent monitoring the Volume Group will lose connectivity

questions.explanation: Volume Group health monitoring includes disk status, which indicates the health of virtual disks within the group. Degraded disk status suggests that one or more virtual disks may be experiencing issues that could affect the Volume Group's performance or availability.

questions.questionText: An administrator is troubleshooting slow iSCSI performance on a Volume Group and wants to check if there are too many active sessions from a single initiator. Where should the administrator look?

options.text: File Analytics top contributors view for the iSCSI-connected host
iSCSI session monitoring showing active session count per initiator
Objects dashboard showing concurrent connections per client endpoint
Prism Central network analytics for iSCSI VLAN traffic distribution

questions.explanation: iSCSI session monitoring for Volume Groups provides details about active sessions per initiator, including session counts and connection status. This helps identify if a single initiator has excessive sessions that could affect performance.

questions.questionText: An administrator reviews Volume Group replication status and sees that a secondary copy is lagging behind the primary. Which Volume Group health metric indicates this condition?

options.text: IOPS variance between primary and secondary Volume Groups
Replication status showing synchronization lag in the Volume Group health dashboard
Objects replication factor mismatch alert in Prism Central
File Analytics audit trail discrepancy between primary and replicated shares

questions.explanation: Volume Group health monitoring includes replication status, which shows the synchronization state between primary and replicated copies. A replication lag is visible in this health metric and indicates the secondary copy is not fully synchronized.

questions.questionText: A security administrator requires a report of all CHAP authentication failures for Volume Groups over the past week. Which monitoring approach provides this data?

options.text: Exporting the File Analytics audit trail filtered by authentication events
Reviewing CHAP authentication monitoring data for failed attempts and session security status
Running a Data Lens compliance scan focused on iSCSI access control
Checking the Objects access log for S3 authentication failures on Volume Group endpoints

questions.explanation: CHAP authentication monitoring specifically tracks failed authentication attempts and session security status for Volume Groups, providing the data needed to generate a report of authentication failures over a given time period.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to monitor the overall throughput of all Volume Groups on a cluster to plan for network bandwidth upgrades. Which metric set is most relevant?

options.text: Objects store data transfer rate aggregated across all buckets
Volume Group throughput metrics aggregated across all Volume Groups
File Analytics write throughput for all shares on the cluster
Data Lens ingestion rate for all connected Files instances

questions.explanation: Volume Group performance metrics include throughput per Volume Group. Aggregating throughput across all Volume Groups on the cluster provides the total iSCSI storage bandwidth consumption needed for network capacity planning.
