questions.questionText: An administrator opens the File Analytics dashboard and sees that the file distribution by size shows 60% of data is in files larger than 1 GB. What is the operational significance of this finding?

options.text: It indicates potential ransomware activity targeting large files
It suggests the storage workload is dominated by large files, which affects capacity planning and backup strategies
It means the Files cluster needs to be rebalanced across additional File Server VMs
It confirms that Data Lens PII scanning will require extended processing time

questions.explanation: File distribution by size provides insight into the workload characteristics of the storage environment. Knowing that the majority of data consists of large files helps with capacity planning, backup window estimation, and understanding storage growth patterns.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to track which applications are generating the most new files on a Nutanix Files share over the past month. Which File Analytics approach is most effective?

options.text: Use the capacity trending forecast to estimate application-driven growth
Review the audit trail for create operations and correlate with user accounts tied to application service accounts
Check the Data Lens data classification for application-generated file types
Review the Objects lifecycle policy execution count for application-specific prefixes

questions.explanation: File Analytics audit trails track create operations per user per share. By filtering on create operations and correlating activity with application service accounts, the administrator can identify which applications are generating the most new files.

questions.questionText: A company has deployed Data Lens and wants to ensure that files containing credit card numbers are automatically identified across all their Nutanix Files shares. Which Data Lens feature should be configured?

options.text: Ransomware behavioral analysis with financial data pattern recognition
Permissions scanning with payment card industry (PCI) templates
Data classification with PII detection for credit card number patterns
Audit trail analysis with keyword search for numeric strings

questions.explanation: Data Lens data classification includes PII detection capabilities that can identify credit card numbers and other sensitive data patterns. This feature automatically scans and tags files containing such data across all connected Nutanix Files shares.

questions.questionText: An administrator is comparing alerts from Data Lens and File Analytics for a suspected ransomware event. Data Lens shows a high anomaly score while File Analytics shows entropy-based alerts. Which statement best explains the complementary nature of these alerts?

options.text: Data Lens and File Analytics use the same detection engine, so the alerts are redundant
Data Lens analyzes behavioral patterns and anomaly scoring while File Analytics analyzes file entropy, providing two different detection perspectives
File Analytics alerts take priority because on-premises detection is always more accurate than cloud-based detection
Data Lens alerts should be ignored if File Analytics has already confirmed the threat

questions.explanation: Data Lens and File Analytics use complementary approaches: Data Lens employs behavioral analysis with anomaly scoring, while File Analytics uses entropy-based analysis. Together they provide two different detection perspectives that strengthen the overall ransomware detection capability.

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to identify Nutanix Files shares where data has not been accessed in over a year and permissions are still set to allow broad access. Which tool provides both pieces of information in a single view?

options.text: File Analytics combining file distribution by age with audit trail access logs
Data Lens combining permissions scanning with stale data identification
Prism Central combining Files Manager share settings with capacity reports
Objects dashboard combining lifecycle transition status with bucket policies

questions.explanation: Data Lens permissions scanning identifies both stale data (files not accessed for extended periods) and excessive permissions (broadly accessible shares), providing the combined visibility needed in a single solution.

questions.questionText: An administrator configures a Nutanix Objects store with tiered storage and lifecycle policies. After several weeks, the administrator wants to verify the total data moved to the lower tier. Where should the administrator look?

options.text: File Analytics capacity trending showing reduced primary storage consumption
Objects lifecycle monitoring showing transition status and data volume moved
Data Lens data classification showing reclassified objects by tier
Prism Element storage container statistics showing tier distribution

questions.explanation: Objects lifecycle monitoring tracks policy execution including transition status, which shows the volume of data that has been successfully moved between storage tiers as a result of lifecycle policy execution.

questions.questionText: An administrator is reviewing the Objects dashboard and notices that latency for a particular bucket has increased by 300% over the past hour. Which additional Objects metric should be correlated to understand the root cause?

options.text: The File Analytics audit trail for the user accessing the bucket
The operations per second (IOPS) for the bucket to check for a workload spike
The Data Lens anomaly score for the Objects store service account
The Volume Group replication lag for the Objects backend storage

questions.explanation: When bucket latency increases dramatically, correlating with operations per second (IOPS) helps determine whether the latency is caused by a workload spike (more operations than the bucket can handle) or by an infrastructure issue.

questions.questionText: A Nutanix administrator is asked to provide a dashboard that shows both Files and Objects utilization metrics in a single management interface. Which approach is correct?

options.text: Deploy File Analytics with Objects monitoring plugins
Use Prism Central, which provides dashboards for both Files and Objects
Configure Data Lens to aggregate Files and Objects metrics into a unified view
Create a custom dashboard in Prism Element combining Files and Objects widgets

questions.explanation: Prism Central serves as the unified management interface for the Nutanix platform, providing dashboards for both Files (via Files Manager) and Objects (via the Objects dashboard) in a single console.

questions.questionText: An administrator deployed File Analytics several months ago and now receives a capacity trending alert indicating that a share will reach capacity in 30 days. What is the most proactive response?

options.text: Delete old files from the share immediately to free space
Review the growth rate analysis, identify top contributors, and plan capacity expansion or data tiering
Disable the capacity alert threshold to prevent recurring notifications
Migrate the share to an Objects bucket where capacity is unlimited

questions.explanation: The proactive response is to use File Analytics capacity trending and top contributors data to understand the growth pattern, identify the sources of growth, and plan either capacity expansion or data management strategies such as tiering or archiving.

questions.questionText: An administrator is configuring the File Analytics ransomware blocklist and wants to add common ransomware extensions. Which statement is true about the blocklist?

options.text: The blocklist automatically includes all known ransomware extensions from a cloud-updated database
The blocklist is configurable, allowing administrators to add specific file extensions to monitor
The blocklist can only be modified through Data Lens, not directly in File Analytics
The blocklist replaces entropy-based detection and cannot be used alongside it

questions.explanation: The File Analytics ransomware blocklist is configurable, allowing administrators to add specific file extensions associated with known ransomware variants. This works alongside entropy-based detection as a complementary protection mechanism.

questions.questionText: A Nutanix administrator needs to generate an audit report showing all delete operations performed on a specific NFS export over the last quarter. Which tool and feature should be used?

options.text: Data Lens compliance scan report filtered to delete actions
File Analytics audit trail filtered by delete operations on the specific share
Objects lifecycle deletion queue history for the NFS-backed bucket
Prism Central task audit log filtered by Files delete events

questions.explanation: File Analytics audit trails track all operations including delete actions per user per share. Filtering the audit trail by delete operations on the specific NFS export for the last quarter generates the required report.

questions.questionText: An administrator reviews the File Analytics file distribution by age and discovers that 40% of data on a critical share is over three years old. What insight does this provide for storage management?

options.text: The ransomware detection is not functioning because old files should have been flagged
A significant portion of storage is consumed by potentially stale data that may be a candidate for archiving or tiering
The capacity trending prediction is inaccurate because growth should have been higher
The audit trail data for the old files has expired and needs to be re-collected

questions.explanation: File distribution by age reveals that a large portion of storage is occupied by old data. This insight helps administrators identify archiving or tiering opportunities to optimize storage utilization on high-performance tiers.

questions.questionText: An administrator has configured Data Lens for a Nutanix Files environment and wants to validate that sensitive data tagging is working correctly. What should the administrator check?

options.text: File Analytics ransomware detection for tagged file anomalies
Data Lens data classification results showing tagged files with their sensitivity labels
Prism Central alert history for data classification policy violations
Objects metadata browser for sensitivity tags applied to file objects

questions.explanation: Data Lens data classification automatically tags files with sensitivity labels. To validate that tagging is working correctly, the administrator should review the data classification results in Data Lens showing tagged files and their assigned sensitivity categories.

questions.questionText: An administrator is monitoring a Volume Group that serves a Microsoft SQL Server Always On Availability Group via iSCSI. The administrator notices that one iSCSI session shows a disconnected status. What is the potential impact?

options.text: The File Analytics audit trail will stop recording operations for the SQL database
The SQL Server may lose access to the storage path, potentially causing failover or performance degradation
The Objects lifecycle policies for the SQL database backup bucket will pause
Data Lens will stop classifying data within the SQL database files

questions.explanation: A disconnected iSCSI session means the SQL Server has lost one of its storage paths. In a multipath configuration, this may cause path failover with potential performance impact, or in a single-path configuration, it could cause complete loss of storage access.

questions.questionText: An administrator configures CHAP authentication for a Volume Group and wants to verify that all connected initiators are using CHAP. Which monitoring metric confirms this?

options.text: File Analytics authentication event log
CHAP authentication session security status for each connected initiator
Data Lens access control compliance report
Objects IAM policy validation for iSCSI service accounts

questions.explanation: CHAP authentication monitoring provides session security status for each connected initiator, allowing the administrator to verify which sessions are authenticated using CHAP and identify any connections that are not using CHAP authentication.

questions.questionText: An administrator notices in the Objects dashboard that one bucket has significantly higher IOPS than all other buckets combined. What is the recommended monitoring action?

options.text: Enable File Analytics for the Objects store to get user-level audit trails
Review the bucket's operations per second, latency, and throughput to assess impact on the overall Objects store performance
Migrate the bucket to a Volume Group for better IOPS performance monitoring
Configure Data Lens behavioral analysis for the high-IOPS bucket

questions.explanation: When a single bucket shows disproportionately high IOPS, the administrator should review its complete performance metrics (operations per second, latency, throughput) to determine whether this workload is impacting the overall Objects store performance for other buckets.

questions.questionText: A storage architect needs to present capacity consumption trends for a Nutanix Objects store to management, including projected growth over the next six months. Which Objects monitoring capability provides this data?

options.text: File Analytics capacity trending report exported for the Objects metadata
Objects capacity management with total store utilization and growth trending
Data Lens storage consumption forecast based on data classification growth
Prism Central cluster runway report for the Objects container

questions.explanation: Objects capacity management provides total store utilization data along with growth trending capabilities. This information can be used to project future capacity needs and present consumption trends to management.

questions.questionText: An administrator enables File Analytics and wants to set up alerts for when a single user deletes more than 100 files in one hour. Which File Analytics capability supports this type of alerting?

options.text: Capacity trending threshold alerts based on free space reduction
Anomaly detection that identifies unusual patterns in user file operations
CHAP authentication alerting for excessive deletion requests
Objects lifecycle deletion queue monitoring with user attribution

questions.explanation: File Analytics anomaly detection identifies unusual patterns in user behavior, including excessive file operations such as mass deletions. This capability can alert administrators when a user's delete activity significantly deviates from normal behavior.

questions.questionText: An administrator is reviewing the Data Lens permissions scanning results and notices that a share shows "inherited permissions inconsistency." What does this finding suggest?

options.text: The share's ransomware protection policy conflicts with its access control list
The effective permissions on files differ from what the share-level permissions should grant due to inheritance issues
The Objects bucket policy linked to the share has overridden the file-level permissions
The Volume Group access control list is conflicting with the Files share permissions

questions.explanation: Data Lens permissions scanning identifies situations where effective permissions on files within a share are inconsistent with expected inherited permissions, which could lead to unintended access or access denial issues.

questions.questionText: A Nutanix administrator is troubleshooting an Objects store where lifecycle policies appear to be running but objects are not transitioning. What Objects monitoring information helps identify the issue?

options.text: File Analytics audit trail showing Objects API calls that failed
Objects lifecycle monitoring showing policy execution tracking and transition status
Data Lens behavioral analysis showing unusual Objects access patterns
Volume Group health showing backend storage issues for the Objects store

questions.explanation: Objects lifecycle monitoring provides detailed policy execution tracking and transition status. When policies run but objects do not transition, this monitoring data shows execution details and any errors preventing successful transitions.

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to monitor whether a Volume Group is meeting the IOPS requirements of a performance-critical application. Which approach provides the most direct measurement?

options.text: Check File Analytics write IOPS for the share hosting the application data
Review the Volume Group IOPS metric in the performance monitoring dashboard
Examine the Objects GET/PUT operations per second for the application's data bucket
Monitor the Data Lens anomaly score for the application's service account

questions.explanation: Volume Group performance metrics include IOPS measured per Volume Group. This provides the most direct measurement of whether the Volume Group is delivering the required IOPS for the application.

questions.questionText: An administrator deployed File Analytics and Data Lens for the same Nutanix Files environment. How do these solutions complement each other for ransomware protection?

options.text: They provide identical protection and only one should be active to avoid alert duplication
File Analytics provides entropy-based detection on-premises while Data Lens provides SaaS-based behavioral analysis and anomaly scoring
File Analytics detects ransomware on Files shares while Data Lens detects ransomware on Objects buckets
Data Lens replaces File Analytics ransomware detection when both are deployed together

questions.explanation: File Analytics and Data Lens use complementary ransomware detection approaches. File Analytics provides on-premises entropy-based analysis with configurable extension blocklists, while Data Lens adds SaaS-based behavioral analysis with anomaly scoring for defense in depth.

questions.questionText: An administrator is reviewing iSCSI session monitoring for a Volume Group and sees sessions from an IP address that does not belong to any known host. What should the administrator investigate?

options.text: Check File Analytics for unauthorized file access from the unknown IP
Review the CHAP authentication status for the unknown initiator and investigate potential unauthorized access
Examine the Objects dashboard for S3 API calls from the unknown IP
Check Data Lens permissions scanning for the unknown IP's access grants

questions.explanation: When an unknown IP appears in iSCSI session monitoring, the administrator should check the CHAP authentication status for that session and investigate whether unauthorized access is occurring, potentially indicating a security breach.

questions.questionText: An administrator is asked to demonstrate that the Nutanix storage environment meets an internal SLA of less than 5ms average latency for database Volume Groups. Which monitoring data provides this evidence?

options.text: File Analytics share-level latency metrics for the database share
Volume Group latency metrics showing average latency per Volume Group over time
Objects bucket latency metrics for the database tier storage
Data Lens performance analytics for the database cluster

questions.explanation: Volume Group performance metrics include per-VG latency tracking over time. This data provides direct evidence of whether each Volume Group serving database workloads is meeting the specified latency SLA.

questions.questionText: An administrator deploys a new Nutanix Objects store and wants to establish performance baselines. Which set of metrics should be collected from the Objects dashboard during the initial monitoring period?

options.text: File distribution by type, audit trail frequency, and anomaly detection score
GET/PUT latency, operations per second, data transfer rate, and per-bucket throughput
iSCSI session count, CHAP authentication rate, and Volume Group IOPS
Data classification hit rate, PII detection count, and compliance scan duration

questions.explanation: Establishing Objects store performance baselines requires collecting key performance metrics including GET/PUT latency, operations per second, data transfer rate, and per-bucket throughput during the initial period of normal operation.

questions.questionText: An administrator notices that Data Lens is reporting an increasing number of anomaly alerts across multiple Nutanix Files clusters. The alerts reference behavioral deviations from baseline for several service accounts. What is the most important immediate action?

options.text: Increase the Data Lens anomaly scoring threshold to reduce alert volume
Investigate the flagged service accounts for potential compromise and verify the behavioral deviations are not indicators of a coordinated attack
Disable the service accounts in File Analytics to stop the alert generation
Migrate the affected shares to Objects to remove them from Data Lens monitoring scope

questions.explanation: When Data Lens reports behavioral anomalies across multiple clusters for several service accounts, it could indicate a coordinated security incident. The immediate priority is to investigate the flagged accounts for potential compromise rather than suppressing alerts or disabling monitoring.

questions.questionText: Users report intermittent access failures to an SMB share hosted on Nutanix Files. The administrator verifies that the FSVMs are online. What should be checked next?

options.text: Verify DNS A records exist for all FSVM IPs and the file server virtual IP
Restart the Stargate service on all CVMs
Check the Objects MSP pod status using kubectl
Review the Volume Group iSCSI target portal configuration

questions.explanation: SMB share access relies heavily on DNS. Each FSVM and the file server VIP must have proper DNS A records. Missing or stale DNS records are the most common cause of intermittent SMB access failures.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to identify the root cause of a Nutanix Files service crash. Which log file should be examined first on the FSVM?

options.text: stargate.INFO on the CVM
minerva_nvm.log on the FSVM
cerebro.INFO on the CVM
msp_controller.log on the Objects worker node

questions.explanation: The minerva_nvm.log is the primary log file for Nutanix Files services running on the FSVM. It contains detailed information about file service operations, errors, and crash events.

questions.questionText: An administrator suspects that an FSVM is not functioning correctly after a recent configuration change. Which command should be run to verify the current FSVM deployment flavor and status?

options.text: ncli cluster get-params
afs info.list
kubectl get pods --all-namespaces
iscsi_client_connections

questions.explanation: The command `afs info.list` retrieves the current FSVM deployment information and status, helping confirm whether the FSVM is configured and operating as expected.

questions.questionText: After deploying a new Nutanix Files instance, the administrator notices that all FSVM health checks are failing in Prism. The FSVMs are powered on and reachable via SSH. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The Prism Element license has expired
The minerva_nvm service on the FSVMs has failed to start
The Objects store endpoint certificate is invalid
The Volume Group flash mode is disabled

questions.explanation: When FSVMs are reachable but health checks fail in Prism, the most common cause is that the minerva_nvm service has not started correctly. Reviewing minerva_nvm.log on the FSVM will reveal startup errors.

questions.questionText: An administrator receives a Prism alert indicating high memory utilization on one FSVM while the other two FSVMs show normal utilization. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: An uneven distribution of shares across FSVMs
A failing disk on the CVM hosting the FSVM
An expired SSL certificate on the file server endpoint
A misconfigured iSCSI multipath policy

questions.explanation: Uneven share distribution can cause one FSVM to handle a disproportionate number of client connections, leading to high memory utilization. Rebalancing shares across FSVMs resolves this imbalance.

questions.questionText: A Windows client receives "Access Denied" when connecting to an SMB share on Nutanix Files. The share permissions are confirmed correct. Kerberos authentication is configured. What should the administrator check next?

options.text: Whether the file server computer account exists in Active Directory
Whether the Objects bucket policy allows anonymous access
Whether CHAP authentication is enabled on the Volume Group
Whether the CVM Stargate service has been restarted recently

questions.explanation: Kerberos authentication for SMB requires the file server to have a valid computer account in Active Directory. If the account is missing, deleted, or the password is out of sync, Kerberos authentication fails and clients receive "Access Denied."

questions.questionText: Users connecting to an SMB share on Nutanix Files from legacy Windows 7 workstations report authentication failures. Users on Windows 10 connect successfully. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: NTLMv1 is disabled on the file server and legacy clients do not support NTLMv2
The file server Objects endpoint requires TLS 1.3
The Volume Group iSCSI IQN does not match the initiator
The FSVM snapshot schedule is blocking new connections

questions.explanation: Nutanix Files may be configured to require NTLMv2 or Kerberos only. Legacy Windows 7 clients attempting NTLMv1 will fail authentication. Verifying the NTLM authentication level on both the file server and the client GPO is necessary.

questions.questionText: An administrator configures SMB signing as required on the Nutanix Files server. After the change, a subset of Linux clients using CIFS mounts can no longer connect. What is the cause?

options.text: The Linux CIFS clients do not support or have not enabled SMB signing
The NFS export policy is blocking CIFS mounts
The Objects lifecycle policy has transitioned the data to cold tier
The MPIO driver on the Linux clients is misconfigured

questions.explanation: When SMB signing is set to "required" on the server, all connecting clients must support and negotiate SMB signing. Some Linux CIFS implementations may not have signing enabled by default, causing connection failures.

questions.questionText: After a DNS server migration, users report they can no longer access any shares on the Nutanix Files server. The FSVMs are healthy and accessible from the CVM. What should the administrator verify?

options.text: That the new DNS server has A records for the file server VIP and all FSVM IPs
That the LCM inventory has been refreshed after the migration
That the Objects worker pods have been restarted
That the iSCSI target portal IP matches the new DNS server IP

questions.explanation: After a DNS server migration, the A records for the Nutanix Files server VIP and individual FSVM IPs must be recreated on the new DNS server. Without these records, clients cannot resolve the file server hostname.

questions.questionText: An administrator notices that SMB share throughput has degraded significantly. The FSVMs show 90%+ CPU utilization in Prism. Network throughput between the CVM and FSVM is normal. What is the recommended first step?

options.text: Vertically scale the FSVMs by adding more vCPUs and RAM
Disable SMB encryption on all shares
Migrate the file server to a different Objects store
Restart the Stargate service across all CVMs

questions.explanation: When FSVM CPU utilization is consistently high, the FSVMs are resource-constrained. Vertically scaling by adding vCPUs and RAM per FSVM directly addresses the resource bottleneck and improves throughput.

questions.questionText: After enabling smart tiering on a Nutanix Files share, users report slower access to files that have not been accessed in several weeks. What explains this behavior?

options.text: Tiered files are moved to capacity-tier storage and must be recalled on access, adding latency
Smart tiering disables the CVM read cache for the affected share
Tiered files are compressed using erasure coding which increases read latency
The FSVM snapshot schedule pauses I/O during tiering operations

questions.explanation: Smart tiering moves infrequently accessed files from performance tier to capacity tier. When users access tiered files, they must be recalled from capacity storage, which introduces additional latency compared to performance-tier access.

questions.questionText: An administrator is troubleshooting slow NFS performance on Nutanix Files. The FSVM CPU and memory utilization are within normal ranges. What should be checked next?

options.text: Network throughput between the clients and the FSVM client network
The Objects bucket replication factor
The Volume Group CHAP authentication timeout
The LCM firmware version on the NIC

questions.explanation: When FSVM resources are not saturated, the next most likely bottleneck for NFS performance is network throughput. Checking for packet loss, bandwidth saturation, or MTU mismatches on the client-facing network is the recommended next step.

questions.questionText: During a planned Nutanix Files upgrade, the administrator notices that the first FSVM has been in "upgrading" state for over 45 minutes. What is the expected behavior during a Files rolling upgrade?

options.text: FSVMs upgrade one at a time; clients connected to the upgrading FSVM fail over to other FSVMs
All FSVMs upgrade simultaneously to minimize the upgrade window
The file server goes completely offline during the entire upgrade process
Only the metadata FSVM upgrades; data FSVMs remain on the previous version

questions.explanation: Nutanix Files performs rolling upgrades where FSVMs are upgraded one at a time. Client connections on the upgrading FSVM are temporarily disrupted and fail over to other healthy FSVMs. Some temporary performance degradation is expected.

questions.questionText: An administrator is about to initiate a Nutanix Files upgrade. The pre-upgrade health check reports a warning about insufficient FSVM resources. What should the administrator do?

options.text: Resolve the FSVM resource warning before proceeding with the upgrade
Ignore the warning since pre-upgrade checks are informational only
Proceed with the upgrade and address the warning afterward
Disable the health check and restart the upgrade process

questions.explanation: Pre-upgrade health checks for Nutanix Files should be resolved before proceeding. Insufficient FSVM resources during a rolling upgrade can lead to overloaded remaining FSVMs when one FSVM is taken offline for upgrade, potentially causing service disruptions.

questions.questionText: During a Nutanix Files rolling upgrade, users report temporary disconnections from SMB shares. The administrator confirms that only one FSVM is upgrading. What should the administrator communicate to users?

options.text: Temporary disconnections are expected during rolling upgrades as connections migrate between FSVMs
The file server has entered maintenance mode and all shares are offline
The disconnections indicate a failed upgrade and a rollback is in progress
Users must manually reconnect to a different FSVM IP address

questions.explanation: During a rolling upgrade, the FSVM being upgraded must drain its client connections. Clients experience brief disconnections as their sessions migrate to other healthy FSVMs. This is expected behavior and connections auto-recover.

questions.questionText: After a Files rolling upgrade completes, the administrator notices one FSVM did not rejoin the cluster. Prism shows it as "unreachable." What should be done first?

options.text: Check the minerva_nvm.log on the affected FSVM for post-upgrade startup errors
Delete the FSVM and redeploy the entire file server
Restart all CVMs in the Nutanix cluster
Re-register the Objects store with the file server

questions.explanation: When an FSVM fails to rejoin after an upgrade, the minerva_nvm.log is the primary diagnostic log. It will reveal startup failures, configuration mismatches, or service errors that prevented the FSVM from becoming operational post-upgrade.

questions.questionText: An administrator configures a multi-protocol share (SMB and NFS) on Nutanix Files. Windows users can access files, but Linux NFS clients receive permission denied errors on files created by Windows users. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: UID/GID mapping between Active Directory users and NFS is not configured correctly
The Objects IAM policy is overriding the file share permissions
The Volume Group is configured in active/passive mode instead of active/active
The FSVM does not have a valid SSL certificate for NFS

questions.explanation: Multi-protocol shares require proper UID/GID mapping between Active Directory and UNIX identities. When this mapping is missing or incorrect, NFS clients cannot translate the Windows SID to a valid UNIX identity, resulting in permission denied errors.

questions.questionText: On a multi-protocol Nutanix Files share, an administrator notices that NFSv4 ACLs set by Linux clients are not being honored when Windows users access the same files via SMB. What should be investigated?

options.text: The NFSv4 ACL to NTFS ACL mapping rules on the file server
The Objects WORM retention policy on the share
The iSCSI initiator IQN registered for the NFS clients
The LCM version compatibility matrix for multi-protocol

questions.explanation: NFSv4 ACLs must be properly mapped to equivalent NTFS ACLs for SMB access. If the mapping rules are not configured correctly, the permissions set by NFS clients will not translate properly when accessed via SMB.

questions.questionText: An administrator enables multi-protocol access on an existing NFS share. After the change, users report that newly created files via SMB do not inherit the parent directory permissions as expected. What is the likely issue?

options.text: Permission inheritance settings conflict between POSIX mode bits and NTFS ACL inheritance
The FSVM does not have enough memory to support multi-protocol
The Objects lifecycle policy is overriding the permission settings
The CVM Cerebro service is not replicating the permission metadata

questions.explanation: When multi-protocol is enabled, there can be conflicts between POSIX permission inheritance (used by NFS) and NTFS ACL inheritance (used by SMB). The administrator must configure the preferred permission model and inheritance behavior.

questions.questionText: A Linux client attempts to access a file on a multi-protocol share using NFSv4 and receives an "operation not permitted" error. The same file is accessible via SMB from a Windows client. The UID/GID mapping is verified correct. What should the administrator check?

options.text: Whether the NFSv4 AUTH_SYS or AUTH_KRB5 authentication method matches the server configuration
Whether the Objects endpoint DNS record is resolvable from the Linux client
Whether the CHAP secret on the iSCSI target matches the Linux initiator
Whether the CVM NTP source is synchronized with the Linux client

questions.explanation: NFSv4 supports multiple authentication methods. If the file server requires Kerberos (AUTH_KRB5) but the Linux client is using AUTH_SYS, access will be denied even if UID/GID mapping is correct.

questions.questionText: A Nutanix Files deployment currently has 3 FSVMs, and the administrator needs to support additional client connections. What is the recommended scaling approach?

options.text: Add additional FSVMs to the file server (up to a maximum of 16 FSVMs)
Deploy a second Objects store to offload file connections
Create additional Volume Groups to distribute the SMB load
Increase the CVM memory on each host to 64 GB

questions.explanation: Nutanix Files supports horizontal scaling by adding FSVMs up to a maximum of 16. Each additional FSVM increases the capacity for client connections and distributes the workload across more resources.

questions.questionText: After adding a fourth FSVM to a Nutanix Files cluster, the administrator notices that the new FSVM has no shares assigned to it. What must be done?

options.text: Rebalance shares across all FSVMs to distribute the workload to the new FSVM
Delete and recreate all existing shares
Restart the Stargate service on the CVM hosting the new FSVM
Manually assign an iSCSI target portal to the new FSVM

questions.explanation: When a new FSVM is added, existing shares are not automatically redistributed. The administrator must initiate a share rebalancing operation to distribute shares across all FSVMs, including the newly added one.

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to increase the performance of each individual FSVM rather than adding more FSVMs. Which action achieves vertical scaling for Nutanix Files?

options.text: Increase the vCPU count and RAM allocation for each FSVM
Add additional disks to the CVM storage pool
Enable deduplication on the Nutanix Files container
Change the Objects replication factor from 2 to 3

questions.explanation: Vertical scaling for Nutanix Files involves increasing the vCPU and RAM resources allocated to each FSVM. This allows each FSVM to handle more concurrent operations and larger working sets.

questions.questionText: Users report that previous versions of files are not available for self-service restore on an SMB share. The administrator confirms that snapshot schedules are configured. What should be checked?

options.text: Whether the "Previous Versions" feature is enabled on the share and snapshots have completed successfully
Whether the Objects bucket versioning is enabled
Whether the Volume Group deduplication is enabled
Whether the CVM Curator service has completed a recent scan

questions.explanation: Self-service restore via Windows Previous Versions requires both a configured snapshot schedule and the Previous Versions feature to be enabled on the share. Additionally, at least one snapshot must have completed successfully.

questions.questionText: An administrator sets up a snapshot replication schedule between two Nutanix Files servers for disaster recovery. Replication fails with a connectivity error. What should be verified first?

options.text: Network connectivity between the source and target FSVMs on the replication network
That the Objects store on the target site has available capacity
That the iSCSI initiator on the target site is configured correctly
That LCM has been updated to the latest version on both clusters

questions.explanation: Nutanix Files replication requires direct network connectivity between the FSVMs at the source and target sites. Firewall rules, routing, and DNS must all allow communication between the FSVM replication interfaces.

questions.questionText: After a site failover, users connecting to the disaster recovery Nutanix Files server report stale data. The last successful replication was 4 hours ago. What explains this?

options.text: The RPO is determined by the replication schedule interval; data created after the last successful snapshot is not replicated
The FSVM cache on the DR site needs to be manually flushed
The Objects lifecycle policy is delaying data availability on the DR site
The iSCSI MPIO failover has not completed on the DR site

questions.explanation: Replication is snapshot-based, so the RPO depends on the configured schedule. Any data created or modified after the last successfully replicated snapshot will not be present on the DR site.

questions.questionText: An administrator configures daily snapshots on a Nutanix Files share but notices that snapshot storage consumption is growing faster than expected. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: A high rate of file changes causing large delta between snapshots
The Objects WORM policy is preventing snapshot deduplication
The Volume Group flash mode is duplicating snapshot data
The CVM Stargate service is not performing garbage collection

questions.explanation: Nutanix Files snapshots are space-efficient and only store changed blocks. However, if the share has a high rate of data modification, the delta between snapshots grows, leading to higher-than-expected storage consumption.

questions.questionText: An administrator receives an alert that the Nutanix Objects MSP cluster is unhealthy. Which tool should be used to diagnose the issue?

options.text: kubectl commands on the MSP cluster to check pod status and resource allocation
The afs info.get_flavor command on the FSVM
The ncli volume-group list command on the CVM
The NFS export policy editor in Prism Element

questions.explanation: Nutanix Objects runs on the Microservices Platform (MSP). Using kubectl commands to check pod health, resource allocation, and logs is the primary method for diagnosing MSP-level issues.

questions.questionText: After deploying Nutanix Objects, an administrator notices that several pods are in CrashLoopBackOff state. What should be investigated first?

options.text: Pod resource limits and available memory/CPU on the MSP worker nodes
The FSVM minerva_nvm.log for related file service errors
The Volume Group iSCSI target IQN for mismatched configurations
The SMB signing policy on the Nutanix Files server

questions.explanation: Pods in CrashLoopBackOff typically indicate resource constraints or configuration errors. Checking resource limits and available capacity on MSP worker nodes is the first diagnostic step, as insufficient resources prevent pods from starting.

questions.questionText: The Nutanix Objects UI in Prism Central shows the object store as "degraded." The administrator confirms that the underlying Nutanix cluster is healthy. Where should troubleshooting begin?

options.text: Check the MSP platform health and individual pod status using kubectl
Restart the Cerebro service on all CVMs to reinitialize replication
Verify the FSVM network configuration in Prism Element
Check the iSCSI session status on connected external hosts

questions.explanation: When the object store shows as degraded but the underlying cluster is healthy, the issue is typically within the MSP platform layer. Checking pod health, node status, and resource consumption in the MSP environment isolates the problem.

questions.questionText: An administrator notices that Nutanix Objects worker nodes are running out of memory, causing pod evictions. What is the recommended remediation?

options.text: Scale out by adding more worker nodes or scale up existing worker node resources
Reduce the FSVM memory allocation to free cluster resources
Disable the Volume Group compression feature
Change the Objects replication from synchronous to asynchronous

questions.explanation: When MSP worker nodes experience resource exhaustion, the solution is to either add more worker nodes (horizontal scaling) or increase the resources allocated to existing worker nodes (vertical scaling) to prevent pod evictions.

questions.questionText: An S3 client application receives a 403 Forbidden error when attempting to upload objects to a Nutanix Objects bucket. The bucket exists and the endpoint is reachable. What should be checked first?

options.text: The IAM user access keys and the bucket policy permissions for the PutObject action
The FSVM health status and DNS A records
The Volume Group CHAP authentication credentials
The NCC health check results on the CVM

questions.explanation: A 403 Forbidden error on Objects indicates an authorization failure. The administrator should verify that the IAM user's access/secret keys are correct and that the bucket policy or IAM policy grants the PutObject permission for that user.

questions.questionText: An administrator configures a bucket policy on Nutanix Objects that grants read access, but an IAM user policy explicitly denies read access. What is the resulting behavior when the user attempts to read an object?

options.text: Access is denied because an explicit deny in any policy always takes precedence
Access is allowed because bucket policies override IAM user policies
Access is allowed because the most recent policy takes precedence
The request enters a pending state until an administrator approves it

questions.explanation: In Nutanix Objects (following S3-compatible policy evaluation), an explicit deny in any policy always overrides an allow in any other policy. This is consistent with the standard deny-override policy evaluation model.

questions.questionText: A web application using Nutanix Objects for storage receives CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) errors in the browser. The bucket contains the correct data. What should the administrator configure?

options.text: A CORS configuration on the bucket to allow the web application's origin domain
A new FSVM with a dedicated network for web traffic
An iSCSI multipath policy for the web application server
A new NFS export with root squash disabled for the web server

questions.explanation: CORS errors occur when a browser-based application makes requests to a different origin. The Nutanix Objects bucket must have a CORS configuration that explicitly allows the web application's origin domain, HTTP methods, and headers.

questions.questionText: An administrator creates a new IAM user for a backup application on Nutanix Objects. The application can list buckets but cannot write to a specific bucket. What is the most likely issue?

options.text: The IAM user has a policy allowing ListBucket but the bucket policy does not grant PutObject for that user
The FSVM minerva_nvm service is blocking write operations
The Volume Group attached to the backup server is read-only
The LCM version does not support write operations for new IAM users

questions.explanation: When a user can list but not write, it indicates a granular permission issue. The IAM user or bucket policy must explicitly grant PutObject (and potentially PutBucketObject) permissions for the specific bucket.

questions.questionText: An administrator is troubleshooting slow upload performance to Nutanix Objects. The object store has the minimum of 3 worker nodes. What is the most likely performance bottleneck?

options.text: Insufficient worker nodes to handle the concurrent upload workload
The FSVM snapshot schedule is consuming all available I/O bandwidth
The Volume Group CHAP authentication is adding overhead to each upload
The CVM Stargate service is throttling Objects I/O

questions.explanation: Three worker nodes is the minimum for Nutanix Objects. For production workloads with significant concurrent access, additional worker nodes are typically required to provide adequate throughput and avoid resource contention.

questions.questionText: An administrator notices high network latency between S3 clients and the Nutanix Objects endpoint. The MSP pods are healthy and have available resources. What should be investigated?

options.text: Network throughput and routing between the client network and the Objects external network
The FSVM tiering policy causing data recall delays
The iSCSI session timeout values on the client
The Cerebro replication backlog between clusters

questions.explanation: When MSP pods are healthy but clients experience latency, the issue is likely in the network path. Checking for bandwidth saturation, routing inefficiencies, or MTU mismatches between the client network and the Objects endpoint network is the next step.

questions.questionText: An administrator enables WORM (Write Once Read Many) on a Nutanix Objects bucket. A compliance officer asks when the WORM protection takes effect on newly uploaded objects. What is the correct answer?

options.text: Objects are writable during the 24-hour grace period after upload, then become immutable
WORM protection is immediate upon object upload with no grace period
WORM protection takes effect after the first Objects snapshot completes
Objects become immutable only after an administrator manually locks each object

questions.explanation: Nutanix Objects WORM has a 24-hour grace period after an object is uploaded. During this period, the object can be deleted or overwritten. After the grace period expires, the object becomes immutable according to the WORM retention policy.

questions.questionText: A user accidentally uploads a file to a WORM-enabled bucket on Nutanix Objects. The upload occurred 2 hours ago. Can the file be deleted?

options.text: Yes, the file can be deleted because it is still within the 24-hour WORM grace period
No, WORM files cannot be deleted under any circumstances once uploaded
Yes, but only if the administrator disables WORM on the entire bucket first
No, but the file will automatically expire after the lifecycle policy interval

questions.explanation: Within the 24-hour grace period, objects in a WORM bucket can still be deleted or modified. This grace period allows administrators or users to correct accidental uploads before immutability is enforced.

questions.questionText: An auditor asks the difference between legal hold and governance mode in Nutanix Objects WORM. Which statement is correct?

options.text: Legal hold prevents deletion indefinitely until explicitly removed, while governance mode allows privileged users to bypass retention
Legal hold applies only to Objects, while governance mode applies to Files shares
Legal hold encrypts the data at rest, while governance mode enables versioning
Legal hold is enforced by the FSVM, while governance mode is enforced by the CVM

questions.explanation: Legal hold locks objects indefinitely regardless of retention period until the hold is explicitly removed, typically for litigation purposes. Governance mode enforces retention but allows users with special permissions to bypass the retention policy if necessary.

questions.questionText: An administrator attempts to delete a WORM-protected object that is past its retention period but has a legal hold applied. What happens?

options.text: The deletion fails because the legal hold must be removed before the object can be deleted
The object is deleted because the retention period has expired
The object is moved to a recycle bin for 30 days before permanent deletion
The deletion succeeds but a copy is retained in the Objects audit log

questions.explanation: A legal hold overrides the retention period. Even if an object's retention period has expired, it cannot be deleted while a legal hold is active. The legal hold must be explicitly removed before deletion is possible.

questions.questionText: Users report that the Nutanix Objects endpoint is showing an SSL certificate warning in their S3 client applications. The object store was recently deployed. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The Objects endpoint is using a self-signed certificate that is not trusted by the client applications
The FSVM Kerberos ticket has expired
The Volume Group iSCSI target certificate is invalid
The CVM NTP time is more than 5 minutes ahead of the clients

questions.explanation: Newly deployed Nutanix Objects endpoints use self-signed certificates by default. S3 client applications that validate SSL certificates will show warnings or refuse connections until a trusted CA-signed certificate is installed.

questions.questionText: An administrator replaces the self-signed certificate on the Nutanix Objects endpoint with a CA-signed certificate. After the change, some S3 clients still report certificate errors while others work fine. What should be checked?

options.text: Whether the certificate includes a wildcard or SAN entries matching all possible Objects endpoint hostnames
Whether the FSVM DNS A records have been updated with the new certificate hash
Whether the iSCSI initiator has re-authenticated with the new certificate
Whether LCM needs to distribute the certificate to all CVMs

questions.explanation: Objects endpoints may use multiple hostnames (including bucket-name prefixed hostnames for virtual-hosted style access). The certificate must use wildcard entries or Subject Alternative Names (SANs) to cover all possible endpoint variations.

questions.questionText: After updating the SSL certificate on Nutanix Objects, the administrator notices that the Objects UI in Prism Central shows a certificate mismatch error. What is the most likely issue?

options.text: The certificate was applied to the Objects endpoint but Prism Central still references the old certificate
The FSVM minerva_nvm service does not recognize the new certificate format
The Volume Group encryption key conflicts with the new certificate
The LCM inventory needs to be refreshed to detect the new certificate

questions.explanation: When updating the Objects endpoint certificate, Prism Central may cache or reference the previous certificate. Ensuring that Prism Central trusts the new certificate and that any cached references are updated resolves the mismatch.

questions.questionText: An administrator configures a lifecycle policy on a Nutanix Objects bucket to transition objects to cold storage after 30 days. Objects that are 45 days old have not been transitioned. What is the most likely reason?

options.text: Lifecycle policy evaluation runs periodically and there may be a processing delay before the transition takes effect on existing objects
The cold tier requires a separate Nutanix Files server to be configured
The Volume Group flash mode is preventing cold tier transitions
The FSVM snapshot schedule is locking the objects from transitioning

questions.explanation: Lifecycle policies evaluate all objects in a bucket, including existing objects (retroactive application), but the evaluation runs periodically. There can be a delay between when an object becomes eligible for transition and when the policy evaluation cycle actually processes it. The administrator should verify the policy configuration and wait for the next policy evaluation cycle.

questions.questionText: An administrator sets up a lifecycle policy to expire objects after 90 days. After 90 days, some objects remain in the bucket. What could cause this delay?

options.text: Lifecycle policy evaluation runs periodically and there may be a processing delay before expiration takes effect
The FSVM replication is creating new copies of the expired objects
The iSCSI session timeout is extending the object's lifecycle
The NCC health check is preventing object deletion during scheduled scans

questions.explanation: Lifecycle policy evaluation is not instantaneous. The Objects service processes lifecycle rules periodically, and there can be a delay between when an object becomes eligible for expiration and when it is actually deleted.

questions.questionText: An administrator configures a lifecycle rule to transition objects from standard to cold storage. After the transition, S3 clients report increased latency when accessing transitioned objects. What explains this?

options.text: Objects in cold storage must be recalled to the standard tier before access, introducing additional latency
The FSVM CPU utilization spikes during cold storage access
The Volume Group MPIO path has failed over to a passive path
The CVM Stargate service deprioritizes cold storage reads

questions.explanation: Cold-tier storage is optimized for cost over performance. Accessing objects that have been transitioned to cold storage requires a retrieval process similar to AWS Glacier, which adds latency compared to accessing objects in standard storage.

questions.questionText: An external Linux server cannot discover iSCSI targets on a Nutanix Volume Group. The VG is created and has an iSCSI client configured. What should the administrator verify first?

options.text: That the iSCSI target portal IP is correct and the discovery port (3260) is reachable from the Linux server
That the FSVM has a DNS A record for the Linux server
That the Objects IAM user has read permissions on the Volume Group
That the NFS export policy allows the Linux server's IP

questions.explanation: iSCSI target discovery requires network connectivity to the target portal IP on the correct port (Nutanix uses port 3260 by default for external iSCSI). If the portal IP or port is unreachable, the initiator cannot discover available targets.

questions.questionText: An administrator configures a Windows iSCSI initiator to connect to a Nutanix Volume Group. The connection fails with an authentication error. CHAP is enabled on the VG. What should be checked?

options.text: That the CHAP username and secret configured on the Windows initiator match the VG CHAP settings exactly
That the FSVM Kerberos keytab has been imported on the Windows host
That the Objects endpoint certificate is trusted by the Windows host
That the SMB signing policy matches between the initiator and the VG

questions.explanation: CHAP authentication requires the initiator's CHAP username and secret to match exactly what is configured on the Nutanix Volume Group. Even minor discrepancies in the secret (including whitespace) will cause authentication failures.

questions.questionText: A Linux administrator reports that the iSCSI connection to a Nutanix Volume Group drops during routine CVM maintenance. The host has a single iSCSI session configured. What should be recommended?

options.text: Configure MPIO with multiple iSCSI sessions to provide path redundancy during CVM failovers
Increase the FSVM memory allocation to improve iSCSI stability
Migrate the VG data to a Nutanix Objects bucket for higher availability
Enable SMB multichannel on the iSCSI connection for redundancy

questions.explanation: A single iSCSI session provides no path redundancy. Configuring MPIO with multiple sessions across different data services IPs ensures that if one path fails during CVM maintenance, traffic automatically fails over to another path.

questions.questionText: An administrator discovers that the IQN (iSCSI Qualified Name) on the external host does not match the IQN registered as a client in the Nutanix Volume Group configuration. What is the impact?

options.text: The host is unable to connect to the Volume Group because the IQN serves as the client identifier for authorization
The host connects but all I/O operations are read-only
The host connects but performance is limited to 1 Gbps regardless of network speed
The Volume Group data is automatically encrypted with the mismatched IQN

questions.explanation: The IQN acts as the client identity for Nutanix Volume Groups. If the host's IQN does not match the registered client IQN in the VG configuration, the connection is refused because the host cannot be authorized.

questions.questionText: An administrator configures MPIO on a Windows Server connecting to a Nutanix Volume Group. The host shows two paths but only one is active while the other is in standby. The administrator expected both to be active. What should be checked?

options.text: The MPIO load balance policy setting, which may be configured as active/passive instead of round-robin
The FSVM tiering policy that limits the number of active connections
The Objects bucket policy that restricts concurrent iSCSI sessions
The NCC health check that throttles multipath connections to one active path

questions.explanation: Windows MPIO supports multiple load balance policies. If configured with Failover Only (active/passive), only one path is active. Changing the policy to Round Robin or Least Queue Depth enables active/active with both paths handling I/O.

questions.questionText: After configuring MPIO for a Nutanix Volume Group on a Linux host, the administrator notices that I/O performance has not improved compared to a single path. Both paths show as active. What should be checked?

options.text: The multipath policy configuration to ensure it is set to round-robin or least-pending for load distribution
The FSVM CPU utilization to ensure multi-protocol is not consuming resources
The Objects lifecycle policy to verify cold tier is not throttling I/O
The Cerebro replication schedule that may be consuming the second path's bandwidth

questions.explanation: Even with both paths active, the multipath daemon must be configured with an appropriate I/O distribution policy like round-robin. If the policy is set to failover, only one path handles I/O regardless of path state.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to troubleshoot iSCSI path failover timing on an external host connected to a Nutanix Volume Group. Users report 30-second I/O pauses during CVM restarts. What should be adjusted?

options.text: The iSCSI timeout and retry values on the initiator to reduce failover detection time
The FSVM snapshot frequency to reduce I/O contention during failover
The Objects WORM grace period to allow faster failover
The CVM Stargate read cache size to buffer I/O during failover

questions.explanation: The iSCSI timeout and retry values on the initiator directly control how long the host waits before declaring a path as failed and switching to an alternate path. Reducing these values shortens the I/O pause during failover.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to connect an external Windows Server to a Nutanix Volume Group using iSCSI. The initiator is configured but cannot find any targets. What is the correct sequence of steps?

options.text: Add the Data Services IP as the target portal in the iSCSI initiator, then perform a target discovery
Install the Nutanix Guest Tools agent on the Windows Server, then restart the FSVM
Configure an NFS export on the Volume Group, then mount it from the Windows Server
Create an IAM user in Objects and use the access key to authenticate the iSCSI session

questions.explanation: External iSCSI clients connect to Nutanix Volume Groups through the cluster's Data Services IP. The Windows iSCSI initiator must have this IP configured as the target portal to discover and connect to available VG targets.

questions.questionText: A Linux administrator configures the iSCSI initiator using iscsiadm but receives a "no portals found" error during discovery. The Data Services IP is confirmed correct. What should be checked?

options.text: That the discovery target address uses the correct port (3260) and network connectivity is verified
That the FSVM has an A record in DNS for the Linux server's hostname
That the Objects bucket allows anonymous access from the Linux server's IP
That the Prism Central license includes iSCSI initiator support for Linux

questions.explanation: Nutanix uses port 3260 for external iSCSI discovery. If the administrator uses the wrong port or if firewall rules block port 3260, discovery will fail with a "no portals found" error.

questions.questionText: An external host connected to a Nutanix Volume Group via iSCSI alternates between static target configuration and discovery-based configuration. The administrator wants the most resilient approach. Which is recommended?

options.text: Use discovery-based targets so the initiator automatically learns all available target portals
Use static targets to avoid DNS dependency for iSCSI resolution
Use NFS mounts instead of iSCSI for external hosts for better resilience
Use SMB multichannel instead of iSCSI multipath for failover

questions.explanation: Discovery-based targets automatically enumerate all available target portals, allowing the initiator to adapt to changes in the environment. This is more resilient than static targets, which require manual updates when portal IPs change.

questions.questionText: An administrator enables flash mode on a Nutanix Volume Group used by a database application. After enabling it, the administrator notices no significant performance improvement. What should be verified?

options.text: That the cluster has sufficient SSD tier capacity and that data has been migrated to the SSD tier
That the FSVM tiering policy is set to "all flash" mode
That the Objects worker nodes have been restarted after flash mode enablement
That the CVM Curator service has completed an erasure coding pass

questions.explanation: Flash mode prioritizes SSD storage for a Volume Group's data. If the cluster has insufficient SSD capacity or the data has not yet been migrated from HDD to SSD tier, the performance benefit will not be realized immediately.

questions.questionText: An administrator considers enabling compression on a Nutanix Volume Group used for a latency-sensitive transactional database. What should the administrator be aware of?

options.text: Compression can add CPU overhead that increases I/O latency, which may impact latency-sensitive workloads
Compression is only available for Nutanix Files and cannot be applied to Volume Groups
Compression automatically enables WORM protection on the Volume Group
Compression requires a minimum of 5 iSCSI paths per host

questions.explanation: Compression on Volume Groups reduces storage consumption but introduces CPU overhead for compress/decompress operations. For latency-sensitive workloads like transactional databases, this overhead may increase I/O latency beyond acceptable thresholds.

questions.questionText: An administrator evaluates enabling deduplication on a Volume Group that hosts virtual desktop infrastructure (VDI) boot images. What is the expected benefit?

options.text: Significant space savings because VDI boot images contain many identical data blocks across desktops
Improved iSCSI connection speed because deduplicated blocks transfer faster
Reduced FSVM memory usage because deduplicated data requires less metadata
Automatic data replication to a secondary Nutanix Objects bucket

questions.explanation: VDI workloads are excellent candidates for deduplication because multiple virtual desktop boot images share many identical blocks. Deduplication detects and eliminates these redundant blocks, resulting in significant storage savings.

questions.questionText: An administrator notices that a Volume Group with both compression and deduplication enabled is experiencing higher-than-expected latency. What is the recommended action for a performance-critical workload?

options.text: Evaluate disabling compression or deduplication to reduce CPU overhead and lower latency
Enable flash mode on the FSVM to accelerate the Volume Group I/O
Increase the Objects worker node count to offload VG processing
Configure WORM on the Volume Group to prevent write amplification

questions.explanation: Both compression and deduplication consume CPU resources. When combined on a performance-critical workload, the cumulative overhead can increase latency. Selectively disabling one or both features for that VG may be necessary to meet latency requirements.

questions.questionText: A Nutanix cluster upgrade initiated through LCM (Life Cycle Manager) fails at the pre-upgrade check stage. The error references an NCC health check failure. What should the administrator do first?

options.text: Run a full NCC health check to identify and resolve the specific failing check before retrying the upgrade
Restart the LCM service and immediately retry the upgrade
Manually upgrade each CVM individually bypassing LCM
Disable the NCC health checks and proceed with the upgrade

questions.explanation: LCM relies on NCC health checks to validate the cluster is in a healthy state before proceeding. Running a full NCC check identifies the specific failure, which must be resolved before the upgrade can proceed safely.

questions.questionText: During a one-click AOS upgrade, the process stalls at 30% completion. The administrator sees that one CVM has not restarted after the upgrade. What is the recommended troubleshooting step?

options.text: Check the CVM services status and upgrade logs on the stalled CVM via SSH
Force a power cycle of all CVMs simultaneously to restart the upgrade
Revert the entire cluster to the previous AOS version
Delete the stalled CVM and create a new one from the Prism template

questions.explanation: One-click upgrades proceed CVM by CVM. If one CVM stalls, SSH into it and check the services status and upgrade logs to identify what prevented the restart. This allows targeted remediation without affecting the rest of the cluster.

questions.questionText: An administrator attempts to update Nutanix Files through LCM but the update does not appear in the LCM inventory. What should be done?

options.text: Perform an LCM inventory refresh to check for available updates and verify internet connectivity or dark site bundle availability
Reinstall the Files server from scratch to get the latest version
Update the Objects store first, which will trigger a Files update
Restart all FSVMs to force LCM to detect the current version

questions.explanation: LCM requires an inventory refresh to detect available updates. If the update still does not appear, the administrator should verify that the cluster has internet connectivity to the Nutanix portal or that the correct dark site bundle has been uploaded.

questions.questionText: An administrator runs an NCC health check and receives a WARN status for the "CVM Services Status" check. Several services show as "DOWN." What is the most appropriate response?

options.text: Identify which specific services are down and attempt to restart them using the genesis restart command
Immediately shut down the cluster to prevent data corruption
Ignore the warning since WARN is not critical
Contact the Objects MSP team to restart the CVM services

questions.explanation: NCC WARN for CVM services indicates one or more services are not running. The administrator should identify the specific services and attempt to restart them using the appropriate cluster commands (e.g., genesis restart). Persistent failures require further investigation.

questions.questionText: An administrator receives an NCC FAIL for the "Disk Health" check on one node. The cluster is currently running with RF2 data protection. What is the immediate risk and recommended action?

options.text: The cluster's fault tolerance is reduced; replace the failed disk promptly and verify data resiliency with NCC
There is no risk because RF2 provides triple redundancy
The FSVM on that node will automatically migrate to another node
The Objects worker pods will rebalance to avoid the failed disk

questions.explanation: With RF2 and a failed disk, the cluster has reduced fault tolerance for the data replicas that were on that disk. While Nutanix automatically re-replicates the data, the failed disk should be replaced promptly to restore full redundancy.

questions.questionText: An NCC check reports a time synchronization failure across multiple CVMs. What is the potential impact on Nutanix services?

options.text: Kerberos authentication, certificate validation, and replication may fail due to clock skew between components
Only the Objects lifecycle policy timing is affected
Only the FSVM snapshot schedule will be off by a few seconds
Only the LCM upgrade scheduler will use incorrect timestamps

questions.explanation: Time synchronization is critical for many Nutanix services. Kerberos authentication (used by Files), SSL/TLS certificate validation, snapshot schedules, and replication all depend on synchronized clocks. Significant clock skew causes authentication and operational failures.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to verify network connectivity between an FSVM and client machines. What is the recommended approach?

options.text: Use SSH to the CVM and run ping/traceroute commands targeting the FSVM client network from the client subnet
Use the Objects MSP kubectl tool to trace the network path
Check the Volume Group iSCSI session log for connectivity details
Review the LCM upgrade log for network diagnostic information

questions.explanation: Network troubleshooting for FSVMs is performed from the CVM using standard tools like ping and traceroute. The administrator should verify connectivity from the client perspective and from the CVM to the FSVM client network interfaces.

questions.questionText: After a network switch replacement, Nutanix Files clients report intermittent connectivity. The FSVMs are healthy. DNS is resolving correctly. What should the administrator check next?

options.text: VLAN tagging, network switch port configuration, and MTU settings matching the FSVM client network requirements
The Objects endpoint SSL certificate validity
The Volume Group CHAP authentication status
The LCM dark site bundle compatibility

questions.explanation: After a network infrastructure change, the most common issues are VLAN tagging, port configuration, and MTU mismatches. The new switch ports must be configured identically to the previous ones for the FSVM client and storage networks.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to verify that DNS and NTP are properly configured for a Nutanix Files deployment. Which commands should be executed from the CVM?

options.text: nslookup to verify DNS resolution and ntpq -p to check NTP synchronization status
kubectl get dns and kubectl get ntp to check MSP service status
ncli volume-group check-dns and ncli volume-group check-ntp
afs dns.verify and afs ntp.verify from the FSVM console

questions.explanation: Standard Linux diagnostic tools like nslookup (or dig) and ntpq are used from the CVM to verify DNS resolution and NTP synchronization. These are foundational services that must be functioning correctly for Nutanix Files to operate properly.

questions.questionText: An administrator needs to collect logs from a Nutanix cluster for a support case. Which tool is the recommended method for log collection?

options.text: Logbay, accessed from the CVM command line, to collect and bundle logs from all cluster components
The Windows Event Viewer on each FSVM
The Objects MSP Kubernetes dashboard log viewer
The Prism Element alert dashboard export to CSV

questions.explanation: Logbay is the Nutanix-recommended log collection utility. It runs from the CVM and can collect logs from all cluster components including CVMs, FSVMs, and hypervisors, bundling them for upload to the Nutanix support portal.

questions.questionText: An administrator runs logbay to collect logs but the resulting bundle is too large to upload to the Nutanix support portal. What approach should be taken?

options.text: Use logbay with time-range and component filters to create a smaller, focused log bundle
Split the bundle manually using a ZIP utility and upload each part
Delete old logs from the CVMs first and then rerun logbay
Upload the logs to a Nutanix Objects bucket instead of the support portal

questions.explanation: Logbay supports filtering by time range and specific components. Narrowing the collection to the relevant timeframe and affected components produces a smaller, more targeted bundle that is easier to upload and more useful for troubleshooting.

questions.questionText: An administrator wants to collect logs specifically for a Nutanix Files issue. Which logbay option should be used?

options.text: Use the logbay component filter to specify Nutanix Files (minerva) components
Use the NCC Files health check with the --export-logs flag
SSH to each FSVM individually and manually copy the log files
Run kubectl logs on the FSVM pods to extract file service logs

questions.explanation: Logbay supports component-specific log collection. By specifying the Nutanix Files (minerva) component, the administrator collects only the relevant FSVM logs, reducing bundle size and focusing on the file service issue.

questions.questionText: After collecting logs with logbay and uploading to the support portal, the support engineer requests additional NCC output. What is the most efficient way to provide this?

options.text: Run ncc log_collector run_all to generate and bundle comprehensive NCC output for upload
Screenshot the NCC results in Prism and email them to support
Re-run logbay with the --include-ncc flag, which adds NCC output to the log bundle
Export the Prism Central alert history as a JSON file

questions.explanation: The `ncc log_collector run_all` command generates a comprehensive bundled output that includes health check results and diagnostic data, which can be uploaded to the support portal. This provides the support engineer with structured diagnostic data alongside the log collection.

questions.questionText: An administrator notices that a Nutanix Files server has been experiencing degraded performance after a recent AOS upgrade. NCC checks pass, but FSVM latency metrics are elevated. What should be investigated?

options.text: Whether the Files version is compatible with the new AOS version and if a Files upgrade is also required
Whether the Objects lifecycle policies need to be reconfigured for the new AOS version
Whether the Volume Group flash mode was disabled during the AOS upgrade
Whether the iSCSI CHAP secrets were rotated as part of the AOS upgrade

questions.explanation: After an AOS upgrade, Nutanix Files may require a corresponding upgrade to maintain compatibility. Version mismatches between AOS and Files can cause degraded performance or unexpected behavior. Checking the compatibility matrix is essential.

questions.questionText: An administrator receives alerts about high latency on a Nutanix Files share used by a video editing team. The FSVMs have adequate CPU and memory. NCC checks pass. What should be investigated next?

options.text: Storage tier utilization and whether hot data has spilled to the HDD capacity tier due to SSD tier exhaustion
The Objects bucket replication status to verify cross-site sync is not consuming bandwidth
The Volume Group path failover count to check for iSCSI instability
The Prism Central license tier to verify performance features are enabled

questions.explanation: When FSVM resources are adequate and NCC passes, the bottleneck may be at the storage tier level. If SSD capacity is exhausted, hot data spills to the HDD tier, significantly increasing latency for I/O-intensive workloads like video editing.

questions.questionText: A newly deployed Nutanix Objects store is showing "Service Unavailable" when clients attempt to access the S3 endpoint. The MSP cluster was deployed successfully. What should the administrator check first?

options.text: That all required MSP pods (including the envoy load balancer and S3 adapter pods) are running and healthy
That the FSVM minerva_nvm service has started on all nodes
That the Volume Group Data Services IP is configured on the Objects network
That the NCC disk health check has completed on all nodes

questions.explanation: A "Service Unavailable" response typically indicates that the S3-facing pods in the MSP cluster are not running correctly. The envoy load balancer and S3 adapter pods are critical components that must be healthy for the endpoint to serve requests.

questions.questionText: An administrator is troubleshooting Nutanix Objects and needs to view logs from a specific MSP pod. Which command should be used?

options.text: kubectl logs <pod-name> -n <namespace> from the MSP management context
afs info.get_logs <pod-name> from the FSVM
ncli volume-group get-logs for the pod name
logbay --component=msp-pod --name=<pod-name> from the CVM

questions.explanation: Since Nutanix Objects runs on the Kubernetes-based MSP platform, standard kubectl commands are used to view pod logs. The `kubectl logs` command with the appropriate pod name and namespace retrieves the container-level logs for diagnosis.
