questions.questionText: An administrator configures a Nutanix Volume Group for an external Oracle database server. After initial configuration, the database reports I/O errors during heavy write workloads. The iSCSI connection is stable. What should be investigated?

options.text: The Volume Group's storage container configuration, including replication factor and erasure coding overhead impacting write performance
The FSVM smart tiering policy that may be interfering with Volume Group writes
The Objects WORM retention policy that may be locking database blocks
The MSP pod resource limits that may be throttling Volume Group I/O

questions.explanation: I/O errors during heavy writes on a Volume Group can be caused by the underlying storage container configuration. High erasure coding overhead, combined with the replication factor, may exceed the available write throughput. Evaluating the container settings and potentially using RF2 instead of EC is recommended.

questions.questionText: An administrator has been troubleshooting a complex Nutanix Files issue involving multi-protocol access, intermittent FSVM health failures, and slow NFS performance. After exhausting local diagnostics, what is the recommended escalation approach?

options.text: Collect a logbay bundle including minerva logs, NCC output, and FSVM configuration, then upload to the Nutanix support portal with a detailed case description
Restart all FSVMs simultaneously and monitor if the issue reoccurs
Delete and redeploy the file server from the latest LCM bundle
Switch all clients to Objects S3 access to bypass the Files issues

questions.explanation: For complex multi-symptom issues, the proper escalation is to collect comprehensive diagnostics (logbay bundle with minerva components, NCC output, and FSVM configuration details), then open a support case with a detailed description. This gives the Nutanix support team the data needed for root cause analysis.

options.text: An on-premises VM deployed alongside each file server for analytics
A cloud-based SaaS analytics service for Files and Objects
A Prism Element plug-in that replaces File Analytics
A hardware appliance installed in the Nutanix cluster

questions.explanation: Nutanix Data Lens is a cloud-based SaaS offering that provides analytics, auditing, and security insights for both Nutanix Files and Objects environments. Unlike File Analytics, which runs as an on-premises VM, Data Lens operates from the cloud and supports multi-cluster visibility.

options.text: File Analytics must be uninstalled from the file server
The file server must have outbound HTTPS connectivity to the Data Lens cloud service
A dedicated Data Lens VM must be deployed on the same cluster
The file server must be running NFSv4 exclusively

questions.explanation: Data Lens is a cloud-based service, so the Nutanix Files environment must have outbound HTTPS (port 443) connectivity to reach the Data Lens cloud endpoint. File Analytics does not need to be removed first—both can coexist during migration.

options.text: Capacity trending analysis
Anomaly detection with configurable alert thresholds
Automated share replication to a secondary cluster
Scheduled snapshot creation based on IOPS patterns

questions.explanation: Data Lens uses anomaly detection to identify unusual file activity patterns—such as mass encryption, bulk renames, or rapid deletions—that may indicate a ransomware attack. Administrators can configure alert thresholds and receive notifications when suspicious activity is detected.

options.text: Smart Tiering report
Audit trails
Data Sync log
Prism Central task history

questions.explanation: Data Lens audit trails provide detailed records of file access, creation, modification, and deletion events on a per-user and per-share basis. The administrator can filter by time range and user to review exactly who accessed what files.

options.text: Data Lens supports NFSv3 while File Analytics does not
Data Lens provides a single pane of glass for analytics across multiple file servers and clusters
Data Lens eliminates the need for DNS configuration
Data Lens runs directly on the CVM and uses fewer resources

questions.explanation: Data Lens provides multi-cluster, multi-file-server visibility from a single cloud-based console. On-premises File Analytics is deployed per file server and only provides visibility into that single instance, making it harder to manage large-scale environments.

options.text: Data Lens only supports Nutanix Files and cannot be used with Objects
Data Lens can provide visibility into object store access patterns, bucket usage, and security posture
Objects must be upgraded to version 6.0 before Data Lens integration is possible
Data Lens for Objects requires deploying a separate MSP cluster

questions.explanation: Data Lens supports both Nutanix Files and Objects, providing analytics on access patterns, bucket utilization, and security auditing for object stores. This integration does not require a separate MSP cluster—it leverages the cloud-based SaaS architecture.

options.text: The file server is running too many FSVMs
A firewall is blocking outbound HTTPS traffic from the Prism Central or file server network
Data Lens requires a minimum of 5 file servers to activate
The NTP configuration on the cluster is set to manual mode

questions.explanation: Data Lens requires outbound HTTPS connectivity (port 443) from the environment to the cloud service. A firewall or proxy blocking this outbound traffic is the most common connectivity issue during onboarding.

options.text: Capacity forecasting
Data classification and PII scanning
Smart DR replication reports
FSVM performance monitoring

questions.explanation: Data Lens includes data classification capabilities that scan file content to identify PII such as Social Security numbers, credit card numbers, and other sensitive data patterns. This works across multiple file servers registered with the Data Lens service.

options.text: A data mining tool for analyzing storage performance metrics
An integrated secondary storage solution that combines Nutanix infrastructure with backup software
A CLI utility for extracting log files from Nutanix clusters
A tiering engine that moves cold data to external cloud providers

questions.explanation: Nutanix Mine is a turnkey integrated backup solution that combines Nutanix HCI infrastructure with supported third-party backup software to deliver a converged secondary storage appliance. It can use Nutanix Objects as its backend storage target.

options.text: Only Veeam
HYCU, Veeam, and Commvault
Veritas NetBackup and Cohesity exclusively
Any vendor that supports the S3 API

questions.explanation: Nutanix Mine has validated integrations with HYCU, Veeam, and Commvault as supported backup vendors. While other vendors may support S3-compatible storage, Mine's turnkey deployment and lifecycle management are specifically validated with these partners.

options.text: NFS v4.1
iSCSI
S3-compatible API
SMB 3.0

questions.explanation: When Nutanix Mine uses Objects as the backend storage target, backup data is written using the S3-compatible API. Objects provides an S3 endpoint that the backup software (HYCU, Veeam, or Commvault) uses to store and retrieve backup data.

options.text: It eliminates the need for Prism Central in the environment
It provides a converged solution that reduces infrastructure silos and simplifies backup management
It allows backup data to bypass the storage network entirely
It automatically creates read-only replicas on a public cloud provider

questions.explanation: Mine with Objects provides a converged backup infrastructure that eliminates the need for separate backup appliances and storage arrays. This reduces infrastructure silos, simplifies management through Prism, and leverages the scalability of Objects for long-term data retention.

options.text: A feature that replicates FSVMs across Nutanix clusters for file server HA
A capability that enables cross-site namespace and bucket access across multiple Objects deployments
A licensing model that pools Objects capacity across data centers
A method of federating Active Directory domains for Objects authentication

questions.explanation: Objects Federation enables cross-site access to namespaces and buckets across multiple Nutanix Objects deployments. It allows applications to access object data from a federated namespace regardless of which site stores the data, supporting multi-site architectures.

options.text: Objects replication policies
Objects Federation
Smart DR for Objects
Data Lens cross-site analytics

questions.explanation: Objects Federation enables a unified namespace across multiple Objects deployments, allowing applications at either data center to access objects through a single logical endpoint. This is distinct from replication, which copies data for DR purposes rather than unifying access.

options.text: Both sites must share the same Prism Central instance
Network connectivity and DNS resolution must be established between the Objects clusters at both sites
Both sites must run File Analytics version 3.4 or later
Federation requires identical hardware models at both sites

questions.explanation: Objects Federation requires reliable network connectivity and proper DNS resolution between the participating Objects clusters. Each site must be able to resolve the S3 endpoints of the other site, and sufficient bandwidth must be available for cross-site operations.

options.text: Replacing Smart DR for file server failover
Enabling a distributed application to read and write objects across geographically dispersed data centers
Consolidating all backup data onto a single Objects cluster
Converting NFS shares to S3 buckets automatically

questions.explanation: Objects Federation is ideal for distributed applications that need to access object data across multiple geographic locations. It allows seamless cross-site access so that applications at any federated site can interact with the same namespace, supporting global workloads and edge-to-core architectures.

options.text: A DNS subdomain automatically created for each bucket
A logical isolation boundary within an Objects deployment that contains buckets and provides multi-tenant separation
A storage pool reserved exclusively for WORM-locked data
A Prism Central category tag applied to Objects workers

questions.explanation: A namespace in Nutanix Objects provides a logical isolation boundary that groups buckets together and supports multi-tenancy. Each namespace has its own access policies, endpoint, and user management, allowing administrators to separate different teams or applications within a single Objects deployment.

options.text: Two separate Prism Central instances
Two separate namespaces within the Objects deployment
Two separate AOS clusters
Two separate storage containers in Prism Element

questions.explanation: Creating separate namespaces provides each business unit with its own isolated environment including independent access keys, bucket policies, and usage quotas. This enables multi-tenancy on a single Objects cluster without requiring separate infrastructure.

options.text: A namespace can span multiple Objects clusters by default
Each namespace has its own S3 endpoint URL and can have independent access policies
Only one namespace is allowed per Prism Central instance
Namespaces are only available when WORM is enabled on the cluster

questions.explanation: Each namespace in Objects has its own dedicated S3 endpoint URL and supports independent IAM policies, access keys, and bucket configurations. Multiple namespaces can be created per Objects deployment to isolate tenants, though each namespace is scoped to a single Objects instance.

options.text: To replicate data between two file servers for DR
To automatically move infrequently accessed (cold) data from high-performance storage to capacity-optimized storage
To compress all data on the file server regardless of access frequency
To synchronize user profiles between VDI sessions

questions.explanation: Smart Tiering in Nutanix Files automatically identifies infrequently accessed (cold) data and moves it from high-performance SSD/NVMe storage to more cost-effective capacity-tier storage (HDD or cloud). This optimizes storage costs while maintaining transparent access for users.

options.text: Standard Tiering
Smart Tiering
Advanced Tiering
Basic Tiering

questions.explanation: Advanced Tiering provides administrators with granular control over tiering policies, including custom rules for data movement based on age, access patterns, and file types. Standard Tiering uses default thresholds, while Smart Tiering uses automated intelligence. Advanced Tiering offers the most flexibility.

options.text: Smart Tiering
Standard Tiering
Advanced Tiering
Manual Tiering

questions.explanation: Standard Tiering uses predefined default thresholds for moving cold data to the capacity tier, requiring minimal configuration from the administrator. It is the simplest option for organizations that want basic tiering without custom policy management.

options.text: The user receives a "file not available" error and must contact the administrator
The file is transparently recalled to the performance tier and served to the user
The user must manually initiate a recall request through Prism Central
The file is served directly from the capacity tier with no recall

questions.explanation: When a user accesses a tiered file, Nutanix Files transparently recalls it from the capacity tier to the performance tier. The file is served to the user without requiring any manual intervention, though there may be a brief increase in access latency during the recall.

options.text: Smart Tiering requires a separate license; Standard Tiering is free
Smart Tiering uses machine learning and access pattern analytics to optimize data placement, while Standard Tiering uses fixed age-based thresholds
Standard Tiering supports NFS shares; Smart Tiering only supports SMB shares
Smart Tiering only works with Nutanix Objects; Standard Tiering works with Files

questions.explanation: Smart Tiering leverages analytics and access pattern intelligence to make dynamic data placement decisions, adapting to workload changes over time. Standard Tiering relies on simpler, fixed age-based thresholds (e.g., move files not accessed for X days) without the adaptive intelligence.

options.text: A feature that synchronizes virtual desktop images between Prism Central instances
A feature that synchronizes user profiles and home directories between file servers at different sites for VDI environments
A tool for benchmarking VDI storage performance on Nutanix clusters
A replication engine for Nutanix Volumes used by VDI desktops

questions.explanation: VDI Sync is a Nutanix Files feature designed for Virtual Desktop Infrastructure environments. It synchronizes user profiles and home directory data between file servers at different sites, ensuring that VDI users have consistent access to their profiles regardless of which site they connect to.

options.text: A single-site VDI deployment using local profiles on each desktop
A multi-site VDI environment where users may log in from different geographic locations and need consistent profile data
A backup scenario where VDI desktops are replicated to a DR site
An environment where all VDI users access the same shared folders

questions.explanation: VDI Sync is most valuable in multi-site VDI deployments where users may connect from different locations. It ensures that user profiles, settings, and home directory data are synchronized across sites, providing a consistent user experience regardless of the login location.

options.text: Distributed share
Standard share
Home share (home directory)
General-purpose SMB share with no specific type

questions.explanation: VDI Sync is typically configured with home shares (home directories) in Nutanix Files, which provide per-user isolated directories. These home shares store user profiles that VDI Sync replicates between file servers at different sites to maintain profile consistency.

options.text: A method of routing iSCSI traffic based on fully qualified domain names
An architecture that uses DNS subdomains or folder structures to provide unique FQDN paths for accessing different shares on a file server
A Prism Central feature for organizing file servers by domain name
A Smart DR configuration that maps DNS zones to replication targets

questions.explanation: FQDN-based pathing allows administrators to organize Nutanix Files share access using DNS subdomains or folder structures. Instead of a flat share namespace, administrators can create structured paths (e.g., `dept.fileserver.company.com`) to organize and access shares hierarchically.

options.text: The organization has no DNS infrastructure
The organization wants each department to have its own DNS-resolvable namespace (e.g., engineering.fs.company.com, finance.fs.company.com)
All shares must be accessed through a single IP address with no DNS dependency
The environment uses only NFS and does not support DNS-based names

questions.explanation: Subdomain-based FQDN pathing is ideal when the organization wants distinct, DNS-resolvable namespaces for different departments or organizational units. Each subdomain resolves to the file server and provides a clean hierarchical structure. This approach requires proper DNS wildcard or A record configuration.

options.text: A single A record for the file server with no additional records
Wildcard DNS records or individual A/CNAME records for each subdomain pointing to the file server
MX records for each share name on the file server
PTR records for all FSVM IP addresses only

questions.explanation: Subdomain-based FQDN pathing requires either wildcard DNS records (e.g., `*.fs.company.com`) or individual A/CNAME records for each subdomain. These records must resolve to the file server's client-side IP addresses so that clients can reach the correct share namespace.

options.text: A physical appliance that replaces Prism Central for cluster management
A cloud-hosted management plane that provides unified visibility and lifecycle management across multiple Nutanix environments
A command-line tool for configuring AOS storage containers
A Data Lens component deployed within the customer's data center

questions.explanation: Nutanix Central is a cloud-hosted management platform that provides a single pane of glass for managing and monitoring multiple Nutanix environments across sites and clouds. It extends beyond Prism Central by offering unified lifecycle management, alerting, and operational intelligence from the cloud.

options.text: Nutanix Central replaces Prism Central for all Files and Objects configuration
Nutanix Central provides centralized monitoring, alerting, and lifecycle management for Files and Objects across multiple clusters
Nutanix Central is only used for Volumes management and has no Files or Objects integration
Nutanix Central can only be used after Data Lens is disabled

questions.explanation: Nutanix Central integrates with Files and Objects deployments to provide centralized monitoring, health alerts, and lifecycle management across all registered clusters. Prism Central remains the primary configuration interface, while Nutanix Central adds the cloud-based multi-site operational layer.

options.text: Prism Element only
The Nutanix Central cloud console
The FSVM local console via SSH
Data Lens audit dashboard

questions.explanation: Nutanix Central provides cross-cluster compatibility checks and upgrade readiness alerts from its cloud console. Before upgrading Files, the administrator should verify that no compatibility issues exist between the Files version, AOS, Prism Central, and Nutanix Central integration requirements.

options.text: The secondary file server does not support SMB
DNS records were not updated to point to the secondary file server's IP addresses
Smart DR automatically disables all shares during failover
The primary site's Prism Central must be online for DNS to work

questions.explanation: After a Smart DR failover, DNS records must be updated (either manually or via scripted automation) to point to the secondary file server's client-side IP addresses. If DNS still resolves to the primary site's IPs, clients cannot reach the failed-over file server.

options.text: Whether the secondary site has network connectivity to the Active Directory domain controllers
Whether Data Lens is enabled on the secondary file server
Whether the secondary cluster has NFS exports enabled
Whether the Volumes service is running on the secondary cluster

questions.explanation: Active Directory authentication requires that the secondary file server can reach AD domain controllers. After failover, the administrator should verify network connectivity and DNS resolution to AD services at the secondary site, including checking that the file server's computer account and Kerberos trust are intact.

options.text: Data Lens must be reconfigured on the primary
Delta replication must complete to synchronize changes made at the secondary site back to the primary
All FSVMs at the secondary site must be powered off manually
The primary cluster's AOS must be upgraded to a newer version

questions.explanation: Before failback, Smart DR must perform delta replication to synchronize any data changes (new files, modifications, deletions) that occurred at the secondary site back to the primary file server. Only after this replication completes should DNS be updated and clients redirected to the primary.

options.text: Whether File Analytics is deployed at both sites
Network connectivity and firewall rules between the two Nutanix clusters
Whether both sites have identical FSVM counts
Whether WORM is enabled on any shares

questions.explanation: Smart DR replication requires reliable network connectivity between clusters. Firewall rules must permit the replication traffic between the file servers. Network issues (blocked ports, insufficient bandwidth, or routing problems) are the most common cause of replication failures.

options.text: Manually edit DNS records on the domain controller after each failover
Configure scripted DNS updates or use dynamic DNS integrated with the Smart DR failover workflow
Disable DNS and use IP addresses exclusively for share access
Deploy a secondary DNS server that only activates during failovers

questions.explanation: The recommended approach is to automate DNS updates using scripts or dynamic DNS that integrate with the Smart DR failover workflow. This ensures that DNS records are updated promptly when failover occurs, minimizing client downtime and eliminating the risk of manual errors.

options.text: The secondary cluster does not have sufficient SSD capacity
Profile directory paths or UNC paths reference the primary site's hostname, which no longer resolves correctly
Nutanix Objects must be restarted to enable profile sync
Smart DR does not support user profile replication

questions.explanation: Cross-cluster user profile issues after failover typically occur because profile paths (UNC paths in GPO or login scripts) reference the primary file server's hostname. If DNS is not updated or if hard-coded paths are used, profiles fail to load. The administrator should verify that all profile path references resolve correctly to the secondary server.

options.text: File Analytics must be completely uninstalled before Data Lens can be enabled
File Analytics and Data Lens can coexist temporarily during a migration period
Data Lens automatically replaces File Analytics with no administrator action required
Migration from File Analytics to Data Lens requires a full cluster rebuild

questions.explanation: File Analytics and Data Lens can coexist during a migration period, allowing administrators to validate Data Lens functionality before decommissioning File Analytics. This phased approach ensures continuity of monitoring and auditing during the transition.

options.text: Delete the File Analytics VM and proceed with the Files upgrade
Check the compatibility matrix for the required File Analytics version and upgrade File Analytics before or alongside the Files upgrade
Disable all shares and recreate them after the upgrade
Convert the File Analytics VM to a Data Lens instance manually

questions.explanation: The administrator should consult the Nutanix compatibility matrix to determine which File Analytics version is compatible with Files 5.0. File Analytics should be upgraded to a compatible version before or as part of the Files upgrade process to maintain analytics functionality.

options.text: File Analytics must be deployed on a separate Nutanix cluster from the file server
The File Analytics VM requires its own IP address on the client network and access to the file server's storage
File Analytics can only be deployed from Nutanix Central, not Prism Central
File Analytics requires a minimum of 16 FSVMs on the file server

questions.explanation: The File Analytics VM requires its own IP address on the client-side network and must have connectivity to the file server's internal storage network. It is deployed from Prism Central (or Prism Element, depending on version) as a separate VM on the same cluster as the file server.

options.text: Whether the Prism Central upgrade changed the outbound proxy or firewall configuration, blocking HTTPS to the Data Lens cloud service
Whether the FSVMs need more RAM allocated
Whether Smart DR was accidentally enabled during the upgrade
Whether Nutanix Objects versioning is interfering with Data Lens

questions.explanation: Prism Central upgrades may modify network configurations, proxy settings, or certificate trust chains that affect outbound HTTPS connectivity to Data Lens. The administrator should verify that outbound communication to the Data Lens cloud endpoint is still functional after the upgrade.

options.text: Smart Tiering
Lifecycle policy with an expiration rule
WORM retention policy
Bucket replication policy

questions.explanation: Objects lifecycle policies allow administrators to define rules that automatically expire (delete) objects after a specified number of days. Setting an expiration rule of 90 days on the bucket will automatically remove objects once they reach that age.

options.text: Versioning can be disabled on a bucket after it has been enabled, permanently deleting all previous versions
When versioning is enabled, each overwrite of an object creates a new version, and previous versions are retained until explicitly deleted or expired
Versioning is only available for buckets with WORM enabled
Versioning doubles the bucket's access key requirements

questions.explanation: When versioning is enabled on a bucket, every PUT operation that overwrites an existing object creates a new version. Previous versions are retained and accessible until they are explicitly deleted by the user or removed by a lifecycle expiration rule.

options.text: The object is moved to a recycle bin and can be recovered
The delete operation fails because the object is within the WORM retention period
The object is deleted but a log entry is created
The WORM policy is automatically overridden for administrators

questions.explanation: WORM compliance mode prevents any deletion or modification of objects until the retention period has expired. Since the object is only 30 days old (within the 365-day retention), the delete request is denied. This ensures regulatory compliance and data immutability.

options.text: Bucket policies and IAM policies are identical and interchangeable

Bucket policies are applied to a specific bucket and control access to that bucket's resources, while IAM policies are attached to users or groups and control what actions they can perform across the Objects environment
IAM policies only apply to NFS access; bucket policies apply to S3 access
Bucket policies require WORM to be enabled; IAM policies do not

questions.explanation: Bucket policies are resource-based and attached to individual buckets, defining who can access that bucket and what operations they can perform. IAM policies are identity-based and attached to users or groups, controlling their permissions across the entire Objects environment. Both work together to provide comprehensive access control.

options.text: Kerberos constrained delegation
RFC 2307 attributes in Active Directory for mapping Windows users to UNIX UIDs/GIDs
LDAP anonymous binding
NTLM passthrough from the NFS client

questions.explanation: Multi-protocol shares require identity mapping between Windows (SID-based) and UNIX (UID/GID-based) user identities. Nutanix Files uses RFC 2307 attributes configured in Active Directory to map Windows user accounts to corresponding UNIX UIDs and GIDs for consistent access control across SMB and NFS.

options.text: The NFS client does not support NFSv4
The SMB user's Active Directory account lacks RFC 2307 UID/GID mapping, so the NFS client cannot match the file owner
Multi-protocol shares only allow read access over NFS
The file was encrypted by BitLocker during creation

questions.explanation: If the SMB user's AD account does not have RFC 2307 UNIX attributes (UID/GID) configured, the file ownership cannot be properly mapped to a UNIX identity. When the NFS client tries to access the file, the permission check fails because the NFS-side identity mapping is incomplete.

options.text: A feature that lists all files regardless of user permissions
A feature that limits the files and folders displayed to only those the connected user has permission to access
A method of enumerating all Active Directory users who have accessed a share
A File Analytics report showing access frequency by file type

questions.explanation: Access-Based Enumeration (ABE) ensures that when users browse an SMB share, they only see files and folders they have permission to access. Files and folders the user cannot access are hidden from the directory listing, improving security and reducing user confusion.

options.text: Data Lens malware scanning module
ICAP (Internet Content Adaptation Protocol) integration with a third-party antivirus server
Built-in ClamAV on the FSVM
Windows Defender running on the Prism Central VM

questions.explanation: Nutanix Files supports ICAP integration for antivirus scanning. When configured, files written to SMB shares are sent to an ICAP-compatible antivirus server (such as Symantec, McAfee, or Trend Micro) for scanning. Infected files can be quarantined or blocked based on the scan results.

options.text: Share permissions can only be managed from Prism Element
Administrators can delegate share-level management to specific AD users through the share admin role, allowing them to manage permissions without full Prism access
Share permissions are inherited from the storage container and cannot be customized
NFS exports do not support any form of access control

questions.explanation: Nutanix Files supports share-level delegation through the share admin role. An AD user designated as share admin can manage permissions (ACLs) on that share without needing access to Prism Central or Prism Element, enabling distributed management in large organizations.

options.text: Mount the VG as an NFS export inside the VM
Attach the Volume Group directly to the VM through Prism Central or the AHV API
Configure iSCSI initiator software inside the VM and connect to the VG target
Use SMB share mounting to connect the VM to the VG

questions.explanation: For VMs running on AHV, Volume Groups can be directly attached to the VM through Prism Central (or the AHV/Acropolis API). This provides native block-level access without requiring iSCSI initiator configuration inside the guest VM, simplifying management and improving performance.

options.text: Adding a volume requires the Volume Group to be detached from all consumers first
New volumes can be added to a Volume Group online without disrupting existing connected hosts
Each Volume Group can only contain a single volume
Volumes can only be added using the nCLI, not Prism

questions.explanation: New volumes (virtual disks) can be added to a Volume Group without detaching it from connected hosts. This is an online operation, and the new capacity becomes available to the host after a rescan of the iSCSI targets or refresh of the direct-attach configuration.

options.text: There is no difference; they are the same feature
Cluster-level whitelists control which initiators can discover iSCSI targets on the cluster, while volume-level whitelists control which initiators can connect to a specific Volume Group
Volume-level whitelists only apply to NFS access
Cluster-level whitelists require CHAP; volume-level whitelists do not

questions.explanation: Cluster-level iSCSI whitelists define which initiator IQNs or IP addresses can discover targets on the cluster's Data Services IP. Volume-level whitelists provide more granular control by restricting access to individual Volume Groups. For security, best practice is to use both levels to implement defense in depth.

options.text: Whether the CHAP secret on the initiator matches the secret configured on the Nutanix Volume Group
Whether Data Lens is blocking the iSCSI connection
Whether the Volume Group has versioning enabled
Whether the server's NFS client is interfering with iSCSI

questions.explanation: When CHAP is enabled, both the initiator (server) and target (Nutanix VG) must have matching CHAP credentials. If the CHAP secret on the server's iSCSI initiator does not match what is configured on the Volume Group in Prism, authentication fails and the connection is rejected.

options.text: WORM on the Volume Group
Microsoft MPIO (Multipath I/O) with the correct load-balancing policy to aggregate the paths into a single device
A second Data Services IP address on a different subnet
CHAP on only one of the two paths

questions.explanation: When multiple iSCSI paths exist to the same Volume Group, the Windows server may see duplicate disk entries. Installing and configuring Microsoft MPIO with the iSCSI DSM (Device Specific Module) and the correct load-balancing policy (e.g., Round Robin) combines the multiple paths into a single multipathed device.

options.text: Reboot the Nutanix cluster
Rescan the iSCSI targets on the Linux server using `iscsiadm` session rescan and then extend the filesystem or LVM volume
Reconfigure Smart DR on the Volume Group
Reinstall the Linux iSCSI initiator software

questions.explanation: When new volumes are added or existing volumes are expanded in a VG, the iSCSI initiator on the host must rescan to discover the changes. On Linux, running `iscsiadm -m session --rescan` forces a target rescan. After the OS recognizes the new capacity, the filesystem or LVM volume must be extended.

options.text: The DNS TTL on the server
The iSCSI login timeout and node replacement timeout on the initiator, along with target portal group settings on the Nutanix side
The NFS export timeout on the file server
The WORM retention period on the Volume Group

questions.explanation: Intermittent iSCSI disconnections can be caused by aggressive timeout settings on the initiator side. The administrator should review the iSCSI login timeout, replacement timeout, and retry parameters. On the Nutanix side, verifying the Data Services IP availability and target portal group configuration is also important.

options.text: Only PUT and GET operations
Standard S3 operations including PUT, GET, DELETE, HEAD, LIST, multipart uploads, and bucket operations
Only operations related to static website hosting
Only operations initiated from Prism Central

questions.explanation: Nutanix Objects provides broad S3 API compatibility, supporting standard operations including PUT/GET/DELETE/HEAD for objects, LIST for buckets, multipart uploads for large files, bucket lifecycle management, and versioning operations. This allows existing S3-compatible applications to work with minimal changes.

options.text: Ping the Prism Element VIP from the application server
Use an S3-compatible CLI tool (such as `aws s3 ls --endpoint-url`) with valid access keys to list buckets on the Objects endpoint
Verify that the FSVM is responding to SMB requests
Check whether File Analytics is receiving data from the Objects deployment

questions.explanation: The most effective way to validate Objects endpoint connectivity is to use an S3-compatible tool (such as the AWS CLI configured with `--endpoint-url`) with valid access keys to perform a simple operation like listing buckets. A successful response confirms DNS resolution, network connectivity, authentication, and API functionality.

options.text: `ncli` on the CVM
`kubectl` commands to inspect pod logs and describe the failing pods
`iscsiadm` on the Objects worker nodes
`nfs-utils` diagnostics

questions.explanation: Nutanix Objects runs on the Microservices Platform (MSP) using Kubernetes. When pods are failing, `kubectl describe pod <pod-name>` and `kubectl logs <pod-name>` are the primary diagnostic tools for inspecting pod status, events, and application logs to identify the root cause.

options.text: Whether WORM is enabled on the source bucket
Network connectivity and bandwidth between the two Objects clusters, and whether the replication endpoint DNS name resolves correctly at the primary site
Whether File Analytics is installed at the secondary site
Whether the secondary site has Smart Tiering enabled

questions.explanation: Cross-site Objects replication requires reliable network connectivity between clusters and correct DNS resolution of the remote Objects endpoint. The administrator should verify that the primary cluster can resolve and reach the secondary cluster's S3 endpoint, and that sufficient network bandwidth is available for replication traffic.

options.text: The bucket policy explicitly denies the requested operation, or the IAM policy for the user does not grant the required permissions
The Objects cluster is running out of SSD capacity
NFSv4 ACLs are conflicting with the S3 request
The FSVM hosting the bucket is in maintenance mode

questions.explanation: HTTP 403 errors with valid credentials typically indicate a policy-based denial. Either the bucket policy contains an explicit deny for the operation, or the user's IAM policy does not include the required action (e.g., `s3:PutObject`, `s3:GetObject`). Both bucket policies and IAM policies must allow the operation.

options.text: Distributed shares only support NFS; standard shares only support SMB
A distributed share spreads data across all FSVMs for higher throughput and parallel access, while a standard share is hosted on a single FSVM
Standard shares support larger file sizes than distributed shares
Distributed shares require Nutanix Objects as a backend

questions.explanation: A distributed share (also called a sharded share) distributes top-level directories across all FSVMs, enabling parallel I/O and higher aggregate throughput. A standard share is hosted by a single FSVM, which can become a bottleneck for high-throughput workloads but is simpler for light-usage scenarios.

options.text: There is no impact to either share type during upgrades

Distributed share clients experience brief, transparent reconnections as each FSVM is upgraded sequentially, while standard share clients may experience a single longer disruption when the owning FSVM is upgraded
Standard shares are not affected; only distributed shares experience downtime
Both share types require all clients to disconnect before the upgrade begins

questions.explanation: During a rolling upgrade, FSVMs are upgraded one at a time. Distributed share clients may see brief reconnections as each FSVM handling a portion of the share is upgraded. Standard share clients experience disruption only when the single FSVM hosting their share is upgraded, but that disruption may be longer since all share I/O depends on that one FSVM.

options.text: NTP is only needed for log timestamp formatting
Kerberos authentication requires time synchronization within 5 minutes between the file server, Active Directory domain controllers, and clients; time skew causes authentication failures
NTP controls the speed of data replication in Smart DR
NTP is required only for NFS shares, not SMB shares

questions.explanation: Kerberos authentication, used by SMB/CIFS for domain-joined clients, requires that the time difference between the file server (FSVMs), Active Directory domain controllers, and clients is within 5 minutes. NTP synchronization prevents Kerberos ticket validation failures due to clock skew.

options.text: All file shares become permanently unavailable
The failed FSVM's workload is redistributed to the remaining healthy FSVMs, and clients experience a brief disruption before reconnecting
A new FSVM is automatically deployed to replace the failed one within seconds
FSVM HA only protects NFS shares, not SMB shares

questions.explanation: When an FSVM fails in a deployment with 3+ FSVMs, the HA mechanism redistributes the failed FSVM's share ownership and IP addresses to the surviving FSVMs. SMB clients using continuous availability experience a brief transparent failover, while standard connections reconnect automatically after a short interruption.

options.text: NFSv2 only
NFSv3 and NFSv4.0/v4.1
Only NFSv4.2
NFSv3 only, with NFSv4 planned for a future release

questions.explanation: Nutanix Files 5.0 supports both NFSv3 and NFSv4.0/v4.1. NFSv3 is commonly used for VMware datastores and legacy workloads, while NFSv4 provides enhanced security features including Kerberos authentication (krb5, krb5i, krb5p) and improved locking mechanisms.

options.text: sys (AUTH_SYS)
krb5 (authentication only)
krb5i (authentication + integrity)
krb5p (authentication + integrity + privacy/encryption)

questions.explanation: The `krb5i` security flavor provides Kerberos authentication plus data integrity checking (checksums) for NFS traffic. `krb5` provides authentication only, while `krb5p` adds encryption on top of authentication and integrity. `AUTH_SYS` provides no Kerberos security.

options.text: A Prism Element widget for monitoring CVM health
A dedicated management interface within Prism Central for configuring and managing individual file server settings, shares, and policies
A third-party application installed on a Windows server
A Data Lens component for generating file server reports

questions.explanation: File Server Manager is a management interface accessible from Prism Central that provides file-server-specific configuration and monitoring. Administrators use it to create and manage shares, configure quotas, set up alerts, and perform file server maintenance tasks directly from the Prism Central console.

options.text: Prism Element storage container view
File Server Manager in Prism Central, which displays per-share and per-FSVM performance metrics
Nutanix Objects management console
The AHV host dashboard

questions.explanation: File Server Manager in Prism Central provides detailed per-share and per-FSVM performance metrics including IOPS, throughput, and latency. This enables administrators to identify performance hotspots at the individual share level and determine whether scaling or rebalancing is needed.

options.text: File Server Manager is a standalone application that does not require Prism Central
File Server Manager is accessed through Prism Central and provides file-server-specific management capabilities that are not available in Prism Element alone
Prism Element provides all file server management features, making File Server Manager redundant
File Server Manager can only be accessed through Nutanix Central, not Prism Central

questions.explanation: File Server Manager is integrated into Prism Central and provides specialized file server management capabilities—such as share creation, quota management, and per-share metrics—that are not available in Prism Element. Prism Element provides cluster-level management but lacks the granular file server controls of File Server Manager.

options.text: Self-Service Restore (SSR) snapshots taken every 24 hours
Smart DR with a replication schedule set to replicate every 60 minutes, combined with a documented failover procedure
Data Lens audit trails configured to retain 30 days of data
Manual file copy to an NFS export on another cluster nightly

questions.explanation: Smart DR with an hourly replication schedule meets the 1-hour RPO requirement. Combined with a documented and tested failover procedure that can be executed within 4 hours, this meets both RPO and RTO targets. SSR snapshots provide point-in-time recovery but are not a cross-site DR solution.

options.text: A standard SMB share and a separate NFS export pointing to the same path
A multi-protocol share with both SMB and NFS access enabled and RFC 2307 identity mapping configured
A distributed share with ICAP integration
Two separate file servers, one for SMB and one for NFS

questions.explanation: A multi-protocol share enables both SMB and NFS access to the same underlying data. RFC 2307 identity mapping in Active Directory must be configured to map Windows SIDs to UNIX UIDs/GIDs, ensuring consistent file ownership and permissions across both protocols.

options.text: A feature that synchronizes Objects bucket data to Files shares
A feature that provides one-way or bi-directional synchronization of file data between Nutanix Files and external NFS/SMB targets or cloud storage
A Smart DR sub-feature that only syncs metadata
A Data Lens reporting feature that syncs analytics data to the cloud

questions.explanation: Data Sync in Nutanix Files provides synchronization of file data to and from external storage targets. It supports syncing to external NFS/SMB targets or cloud storage, enabling hybrid cloud workflows and data mobility between Nutanix Files and other storage systems.

options.text: The nested export automatically inherits all permissions from the parent export with no option to override
The nested export can have its own independent export permissions, but file-level POSIX ACLs are inherited from the parent unless explicitly modified
Nested exports are not supported in Nutanix Files
The parent export is automatically deleted when a nested export is created

questions.explanation: Nested exports in Nutanix Files can have their own export-level access controls (client IP restrictions, squash settings). However, the underlying file-level POSIX permissions are inherited from the parent directory structure unless an administrator explicitly changes them at the file or directory level.

options.text: All shares are taken offline simultaneously during the entire upgrade process
Distributed shares experience minimal impact as FSVMs are upgraded in a rolling fashion, while standard shares experience brief downtime when their hosting FSVM is upgraded
Only NFS exports are affected during upgrades; SMB shares remain fully available
Share types have no impact on upgrade behavior

questions.explanation: Nutanix Files performs rolling upgrades one FSVM at a time. Distributed shares, which spread data across FSVMs, maintain most of their capacity during the process with brief reconnections. Standard shares, hosted on a single FSVM, experience a brief outage when their specific FSVM is being upgraded.

options.text: On each individual Objects worker VM via SSH
In the Objects configuration within Prism Central, where the S3 endpoint SSL certificate can be uploaded and applied
In Prism Element under the storage container settings
In Data Lens under the security settings

questions.explanation: Custom SSL/TLS certificates for the Objects S3 endpoint are configured through Prism Central in the Objects management section. The administrator uploads the certificate and private key, and Prism Central applies it to the Objects endpoint, enabling trusted HTTPS connections for S3 client applications.

options.text: Verify that the secondary file server has joined the correct Active Directory domain and that Group Policy profile path settings reference a DNS name (not a hardcoded IP) that has been updated for the secondary site
Reinstall Nutanix Objects at the secondary site
Delete all user profiles and ask users to recreate them
Disable Smart DR and use manual file copy instead

questions.explanation: Cross-cluster profile issues after failover are typically caused by two factors: the secondary file server not being properly joined to the AD domain (preventing authentication), and profile path references in Group Policy using hardcoded IPs or hostnames that still point to the primary site. Ensuring proper AD integration and DNS-based profile paths resolves both issues.
